topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Criteria** are the explicit set of functional, structural, economic, and operational requirements that a machine element must satisfy. They form the quantitative and qualitative constraints (loads, speeds, life expectancy, environment, materials, manufacturing feasibility, cost, safety, standards) that govern every subsequent decision in the design synthesis and analysis loop.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, *Design Criteria* sits at the very top of the design hierarchy — it is the **input specification** to the iterative design procedure. Without a clearly defined criterion, the designer cannot select materials, calculate stresses, or determine dimensions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are commissioning an architect to build a bridge between two cliffs. Before the architect draws a single line, you must answer: *How heavy are the trucks that will cross it? Will it face salty sea air? What is the budget? How many decades must it last?* Those answers are the **design criteria** of the bridge.

For a machine element, the same logic applies: every numerical answer (shaft diameter, gear tooth width, bolt size) is a *consequence* of the design criteria chosen at the start.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Design criteria encompass *strength, stiffness, wear resistance, corrosion resistance, manufacturability, economy, reliability, and standardization*. These are the pillars of all machine element design problems in ESE.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol / Term | Standard Value (SI) | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| Density of Steel ($\rho$) | **7850 kg/m³** | Mass / weight calculations |
| Density of Cast Iron ($\rho$) | **7200 kg/m³** | Flywheel / housing design |
| Young's Modulus of Steel ($E$) | **210 GPa** | Deflection / stiffness |
| Young's Modulus of Cast Iron ($E$) | **100 – 150 GPa** | Deflection / stiffness |
| Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) for steel | **0.30** | Lateral strain problems |
| Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) | **9.81 m/s²** | Weight calculations |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength ($S_{ut}$) | Material property | Static strength check |

### 1.4 Categories of Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Principal Types of Engineering Design**
> 1. **Adaptive Design** — Minor changes to an existing product to suit a new requirement (most common in industry, ~70% of real designs).
> 2. **Developmental Design** — Significant modifications or improvements based on feedback, R&D, or new technology.
> 3. **New (Original / Innovative) Design** — A completely novel design created from first principles (rare, e.g., first jet engine, first CNC machine).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Design Procedure (Logical Flow)

A machine element design always follows a structured sequence:

1. **Need Identification** — Identify the problem from a market gap or functional requirement.
2. **Problem Definition** — State the design criteria clearly (load, speed, life, environment).
3. **Synthesis** — Propose a layout, select material, and choose manufacturing process.
4. **Analysis** — Apply mechanics of materials (stress, strain, deflection, fatigue).
5. **Optimization** — Iterate to minimize weight, cost, or size while satisfying constraints.
6. **Evaluation** — Compare against codes/standards (IS, ISO, AGMA, DIN, ASME).
7. **Production** — Release drawings and specifications.

### 2.2 Fundamental Design Criteria (The Nine Pillars)

These pillars are tested repeatedly in KTU examinations:

- **Strength** — Ability to resist applied loads without yielding or fracturing.
- **Stiffness** — Ability to resist deformation under load.
- **Wear Resistance** — Resistance to surface damage during relative motion.
- **Corrosion Resistance** — Resistance to environmental degradation.
- **Manufacturability** — Ease of casting, forging, machining, or forming.
- **Economy** — Minimum cost over the product life cycle.
- **Reliability** — Probability of performing intended function under stated conditions.
- **Standardization** — Use of standard parts, tolerances, and fits.
- **Safety** — Inclusion of factor of safety against failure.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Criterion

| Criterion | The 'Why' | Real-world Failure if Ignored |
|---|---|---|
| Strength | Prevent plastic deformation / fracture | Shaft snaps, bolt shears, gear tooth breaks |
| Stiffness | Maintain dimensional accuracy | Shaft deflects excessively, bearing misaligns |
| Wear Resistance | Prolong service life | Cam profile erodes, brake pad disintegrates |
| Corrosion Resistance | Survive environment | Rust pits fatigue crack initiation |
| Manufacturability | Produce at scale | Tolerances unachievable, cost skyrockets |
| Economy | Compete in market | Product never commercialized |
| Reliability | Customer trust | Field failures, warranty costs |
| Standardization | Interchangeability, lower cost | Custom bearings, custom fasteners |
| Safety | Protect human life | Catastrophic failure, lawsuits |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR DESIGN CRITERIA**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Direct (normal) stress | Pa |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{J} \cdot r = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Shear stress in circular shaft | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Bending stress in circular shaft | Pa |
| 4 | $FOS = \dfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_{design}} \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{S_{y}}{\sigma_{design}}$ | Factor of Safety (yield or ultimate based) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Axial deflection | m |
| 6 | $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{JG}$ | Angular twist of shaft | rad |
| 7 | $y_{max} = \dfrac{w L^{4}}{8EI}$ | Maximum deflection of simply supported beam with UDL | m |
| 8 | $\sigma_{von} = \sqrt{\sigma^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}$ | Von-Mises equivalent stress | Pa |
| 9 | $P_{von} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\left(\sigma_{1}-\sigma_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\sigma_{2}-\sigma_{3}\right)^{2}+\left(\sigma_{3}-\sigma_{1}\right)^{2}\right]$ | Principal stress form of Von-Mises | Pa |
| 10 | $S_{e} = k_{a} \cdot k_{b} \cdot k_{c} \cdot k_{d} \cdot k_{e} \cdot k_{f} \cdot S_{e}'$ | Modified endurance limit | Pa |
| 11 | $S_{e}' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa | Uncorrected endurance limit | Pa |
| 12 | $S_{e}' = 700$ MPa for $S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa | Uncorrected endurance limit | Pa |

> **Note:** *Pipe symbol banned inside table rows.* Vertical bars (as in absolute values) have been replaced with named parameters or word descriptions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design criteria framework is the backbone of every mechanical, civil, aerospace, and automotive component. From the **crankshaft of a Maruti engine** (designed for fatigue endurance of ~$10^{8}$ cycles) to the **reactor pressure vessel** (designed for creep rupture), the chosen criteria determine the safety factor and the dimensions. In the KTU context, almost every Part B 14-mark question begins with "Given the design criteria ... design a shaft/gear/spring/belt/bolt."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Bending Moment from Combined Loading

When a shaft carries both bending and torsion, the most common design criterion is the **equivalent bending moment** approach using a failure theory (typically *Maximum Shear Stress Theory* or *Von-Mises Theory*).

**Starting point:**
A circular shaft of diameter $d$ is subjected to a bending moment $M$ and a torque $T$. The bending stress and torsional shear stress at the outer fiber are:

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{32M}{\pi d^{3}} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum principal stresses in the element:**

Since the outer fiber is in a state of plane stress with $\sigma_{x} = \sigma_{b}$, $\sigma_{y} = 0$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_{b}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{b}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca):**

Tresca theory states that failure occurs when the maximum shear stress reaches the shear yield stress:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{1} - \sigma_{2}}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{b}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}} = \frac{S_{y}}{2 \cdot N}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions for $\sigma_{b}$ and $\tau$:**

$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{16M}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}} = \frac{S_{y}}{2N}$$

$$\frac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \frac{S_{y}}{2N}$$

**Step 4 — Define the equivalent bending moment:**

Let $M_{e}$ be a fictitious bending moment which, acting alone, produces the same maximum stress as the combined loading:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 M_{e}}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{S_{y}}{N}$$

Equating with the combined case:

$$\frac{32 M_{e}}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form expression:**

$$\boxed{M_{e} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}}$$

This is the **ASME equivalent bending moment**, universally used in shaft design.

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Torque (Alternative Expression)

For components where torque is the dominant variable, we may analogously define:

$$\boxed{T_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Von-Mises Equivalent Stress

Using the distortion-energy theory, the equivalent stress is:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_{b}^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}$$

Substituting the stress expressions:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32M}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2} + 3\left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}}$$

Factoring $\dfrac{32}{\pi d^{3}}$:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \frac{32}{\pi d^{3}}\sqrt{M^{2} + \frac{3}{4}T^{2}}$$

Defining the **Von-Mises equivalent bending moment**:

$$\boxed{M_{e,VM} = \sqrt{M^{2} + \tfrac{3}{4}T^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examinations accept both Tresca ($M_{e} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{M^{2}+T^{2}}$) and Von-Mises ($M_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2}+0.75T^{2}}$) unless the question specifically mentions the theory. The ASME shaft design code uses Tresca; many European textbooks prefer Von-Mises.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Failure Theory

| Theory | Conservative? | When to Use | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Normal Stress | No | Brittle materials (cast iron, concrete) | $\sigma_{1} = \dfrac{S_{ut}}{N}$ |
| Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) | Yes (more conservative) | Ductile, simple analysis | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{S_{sy}}{N}$ |
| Von-Mises (Distortion Energy) | Slightly less conservative than Tresca | Ductile, modern design | $\sigma_{VM} = \dfrac{S_{y}}{N}$ |
| Coulomb-Mohr | No | Brittle with both $\sigma_{t} \neq \sigma_{c}$ | Two-equation envelope |

### 3.5 Worked Example (Establishing the Criterion)

**Problem:** A steel shaft ($S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_{y} = 380$ MPa) carries $M = 1.2$ kN·m bending moment and $T = 0.9$ kN·m torque. Using ASME equivalent bending moment, determine the required shaft diameter with $FOS = 3$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $M_{e}$:

$$M_{e} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(1200)^{2} + (900)^{2}} \quad \text{N·m}$$

$$M_{e} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{6} + 0.81 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2.25 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$M_{e} = \frac{1}{2}(1500) = 750 \text{ N·m} = 0.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

Step 2 — Allowable stress with factor of safety:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{S_{y}}{N} = \frac{380}{3} = 126.67 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 3 — Apply bending formula:

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{32 M_{e}}{\pi d^{3}} = \sigma_{allow}$$

$$\frac{32 \times 0.75 \times 10^{6}}{\pi d^{3}} = 126.67$$

$$d^{3} = \frac{32 \times 0.75 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 126.67} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{397.91}$$

$$d^{3} = 60323 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$d = \sqrt[3]{60323} = 39.23 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4 — Round up to standard:

$$\boxed{d = 40 \text{ mm (adopt standard diameter)}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Design Procedure with Criteria Inputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need Identification] --> B[Define Design Criteria]
    B --> C1[Strength]
    B --> C2[Stiffness]
    B --> C3[Wear Resistance]
    B --> C4[Corrosion Resistance]
    B --> C5[Manufacturability]
    B --> C6[Economy]
    B --> C7[Reliability]
    B --> C8[Standardization]
    B --> C9[Safety and FOS]
    C1 --> D[Select Material]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    C8 --> D
    C9 --> D
    D --> E[Synthesis of Geometry]
    E --> F[Apply Failure Theory]
    F --> G[Compute Stresses and Deflections]
    G --> H{All Criteria Satisfied?}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| I[Finalize Dimensions]
    I --> J[Compare to IS or ISO Standards]
    J --> K[Documentation and Drawing]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Design Criteria Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F[Functional Criteria]
        F1[Load Capacity]
        F2[Speed and Motion]
        F3[Life Expectancy]
    end
    subgraph S[Structural Criteria]
        S1[Strength]
        S2[Stiffness]
        S3[Stability]
    end
    subgraph M[Material Criteria]
        M1[Wear Resistance]
        M2[Corrosion Resistance]
        M3[Fatigue Strength]
    end
    subgraph P[Process Criteria]
        P1[Manufacturability]
        P2[Standardization]
        P3[Assembly]
    end
    subgraph E[Economic Criteria]
        E1[Initial Cost]
        E2[Lifecycle Cost]
        E3[Maintainability]
    end
    F --> S
    S --> M
    M --> P
    P --> E
    E --> OUT[Optimal Design Solution]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Factor of Safety Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Start: Loading Analysis] --> Q1{Static or Dynamic Load?}
    Q1 -->|Static| Q2{Ductile or Brittle Material?}
    Q1 -->|Dynamic| Q3{Finite or Infinite Life?}
    Q2 -->|Ductile| F1[Use Sy divided by N]
    Q2 -->|Brittle| F2[Use Sut divided by N]
    Q3 -->|Finite| F3[Use Goodman or Soderberg]
    Q3 -->|Infinite| F4[Use Se divided by N]
    F1 --> V[Verify Deflection and Wear]
    F2 --> V
    F3 --> V
    F4 --> V
    V --> DONE[FOS Selected]
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture — Information Flow in a Typical Design Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Given: Load, Speed, Material] --> IN2[Given: Life and Reliability Targets]
    IN1 --> CALC[Design Calculations]
    IN2 --> CALC
    IN1 --> CHECK1[Strength Check]
    IN1 --> CHECK2[Stiffness Check]
    IN1 --> CHECK3[Wear Check]
    IN1 --> CHECK4[Buckling or Stability Check]
    CHECK1 --> OUT[Recommended Diameter or Cross Section]
    CHECK2 --> OUT
    CHECK3 --> OUT
    CHECK4 --> OUT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*List any six criteria that a machine designer must consider while designing a machine element.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **Strength** — Ability to withstand applied loads without failure.
2. **Stiffness** — Resistance to elastic deformation.
3. **Wear resistance** — Surface durability under sliding or rolling contact.
4. **Corrosion resistance** — Resistance to environmental and chemical attack.
5. **Manufacturability** — Ease of production using available processes.
6. **Economy** — Minimum initial and operational cost.
7. **Reliability and standardization** (bonus points).

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*Define "Factor of Safety" and explain how it influences the choice of design criteria.*

**Model Answer:**

Factor of Safety (FOS), denoted $N$, is the ratio of the material's failure strength (yield $S_{y}$ for ductile, ultimate $S_{ut}$ for brittle) to the allowable working stress:

$$N = \frac{S_{y}}{\sigma_{allow}} \quad \text{or} \quad N = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

**Influence on design criteria:**
- Higher $N$ → larger cross-section → heavier but safer.
- Lower $N$ → compact design → risk of failure.
- Choice depends on **load predictability**, **consequence of failure**, **material uniformity**, **inspection frequency**, and **operating environment** (per *Shigley / V.B. Bhandari* guidelines).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q(A).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]
(a) *Explain the step-by-step procedure for the design of a machine element. List the primary and secondary design criteria.* **(7 marks)**
(b) *A shaft transmits 20 kW power at 250 rpm. It carries a bending moment of 600 N·m and a torque equivalent to the full power transmission. Using ASME equivalent bending moment theory and a factor of safety of 3, design the shaft diameter for a material with $S_{y} = 360$ MPa.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Procedure (7 marks):**

1. Identification of need.
2. Problem definition and criteria specification.
3. Synthesis — propose a conceptual design and select material.
4. Analysis — stress, deflection, fatigue, thermal, wear.
5. Optimization — iterate to minimize weight, size, cost.
6. Evaluation — compare with codes and standards (IS, ISO, ASME).
7. Detailed drawings, BOM, and documentation.

**Primary criteria:** strength, stiffness, wear resistance.
**Secondary criteria:** corrosion resistance, noise, vibration, manufacturability, cost.

**(b) Shaft Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute torque from power:**

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 250} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{1570.8}$$

$$T = 763.94 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute equivalent bending moment (ASME):**

$$M_{e} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(600)^{2} + (763.94)^{2}}$$

$$M_{e} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{360{,}000 + 583{,}608} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{943{,}608}$$

$$M_{e} = \frac{1}{2}(971.39) = 485.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Allowable stress:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{S_{y}}{N} = \frac{360}{3} = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Shaft diameter from bending equation:**

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{32 M_{e}}{\pi d^{3}} \le \sigma_{allow}$$

$$d^{3} \ge \frac{32 \times 485.7 \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times 120}$$

$$d^{3} \ge \frac{15{,}542{,}400}{376.99} = 41{,}225 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$d \ge \sqrt[3]{41{,}225} = 34.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Adopt standard diameter:**

$$\boxed{d = 35 \text{ mm (standard preferred)}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q(B).** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]
(a) *Differentiate between adaptive, developmental, and new design. Give two real-world examples for each.* **(7 marks)**
(b) *A circular shaft carries a bending moment of 2 kN·m and a torque of 1.5 kN·m. The shaft is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 650$ MPa and $S_{y} = 410$ MPa. Using the Von-Mises failure theory and a factor of safety of 2.5, determine the shaft diameter. Use the modification factor $S_{e}' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Design (7 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adaptive | Minor modification to existing product | New colour variant of a car | Modified handle of a screwdriver |
| Developmental | Significant R&D-based improvement | Maruti Swift (new gen) | Bosch power tool upgrade |
| New (Original) | Fresh design from first principles | First jet engine | First 3D-printed prosthetic limb |

**(b) Shaft Design — Von-Mises (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Equivalent bending moment (Von-Mises):**

$$M_{e,VM} = \sqrt{M^{2} + 0.75 \, T^{2}} = \sqrt{(2{,}000{,}000)^{2} + 0.75 \times (1{,}500{,}000)^{2}} \quad \text{N·mm}$$

$$M_{e,VM} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{12} + 1.6875 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{5.6875 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$M_{e,VM} = 2.385 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Endurance limit (uncorrected):**

$$S_{e}' = 0.5 \times 650 = 325 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Allowable stress (using yield for static-equivalent):**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{S_{y}}{N} = \frac{410}{2.5} = 164 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_{allow} < S_{e}'$, the design is **safe for infinite life** as well as static loading.

**Step 4 — Shaft diameter:**

$$d^{3} \ge \frac{32 \times 2.385 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 164}$$

$$d^{3} \ge \frac{76.32 \times 10^{6}}{515.22} = 148{,}128 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$d \ge \sqrt[3]{148{,}128} = 52.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Adopt standard diameter:**

$$\boxed{d = 55 \text{ mm (standard preferred)}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Never mix units** — kW vs W, N vs kN, mm vs m. Most valuation deductions (1–2 marks) come from unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Always state which failure theory you are using** — Tresca (ASME) vs Von-Mises. Examiners deduct full 2 marks if the theory is omitted.
> 3. **Round up the diameter, not down** — adopt the next standard size (e.g., 34.6 mm → 35 mm).
> 4. **Show the substitution step explicitly** — do not jump from formula to numerical answer.
> 5. **Always cross-check against endurance limit** when the loading is fluctuating.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — DESIGN CRITERIA**
> - **9 pillars:** Strength, Stiffness, Wear, Corrosion, Manufacturability, Economy, Reliability, Standardization, Safety.
> - **3 design types:** Adaptive (most common), Developmental (improvement), New (rare).
> - **Primary FOS formula:** $N = S_{y} / \sigma_{allow}$ for ductile, $N = S_{ut} / \sigma_{allow}$ for brittle.
> - **ASME equivalent moment:** $M_{e} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$ (Tresca, conservative).
> - **Von-Mises equivalent moment:** $M_{e,VM} = \sqrt{M^{2} + 0.75 \, T^{2}}$ (preferred for ductile).
> - **Uncorrected endurance limit:** $S_{e}' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa; else $700$ MPa.
> - **Modified endurance limit:** $S_{e} = k_{a} k_{b} k_{c} k_{d} k_{e} k_{f} \, S_{e}'$.
> - **Shaft diameter equation:** $d \ge \left( \dfrac{32 \, M_{e}}{\pi \, \sigma_{allow}} \right)^{1/3}$.
> - **Always check both static and fatigue criteria** — whichever gives the larger diameter governs.
> - **Adopt standard sizes** — round up and refer to IS 2641 / IS 2670 for shaft diameters.
> - **Key SI constants to memorize:** $E_{steel} = 210$ GPa, $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\nu = 0.30$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stresses in Machine Parts - Working Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stresses in Machine Parts - Working Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Working Stress

In the design of machine elements, **Working Stress** (also referred to as **Induced Stress**, **Actual Stress**, or **Service Stress**) is the stress that actually develops in a machine component when it is subjected to the expected external service loads during normal operation.

Mathematically expressed as the ratio of the applied load to the resisting cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma_w = \frac{\text{Applied Service Load}}{\text{Resisting Cross-Sectional Area}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Working stress is the stress produced in a machine part by the application of the external service (working) loads. It is the *actual* stress that the component must withstand during its functional life — NOT the maximum stress at failure.

### 1.2 Working Stress vs. Allowable Stress — A Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> - **Working Stress (σ_w):** Stress induced under actual service loads.
> - **Allowable Stress / Permissible Stress (σ_allow):** Maximum stress a material can safely tolerate. It is the *limit* stress derived from the material's strength divided by the factor of safety.
>
> **Relationship:** σ_allow = σ_ultimate / FOS  (or)  σ_allow = σ_yield / FOS

> **Design Condition (Mandatory for a Safe Design):**
> $$\boxed{\sigma_w \leq \sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{material\ strength}}{FOS}}$$

If σ_w exceeds σ_allow, the machine part will fail. Hence, *every design begins by computing σ_w and comparing it against σ_allow*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge Rope" Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a steel cable being used to suspend a 5-tonne elevator in a building.
> - The **actual tension** in the cable while the elevator moves = **Working Stress** (induced by service load).
> - The **maximum tension** the cable can hold before snapping = **Ultimate Stress**.
> - The cable manufacturer specifies a **safe working load** (= Ultimate / Safety Factor) = **Allowable Stress**.
>
> So: σ_working < σ_allow << σ_ultimate — just like your elevator cable must never reach its breaking tension.

### 1.4 Why Study Working Stress?

1. **Verification of Safety** — to check whether a chosen cross-section can carry the actual service load.
2. **Material Selection** — knowing σ_w helps identify the right material grade.
3. **Sizing of Components** — the working stress directly determines the cross-sectional area required: A = P/σ_allow.
4. **Predicting Failure Modes** — type of σ_w (tensile, shear, torsional) hints at the likely failure mode.
5. **Compliance with KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** — direct application of **CO1**: *Apply principles of stress analysis to design machine elements*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Stresses in Machine Parts

Machine parts are typically subjected to a combination of the following fundamental stresses. Each is computed separately and then combined (superposed) to obtain the resultant working stress.

#### (A) Direct / Axial Stress
When a force acts along the centroidal axis of a member (pulling or pushing).

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

Where:
- $P$ = axial load (N)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (mm²)
- Tensile: σ positive; Compressive: σ negative

#### (B) Bending Stress (Flexural Stress)
Caused by a bending moment $M$ acting on a beam/shaft.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

Where:
- $M$ = bending moment (N·mm)
- $y$ = distance from neutral axis (mm)
- $I$ = second moment of area (mm⁴)
- $Z = I/y_{max}$ = section modulus (mm³)

For a **solid circular shaft**: $Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32}$

#### (C) Torsional Shear Stress
Caused by a torque $T$ acting on a shaft.

$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$

Where:
- $T$ = torque (N·mm)
- $r$ = radius (mm)
- $J$ = polar second moment of area (mm⁴)
- $Z_p$ = polar section modulus (mm³) = $J/r$

For a **solid circular shaft**: $Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$

#### (D) Transverse Shear Stress
Caused by a transverse shear force $F$.

$$\tau = \frac{F \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = first moment of area
- $b$ = width at the section

For solid shafts, $\tau_{max} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{F}{A}$

#### (E) Combined / Compound Stress
A real machine part often experiences axial, bending, and torsion simultaneously (e.g., a gear shaft with bending from gear forces + torsion from transmitted power).

**Resultant Direct Stress:** $\sigma = \sigma_{axial} + \sigma_{bending} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M}{Z}$

**Resultant Shear Stress:** $\tau = \tau_{torsional} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$

### 2.2 Principal Stresses for Combined Loading

For a 2D stress element with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Maximum shear stress:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

### 2.3 Equivalent (Working) Stresses for Combined Bending & Torsion

When a shaft carries both bending moment $M$ and torque $T$, the **equivalent working stress** depends on the failure theory adopted:

**Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's / Tresca):**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2}$$

**Distortion Energy Theory (Von Mises):**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically adopts the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** for ductile materials (most machine components are ductile, e.g., mild steel shafts).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Stress Type | Formula | Standard Units | Field of Use |
|---|-------------|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Direct/Axial stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** | Tie-rods, columns, bolts |
| 2 | Bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** | Beams, shafts, levers |
| 3 | Torsional shear | $\tau_t = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** | Shafts, axles |
| 4 | Transverse shear | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | **MPa (N/mm²)** | Pins, rivets, keys |
| 5 | Principal stresses | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | **MPa** | Combined loading |
| 6 | Maximum shear stress | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | **MPa** | 2D analysis |
| 7 | Equivalent stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2}$ | **MPa** | Combined bending & torsion |
| 8 | Equivalent stress (Von Mises) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | **MPa** | Combined bending & torsion |
| 9 | Allowable stress | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{S_{ut}}{n}$ or $\dfrac{S_y}{n}$ | **MPa** | Design verification |
| 10 | Design Condition | $\sigma_w \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | — | Universal check |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Crankshafts:** Combine bending (from combustion load) + torsion (from power transmission) → equivalent stress used for sizing.
- **Aerospace Landing Gear:** Tensile working stress in oleo struts governs the wall thickness.
- **Pressure Vessels:** Hoop (circumferential) stress = $pd/2t$ is the working stress governing wall thickness.
- **Civil Tie Rods:** Direct tensile stress is the primary design driver.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Principal Stresses from a 2D Stress Element

Consider an elemental area $dA$ of a 2D stressed body. On faces perpendicular to the x-axis, the stresses are $\sigma_x$ (normal) and $\tau_{xy}$ (shear). On the perpendicular y-faces, the stresses are $\sigma_y$ and $\tau_{yx}$.

Now consider a plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. The area of the inclined face is $dA/\cos\theta$.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium on the wedge.**

Let the wedge have unit thickness (out of plane). On the inclined face of area $dA_\theta$, the resultant force has components $\sigma_\theta \cdot dA_\theta$ (normal) and $\tau_\theta \cdot dA_\theta$ (shear). Force balance along the normal direction to the inclined plane gives:

$$\sigma_\theta \cdot dA_\theta = \sigma_x \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta + \sigma_y \cdot dA \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cdot \sin\theta$$

But $dA = dA_\theta \cdot \cos\theta$, hence $dA_\theta = dA/\cos\theta$. Substituting and simplifying (after dividing by $dA_\theta$):

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Apply trigonometric identities.**

We use the identities:

$$\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}, \quad \sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}, \quad 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$$

Substituting into the equation above:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to find extrema (principal stresses).**

For principal stresses, $\dfrac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the principal stress values.**

From the identity:

$$\sin 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}, \quad \cos 2\theta_p = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

Substituting back into equation (i):

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

This is the classical **Principal Stress Formula** used universally in machine design. **∎**

### 3.2 Derivation of Equivalent Bending Moment (Maximum Shear Stress Theory)

Consider a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ subjected to a bending moment $M$ and a torque $T$ simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Compute individual stresses.**

Bending stress (maximum at outer fibre, $c = d/2$):

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4/64} = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$$

Torsional shear stress (maximum at outer fibre, $r = d/2$):

$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4/32} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 2 — State the stress state at the critical point.**

At the outer fibre, the state of stress is:
- $\sigma_x = \sigma_b = \dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3}$
- $\sigma_y = 0$
- $\tau_{xy} = \tau_t = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$

**Step 3 — Apply Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) Theory.**

The principal stresses for this biaxial state are:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 0$$

$$\sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

Maximum shear stress:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau_t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Define Equivalent Stress.**

The **equivalent working stress** $\sigma_{eq}$ is defined such that the shaft would experience the same $\tau_{max}$ under *pure bending alone*. This means:

$$\sigma_{eq} = 2\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau_t^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute bending and torsional stress formulas.**

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 4\left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

Factor out $\left(\dfrac{32}{\pi d^3}\right)^2$:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \frac{32}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Equivalent Bending Moment.**

Comparing with the pure bending equation $\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\boxed{M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}$$

And equivalently:

$$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}$$

This is the **KTU-favoured equivalent moment/torque formula** for shaft design under combined loading. **∎**

### 3.3 Derivation of Equivalent Bending Moment (Von Mises Theory)

Following the same procedure but using the Von Mises yield criterion:

$$\sigma_{eq}^2 = \sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2$$

Substituting $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32M}{\pi d^3}$ and $\tau_t = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\sigma_{eq}^2 = \left(\frac{32}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 M^2 + 3\left(\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 T^2 = \left(\frac{32}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 \left(M^2 + 0.75T^2\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{M_{e,VM} = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75T^2}}$$

And:

$$\boxed{T_{e,VM} = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75T^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Preference:** For ductile materials (mild steel, medium carbon steel), the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** is most commonly used because it is conservative and yields safer designs. Von Mises is used when economy of material is preferred.

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Working Stress in a Shaft

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $60$ mm transmits a torque of $2$ kN·m. At a section, it is also subjected to a bending moment of $1.5$ kN·m. Find the working stresses (bending, torsional) and the equivalent working stress using both Tresca and Von Mises theories.

**Given:**
- $d = 60$ mm
- $T = 2$ kN·m $= 2 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $M = 1.5$ kN·m $= 1.5 \times 10^6$ N·mm

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Section modulus.**

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 216000}{32} = 21205.75 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = 2Z = 42411.5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2: Bending (working) stress.**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{21205.75} = 70.74 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Torsional shear (working) stress.**

$$\tau_t = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{42411.5} = 47.16 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Equivalent working stress (Tresca).**

$$\sigma_{eq,T} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 4\tau_t^2} = \sqrt{(70.74)^2 + 4(47.16)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{5004.15 + 8898.11} = \sqrt{13902.26} = 117.91 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Equivalent working stress (Von Mises).**

$$\sigma_{eq,VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2} = \sqrt{(70.74)^2 + 3(47.16)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{5004.15 + 6673.58} = \sqrt{11677.73} = 108.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification using the moment-formula:**

$$M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(1.5)^2 + (2)^2} = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\sigma_{eq,T} = \frac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 2.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} = 117.91 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**
- Bending working stress: $\sigma_b = 70.74$ MPa
- Torsional working stress: $\tau_t = 47.16$ MPa
- Equivalent stress (Tresca): $\sigma_{eq,T} = 117.91$ MPa
- Equivalent stress (Von Mises): $\sigma_{eq,VM} = 108.06$ MPa

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Stresses in Machine Parts

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Working Stress in Machine Parts))
        DIRECT_AXIAL
            Tensile: sigma equals P over A
            Compressive: sigma equals minus P over A
        BENDING
            Pure Bending: sigma_b equals M over Z
            Combined with axial
        SHEAR
            Direct Shear: tau equals F over A
            Torsional: tau equals T over Z_p
        COMBINED
            Bending plus Torsion
            Multi-axial Loading
        THERMAL
            Thermal Stresses
            Residual Stresses
```

### 4.3 State of Stress at a Critical Point on a Shaft (Combined Loading)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Outer Fibre of Shaft (Critical Point)"] --> B["Bending Stress sigma_b acts NORMAL"]
    A --> C["Torsional Shear tau_t acts TANGENTIAL"]
    B --> D["Combined 2D Stress Element"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Principal Stresses via Mohr's Circle"]
    E --> F["Equivalent Working Stress via Failure Theory"]
    F --> G["Compare with Allowable Stress"]
```

### 4.4 Relationship Between Working, Allowable, and Ultimate Stresses

```mermaid
graph TB
    U["ULTIMATE STRESS sigma_ut or sigma_y"] -->|"divide by FOS n"| A["ALLOWABLE STRESS sigma_allow"]
    W["WORKING STRESS sigma_w (induced)"]
    A -->|"must satisfy"| C{"sigma_w ≤ sigma_allow ?"}
    W -->|"must satisfy"| C
    C -->|YES| SAFE["DESIGN SAFE — Accept"]
    C -->|NO| RED["REDESIGN required"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **factor of safety (n)** is the bridge between material strength and allowable stress. The working stress (induced by loads) is always checked against the allowable stress. **All KTU 2024 numerical problems end here.**

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** Define working stress and state its significance in the design of machine elements. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Working stress is the stress actually induced in a machine component when it is subjected to the expected external service loads during normal operation. It is the ratio of the applied load to the resisting cross-sectional area.

**Significance:**
1. It is the actual stress a component must withstand in service and therefore governs the design.
2. It must be compared with the allowable stress to verify the safety of the design: $\sigma_w \leq \sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{strength}/n$.
3. It determines the required cross-sectional dimensions of the component: $A = P/\sigma_{allow}$.
4. It helps in selecting the appropriate material based on its strength.
5. It is the foundation for sizing all machine elements — shafts, keys, couplings, etc.

---

#### **Question 2:** Distinguish between working stress and allowable stress. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Aspect | Working Stress (σ_w) | Allowable Stress (σ_allow) |
|---------|--------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Definition** | Stress induced in the part by actual service loads | Maximum stress the part can safely carry |
| 2 | **Nature** | Induced / actual / service stress | Permissible / safe stress |
| 3 | **Depends on** | External loads and geometry of the part | Material strength and factor of safety |
| 4 | **Value** | Variable (depends on load) | Fixed (depends on material choice) |
| 5 | **Computation** | $\sigma_w = P/A$ (from load analysis) | $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y/n$ or $\sigma_{ut}/n$ |
| 6 | **Design check** | Is compared against allowable stress | Is the reference limit for comparison |

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A machine component is subjected to the following loads simultaneously at a critical section:
- An axial tensile load of 30 kN
- A bending moment of 800 N·m
- A torque of 600 N·m

The component has a circular cross-section of diameter 50 mm. Compute the working stresses (axial, bending, torsional) and the principal stresses at the critical point. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shaft is required to transmit 30 kW at 250 rpm. It is simultaneously subjected to a bending moment of 1.2 kN·m due to a gear mounted on it. Determine the required shaft diameter using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory. Assume allowable shear stress = 60 MPa. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a): Solution**

Given:
- $P = 30 \text{ kN} = 30{,}000$ N (axial, tensile)
- $M = 800$ N·m $= 8 \times 10^5$ N·mm
- $T = 600$ N·m $= 6 \times 10^5$ N·mm
- $d = 50$ mm

**Step 1: Section properties.**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2500}{4} = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 125000}{32} = 12271.85 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = 24543.7 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2: Direct (axial) working stress.**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{30000}{1963.5} = 15.28 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Bending working stress.**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{8 \times 10^5}{12271.85} = 65.19 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Torsional shear working stress.**

$$\tau_t = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{6 \times 10^5}{24543.7} = 24.45 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Total normal stress at outer fibre.**

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_d + \sigma_b = 15.28 + 65.19 = 80.47 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_y = 0$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_t = 24.45 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Principal stresses.**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$= \frac{80.47}{2} \pm \sqrt{(40.235)^2 + (24.45)^2}$$

$$= 40.235 \pm \sqrt{1618.85 + 597.80}$$

$$= 40.235 \pm \sqrt{2216.65}$$

$$= 40.235 \pm 47.08$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 87.31 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -6.85 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

**Part (b): Solution**

Given:
- Power $P = 30$ kW $= 30{,}000$ W
- Speed $N = 250$ rpm
- Bending moment $M = 1.2$ kN·m $= 1.2 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- Allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 60$ MPa

**Step 1: Torque transmitted by the shaft.**

$$T = \frac{60 \times P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 30000}{2\pi \times 250} = \frac{1800000}{1570.8} = 1145.92 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = 1.1459 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent bending moment (Tresca Theory).**

$$M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^6)^2 + (1.1459 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$M_e = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{12} + 1.313 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{2.753 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$M_e = 1.6592 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Shaft diameter from bending equation.**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{32 M_e}{\pi d^3}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{32 M_e}{\pi \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{32 \times 1.6592 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{5.309 \times 10^7}{188.5} = 2.8167 \times 10^5$$

$$\boxed{d = 65.57 \text{ mm} \approx 66 \text{ mm}}$$

---

#### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of stresses that occur in machine parts. Derive expressions for the principal stresses in a 2D stress element. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of length 1.2 m carries a central point load of 10 kN. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of width 60 mm and depth 120 mm. Determine the maximum bending working stress and the maximum transverse shear stress in the beam. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a): Explanation**

The different types of stresses in machine parts are:
1. **Direct/Axial stress** ($\sigma = P/A$): due to forces along the longitudinal axis (tie-rods, columns).
2. **Bending stress** ($\sigma_b = M/Z$): due to bending moments acting on the cross-section (beams, levers).
3. **Torsional shear stress** ($\tau = T/Z_p$): due to twisting moments (shafts, axles).
4. **Transverse/Shear stress** ($\tau = F/A$): due to transverse shear forces (pinned joints, rivets).
5. **Combined stresses**: due to simultaneous application of multiple loads.

**Derivation of principal stresses** (refer to derivation in Section 3.1):

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

The angle of principal plane is given by:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Part (b): Solution**

Given:
- Span $L = 1.2$ m $= 1200$ mm
- Central load $W = 10$ kN $= 10{,}000$ N
- Width $b = 60$ mm
- Depth $h = 120$ mm

**Step 1: Maximum bending moment (at centre).**

$$M_{max} = \frac{WL}{4} = \frac{10000 \times 1200}{4} = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear force.**

$$F_{max} = \frac{W}{2} = 5000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Section modulus (rectangular).**

$$Z = \frac{b h^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times (120)^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 14400}{6} = 144000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4: Maximum bending working stress.**

$$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{144000} = 20.83 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Maximum transverse shear stress (for rectangular cross-section).**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3F}{2A} = \frac{3 \times 5000}{2 \times 60 \times 120} = \frac{15000}{14400}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 1.042 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{b,max} = 20.83 \text{ MPa}, \quad \tau_{max} = 1.042 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up σ_w and σ_allow:** Many students confuse working stress (induced) with allowable stress (permitted). The *comparison* is what ensures safety — not the equality.
> 2. **Wrong section modulus formula:** For a **solid round shaft**, $Z = \pi d^3/32$ and $Z_p = \pi d^3/16$. Confusing $Z$ with $Z_p$ costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 3. **Forgetting the Tresca vs. Von Mises distinction:** $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2}$ (Tresca) vs. $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ (Von Mises) — both are valid, but they give *different* answers. KTU examiners expect the student to **state which theory is being used**.
> 4. **Unit mismatches:** Mixing N·m with N·mm or MPa with Pa results in massive numerical errors. Always convert: 1 kN·m = $10^6$ N·mm; 1 MPa = 1 N/mm².
> 5. **Sign convention errors:** Tensile stress is positive, compressive is negative. Forgetting this gives incorrect principal stresses.
> 6. **Skipping the design check:** The final answer must explicitly verify $\sigma_w \leq \sigma_{allow}$. A bare numerical answer without this verification is marked down by 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Incorrect trigonometric identity for principal plane angle:** The angle is $2\theta_p$, not $\theta_p$ directly. Examiners specifically test this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Working Stress**
>
> - **Working Stress Definition:** Stress induced under actual service loads = $P/A$, $M/Z$, $T/Z_p$, $F/A$.
> - **Allowable Stress:** $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{material\ strength}/FOS = \sigma_{ut}/n$ or $\sigma_y/n$.
> - **Universal Design Condition:** $\sigma_w \leq \sigma_{allow}$ (always must be verified).
> - **Solid Round Shaft Section Properties:** $Z = \pi d^3/32$, $Z_p = \pi d^3/16 = 2Z$.
> - **Hollow Round Shaft:** $Z = \pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)/(32 d_o)$, $Z_p = \pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)/(16 d_o)$.
> - **Principal Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{1,2} = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/2 \pm \sqrt{((\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
> - **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$.
> - **Equivalent Moment (Tresca):** $M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$.
> - **Equivalent Moment (Von Mises):** $M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75T^2}$.
> - **Equivalent Torque (Both Theories):** $T_e = M_e$ (numerically equal to $M_e$ in the formula).
> - **Shaft Diameter (Tresca):** $d = \sqrt[3]{32 M_e/(\pi \sigma_{allow})}$.
> - **Shaft Diameter (Von Mises):** $d = \sqrt[3]{32 M_{e,VM}/(\pi \sigma_{allow})}$.
> - **Torsional Power Formula:** $T = 60 P/(2\pi N)$ where $P$ is in watts and $N$ in rpm.
> - **Bending Moment for Point Load on Simply Supported Beam:** $M = WL/4$ (central load).
> - **Maximum Shear for Rectangular Cross-Section:** $\tau_{max} = 3F/(2A)$.
> - **KTU Standard:** Use **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** for ductile materials (most common in exams).
> - **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** Topic directly addresses **CO1** (Apply principles of stress analysis to design machine elements).
> - **Always state** which failure theory (Tresca or Von Mises) is being used in the solution.
> - **Always convert units:** Length in mm, Force in N, Stress in MPa (= N/mm²).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safe Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safe Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safe Stress (Allowable Stress / Working Stress)** is defined as the **maximum stress** that a machine component can safely withstand under service conditions **without causing failure**, deformation, or loss of functionality. It is obtained by dividing the failure stress (ultimate or yield) by a numerical value greater than unity known as the **Factor of Safety (FOS)** or **Factor of Ignorance**.

Mathematically, the safe stress is expressed as:

$$\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{n}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{safe}$ = Safe (allowable) stress in **MPa** or **N/mm²**
- $\sigma_{failure}$ = Failure stress (ultimate stress $\sigma_u$, yield stress $\sigma_y$, or endurance limit $\sigma_e$, depending on loading)
- $n$ = Factor of Safety (dimensionless, always $n > 1$)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Weight-Limit Sign" on a Bridge**
>
> Imagine you have an old rope bridge that snaps when **100 kg** is loaded on it. But you cannot risk the lives of people crossing it. So, you put up a sign that says **"MAX 25 kg"**. Why 25? Because you know the rope is old, wet, slightly frayed, and people might jump on it. The **failure limit is 100 kg**, but the **safe limit is 25 kg**. The ratio (100 / 25 = 4) is your **Factor of Safety**.
>
> In the same way, a steel rod may break at **400 MPa**, but engineers only allow it to carry **100 MPa** in actual machines. The **safe stress is the "sign on the bridge"** for every machine element.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (for Mild Steel) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Ultimate Tensile Stress ($\sigma_u$) | **400 – 600** | **MPa** |
| Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$) | **250 – 350** | **MPa** |
| Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$) | **0.4 to 0.5 $\times \sigma_u$** | **MPa** |
| Typical Factor of Safety ($n$) | **1.5 to 4.0** | Dimensionless |

---

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 Highlights (Safe Stress):**
> - Static loading safe stress uses **$\sigma_y$** (or $\sigma_u$ for brittle materials).
> - Fluctuating / Variable loading safe stress uses **$\sigma_e$ (endurance limit)**.
> - Stress concentration factor ($K_t$) must be applied to nominal stresses.
> - **Mean stress** corrections (Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber) are mandatory for fluctuating loads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Safe Stress Needed?

A machine component never operates under **ideal** conditions. Engineers must account for:

- **Material imperfections** (inclusions, voids, micro-cracks)
- **Uncertainty in loading** (shock, vibration, overload)
- **Uncertainty in material properties** (batch-to-batch variation)
- **Environmental degradation** (corrosion, temperature, wear)
- **Stress concentrations** at holes, keyways, fillets
- **Type of loading**: static, fluctuating, or impact

All these uncertainties are bundled into a single number — the **Factor of Safety**.

---

## 2.2 Classification Based on Loading Conditions

### (A) Static Loading (Constant Load)
When the load on the component is **constant** in magnitude and direction:
- For **ductile materials** (e.g., mild steel): use yield stress as the failure criterion.
- For **brittle materials** (e.g., cast iron): use ultimate stress as the failure criterion.

### (B) Variable / Fluctuating Loading
When the load varies with time (e.g., crankshaft, spring, connecting rod):
- The component fails by **fatigue** even if the maximum stress is **below the yield stress**.
- Failure stress becomes the **Endurance Limit $\sigma_e$**.
- Must correct for **mean stress** using empirical failure criteria.

### (C) Impact Loading
When the load is applied suddenly:
- Use **maximum stress concentration factor ($K_t$)** and **impact factor ($K_i$)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Master the units and boundary conditions.

### Table 1: Master Formula Sheet for Safe Stress

| # | Loading Condition | Safe Stress Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static — Ductile material | $\sigma_{safe} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | Steady load, mild steel, aluminium |
| 2 | Static — Brittle material | $\sigma_{safe} = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{n}$ | Steady load, cast iron, ceramics |
| 3 | Variable — Soderberg (conservative) | $\sigma_{safe} = \dfrac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_y}{n \cdot \sigma_y - \sigma_m}$ | General rotating shafts (most used in KTU) |
| 4 | Variable — Goodman (general) | $\sigma_{safe} = \dfrac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_u}{n \cdot \sigma_u - \sigma_m}$ | Where data fits linear envelope |
| 5 | Variable — Gerber (parabolic) | $\sigma_{safe} = \dfrac{\sigma_e}{n} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)^2\right]$ | Best fit for ductile materials |
| 6 | With stress concentration | $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom} \leq \sigma_{safe}$ | Holes, keyways, fillets |
| 7 | Impact loading | $\sigma_{max} = K_i \cdot \sigma_{nom}$ | Sudden applied loads |

### Table 2: Mean and Amplitude Stress Definitions

| Parameter | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum stress | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$ | Highest in cycle |
| Minimum stress | $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$ | Lowest in cycle |
| Mean stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Average load |
| Amplitude stress | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Reversed component |
| Stress ratio | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | Characterizes cycle type |

### Table 3: Typical Factors of Safety (KTU Reference)

| Application | $n$ |
|---|---|
| Highly reliable, well-known loads (e.g., aircraft components) | 1.2 – 1.5 |
| General machinery | 2 – 3 |
| Civil structures, less inspected | 4 – 6 |
| Castings with unpredictable defects | 6 – 10 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical engineering**: Designing shafts, gears, pressure vessels, springs.
- **Aerospace**: Lightweight materials with low FOS (1.2 – 1.5) due to high inspection standards.
- **Civil**: Higher FOS (4–6) due to human safety and uncertainty.
- **Automotive**: Crankshafts, connecting rods — all use **fatigue-based safe stress**.

---

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions & Validity Limits

- $n > 1$ always. If $n \leq 1$, the design is unsafe.
- For $\sigma_{min} = -\sigma_{max}$ (fully reversed cycle): $\sigma_m = 0$, so formulas reduce to $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_e / n$.
- For $\sigma_{min} = 0$ (pulsating cycle, e.g., leaf spring): $\sigma_m = \sigma_a = \sigma_{max}/2$.
- Endurance limit $\sigma_e$ is only defined for **ferrous steels** under $10^6$ cycles. For non-ferrous metals (aluminium), use $\sigma_e \approx 0.4 \sigma_u$ but it is not as well-defined.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Factor of Safety from Basic Stress Definition

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the failure condition.**
A component is said to have failed when the applied stress equals the **failure stress**:

$$\sigma_{applied} = \sigma_{failure}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the Factor of Safety.**
The factor of safety is defined as the ratio of failure load to the actual applied load:

$$n = \frac{F_{failure}}{F_{applied}}$$

Since stress $\sigma$ is proportional to load $F$ for the same geometry:

$$n = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{applied}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to find the allowable (safe) stress.**
Rearranging the equation for the applied (working) stress:

$$\sigma_{applied} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Define the safe stress.**
By design philosophy, the applied stress should never exceed the **safe stress**:

$$\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{n} \geq \sigma_{applied}$$

> **Final Result:**
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{n}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Soderberg Equation for Fluctuating Stresses

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 1.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Represent the actual variable stress.**
Any fluctuating stress can be decomposed into:

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$$

where $\sigma_m$ = mean stress and $\sigma_a$ = amplitude stress.

**Step 2 — Define the two failure envelopes.**
Failure occurs if either of two conditions is violated:

- **Yielding (static):** $\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_y / n$
- **Fatigue:** $\sigma_a \leq \sigma_e / n$

**Step 3 — Write the two design equations.**
Design against yielding:

$$\sigma_m + \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_y}{n_{yield}}$$

Design against fatigue:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e}{n_{fatigue}}$$

**Step 4 — Assume a single combined factor of safety $n$.**
For combined loading, let $n$ be a single safety factor. Substituting $\sigma_a$ from fatigue equation:

$$\sigma_m + \frac{\sigma_e}{n} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $n$.**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_y}{n} - \frac{\sigma_e}{n} = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_e}{n}$$

Therefore:

$$n = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_e}{\sigma_m}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to obtain the safe stress.**
Starting from $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_e / n$:

$$\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e}{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_y - \sigma_e}{\sigma_m}\right)} = \frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_y - \sigma_e}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sigma_m$:

$$\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e}{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_m} - 1}$$

Equivalent form (most commonly used in KTU problems):

> **Final Result:**
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_y}{n \cdot \sigma_y - \sigma_m}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Goodman Criterion

**Step 1 — Replace $\sigma_y$ with $\sigma_u$ in Soderberg equation.**
Goodman assumes failure along the line connecting $(\sigma_u, 0)$ and $(0, \sigma_e)$ on the $\sigma_m$ – $\sigma_a$ diagram.

**Step 2 — Write the equation of the line.**
By similar triangles:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\sigma_a$:**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e}{n}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Express the safe stress.**
Combining $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$:

> **Final Result:**
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_u}{n \cdot \sigma_u - \sigma_m}}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked Problem)

**Problem:** A steel shaft has $\sigma_u = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 280$ MPa. It is subjected to a bending stress that fluctuates between $\sigma_{max} = 250$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -50$ MPa. Find the factor of safety using (i) Soderberg, (ii) Goodman.

**Step 1 — Compute $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$.**

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 + (-50)}{2} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{250 - (-50)}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

**(i) Soderberg Criterion:**

$$n_{Soderberg} = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot \sigma_e}{\sigma_a \cdot \sigma_y + \sigma_m \cdot \sigma_e}$$

Wait — let's derive the correct form. From Soderberg:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}$$

Substituting values:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{150}{280} + \frac{100}{400} = 0.5357 + 0.25 = 0.7857$$

$$n = \frac{1}{0.7857} \approx 1.27$$

**(ii) Goodman Criterion:**

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{150}{280} + \frac{100}{600} = 0.5357 + 0.1667 = 0.7024$$

$$n = \frac{1}{0.7024} \approx 1.42$$

**Result:** Goodman gives a higher (less conservative) FOS than Soderberg — a common KTU exam observation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Safe Stress Determination — Decision Flow Architecture

The following block diagram captures the logical flow of choosing the **right safe stress formula** in a KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Loading Type] --> B{Is Loading Static or Variable?}
    B -- Static --> C{Is Material Ductile or Brittle?}
    C -- Ductile --> D[Use sigma safe equals sigma y over n]
    C -- Brittle --> E[Use sigma safe equals sigma u over n]
    B -- Variable --> F{Which Failure Theory Required?}
    F -- Soderberg --> G[Use sigma safe equals sigma e sigma y over n sigma y minus sigma m]
    F -- Goodman --> H[Use sigma safe equals sigma e sigma u over n sigma u minus sigma m]
    F -- Gerber --> I[Use sigma safe equals sigma e over n times bracket 1 minus sigma m over sigma u squared]
    G --> J[Apply Stress Concentration Factor Kt if Geometry has Discontinuity]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    D --> K[Final Check: sigma applied must be less than or equal to sigma safe]
    E --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[End: Safe Design Confirmed]
```

---

## 4.2 Stress Cycle Identification Block

For identifying the type of fluctuating cycle, refer to this functional architecture:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input sigma max and sigma min] --> B[Compute sigma m and sigma a]
    B --> C{Is sigma min equal to zero?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Pulsating Cycle R equals zero]
    C -- No --> E{Is sigma min equal to minus sigma max?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Fully Reversed Cycle R equals minus one]
    E -- No --> G[Random Fluctuating Cycle]
    D --> H[Apply Corresponding Safe Stress Formula]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

---

## 4.3 Failure Envelope Comparison (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Failure Envelope for Variable Loading] --> B[Vertical Axis sigma a Amplitude]
    A --> C[Horizontal Axis sigma m Mean]
    B --> D[Soderberg Line Connects sigma e and sigma y]
    B --> E[Goodman Line Connects sigma e and sigma u]
    B --> F[Gerber Curve Parabolic between sigma e and sigma u]
    D --> G[Soderberg Most Conservative]
    E --> H[Goodman Moderate]
    F --> I[Gerber Least Conservative Best for Ductile]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Any operating point $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ **inside** the envelope is safe; any point **outside** indicates failure. The Soderberg line is the closest to the origin and therefore the safest.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define safe stress. Why is a factor of safety used in machine design?**

> **Model Answer:**
> Safe stress (or allowable stress) is the maximum stress that a machine component can safely withstand under working conditions without failure. It is obtained by dividing the failure stress (yield, ultimate, or endurance) by the factor of safety $n$.
>
> The factor of safety accounts for **uncertainties** in:
> (i) material properties and homogeneity,
> (ii) magnitude and nature of applied loads (shock, fatigue),
> (iii) environmental effects (corrosion, temperature),
> (iv) stress concentrations at geometric discontinuities.
>
> Without FOS, even minor unpredictable variations could cause catastrophic failure.

---

### Question 2 (Numerical — Mean/Amplitude)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q: A shaft is subjected to a bending stress varying from +200 MPa to -100 MPa. Calculate the mean stress and amplitude stress.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> Given $\sigma_{max} = +200$ MPa, $\sigma_{min} = -100$ MPa.
>
> $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 + (-100)}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> $$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 - (-100)}{2} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Cycle type:** Since $\sigma_{min} \neq -\sigma_{max}$, this is a **fluctuating cycle** (not fully reversed). Stress ratio $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max} = -0.5$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 1]**

**A (a) Derive the Soderberg equation for safe stress in a component subjected to combined static and fluctuating loads.** [7 Marks]

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Given:** A component subjected to mean stress $\sigma_m$ and amplitude stress $\sigma_a$. Material has yield strength $\sigma_y$ and endurance limit $\sigma_e$.
>
> **Failure Modes Considered:**
> (i) Yielding due to maximum stress.
> (ii) Fatigue due to reversed stress.
>
> **Derivation:**
>
> The maximum stress in any cycle is $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$.
>
> Against yielding, the design equation is:
>
> $$\sigma_m + \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$
>
> Against fatigue, the design equation is:
>
> $$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_e}{n}$$
>
> Substituting $\sigma_a$ from the fatigue equation into the yielding equation:
>
> $$\sigma_m + \frac{\sigma_e}{n} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$$
>
> Multiplying throughout by $n$:
>
> $$\sigma_m \cdot n + \sigma_e = \sigma_y$$
>
> Solving for $n$:
>
> $$n = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_e}{\sigma_m}$$
>
> The safe stress is given by $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_e / n$:
>
> $$\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_y - \sigma_e}$$
>
> Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sigma_y / \sigma_m$:
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_y}{n \cdot \sigma_y - \sigma_m}}$$
>
> **Validity:** This equation is conservative and is the most commonly used criterion in KTU board exams for shaft design.

---

**A (b) A shaft made of steel has $\sigma_u = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 270$ MPa. It is subjected to a bending stress fluctuating between +180 MPa and -60 MPa. Using the Soderberg criterion, determine the factor of safety.** [7 Marks]

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Compute $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$:**
>
> $$\sigma_m = \frac{180 + (-60)}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> $$\sigma_a = \frac{180 - (-60)}{2} = 120 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Apply Soderberg Equation:**
>
> $$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}$$
>
> $$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{120}{270} + \frac{60}{380}$$
>
> $$\frac{1}{n} = 0.4444 + 0.1579 = 0.6023$$
>
> $$n = \frac{1}{0.6023} \approx 1.66$$
>
> **Step 3 — Safe stress:**
>
> $$\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e}{n} = \frac{270}{1.66} \approx 162.7 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Result:** Factor of safety is **1.66**, which is acceptable for general machine design (typical range 1.5 – 3.0).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 — Module 1]**

**B (a) Explain the Goodman and Gerber criteria for safe stress in fluctuating loading. Compare them with the Soderberg criterion.** [7 Marks]

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Goodman Criterion:**
> Failure occurs when the operating point $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ crosses the straight line joining $(\sigma_u, 0)$ and $(0, \sigma_e)$:
>
> $$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u} = \frac{1}{n}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_u}{n \cdot \sigma_u - \sigma_m}}$$
>
> **Gerber Criterion:**
> Failure follows a parabolic envelope:
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e}{n}\left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)^2\right]}$$
>
> **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Criterion | Envelope Shape | Conservatism | Typical Use |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Soderberg | Straight line, $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_e$ | **Most conservative** | Steel shafts (general) |
> | Goodman | Straight line, $\sigma_u$ and $\sigma_e$ | Moderate | General ductile materials |
> | Gerber | Parabola through $\sigma_u$ and $\sigma_e$ | **Least conservative** | Ductile materials, brittle less so |
>
> **Order of Conservatism:** Soderberg > Goodman > Gerber.
>
> **Verdict:** KTU examiners prefer **Soderberg** for shafts (as per standard design handbooks); Gerber gives the most accurate (highest) safe stress for ductile materials.

---

**B (b) A component is subjected to a tensile load fluctuating between 50 kN and 30 kN. The cross-sectional area is 500 mm². Material: $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa, $\sigma_e = 250$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 500$ MPa. Determine the safe stress and factor of safety using Soderberg.** [7 Marks]

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Convert loads to stresses:**
>
> $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{F_{max}}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{500} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> $$\sigma_{min} = \frac{F_{min}}{A} = \frac{30{,}000}{500} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Mean and amplitude:**
>
> $$\sigma_m = \frac{100 + 60}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> $$\sigma_a = \frac{100 - 60}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Apply Soderberg:**
>
> $$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{20}{250} + \frac{80}{350} = 0.08 + 0.2286 = 0.3086$$
>
> $$n = \frac{1}{0.3086} \approx 3.24$$
>
> **Step 4 — Safe stress:**
>
> $$\sigma_{safe} = \frac{\sigma_e}{n} = \frac{250}{3.24} \approx 77.2 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Verification:**
>
> Maximum applied stress = 100 MPa > $\sigma_{safe}$ = 77.2 MPa. **Design is unsafe!** Need to either increase area, choose stronger material, or reduce load.
>
> *This is an important KTU lesson — sometimes the calculation reveals that the design must be revised.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Using $\sigma_u$ instead of $\sigma_y$ for ductile static loading** → Up to 2 marks lost. Remember: **ductile = yield**, **brittle = ultimate**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert loads to stresses** in numerical problems → 1 mark lost per sub-part.
> 3. **Mixing units** (MPa vs N/mm² — they are equal, but ensure consistency) → 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Using Goodman instead of Soderberg** when the question specifically asks for Soderberg → Lose entire 7 marks.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps** in 14-mark questions → KTU expects **every algebraic step** written explicitly. Skipping earns at most 3 of 7 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring stress concentration factor $K_t$** in geometric discontinuity problems → Up to 2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Safe Stress":**
>
> ☐ **Definition:** Safe stress = Failure stress / Factor of Safety
>
> ☐ **Static loading (ductile):** $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_y / n$
>
> ☐ **Static loading (brittle):** $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_u / n$
>
> ☐ **Soderberg (most used):** $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_e \cdot \sigma_y / (n \cdot \sigma_y - \sigma_m)$
>
> ☐ **Goodman:** $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_e \cdot \sigma_u / (n \cdot \sigma_u - \sigma_m)$
>
> ☐ **Gerber:** $\sigma_{safe} = (\sigma_e / n) \cdot [1 - (\sigma_m / \sigma_u)^2]$
>
> ☐ **Mean stress:** $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$
>
> ☐ **Amplitude stress:** $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$
>
> ☐ **Conservatism order:** Soderberg > Goodman > Gerber
>
> ☐ **Endurance limit** applies only for ferrous materials under $10^6$ cycles
>
> ☐ **Stress concentration factor** $K_t$ must be applied for notches, holes, fillets
>
> ☐ **Typical FOS:** Aircraft 1.2–1.5, Machinery 2–3, Civil 4–6
>
> ☐ **Fully reversed cycle:** $\sigma_m = 0$, so formulas reduce to $\sigma_{safe} = \sigma_e / n$
>
> ☐ **Pulsating cycle:** $\sigma_{min} = 0$, so $\sigma_m = \sigma_a = \sigma_{max}/2$
>
> ☐ **Verification rule:** $\sigma_{applied} \leq \sigma_{safe}$ — if not, redesign!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factor of Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factor of Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Factor of Safety (FoS)**, also known as the **Safety Factor (SF)** or **Factor of Ignorance**, is a dimensionless scalar quantity that expresses the structural capacity of a machine component beyond its expected or actual load. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio between the **maximum stress a component can sustain** (failure stress) and the **working stress (allowable/designed stress)** applied on it during service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Factor of Safety is the ratio of the failure stress (ultimate stress, yield stress, or endurance limit, depending on the mode of failure and material behavior) to the working stress (design/allowable stress) to which the component is subjected in service."

The general mathematical representation is:

$$
n = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{working}} = \frac{\sigma_{failure}}{\sigma_{allowable}}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = Factor of Safety (dimensionless, always $\geq 1$)
- $\sigma_{failure}$ = Failure stress of the material (Ultimate Tensile Strength, Yield Strength, or Endurance Limit)
- $\sigma_{working}$ = Allowable/Design/Working stress

> [!NOTE]
> The **inverse of the factor of safety** is called the **Design Factor** or **Factor of Utilization**, denoted as $C = 1/n$. KTU examiners often ask students to convert between the two.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are carrying a heavy bag that weighs **20 kg**, but your maximum carrying capacity is **50 kg**. You would naturally feel safe because the load is well below your limit. The ratio $50/20 = 2.5$ is your "Factor of Safety" — a measure of how much **extra margin** you have before failure.

In the same way, machine designers never load a component up to its breaking point. They ensure that the actual working stress remains a fraction of the failure stress. A higher FoS means a **more conservative (safer) design**, while a lower FoS means a **more optimized (efficient) design** that uses material economically.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** A steel wire used in a crane hoist. The wire has an ultimate tensile strength of 1000 MPa but is subjected to only 250 MPa. Hence $n = 1000/250 = 4$. The crane can lift four times the design load before the wire breaks — that's the safety margin.

## 1.3 Why is Factor of Safety Necessary?

A designer applies a Factor of Safety to account for the following uncertainties:

1. **Uncertainties in material properties** — Variation in strength from batch to batch.
2. **Uncertainties in manufacturing** — Defects like porosity, cracks, surface roughness.
3. **Uncertainties in loading** — Unexpected overloads, shock loads, vibration.
4. **Uncertainties in analysis** — Simplifications in stress formulas, neglected stress concentrations.
5. **Environmental effects** — Corrosion, temperature fluctuations, wear.
6. **Consequence of failure** — Loss of life, economic loss, safety hazards.
7. **Mode of failure** — Brittle, ductile, fatigue, creep.
8. **Reliability requirements** — Mission-critical vs non-critical applications.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why cannot a machine element be designed with a factor of safety equal to 1?"* Answer: Because $n = 1$ means the working stress equals the failure stress, leaving **zero margin** for uncertainties, defects, and unexpected loads. This is structurally dangerous and unacceptable in real engineering practice.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factor of Safety

In machine design, the factor of safety is not a single number. It is classified based on the **mode of failure** the designer is safeguarding against. KTU 2024 expects students to know the following classifications:

### (a) Factor of Safety based on Yielding (for Ductile Materials under Static Loading)

Used when the material yields before it fractures. The design ensures working stress stays below yield stress.

$$
n_y = \frac{S_{yt} \text{ (or } S_{yc}\text{)}}{\sigma_{working}}
$$

- $S_{yt}$ = Yield Strength in Tension
- $S_{yc}$ = Yield Strength in Compression

### (b) Factor of Safety based on Ultimate Stress (for Brittle Materials under Static Loading)

Used when material fractures without appreciable yielding.

$$
n_u = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_{working}}
$$

- $S_{ut}$ = Ultimate Tensile Strength

### (c) Factor of Safety based on Endurance Limit (for Fatigue Loading)

Used when the component is subjected to cyclic/fluctuating loads.

$$
n_f = \frac{S_e}{\sigma_{working}}
$$

- $S_e$ = Endurance Limit of the material

### (d) Factor of Safety based on Buckling (for Columns under Compression)

$$
n_{b} = \frac{P_{cr}}{P_{applied}}
$$

- $P_{cr}$ = Euler's critical buckling load

## 2.2 The Generalized Multiplicative Factor of Safety (KTU Most-Asked Concept)

In KTU 2024 examinations, the **overall factor of safety** is often expressed as the product of several **partial safety factors**, each addressing a specific source of uncertainty. This is the most important concept for problem-solving:

$$
n = n_1 \times n_2 \times n_3 \times n_4 \times \ldots
$$

Where the partial factors typically represent:

| Partial Factor | Symbol | Represents | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material reliability factor | $n_1$ | Variability in material properties | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| Stress concentration factor adjustment | $n_2$ | Notch sensitivity and stress raisers | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Mode of failure factor | $n_3$ | Brittle vs ductile, fatigue vs static | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Reliability/Inspection factor | $n_4$ | Quality of inspection & maintenance | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| Service/Operating conditions factor | $n_5$ | Temperature, corrosion, wear, impact | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| Design accuracy factor | $n_6$ | Accuracy of analytical method | 1.0 – 1.5 |

> [!NOTE]
