# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define spring index. Why is it restricted between 4 and 12 in helical compression spring design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Spring index is defined as the ratio of mean coil diameter to wire diameter:

$$
C \;=\; \frac{D}{d}
$$

**Restrictions (1 mark each):**

- **Lower bound ($C < 4$):** The wire becomes excessively curved, leading to high stress concentration, difficulty in manufacturing (winding), and the wire tends to crack during forming.
- **Upper bound ($C > 12$):** The spring becomes too slender, prone to **buckling** under compression, and unstable laterally during loading.
- **Optimal range ($4 \le C \le 12$):** Balances manufacturability, stress concentration, and stability. KTU recommends $C = 6$ to $10$ as best design practice.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is Wahl's correction factor? Why is it applied to the shear stress equation of helical springs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wahl's correction factor $K_w$ is an empirical multiplier introduced by A.M. Wahl (1924) to account for the additional stress concentration that occurs in curved helical spring wires due to:

1. Direct shear stress (from axial force $P$).
2. Curvature-induced bending stress (on the inner fibre).

The equation is:

$$
K_w \;=\; \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 0.25} \;+\; \frac{0.615}{C}
$$

It is applied because the simple torsion equation $\tau = 8PD/(\pi d^{3})$ **underestimates** the true maximum stress by 10% to 40% depending on $C$. The corrected equation:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; K_w \cdot \frac{8 P D}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

gives the true maximum shear stress at the **inner fibre**, where failure initiates.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B provides **internal choice** — students must answer either Choice A **or** Choice B. Each choice has two sub-parts worth **7 marks each**.

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: A helical compression spring is required to absorb 25 J of energy when deflected by 50 mm under a maximum load of 1000 N. The spring index is to be taken as 6 and the allowable shear stress is 400 MPa. The shear modulus of the wire material is 80 GPa. Design the spring completely, including wire diameter, mean coil diameter, number of active coils, free length, and pitch. Also check for surge frequency if the operating frequency is 5 Hz.**

#### Sub-part (a) — Determine wire diameter and mean coil diameter (7 marks).

**Step 1 — Spring rate and energy check:**

Given $\delta_{\max} = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $P_{\max} = 1000$ N:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{P_{\max}}{\delta_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{1000}{0.05} \;=\; 20000 \;\; \text{N/m}
$$

Energy stored:
$$
U \;=\; \frac{1}{2} P \delta \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(1000)(0.05) \;=\; 25 \;\; \text{J} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Wahl's factor for $C = 6$:**

$$
K_w \;=\; \frac{4(6)-1}{4(6)-0.25} + \frac{0.615}{6} \;=\; \frac{23}{23.75} + 0.1025 \;=\; 0.9684 + 0.1025 \;=\; 1.071
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for wire diameter $d$ using corrected shear stress equation:**

Equate $\tau_{\max} = \tau_{\text{allow}}$:

$$
\tau_{\text{allow}} \;=\; K_w \cdot \frac{8 P_{\max} D}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

Since $D = C d = 6 d$:

$$
400 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 1.071 \cdot \frac{8 \times 1000 \times 6 d}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

$$
400 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 1.071 \cdot \frac{48000}{\pi d^{2}}
$$

$$
d^{2} \;=\; \frac{1.071 \times 48000}{\pi \times 400 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; \frac{51408}{1.2566 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
d^{2} \;=\; 4.091 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m}^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; d \;=\; 6.396 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m} \;\approx\; 6.4 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

**Adopt standard $d = 6$ mm** (round to nearest standard gauge).

**Step 4 — Recompute $D$ with new $d$:**

$$
D \;=\; C d \;=\; 6 \times 6 \;=\; 36 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

#### Sub-part (b) — Determine active coils, free length, pitch, and surge frequency (7 marks).

**Step 1 — Active coils from deflection equation:**

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{8 P D^{3} n_a}{G d^{4}}
$$

$$
0.05 \;=\; \frac{8 \times 1000 \times (0.036)^{3} \times n_a}{80 \times 10^{9} \times (0.006)^{4}}
$$

$$
0.05 \;=\; \frac{8 \times 1000 \times 4.666 \times 10^{-5} \times n_a}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.296 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
0.05 \;=\; \frac{0.3733 \, n_a}{103.68}
$$

$$
n_a \;=\; \frac{0.05 \times 103.68}{0.3733} \;\approx\; 13.88
$$

**Adopt $n_a = 14$ coils.**

**Step 2 — Total coils (with closed and ground ends):**

$$
n_t \;=\; n_a + 2 \;=\; 14 + 2 \;=\; 16
$$

**Step 3 — Solid length:**

$$
L_s \;=\; n_t \cdot d \;=\; 16 \times 6 \;=\; 96 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Free length:**

$$
L_f \;=\; L_s + \delta_{\max} + \text{clearance}
$$

Take clearance $= 0.15 \times \delta_{\max} = 0.15 \times 50 = 7.5$ mm:

$$
L_f \;=\; 96 + 50 + 7.5 \;=\; 153.5 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Pitch:**

$$
p \;=\; \frac{L_f}{n_t - 1} \;=\; \frac{153.5}{15} \;\approx\; 10.23 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

Since $p = 10.23$ mm $> d = 6$ mm ✓ — no coil interlocking.

**Step 6 — Surge frequency check (steel spring):**

$$
f_n \;=\; \frac{3.56 \times 10^{5} \, d}{n_a \, D^{2}} \;=\; \frac{3.56 \times 10^{5} \times 6}{14 \times (36)^{2}}
$$

$$
f_n \;=\; \frac{2.136 \times 10^{6}}{14 \times 1296} \;=\; \frac{2.136 \times 10^{6}}{18144} \;\approx\; 117.7 \;\; \text{cycles/min} \;\approx\; 1.96 \;\; \text{Hz}
$$

Forcing frequency $= 5$ Hz, but surge should be **at least 20 times the forcing frequency** to avoid resonance:

$$
20 \times 5 \;=\; 100 \;\; \text{Hz}
$$

Since $f_n = 1.96$ Hz $\ll 100$ Hz — **the spring will surge** ✗. **Redesign required** with smaller $D$ or fewer coils.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: A helical compression spring of mean coil diameter 50 mm is made from a wire of 5 mm diameter. The spring has 12 active coils and is subjected to a maximum load of 250 N. Take the shear modulus as 80 GPa. Calculate: (a) the maximum shear stress in the wire using Wahl's correction, and (b) the deflection, spring rate, and strain energy stored at maximum load.**

#### Sub-part (a) — Maximum Shear Stress (7 marks).

**Given:** $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m, $d = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m, $n_a = 12$, $P = 250$ N, $G = 80$ GPa.

**Step 1 — Spring index:**

$$
C \;=\; \frac{D}{d} \;=\; \frac{50}{5} \;=\; 10
$$

**Step 2 — Wahl's factor:**

$$
K_w \;=\; \frac{4(10) - 1}{4(10) - 0.25} + \frac{0.615}{10} \;=\; \frac{39}{39.75} + 0.0615
$$

$$
K_w \;=\; 0.9811 + 0.0615 \;=\; 1.0426
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; K_w \cdot \frac{8 P D}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; 1.0426 \cdot \frac{8 \times 250 \times 0.05}{\pi \times (0.005)^{3}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; 1.0426 \cdot \frac{100}{3.927 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 1.0426 \times 2.546 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;\approx\; 2.654 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{Pa} \;\approx\; 265.4 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

#### Sub-part (b) — Deflection, Spring Rate, and Energy (7 marks).

**Step 1 — Deflection:**

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{8 P D^{3} n_a}{G d^{4}}
$$

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{8 \times 250 \times (0.05)^{3} \times 12}{80 \times 10^{9} \times (0.005)^{4}}
$$

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{8 \times 250 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 12}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{0.3}{50} \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m} \;=\; 6.0 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Spring rate:**

$$
k \;=\; \frac{P}{\delta} \;=\; \frac{250}{0.006} \;\approx\; 41666.7 \;\; \text{N/m} \;\approx\; 41.67 \;\; \text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Strain energy stored:**

$$
U \;=\; \frac{1}{2} P \delta \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(250)(0.006) \;=\; 0.75 \;\; \text{J}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---------|:------|
| Spring index $C$ | 10 |
| Wahl's factor $K_w$ | 1.0426 |
| Max shear stress $\tau_{\max}$ | 265.4 MPa |
| Deflection $\delta$ | 6.0 mm |
| Spring rate $k$ | 41.67 N/mm |
| Strain energy $U$ | 0.75 J |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong $n_a$** — For closed-ground ends, $n_a = n - 2$. Students often forget this and use $n$ directly, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting Wahl's factor entirely** — This is the single most common error. KTU examiners will deduct **2 marks** if the corrected equation $\tau = K_w \cdot 8PD/(\pi d^{3})$ is not used.
> 3. **Unit mixing** — Always use SI (N, m, Pa) OR consistently use mm, N, MPa throughout. Mixing mm and cm is a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Pitch calculation error** — The formula $p = L_f / (n_t - 1)$ has the $(n_t - 1)$, not $n_t$. This is a frequent slip.
> 5. **Not verifying $p \ge d$** — Always check that the pitch exceeds the wire diameter to ensure no coil interlocking.
> 6. **Omitting the surge frequency check** — For springs used in cyclic or high-frequency applications (e.g., valve springs), surge frequency must be verified. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Buckling check omitted** — For long compression springs ($L_f / D > 5.26$), buckling must be addressed using slenderness ratio criteria.
> 8. **Incorrect choice of spring index** — KTU expects $C$ in the range 6–10 for "well-designed" springs. Choosing $C = 3$ or $C = 15$ leads to a poorly designed spring, even if calculations are correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A spring is an elastic element that stores energy via deflection; design is governed by allowable stress and permissible deflection.
- **Spring index:** $C = D/d$, optimal range **6 to 10**.
- **Wahl's factor:** $K_w = (4C - 1)/(4C - 0.25) + 0.615/C$ — applies the curvature correction.
- **Maximum shear stress:** $\tau_{\max} = K_w \cdot 8PD/(\pi d^{3})$ — must be $\le \tau_{\text{allow}}$.
- **Deflection:** $\delta = 8PD^{3}n_a/(Gd^{4})$.
- **Spring rate:** $k = Gd^{4}/(8D^{3}n_a) = P/\delta$.
- **Strain energy:** $U = (1/2) P\delta = \tau^{2}V/(4G)$.
- **Solid length:** $L_s = n_t \cdot d$.
- **Free length:** $L_f = L_s + \delta_{\max} + \text{clearance}$, with clearance $\approx 0.15\,\delta_{\max}$.
- **Pitch:** $p = L_f / (n_t - 1)$, must satisfy $p \ge d$.
- **Active coils (closed-ground ends):** $n_a = n - 2$.
- **Surge frequency (steel):** $f_n = 3.56 \times 10^{5} \cdot d / (n_a D^{2})$ cycles/min.
- **Allowable stress:** $\tau_{\text{allow}} = S_{sy}/N$ for static; $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 0.3\,S_{ut}/N$ for cyclic (KTU standard).
- **Material selection:** Hard-drawn carbon steel, chrome-vanadium, stainless steel — chosen based on environment, load, and fatigue life.
- **Buckling check:** Required if $L_f / D > 5.26$ (fixed-fixed ends) or 3.7 (free-fixed).
- **Energy equivalence:** A spring designed for given $P$ and $\delta$ stores $U = (1/2)P\delta$ — useful in clutch, brake, and buffer design.
- **KTU Board pattern:** Expect a numerical sub-part worth 7 marks (compute $K_w$, $\tau$, $\delta$, $k$, $U$) and a conceptual sub-part worth 7 marks (define Wahl's factor, explain $C$ limits, justify end-type choice).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Revision Tip:** Always carry the following 5 quantities in your head before solving any spring numerical: $C$, $K_w$, $\tau_{\max}$, $\delta$, $k$. If you can compute these five, you have solved 80% of any KTU spring problem.

---

**END OF NOTES — ALLOWABLE STRESSES AND DEFLECTIONS OF SPRINGS (MODULE 1, PENST744)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Fluctuating Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Design for Fluctuating Loads # 📘 Design for Fluctuating Loads — KTU Module 1 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fluctuating Load:** A load whose magnitude (or both magnitude and direction) varies with time in a regular or irregular pattern during the operation of a machine element. In the context of machine design, this translates to **fluctuating stress**, which is the primary cause of **fatigue failure**. In KTU 2024 Scheme machine design, a complete design for fluctuating loads requires the designer to: 1. Characterize the type of stress cycle. 2. Evaluate the **endurance limit** of the selected material. 3. Apply one of the well-known failure criteria (**Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, or ASME-Elliptic**) to obtain a **factor of safety** against yielding and fatigue. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Paper Clip** > Take a standard paper clip and bend it back-and-forth repeatedly. Each bend applies a small force, well below the strength needed to snap it in one shot. Yet after 20–30 cycles, the clip **breaks cleanly** at the bend. This is **fatigue failure** — the *load fluctuated*, but the *peak load was never high enough to cause static failure*. Machine elements (shafts, springs, gears, connecting rods) face the same invisible enemy. ### 1.3 Types of Fluctuating Loads (Stress Cycles) | S.No. | Type of Stress | Description | Real Example | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Steady (Static) Stress** | Magnitude & direction do **not** change with time. | Bolt preload, weight of a structure. | | 2 | **Fluctuating (or Pulsating) Stress** | Varies between a **maximum** and a **minimum** value, both of which are **tensile** (or both compressive). | Friction-clutch shaft, leaf spring. | | 3 | **Reversed Stress (Completely)** | Varies between equal **tensile** and **compressive** magnitudes. | Bending of a rotating shaft, I.C. engine valve spring. | | 4 | **Combined Steady + Variable Stress** | A steady stress is **superimposed** with a fluctuating stress. | Bolted joint with vibration. | | 5 | **Shock (Impact) Load** | Sudden application of a load, often considered a special fluctuating case. | Punch press, cam impact. | ### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Stress Concentration Factor, $K_t$:** The ratio of the theoretical (max.) stress at a notch/hole/fillet to the nominal stress. $K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$. > > **Notch Sensitivity, $q$:** A material property $(0 \le q \le 1)$ describing how sensitive the material is to a stress concentrator. $q = 0$ → fully insensitive, $q = 1$ → fully sensitive. > > **Fatigue Stress Concentration Factor, $K_f$:** Used in design to discount the theoretical concentration. $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$. > > **Endurance Limit, $S_e$:** The stress level below which the material can theoretically endure **infinite cycles** without failure. For steels, it is approximately $0.5 \, S_{ut}$. > > **Fatigue Strength, $S_f$:** The stress level at which failure occurs after a **specified finite number of cycles** (e.g., $10^3$, $10^4$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Stress Cycle Parameters — The 'Core' of Fluctuating Load Design Every stress cycle can be described using **four** parameters: $$ \sigma_{max}, \quad \sigma_{min}, \quad \sigma_m, \quad \sigma_a $$ **Step-by-step Logic:** 1. **Maximum Stress ($\sigma_{max}$):** The algebraically largest stress in the cycle. 2. **Minimum Stress ($\sigma_{min}$):** The algebraically smallest stress in the cycle. 3. **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$):** The steady (average) component. 4. **Amplitude (Variable) Stress ($\sigma_a$):** The alternating component. These quantities can be visualised on a sinusoidal cycle as a constant mean level plus an oscillating sinusoidal component. **Why these four?** — Because failure theories (Soderberg, Goodman, etc.) are *empirically* derived as functions of $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$. The design becomes a 2D problem on the $(\sigma_a, \sigma_m)$ plane. ### 2.2 Primary Relations $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} $$ $$ \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} $$ $$ \sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a $$ $$ \sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a $$ ### 2.3 Stress Ratio (R) and Amplitude Ratio $$ R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} $$ Special values: - **Fully Reversed:** $\sigma_{min} = -\sigma_{max}$ → $R = -1$ - **Repeated (Pulsating):** $\sigma_{min} = 0$ → $R = 0$ - **Static:** $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{max}$ → $R = +1$ ### 2.4 Endurance Limit — The Boundary of Infinite Life For **steels**: $$ S'_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut} $$ For **non-ferrous metals** (no true endurance limit): $$ S'_e \approx 0.4 \, S_{ut} \quad \text{to} \quad 0.5 \, S_{ut} $$ ### 2.5 Marin’s Modification Factors — Correcting the Endurance Limit The "uncorrected" endurance limit $S'_e$ is valid for a standard test specimen. For a real machine element, we apply Marin’s multiplicative factors: $$ S_e = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \, S'_e $$ | Factor | Name | Formula / Description | |:---:|:---|:---| | $k_a$ | Surface Factor | $k_a = a \, S_{ut}^{\,b}$ (Shigley’s table for finish) | | $k_b$ | Size Factor | For axial loading $k_b = 1$; for rotating bending $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ (for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm) | | $k_c$ | Load Factor | $k_c = 1$ (bending), $0.85$ (axial), $0.59$ (torsion) | | $k_d$ | Temperature Factor | $k_d = 1$ for $T \le 450^\circ$C | | $k_e$ | Reliability Factor | $k_e = 1$ for 50% reliability; 0.897 for 90%; 0.814 for 99% | | $k_f$ | Miscellaneous Factor | $k_f = 1$ typically | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Favourite (very high yield):** A standard KTU 2024 question asks to "estimate the endurance limit of a component" — students are expected to know **all six Marin factors** and apply them in a single multiplication. ### 2.6 Failure Criteria — The "Big Four" Equations Let $\sigma_a$ = alternating (variable) stress, $\sigma_m$ = mean stress, $S_e$ = corrected endurance limit, $S_{ut}$ = ultimate tensile strength, $S_y$ = yield strength, $N$ = factor of safety. #### (a) Soderberg Equation (Most Conservative) $$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{N} $$ #### (b) Goodman Equation $$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{N} $$ #### (c) Gerber Equation (Parabolic) $$ \frac{N \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{N \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1 $$ #### (d) ASME-Elliptic (for ductile steels) $$ \left(\frac{N \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{N \sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^2 = 1 $$ ### 2.7 Compact KTU Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Mean stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ | Algebraic average | | 2 | Amplitude stress | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$ | Half range | | 3 | Stress ratio | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | $-1 \le R \le 1$ typical | | 4 | Fatigue stress conc. | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Notch sensitivity $q$ | | 5 | Endurance limit (uncorr.) | $S'_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | Steels only | | 6 | Marin corrected | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$ | Six factors | | 7 | Soderberg | $1/N = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_y$ | Conservative | | 8 | Goodman | $1/N = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut}$ | Linear | | 9 | Gerber (solved) | $N = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\tfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m}\right)^2 \tfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\left[-1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\tfrac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a}\right)^2}\right]$ | Parabolic | | 10 | ASME-Elliptic (solved) | $N = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + (\sigma_m/S_y)^2}}$ | Ductile metals | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Use-Case:** These equations are used in the design of **rotating shafts, axles, springs, crankshafts, connecting rods, gear teeth, and bolted joints** — all of which undergo fluctuating stresses. The criterion chosen (Soderberg, Goodman, etc.) determines how **conservative** your final shaft diameter will be. Soderberg yields the largest (safest) diameter. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Amplitude Stress (from $\sigma_{max}$, $\sigma_{min}$) **Given:** A stress cycle whose maximum is $\sigma_{max}$ and minimum is $\sigma_{min}$. **Step 1** — Write $\sigma_{max}$ as the sum of its mean and amplitude parts. By definition of mean and amplitude: $$ \sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a $$ **Step 2** — Write $\sigma_{min}$ similarly. The minimum corresponds to subtracting the amplitude from the mean: $$ \sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a $$ **Step 3** — Add the two equations to eliminate $\sigma_a$: $$ \sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} = (\sigma_m + \sigma_a) + (\sigma_m - \sigma_a) = 2\sigma_m $$ **Step 4** — Solve for the mean stress: $$ \boxed{\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}} $$ **Step 5** — Subtract the two equations to eliminate $\sigma_m$: $$ \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = (\sigma_m + \sigma_a) - (\sigma_m - \sigma_a) = 2\sigma_a $$ **Step 6** — Solve for the amplitude stress: $$ \boxed{\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Soderberg Failure Criterion (The Conservative Line) **Conceptual Basis:** Soderberg assumes that failure occurs when the *combined* state $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ reaches a straight line joining two safe points on the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane: - Point A: $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (S_y, 0)$ → purely static loading at the yield point. - Point B: $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (0, S_e)$ → purely reversed loading at the endurance limit. **Step 1** — Equation of a straight line passing through $(S_y, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$ using the intercept form: $$ \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 $$ **Step 2** — Introduce the **factor of safety, $N$**. A safe design must lie *inside* the line, so each stress component is reduced by $N$: $$ \frac{N\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{N\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 $$ **Step 3** — Divide both sides by $N$: $$ \boxed{\frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{1}{N}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Soderberg's line is **linear**, joining yield on the $\sigma_m$-axis to the endurance limit on the $\sigma_a$-axis. It is the most conservative of the four criteria and is the **default choice in KTU examinations unless otherwise specified**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Goodman Failure Criterion (Linear, Slightly Less Conservative) **Conceptual Basis:** Identical to Soderberg, except the right-hand intercept on the $\sigma_m$-axis is taken as $S_{ut}$ (ultimate tensile strength) instead of $S_y$. This reflects the empirical observation that a static overload can momentarily exceed yield without causing yielding failure. **Step 1** — Intercept form joining $(S_{ut}, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$: $$ \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 $$ **Step 2** — Apply factor of safety $N$: $$ \frac{N\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + \frac{N\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 $$ **Step 3** — Divide by $N$: $$ \boxed{\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{1}{N}} $$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Gerber Parabolic Criterion **Conceptual Basis:** Gerber (1874) observed that the *actual* safe region on the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane is a **parabola** that passes through $(S_{ut}, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$ and bows downward (i.e., is less conservative than Goodman for a given $\sigma_m$). **Step 1** — Standard form of Gerber's parabola (without factor of safety): $$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1 $$ **Step 2** — Introduce the factor of safety $N$ (each stress component is multiplied by $N$): $$ \frac{N\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{N\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1 $$ **Step 3** — Solve for $N$ explicitly (this is the form expected in KTU 2024 numericals). Let $X = N \sigma_m / S_{ut}$ and $Y = N \sigma_a / S_e$. Then $X^2 + Y/S_e \cdot S_e - 1 = 0$ ... we can write: $$ N^2 \left(\frac{\sigma_m^2}{S_{ut}^2}\right) + N \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right) - 1 = 0 $$ **Step 4** — This is a quadratic in $N$ of the form $aN^2 + bN + c = 0$, where: - $a = (\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2$ - $b = \sigma_a/S_e$ - $c = -1$ **Step 5** — Apply the quadratic formula $N = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ (taking the positive root since $c < 0$): $$ N = \frac{-\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + 4 \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2}}{2 \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2} $$ **Step 6** — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(S_{ut}/\sigma_m)^2 \cdot S_e$ to obtain the more compact engineering form: $$ \boxed{N = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m}\right)^2 \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\left[-1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2 S_e \sigma_m}{S_{ut} \sigma_a}\right)^2}\right]} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** In KTU exams, if a numerical asks for the **factor of safety using Gerber's criterion**, students are expected to plug into the **quadratic form** in Step 5 — that is the most direct, marks-friendly method. --- ### 3.5 Derivation: ASME-Elliptic Criterion (Quadratic) **Step 1** — Standard ellipse equation in $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ passing through $(S_y, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$: $$ \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 = 1 $$ **Step 2** — Apply factor of safety $N$: $$ \left(\frac{N\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{N\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 = 1 $$ **Step 3** — Factor $N^2$ out: $$ N^2 \left[\left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2\right] = 1 $$ **Step 4** — Solve for $N$: $$ \boxed{N = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2}}} $$ --- ### 3.6 Worked Numerical: Combined Steady + Variable Bending **Problem (KTU-Style):** A shaft is subjected to a bending moment that fluctuates between $+1500$ N·m and $+500$ N·m. The shaft is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa. The corrected endurance limit is $S_e = 220$ MPa. The shaft has a shoulder fillet with $K_t = 2.0$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.85$. Using the Soderberg criterion, find the factor of safety and the required shaft diameter. Use a factor of safety $N = 2$ for the final diameter calculation. **Solution:** **Step 1** — Compute $K_f$ (fatigue stress concentration factor): $$ K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85(2.0 - 1) = 1.85 $$ **Step 2** — Compute mean and amplitude bending stresses in terms of the section modulus $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$. (Note: $M$ in N·mm; here convert to N·mm: $M = 1500 \times 10^3$ N·mm.) $$ M_{max} = 1500 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}, \quad M_{min} = 500 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm} $$ **Step 3** — Mean bending moment: $$ M_m = \frac{M_{max} + M_{min}}{2} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3 + 500 \times 10^3}{2} = 1000 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm} $$ **Step 4** — Amplitude bending moment: $$ M_a = \frac{M_{max} - M_{min}}{2} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3 - 500 \times 10^3}{2} = 500 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm} $$ **Step 5** — Mean and amplitude stresses (in terms of $Z$): $$ \sigma_m = \frac{M_m}{Z} = \frac{1000 \times 10^3}{Z} \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{M_a}{Z} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{Z} \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 6** — Apply Soderberg with $K_f$ (the factor is applied to the *alternating* component only, since the notch is the source of fatigue, not yielding): $$ \frac{1}{N} = \frac{K_f \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} $$ **Step 7** — Substitute. Let $C = 10^3 / Z$ (units: MPa per N·mm). Then $\sigma_m = 1000 C$, $\sigma_a = 500 C$: $$ \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1.85 \times 500 \, C}{220} + \frac{1000 \, C}{380} $$ **Step 8** — Evaluate numerical coefficients: $$ \frac{1}{2} = 4.2045 \, C + 2.6316 \, C = 6.8361 \, C $$ **Step 9** — Solve for $C$: $$ C = \frac{0.5}{6.8361} = 0.07314 \text{ (MPa per N·mm)} $$ **Step 10** — Solve for $Z$: $$ Z = \frac{10^3}{0.07314} = 13{,}672 \text{ mm}^3 $$ **Step 11** — Solve for $d$ using $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$: $$ d^3 = \frac{32 Z}{\pi} = \frac{32 \times 13{,}672}{\pi} = 139{,}248 \text{ mm}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{d = (139{,}248)^{1/3} \approx 51.8 \text{ mm}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The shaft diameter is approximately **52 mm**. If Goodman or ASME-Elliptic were used, the required diameter would be **smaller** (less conservative). KTU marks the conservative Soderberg solution highest when the question says "use the conservative criterion". ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Types of Stress Cycles ```mermaid graph TD A["STRESS CYCLE TYPES"]:::main A --> B["STEADY STATIC STRESS"]:::type A --> C["FLUCTUATING STRESS"]:::type A --> D["REVERSED STRESS"]:::type A --> E["COMBINED STEADY + VARIABLE"]:::type A --> F["SHOCK OR IMPACT LOAD"]:::type B --> B1["R EQUALS PLUS 1"] C --> C1["SIGMA MAX TENSILE, SIGMA MIN ZERO OR POSITIVE"] D --> D1["R EQUALS MINUS 1 EQUAL TENSION AND COMPRESSION"] E --> E1["MEAN PLUS SINUSOIDAL COMPONENT"] F --> F1["SUDDEN APPLICATION OF LOAD"] G["KEY OUTPUT PARAMETERS"]:::output B1 --> G C1 --> G D1 --> G E1 --> G F1 --> G G --> G1["SIGMA MAX, SIGMA MIN"] G --> G2["SIGMA M MEAN STRESS"] G --> G3["SIGMA A AMPLITUDE STRESS"] G --> G4["STRESS RATIO R"] classDef main fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a1f3a,stroke-width:3px classDef type fill:#2d6a4f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#9d174d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#6b0f3a,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Marin Endurance Limit Modification Chain ```mermaid graph LR A["S UT
ULTIMATE TENSILE STRENGTH"]:::input A --> B["S PRIME E
0.5 TIMES S UT
UNCORRECTED ENDURANCE"]:::stage B --> C["S E
CORRECTED ENDURANCE LIMIT"]:::final C --> D["FAILURE CRITERION
SODERBERG GOODMAN
GERBER ASME"]:::crit C --> C1["KA SURFACE FACTOR"]:::factor C --> C2["KB SIZE FACTOR"]:::factor C --> C3["KC LOAD FACTOR"]:::factor C --> C4["KD TEMPERATURE FACTOR"]:::factor C --> C5["KE RELIABILITY FACTOR"]:::factor C --> C6["KF MISC FACTOR"]:::factor D --> E["FACTOR OF SAFETY N"]:::ns classDef input fill:#0b3d91,color:#ffffff,stroke:#062863,stroke-width:2px classDef stage fill:#6f1d1b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4a1411,stroke-width:2px classDef final fill:#003049,color:#ffd60a,stroke:#001a2c,stroke-width:3px classDef factor fill:#386641,color:#ffffff,stroke:#264a30,stroke-width:2px classDef crit fill:#7b2cbf,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4d1a7a,stroke-width:2px classDef ns fill:#bb3e03,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a2801,stroke-width:3px ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Design Procedure for Fluctuating Loads ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1 IDENTIFY LOADING
SIGMA MAX AND SIGMA MIN"]:::step S2["STEP 2 COMPUTE SIGMA M AND SIGMA A"]:::step S3["STEP 3 IDENTIFY MATERIAL
S UT AND S Y"]:::step S4["STEP 4 COMPUTE S PRIME E EQUALS 0.5 S UT"]:::step S5["STEP 5 APPLY MARIN FACTORS
KA KB KC KD KE KF"]:::step S6["STEP 6 OBTAIN S E CORRECTED"]:::step S7["STEP 7 APPLY K F TO SIGMA A
K F EQUALS 1 PLUS Q K T MINUS 1"]:::step S8["STEP 8 SELECT FAILURE CRITERION
SODERBERG GOODMAN GERBER ASME"]:::step S9["STEP 9 SOLVE FOR N OR DIMENSION D"]:::step S10["STEP 10 CHECK DEFLECTION AND BEARING"]:::step S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10 classDef step fill:#1a659e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d3f6b,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The block flow above is the **canonical 10-step procedure** for any KTU numerical on "Design for Fluctuating Loads". Following these steps in order guarantees full marks. ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Failure Envelope Comparison ```mermaid graph TD A["SIGMA A AXIS
ALTERNATING STRESS"]:::ax P1["POINT B
0, S E
ENDURANCE LIMIT"]:::pt P2["POINT C
S Y, 0
YIELD STRENGTH SODERBERG"]:::pt P3["POINT D
S UT, 0
ULTIMATE STRENGTH GOODMAN"]:::pt P4["GERBER PARABOLA
DOWNWARD BOWED"]:::curve P5["ASME ELLIPSE
INSCRIBED IN SODERBERG RECT"]:::curve P6["SODERBERG LINE
STRAIGHT MOST CONSERVATIVE"]:::curve P7["GOODMAN LINE
STRAIGHT LESS CONSERVATIVE"]:::curve A --> P1 A --> P2 A --> P3 P1 --> P4 P2 --> P5 P3 --> P6 P4 --> P7 classDef ax fill:#1b263b,color:#ffd60a,stroke:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px classDef pt fill:#6a040f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:2px classDef curve fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1d6f64,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The diagram above is a *schematic representation* of the failure envelopes on the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane. From most to least conservative: **Soderberg > ASME-Elliptic > Goodman > Gerber**. This ordering is a **favourite KTU conceptual question**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the terms: (a) **Mean stress**, (b) **Amplitude stress**, and (c) **Stress ratio** with respect to a fluctuating load. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **(a) Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$):** It is the average (algebraic) value of the maximum and minimum stresses in a fluctuating stress cycle. Mathematically, $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$. > > **(b) Amplitude (Variable) Stress ($\sigma_a$):** It is the magnitude of the alternating component of the stress cycle, i.e., the deviation of the stress from its mean value. $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$. > > **(c) Stress Ratio ($R$):** It is the algebraic ratio of the minimum stress to the maximum stress in a cycle, $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$. It defines the *type* of cycle: $R = -1$ for fully reversed, $R = 0$ for pulsating, $R = +1$ for static. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is **Marin's modification** for the endurance limit? List any four modifying factors. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The uncorrected endurance limit $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ is valid only for a standard rotating-beam specimen. For a real machine element, Marin proposed six multiplicative correction factors: > > $$ > S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \, S'_e > $$ > > Four factors are: > 1. **$k_a$ (Surface factor)** — accounts for surface finish (grinding, polishing, machining, forging, as-cast). > 2. **$k_b$ (Size factor)** — accounts for the size of the component being larger than the test specimen. > 3. **$k_c$ (Load factor)** — distinguishes between bending, axial, and torsional loading. > 4. **$k_e$ (Reliability factor)** — accounts for statistical reliability requirement (90%, 99%, etc.). --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the **Soderberg equation** for the design of machine elements subjected to combined steady and variable stresses. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A round shaft is subjected to a bending moment that varies from $+400$ N·m to $-200$ N·m and an axial tensile load that varies from $20$ kN to $10$ kN simultaneously. The shaft is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 560$ MPa, $S_y = 400$ MPa, and corrected endurance limit $S_e = 200$ MPa. The shoulder fillet has $K_t = 2.4$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.8$. Using the **Soderberg criterion** with a factor of safety $N = 1.8$, determine the shaft diameter. **(7 marks)** --- #### 📋 Model Solution — Question A (Part a) **Step 1** — Soderberg proposed a *linear* failure envelope on the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane. **Step 2** — Two extreme points define the line: - Pure static yield: $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (S_y, 0)$. - Pure reversed loading at endurance limit: $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (0, S_e)$. **Step 3** — Intercept form of the line: $$ \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 $$ **Step 4** — Applying factor of safety $N$: $$ \frac{N\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{N\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 $$ **Step 5** — Final Soderberg equation: $$ \boxed{\frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{1}{N}} $$ **Conclusion:** Soderberg is the most conservative of the four criteria. It guards against *both* fatigue failure and yielding simultaneously, which is why it is preferred for ductile components. --- #### 📋 Model Solution — Question A (Part b) **Step 1** — Compute the **fatigue stress concentration factor**: $$ K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.8(2.4 - 1) = 1 + 1.12 = 2.12 $$ **Step 2** — Convert moments and forces to N·mm and N: - $M_{max} = +400 \times 10^3$ N·mm, $M_{min} = -200 \times 10^3$ N·mm. - $F_{max} = 20{,}000$ N (tensile), $F_{min} = 10{,}000$ N (tensile). **Step 3** — Mean and amplitude bending moments: $$ M_m = \frac{400{,}000 + (-200{,}000)}{2} = 100{,}000 \text{ N·mm} $$ $$ M_a = \frac{400{,}000 - (-200{,}000)}{2} = 300{,}000 \text{ N·mm} $$ **Step 4** — Mean and amplitude axial loads: $$ F_m = \frac{20{,}000 + 10{,}000}{2} = 15{,}000 \text{ N} $$ $$ F_a = \frac{20{,}000 - 10{,}000}{2} = 5{,}000 \text{ N} $$ **Step 5** — Section modulus $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$, area $A = \pi d^2 / 4$. Define $C = 1/Z$ (MPa per N·mm) and $D = 1/A$ (MPa per N). **Step 6** — Stresses: $$ \sigma_{m,bend} = M_m C = 100{,}000 C, \quad \sigma_{a,bend} = M_a C = 300{,}000 C $$ $$ \sigma_{m,axial} = F_m D = 15{,}000 D, \quad \sigma_{a,axial} = F_a D = 5{,}000 D $$ **Step 7** — **Total** mean and amplitude stresses: $$ \sigma_m = 100{,}000 C + 15{,}000 D $$ $$ \sigma_a = 300{,}000 C + 5{,}000 D $$ **Step 8** — Apply Soderberg. (The $K_f$ factor is applied to the *alternating* component only.) $$ \frac{1}{N} = \frac{K_f \sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} $$ **Step 9** — Substitute $N = 1.8$ and the numerical constants. Since $Z = \pi d^3/32$ and $A = \pi d^2/4$, note that $D = 4/(\pi d^2)$ and $C = 32/(\pi d^3)$. The bending terms will dominate, so a useful trick is to express the axial contributions with the equivalent factor $C$ by noting $D/C = d/8$: - Axial mean stress in terms of $C$: $15{,}000 D = 15{,}000 \cdot (d/8) \cdot C = 1875 d \cdot C$. - Axial amplitude in terms of $C$: $5{,}000 D = 5{,}000 \cdot (d/8) \cdot C = 625 d \cdot C$. **Step 10** — Substituting back: $$ \sigma_m = (100{,}000 + 1875 d) \, C $$ $$ \sigma_a = (300{,}000 + 625 d) \, C $$ **Step 11** — Soderberg equation becomes: $$ \frac{1}{1.8} = \frac{2.12 \cdot (300{,}000 + 625 d) \, C}{200} + \frac{(100{,}000 + 1875 d) \, C}{400} $$ **Step 12** — Compute the coefficients: - $2.12 \times 300{,}000 / 200 = 3180$; $2.12 \times 625 / 200 = 6.625$. - $100{,}000 / 400 = 250$; $1875 / 400 = 4.6875$. $$ 0.5556 = (3180 + 6.625 d) C + (250 + 4.6875 d) C $$ $$ 0.5556 = (3430 + 11.3125 d) C $$ **Step 13** — Substitute $C = 32/(\pi d^3)$: $$ 0.5556 = \frac{(3430 + 11.3125 d) \cdot 32}{\pi d^3} $$ **Step 14** — Multiply both sides by $\pi d^3 / 32$: $$ 0.5556 \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = 3430 + 11.3125 d $$ $$ 0.05453 \, d^3 = 3430 + 11.3125 d $$ **Step 15** — This is a cubic. Solve by iteration. **Try $d = 40$ mm:** - LHS: $0.05453 \times 64{,}000 = 3489.9$ - RHS: $3430 + 11.3125 \times 40 = 3430 + 452.5 = 3882.5$ - LHS < RHS, so $d$ must be **larger**. **Try $d = 42$ mm:** - LHS: $0.05453 \times 74{,}088 = 4039.8$ - RHS: $3430 + 11.3125 \times 42 = 3430 + 475.1 = 3905.1$ - LHS > RHS. So $d$ is between 40 and 42 mm. **Step 16** — Linear interpolation: $$ d = 40 + 2 \cdot \frac{3882.5 - 3489.9}{4039.8 - 3489.9} = 40 + 2 \cdot \frac{392.6}{549.9} = 40 + 1.43 \approx 41.4 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 17** — Round up to a standard size: $$ \boxed{d \approx 42 \text{ mm}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** > - **Forgetting $K_f$:** Many students apply Soderberg with the *uncorrected* $\sigma_a$ and lose 2 marks. > - **Applying $K_f$ to $\sigma_m$:** The fatigue stress concentration factor affects *alternating* stresses only, not the mean. > - **Forgetting units conversion:** Moments must be in N·mm, stresses in MPa. Mismatch loses 1 mark. > - **Not rounding up:** Always use the next standard shaft diameter above the calculated value. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **Goodman** and **Gerber** failure criteria for fluctuating stresses. Compare their conservatism with the Soderberg criterion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel shaft has ultimate strength $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa, yield strength $S_y = 420$ MPa, and a corrected endurance limit $S_e = 240$ MPa. The bending stress fluctuates between $\sigma_{max} = 220$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = -60$ MPa. Using the **Goodman criterion**, determine the factor of safety. Also compute the factor of safety using the **Gerber criterion** for the same loading. **(7 marks)** --- #### 📋 Model Solution — Question B (Part a) **Goodman Criterion:** - Linear envelope joining $(S_{ut}, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$. - Equation: $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{N}$. - It uses $S_{ut}$ (not $S_y$) on the mean axis, so it permits a small amount of mean stress above yield. - Less conservative than Soderberg but more conservative than Gerber. **Gerber Criterion:** - Parabolic envelope that bows downward, passing through $(S_{ut}, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$. - Equation: $\dfrac{N\sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{N\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$. - Most accurate of the three for ductile metals (matches experimental data closely). - Least conservative — allows the largest permissible $\sigma_a$ for a given $\sigma_m$. **Comparison Summary (most → least conservative):** $$ \text{Soderberg} > \text{ASME-Elliptic} > \text{Goodman} > \text{Gerber} $$ **Engineering Use:** For ductile components where weight-minimisation is critical, Gerber is preferred. For ductile components where *no yielding* is permitted even momentarily, Soderberg is mandatory. For general design, Goodman is the **industry default**. --- #### 📋 Model Solution — Question B (Part b) **Step 1** — Compute mean and amplitude stresses: $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{220 + (-60)}{2} = \frac{160}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{220 - (-60)}{2} = \frac{280}{2} = 140 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2** — **Goodman criterion:** $$ \frac{1}{N} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{140}{240} + \frac{80}{620} $$ **Step 3** — Evaluate: $$ \frac{140}{240} = 0.5833, \quad \frac{80}{620} = 0.1290 $$ $$ \frac{1}{N} = 0.5833 + 0.1290 = 0.7123 $$ $$ \boxed{N_{Goodman} = \frac{1}{0.7123} \approx 1.40} $$ **Step 4** — **Gerber criterion** (using the quadratic form). Set: $$ a = \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{80}{620}\right)^2 = (0.1290)^2 = 0.01665 $$ $$ b = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{140}{240} = 0.5833 $$ $$ c = -1 $$ **Step 5** — Solve $aN^2 + bN + c = 0$: $$ 0.01665 \, N^2 + 0.5833 \, N - 1 = 0 $$ **Step 6** — Apply quadratic formula $N = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ (positive root): $$ N = \frac{-0.5833 + \sqrt{(0.5833)^2 - 4(0.01665)(-1)}}{2(0.01665)} $$ $$ N = \frac{-0.5833 + \sqrt{0.3402 + 0.0666}}{0.0333} $$ $$ N = \frac{-0.5833 + \sqrt{0.4068}}{0.0333} $$ $$ N = \frac{-0.5833 + 0.6378}{0.0333} $$ $$ N = \frac{0.0545}{0.0333} $$ $$ \boxed{N_{Gerber} \approx 1.64} $$ **Step 7** — **Comparison:** | Criterion | Factor of Safety | |:---|:---:| | Goodman | 1.40 | | Gerber | 1.64 | > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Gerber yields a **higher** factor of safety than Goodman for the same loading, confirming that **Gerber is less conservative**. The difference (≈17%) is significant in KTU 2024 numericals. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** > - **Sign error in $\sigma_m$:** For fully reversed loading, $\sigma_{min}$ is **negative** — students frequently forget to include the negative sign and lose 1 mark. > - **Unit consistency:** Always use MPa throughout the Soderberg/Goodman/Gerber equations. > - **Quadratic formula sign:** In the Gerber solution, use the *positive* root — the negative root is physically meaningless. > - **Confusing $S_e$ and $S'_e$:** $S_e$ is the *corrected* endurance limit (after Marin factors); $S'_e$ is uncorrected. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Design for Fluctuating Loads** - ✅ A **fluctuating load** produces a varying stress that can cause **fatigue failure** even at stresses well below yield. - ✅ **Mean stress** $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ ; **Amplitude stress** $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$. - ✅ **Stress ratio** $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$: equals $-1$ (fully reversed), $0$ (pulsating), $+1$ (static). - ✅ **Fatigue stress concentration factor** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, where $q$ is the notch sensitivity. - ✅ **$K_f$ applies to the alternating component only**, not the mean. - ✅ **Uncorrected endurance limit** $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for steels. - ✅ **Marin's corrected** $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$ — all **six** factors should be known by name. - ✅ **Soderberg** (most conservative): $1/N = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_y$. - ✅ **Goodman** (linear, less conservative): $1/N = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut}$. - ✅ **Gerber** (parabolic, least conservative, most accurate): quadratic in $N$. - ✅ **ASME-Elliptic**: $1/N = \sqrt{(\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + (\sigma_m/S_y)^2}$. - ✅ Ordering (most → least conservative): **Soderberg > ASME-Elliptic > Goodman > Gerber**. - ✅ For a **rotating shaft under bending**, the stress is *fully reversed* ($R = -1$) even if the applied load is constant — this is the most common KTU pitfall. - ✅ For **combined axial + bending loading**, add the axial and bending stresses (mean and amplitude components separately) *before* applying the failure criterion. - ✅ Always use **standard shaft diameters** (rounded up) after the calculation. - ✅ **Soderberg** is the **default conservative criterion** in KTU 2024 unless otherwise stated. - ✅ For a numerical to be *complete*, the answer must include the **factor of safety $N$** explicitly — examiners deduct 1 mark if $N$ is missing.

topics.title: Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Machine Joints

A **mechanical joint** is a point of interface or assembly between two or more machine/member components that transmits mechanical forces, moments, or loads while maintaining structural integrity. In the context of KTU's **Design of Machine Elements (PENST744)**, the term "Joints" within Module 2 refers specifically to the design of **permanent (non-detachable) joints**—namely **Rivet Joints** and **Welded Joints**—which are foundational to fabricating pressure vessels, structural frames, bridges, boilers, and aerospace structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive — Module 2 Focus:** Permanent joints using **Riveting** and **Welding** processes. The design aspects cover both **unloaded (static)** and **eccentrically loaded** conditions. Detachable joints (bolts, keys, pins) belong to a separate module and are **not** covered under this topic.

## 1.2 Classification of Machine Joints

For KTU examination purposes, the classification tree is given below:

- **A. Permanent (Non-detachable) Joints** — *This Module*
  - Riveted Joints
  - Welded Joints
- **B. Semi-Permanent Joints** — *Other modules*
  - Bolted Joints, Cotter Joints, Knuckle Joints
- **C. Temporary (Detachable) Joints**
  - Keys, Splines, Pins

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of This Module

### 1.3.1 Riveted Joints

A **rivet** is a short cylindrical rod (or button-headed pin) made from ductile metal (mild steel, aluminum, brass, copper). It is inserted through a pre-drilled hole spanning two or more plates, and its protruding tail end is **plastic-deformed (upset)** by hammering or hydraulic pressing to form a second head. After upsetting, the joint becomes permanent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Book Stapler:** Think of a rivet as a **single-use metal staple**. Just as a stapler pierces through a stack of papers and folds the wire ends flat to lock them permanently, a rivet pierces through two metal plates and its tail is hammered flat into a head. Once set, you cannot "un-set" it without destroying it. This is why riveted joints dominate aircraft skins, railway bridges, and old boilers—they are vibration-proof, leak-proof, and self-locking.

**Standard Industrial Rivet Head Shapes:**

| Head Type | Application |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Snap (Button) Head** | General purpose; most common in pressure vessels |
| **Pan Head** | Sheet metal work where bearing area is needed |
| **Countersunk Head** | Flush surfaces (aerospace, ship hulls) |
| **Conical Head** | Boiler and high-pressure applications |

### 1.3.2 Welded Joints

A **welded joint** is formed by locally heating two workpieces to a plastic/semi-molten state (with or without filler metal) and allowing them to fuse upon cooling. The bonding occurs at the **atomic level** through metallurgical diffusion, creating a joint whose strength often matches the parent metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Plastic Glue Joint:** Consider two plastic toys being joined using solvent cement. The solvent melts the surface polymer chains of both pieces, and when they harden, the chains entangle seamlessly—the joint is actually as strong as the material itself. Welding works analogously: an electric arc (in arc welding) or an oxy-fuel flame (in gas welding) creates a localized molten pool that, upon solidification, creates a joint that is metallurgically continuous with the base metals. There is **no separate fastener**—the joint and the plate are essentially one piece.

**Common Welding Processes (KTU-relevant):**

- **Arc Welding** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding – SMAW)
- **Gas Welding** (Oxy-Acetylene)
- **Resistance Welding** (Spot, Seam, Projection)
- **TIG/GIG Welding** (Gas Tungsten Arc / Gas Metal Arc)
- **Friction Stir Welding** (Modern, for aluminum)

## 1.4 Why Permanent Joints Matter — Engineering Context

Permanent joints are the **backbone of mass-fabricated engineering infrastructure**:

- **Boilers and Pressure Vessels** → Riveted (older) or Welded (modern); safety-critical leak-proof sealing.
- **Bridges and Structural Frames** → Riveting was historically dominant; welding has now largely replaced it.
- **Aerospace Skins** → Riveting remains dominant (aluminum alloys are not easily welded).
- **Shipbuilding** → Welded (modern) for hulls; riveted for some internal structures.
- **Automotive Chassis** → Spot-welded (resistance welding).

The **factor of safety** in these joints typically ranges from **3 to 6**, depending on the loading type (static, fatigue, impact).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RIVETED JOINTS — Terminology

Before deriving formulas, a student **must** internalize the following geometric vocabulary used in every KTU question:

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Pitch** | $p$ | Centre-to-centre distance between two adjacent rivets in a row. |
| **Gauge** | $g$ | Perpendicular distance between adjacent rows of rivets. |
| **Margin** | $m$ | Distance from the centre of the edge rivet to the edge of the plate. |
| **Nominal Rivet Diameter** | $d$ | The basic diameter of the rivet shank (before driving). |
| **Gross Diameter** | $d_g$ | Diameter of the hole drilled (≈ $d + 1.5$ to $3\,\text{mm}$ clearance). |
| **Pitch of Rivets in a Row** | $p$ | Distance between consecutive rivets measured along the load direction. |
| **Tearing Efficiency** | $\eta_t$ | Ratio of strength of plate between rivets to plate strength without holes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unwin's Formula (for determining pitch):** 
> $$p = C \cdot d + 41.3\,\text{mm}\quad\text{(in SI units)}$$
> where $C$ is a constant (≈ 1.31 for steel, 1.37 for wrought iron). KTU frequently asks the derivation of this empirical relation or its application in design.

## 2.2 Modes of Failure of a Riveted Joint

A riveted joint is designed such that it does **not** fail under any of the three classical modes. KTU problems routinely require the **strength calculation under each mode**, and the joint strength is the **minimum** of the three.

### Mode 1 — Shearing of Rivet

The rivet acts as a small pin in double/triple shear. The shear area is the cylindrical cross-section of the rivet.

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot n \cdot \tau$$

where:
- $d$ = nominal rivet diameter (mm)
- $n$ = number of shear planes (= 1 for lap joint, 2 for double-shear butt joint)
- $\tau$ = permissible shear stress of rivet material (MPa or N/mm²)

### Mode 2 — Crushing (Bearing) of Rivet or Plate

The cylindrical surface of the rivet shank bears against the hole wall. The projected bearing area governs.

$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot n_r \cdot \sigma_c$$

where:
- $t$ = thickness of the plate (mm)
- $n_r$ = number of rivets
- $\sigma_c$ = permissible crushing stress (N/mm²)

### Mode 3 — Tearing of Plate Between Rivets

This is the failure of the **plate itself** between consecutive rivet holes along the load line. It is governed by the **net section area**.

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

where:
- $(p - d)$ = net width resisting tearing
- $\sigma_t$ = permissible tensile stress of plate

> [!NOTE]
> **The Critical Design Principle:** For a properly designed joint, we must have $P_s = P_c = P_t$. Any imbalance wastes material. This is why **efficiency** is the supreme design metric.

## 2.3 Efficiency of a Riveted Joint

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Strength of riveted joint per pitch length}}{\text{Strength of un-riveted (solid) plate of same dimensions}} = \frac{\text{min}(P_s, P_c, P_t)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$$

For a **single-riveted lap joint** in a single shear, the typical efficiency is **60–70%**. For a **double-riveted double-strap butt joint**, it can reach **80–90%**.

## 2.4 WELDED JOINTS — Core Theory

### 2.4.1 Types of Welds

| Type | Geometry | Load Resisted |
|------|----------|---------------|
| **Fillet Weld** (Triangular cross-section) | Most common; leg size $s$ | Shear on the throat |
| **Butt Weld** (Square or V-groove) | Plates in same plane; full penetration | Direct tension or compression |
| **Spot/Seam Weld** | Localized fusion nugget | Shear on the nugget circumference |

### 2.4.2 The Throat Thickness Concept

For a fillet weld of **leg size $s$** (the two equal sides of the isosceles right-triangle cross-section), the **throat thickness** $t_t$ is the perpendicular distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse face:

$$t_t = s \cdot \cos(45^\circ) = 0.707 \cdot s$$

The fillet weld is assumed to fail by **shear along the throat plane**. The strength per unit length is:

$$P_{\text{weld}} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}$$

where $\tau_{\text{allow}}$ is the allowable shear stress of the weld metal (≈ 70–80 MPa for structural steel in KTU problems unless otherwise specified).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU numerical problems):**
> - $\tau_{\text{weld}} = 0.7 \cdot \sigma_t$ of the parent plate
> - Leg size $s$ should be **≤ minimum plate thickness** (and ≥ 3 mm for structural work)
> - For **transverse fillet welds** (welds oriented perpendicular to the load), the failure plane is rotated. The KTU-accepted simplification is to take the **same throat** $0.707s$ but allow a higher permissible stress because the failure is partly tensile.

### 2.4.3 Parallel vs. Transverse Fillet Welds

| Weld Type | Orientation | Failure Mode | Allowable Stress (KTU) |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Parallel Fillet Weld** | Weld axis along load direction | Pure shear on throat | $\tau = 0.7 \cdot \sigma_t$ |
| **Transverse Fillet Weld** | Weld axis perpendicular to load | Combined shear + normal stress | Effective $\tau = \sigma_t$ (used as a tensile equivalent) |

## 2.5 Eccentric Loading (KTU Hot Topic)

When the load line **does not pass through the centroid of the rivet/weld group**, the joint must resist both:
1. **Direct Shear** from the load
2. **Torsional Shear** from the moment $M = P \cdot e$ (where $e$ is eccentricity)

This is extensively tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

### 2.5.1 Eccentric Riveted Joint — Shear at Any Rivet

For a rivet located at radial distance $r$ from the centroid of the rivet group:

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_{n}} \quad \text{(direct)}$$

$$\tau_t = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r}{J} \quad \text{(torsional)}$$

$$J = \sum (r_i^2 \cdot A_i) = A_{n} \cdot \sum r_i^2$$

The **resultant shear stress** at the critical (farthest) rivet is:

$$\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2 + 2 \cdot \tau_d \cdot \tau_t \cdot \cos\theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the direct shear vector and the radial line to the rivet.

For a **symmetric group** where the critical rivet is aligned with the load, $\cos\theta = 1$, simplifying to:

$$\tau_{\max} = \tau_d + \tau_t$$

### 2.5.2 Eccentric Welded Joint

The same principle applies, but the "rivet area" is replaced by a **differential weld length element** $dL$ at radius $r$:

$$\tau_t = \frac{M \cdot r}{J_w}, \quad J_w = \int_L r^2 \cdot dL \approx 0.707 \cdot s \int_L r^2 \cdot dL$$

## 2.6 Complete KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $p = C \cdot d + 41.3$ (mm) | Pitch by Unwin's formula |
| 2 | $P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot n \cdot \tau$ | Shear strength of rivet |
| 3 | $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot n_r \cdot \sigma_c$ | Crushing strength |
| 4 | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Plate tearing strength |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{min}(P_s, P_c, P_t)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$ | Joint efficiency |
| 6 | $P_{\text{weld}} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot L$ | Fillet weld strength |
| 7 | $\tau_{\text{weld}} = 0.7 \cdot \sigma_t$ | Allowable weld shear |
| 8 | $\tau_{\max} = \tau_d + \tau_t$ (symmetric) | Eccentric load resultant |
| 9 | $J = A \sum r^2$ | Polar moment for rivet group |
| 10 | $J_w = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \sum r^2 \cdot L$ | Polar moment for weld group |
| 11 | $d = 6 \sqrt{t}$ (Unwin's empirical) | Initial rivet diameter guess |
| 12 | $e = \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot r}{J}$ | Moment-induced shear |
| 13 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_x}$ | Direct bending stress on weld (for welds in line) |
| 14 | $t_t = 0.707 \cdot s$ | Throat thickness |
| 15 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{(p - d) \cdot t}$ | Tensile stress in plate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in pressure vessel design (ASME Section VIII), steel structure design (IS 800, AISC), and ship classification rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register). A mechanical engineer designing any leak-proof pressure containment system is essentially applying these equations.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strength of a Single-Riveted Lap Joint

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider two steel plates of thickness $t$ connected by a single rivet of diameter $d$ under an axial tensile load $P$. The plate is of width $b$ and the rivet passes through a hole of diameter $d$.

**Shear Failure of the Rivet:**

The rivet is in **single shear** (one shear plane between the two plates). The shear plane area is the cross-sectional area of the rivet shank:

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

The shear strength of the joint is therefore:

$$P_s = A_s \cdot \tau = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$$

**Crushing (Bearing) Failure of the Rivet Hole:**

The projected bearing area on which the rivet presses against the plate is:

$$A_b = d \cdot t$$

The crushing (bearing) strength is:

$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$$

**Tearing of the Plate:**

The plate is weakened by the rivet hole. The **net section area** resisting tension is:

$$A_t = (b - d) \cdot t$$

The tearing strength of the plate is:

$$P_t = (b - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Algebraic Form

Starting from a **single-riveted lap joint** subjected to a tensile load $P$ with one row of rivets at pitch $p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:}\quad & p, t, d, \sigma_t, \tau, \sigma_c \text{ (all material/geometry data)} \\
\text{Goal:}\quad & \text{Find the safe load } P \text{ the joint can carry.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **strength of the joint** is governed by the **weakest mode**:

$$P_{\text{joint}} = \min(P_s, P_c, P_t)$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau \quad \text{(single rivet, single shear plane)} \\
P_c &= d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c \quad \text{(bearing)} \\
P_t &= (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \quad \text{(plate tearing per pitch length)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (Single-Riveted Lap Joint)

**Problem (KTU style):** A single-riveted lap joint connects two $10\,\text{mm}$ thick steel plates. Rivet diameter $d = 18\,\text{mm}$, pitch $p = 60\,\text{mm}$, margin $m = 25\,\text{mm}$. Permissible stresses: $\sigma_t = 120\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 90\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 160\,\text{MPa}$. Determine the strength of the joint per pitch length and the joint efficiency.

**Step 1: Shear Strength**

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (18)^2 \cdot 90
$$

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 324 \cdot 90 = 0.7854 \cdot 29160 = 22{,}902\,\text{N} \approx 22.9\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 2: Crushing Strength**

$$
P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 18 \cdot 10 \cdot 160 = 28{,}800\,\text{N} = 28.8\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 3: Tearing Strength of Plate**

$$
P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (60 - 18) \cdot 10 \cdot 120 = 42 \cdot 10 \cdot 120 = 50{,}400\,\text{N} = 50.4\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 4: Joint Strength (Minimum)**

$$
P_{\text{joint}} = \min(22.9, 28.8, 50.4) = 22.9\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 5: Strength of Solid Plate (no holes)**

$$
P_{\text{solid}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 60 \cdot 10 \cdot 120 = 72{,}000\,\text{N} = 72.0\,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 6: Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{22.9}{72.0} = 0.318 = 31.8\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice the joint is **shear-critical**. The plate tearing strength is more than double the shear strength—this means we are wasting plate material. To improve efficiency, the KTU-expectant answer is to **either increase rivets per pitch (chain-riveting) or switch to a butt joint** so that the rivet goes into double shear.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strength of a Fillet Weld Under Direct Shear

### 3.2.1 Setup

A fillet weld of uniform leg size $s$ joins two plates at right angles. The cross-section of the weld is an **isosceles right triangle** with the two equal sides = $s$. The failure plane is assumed to be along the **throat**, i.e., the plane from the weld root to the hypotenuse, inclined at $45^\circ$ to both plate surfaces.

### 3.2.2 Geometry of the Throat Plane

The throat thickness $t_t$ is the altitude of the right triangle from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse:

For a right isosceles triangle with legs $s$ and $s$:

$$
t_t = s \cdot \sin(45^\circ) = s \cdot \cos(45^\circ) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Since $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071$:

$$
t_t = 0.7071 \cdot s \approx 0.707\,s
$$

### 3.2.3 Strength of the Weld

For a weld of length $L$ (assuming uniform throat):

$$
A_{\text{throat}} = t_t \cdot L = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L
$$

The shear force resisted:

$$
P = A_{\text{throat}} \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Example — Fillet Weld Under Direct Shear

**Problem:** Two plates of $12\,\text{mm}$ thickness are joined by a parallel fillet weld of leg size $s = 10\,\text{mm}$ and length $L = 200\,\text{mm}$. Permissible shear stress in the weld $= 80\,\text{MPa}$. Find the load the weld can carry.

**Step 1: Throat thickness**

$$
t_t = 0.707 \cdot s = 0.707 \cdot 10 = 7.07\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Throat area**

$$
A = t_t \cdot L = 7.07 \cdot 200 = 1414\,\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Load capacity**

$$
P = A \cdot \tau = 1414 \cdot 80 = 113{,}120\,\text{N} \approx 113.1\,\text{kN}
$$

**Alternative using the direct formula:**

$$
P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau = 0.707 \cdot 10 \cdot 200 \cdot 80 = 113{,}120\,\text{N}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Eccentric Load on a Welded Bracket

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A bracket is welded to a column using **three vertical fillet welds** of length $L$ each, arranged symmetrically about the vertical axis. A vertical load $P$ is applied at a horizontal distance $e$ from the weld centroid (i.e., the column face). Determine the size $s$ of the weld.

### 3.3.2 Forces on Each Weld Element

Every infinitesimal element $dL$ of the weld at radius $r$ from the centroid experiences:

**(a) Direct Shear (Primary):** uniform across the weld group

$$
\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_{\text{total}}} = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L_{\text{total}}}
$$

**(b) Torsional Shear (Secondary):** due to the moment $M = P \cdot e$

The element at radius $r$ experiences a tangential shear:

$$
dF_t = \frac{M \cdot r}{J_w} \cdot dA = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r}{J_w} \cdot 0.707 \cdot s \cdot dL
$$

The shear stress on this element is:

$$
\tau_t = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r}{J_w}
$$

where $J_w$ is the **polar second moment of the weld group** about the centroid:

$$
J_w = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \sum_i r_i^2 \cdot L_i
$$

For a continuous weld at radius $r$:

$$
J_w = 0.707 \cdot s \int_L r^2\,dL
$$

### 3.3.3 Critical Weld Point

The maximum combined stress occurs at the weld point **farthest from the centroid** (largest $r$) and where the direct and torsional shears are **codirectional**.

For a symmetric bracket, this is typically at the top or bottom of the weld group, on the side away from the load.

### 3.3.4 Worked Example — Eccentric Fillet Weld

**Problem:** A bracket carries a vertical load of $P = 50\,\text{kN}$ at an eccentricity $e = 200\,\text{mm}$ from the centroid of a weld group. The weld group consists of two vertical fillet welds, each of length $L = 150\,\text{mm}$, placed $100\,\text{mm}$ apart (symmetric about the load axis). Allowable shear stress in the weld $= 70\,\text{MPa}$. Find the required leg size $s$.

**Step 1: Total weld length**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot 150 = 300\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Total throat area**

$$
A_{\text{throat}} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot 300 = 212.1 \cdot s\,\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Direct shear stress**

$$
\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_{\text{throat}}} = \frac{50{,}000}{212.1 \cdot s} = \frac{235.7}{s}\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Polar moment of the weld group**

For two welds each at radius $r = 50\,\text{mm}$ from the centroid:

$$
J_w = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \left[ 2 \cdot (50)^2 \cdot 150 \right] = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot 750{,}000 = 530{,}250 \cdot s\,\text{mm}^3
$$

**Step 5: Moment and Torsional shear**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 50{,}000 \cdot 200 = 10^7\,\text{N}\cdot\text{mm}
$$

$$
\tau_t = \frac{M \cdot r}{J_w} = \frac{10^7 \cdot 50}{530{,}250 \cdot s} = \frac{942.8}{s}\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 6: Combined shear (at critical point, $\cos\theta = 1$)**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \tau_d + \tau_t = \frac{235.7}{s} + \frac{942.8}{s} = \frac{1178.5}{s}\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 7: Apply allowable stress**

$$
\tau_{\max} \leq 70\,\text{MPa} \implies \frac{1178.5}{s} = 70 \implies s = \frac{1178.5}{70} \approx 16.84\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 8: Round up to standard size**

Standard weld leg sizes are in steps of 1 mm or 2 mm (3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20 mm). So we take $s = 18\,\text{mm}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often forget to multiply by 0.707 in $J_w$ computation. The polar moment for a weld group is **NOT** simply $\sum r^2 \cdot L$; it is $0.707 \cdot s \cdot \sum r^2 \cdot L$. Missing the $0.707$ factor gives a non-conservative (larger) allowable load — a critical safety error in pressure vessel design.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Unwin's Formula Origin

Unwin's formula is empirical but can be rationalized as follows:

For a single-riveted lap joint, equate the **shear strength of the rivet** to the **tearing strength of the plate**:

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

Solving for $p$:

$$
p = d + \frac{\pi d^2 \tau}{4 t \sigma_t}
$$

Now, applying Unwin's empirical relation $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ (where $d, t$ in mm), substitute:

$$
p = d + \frac{\pi d^2 \tau}{4 \cdot (d/6)^2 \cdot \sigma_t} = d + \frac{\pi d^2 \tau \cdot 36}{4 d^2 \sigma_t} = d + \frac{9\pi \tau}{\sigma_t}
$$

For mild steel, $\tau / \sigma_t \approx 0.6$, so:

$$
p \approx d + 9\pi \cdot 0.6 \cdot d \cdot \text{scale} + 41.3 = C \cdot d + 41.3\,\text{mm}
$$

The constant **41.3 mm** accounts for practical considerations (corrosion allowance, gauge distance, etc.) and is essentially empirical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Unwin's formula gives a **conservative pitch**. In modern codes (ASME, IS 800), the pitch is computed from first principles using the actual stresses, but Unwin's formula remains useful for **preliminary design** in KTU exams due to its simplicity.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Riveted vs. Welded

| Parameter | Riveted Joint | Welded Joint |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Weight** | Heavy (rivet + plates) | Lighter (no extra material) |
| **Strength/Weight Ratio** | Lower | Higher (≈ 1.5–2×) |
| **Initial Cost** | High (drilling, riveting labour) | Moderate (welding equipment) |
| **Skill Required** | Moderate (riveting) | High (certified welder) |
| **Inspection** | Easy (visual, tap test) | Difficult (NDT required: X-ray, UT) |
| **Fatigue Strength** | Good (rivets don't fail easily) | Good (but weld defects reduce life) |
| **Leak Tightness** | Excellent (proven for boilers) | Excellent (no through-holes) |
| **Repairability** | Difficult (drill out rivet) | Moderate (cut and re-weld) |
| **Best for** | Sheet metal, aerospace, vibration | Thick plates, structural, tanks |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | 60% of Module 2 questions | 40% of Module 2 questions |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Riveted Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Load P applied to plates] --> B[Force distribution at joint interface]
    B --> C{Force Transmission Path}
    C -->|Path 1| D[Plate 1 → Rivet Shank]
    C -->|Path 2| E[Plate 2 → Rivet Shank]
    D --> F[Stress State in Rivet]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Check Failure Mode}
    G -->|Mode A| H[Shear Failure of Rivet: tau = 4P over pi d^2]
    G -->|Mode B| I[Crushing Failure: sigma_c = P over d t]
    G -->|Mode C| J[Tearing of Plate: sigma_t = P over p-d t]
    H --> K[Compute Ps, Pc, Pt]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Pjoint = min of Ps, Pc, Pt}
    L --> M[Compute Efficiency eta = Pjoint over Psolid]
    M --> N[Output: Design Verdict]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Welded Joint Under Eccentric Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Eccentric Load P at distance e] --> B[Resolve into Direct Force and Moment M = Pe]
    B --> C[Direct Shear Path]
    B --> D[Torsional Shear Path]
    C --> E[Compute tau_d = P over Athroat]
    D --> F[Compute tau_t = M r over Jw]
    E --> G[Locate Critical Weld Point at maximum r]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Vector Angle theta between tau_d and tau_t}
    H -->|Parallel| I[tau_max = tau_d + tau_t]
    H -->|Perpendicular| J[tau_max = sqrt tau_d^2 + tau_t^2]
    H -->|General| K[tau_max = sqrt tau_d^2 + tau_t^2 + 2 tau_d tau_t cos theta]
    I --> L[Apply tau_max less than tau_allowable]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Output: Required Leg Size s]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: Define the term "efficiency of a riveted joint" and list the three classical modes of failure.**

**Model Answer:**

The **efficiency of a riveted joint** is defined as the ratio of the strength of the riveted joint (per pitch length) to the strength of an un-riveted (solid) plate of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\,\%$$

The **three classical modes of failure** of a riveted joint are:

1. **Shearing of the rivet** — Rivet shank fails in single/double shear along a plane between the connected plates.
2. **Crushing (Bearing) of the rivet or plate** — The rivet shank bears against the hole wall and crushes the contact surface.
3. **Tearing of the plate** — The plate fails in tension at the net section (between consecutive rivet holes).

The **joint strength** is the minimum of the three failure loads.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: What is a fillet weld? Derive the expression for its throat thickness.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces meeting at an angle (typically $90°$). The two legs of the triangle lie along the surfaces of the plates being joined, and the hypotenuse is the exposed weld face.

**Derivation of throat thickness:**

Let the leg size of the fillet weld be $s$. The cross-section is an isosceles right triangle with both legs equal to $s$. The throat is the shortest distance from the root (right-angle vertex) to the hypotenuse.

By the geometry of a right isosceles triangle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hypotenuse}^2 &= s^2 + s^2 = 2s^2 \\
\text{Hypotenuse} &= s\sqrt{2} \\
\text{Altitude from right angle} &= \frac{\text{leg}_1 \cdot \text{leg}_2}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{s \cdot s}{s\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071$:

$$\boxed{t_t = 0.707\,s}$$

The throat plane is inclined at $45°$ to both plate surfaces and is the assumed failure plane for shear.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) A single-riveted lap joint connects two $12\,\text{mm}$ thick mild steel plates. The rivet diameter is $d = 22\,\text{mm}$, pitch $p = 70\,\text{mm}$, and the permissible stresses are: $\sigma_t = 120\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 80\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 160\,\text{MPa}$. Determine: (i) the shear strength of the joint, (ii) the crushing strength, (iii) the tearing strength of the plate, and (iv) the efficiency of the joint. [7 Marks]**

**(b) With the help of a clear sketch, explain the various types of riveted joints. Compare the strength and efficiency of a single-riveted lap joint with a double-riveted double-strap butt joint. [7 Marks]**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (a) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Plate thickness $t = 12\,\text{mm}$
- Rivet diameter $d = 22\,\text{mm}$
- Pitch $p = 70\,\text{mm}$
- $\sigma_t = 120\,\text{MPa}$, $\tau = 80\,\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_c = 160\,\text{MPa}$

**(i) Shear Strength of the Joint:**

For a single-riveted lap joint, the rivet is in **single shear** ($n = 1$):

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (22)^2 \cdot 80$$

$$
P_s = 0.7854 \cdot 484 \cdot 80 = 30{,}408.3\,\text{N} \approx 30.41\,\text{kN}
$$

**(ii) Crushing Strength:**

$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 22 \cdot 12 \cdot 160 = 42{,}240\,\text{N} = 42.24\,\text{kN}$$

**(iii) Tearing Strength of the Plate:**

$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (70 - 22) \cdot 12 \cdot 120 = 48 \cdot 12 \cdot 120 = 69{,}120\,\text{N} = 69.12\,\text{kN}$$

**(iv) Efficiency of the Joint:**

The joint is weakest in shear: $P_{\text{joint}} = 30.41\,\text{kN}$.

Strength of the solid plate per pitch length:

$$P_{\text{solid}} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 70 \cdot 12 \cdot 120 = 100{,}800\,\text{N} = 100.8\,\text{kN}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid}}} = \frac{30.41}{100.8} = 0.3017 = 30.17\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A(a):** Many students forget to convert units consistently. Plate thickness and pitch are in **mm**, stress in **N/mm²** (MPa) — when multiplied, you get force in **Newtons (N)**, not kN. Always do a unit check before finalizing the answer. Also, do not confuse single shear with double shear in a lap joint — it is **single** shear.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (b) — Theory: Types of Riveted Joints

**Classification of Riveted Joints:**

**A. Lap Joints** — Plates overlap each other.

- *Single-riveted lap joint*: One row of rivets.
- *Double-riveted lap joint*: Two rows of rivets (chain or zigzag).

**B. Butt Joints** — Plates meet edge-to-edge; cover straps are used.

- *Single-strap butt joint*
- *Double-strap butt joint* (stronger, symmetric)

Within each row arrangement, the rivets may be **chain-riveted** (rows are in line with load) or **zigzag-riveted** (rows are staggered, providing better resistance to tearing).

**Comparison Table (Lap vs. Butt Joint):**

| Parameter | Single-Riveted Lap Joint | Double-Riveted Double-Strap Butt Joint |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Number of rivets per pitch | 1 | 4 (typically) |
| Shear planes per rivet | 1 | 2 |
| Shear strength | Lowest | Highest (4× per pitch area) |
| Plate tearing (net section) | $(p - d)t\sigma_t$ | $(p - 2d)t\sigma_t$ |
| Efficiency | 60–70% | 80–90% |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (requires straps) |
| Application | Sheet metal, light frames | Pressure vessels, boilers |

**Conclusion:** Butt joints are mechanically superior because they put the rivets in **double shear** and reduce eccentricity of the load path, thereby increasing efficiency. However, they are more expensive to fabricate.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) A bracket welded to a vertical column carries a vertical load of $P = 60\,\text{kN}$ at an eccentricity of $e = 250\,\text{mm}$ from the centroid of the weld group. The weld group consists of two vertical fillet welds, each of length $180\,\text{mm}$, placed $120\,\text{mm}$ apart on either side of the load axis. The allowable shear stress in the weld is $70\,\text{MPa}$. Find the required leg size of the weld. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Explain with neat sketches the difference between a parallel fillet weld and a transverse fillet weld. How is the strength of each calculated? [7 Marks]**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (a) — Eccentric Welded Joint

**Given:**
- $P = 60\,\text{kN} = 60{,}000\,\text{N}$
- $e = 250\,\text{mm}$
- Two welds, each $L = 180\,\text{mm}$, spacing $= 120\,\text{mm}$ (so each weld is at $r = 60\,\text{mm}$ from the centroid)
- $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 70\,\text{MPa}$

**Step 1: Total Weld Length**

$$L_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 180 = 360\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Direct Shear Stress**

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L_{\text{total}}} = \frac{60{,}000}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 360} = \frac{60{,}000}{254.52 \cdot s} = \frac{235.7}{s}\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Polar Moment of Weld Group**

$$J_w = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \left[ 2 \times (60)^2 \times 180 \right]$$

$$J_w = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot [2 \times 3600 \times 180] = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot 1{,}296{,}000$$

$$J_w = 916{,}272 \cdot s\,\text{mm}^3$$

**Step 4: Torsional Shear Stress at the Critical Point**

Moment $M = P \cdot e = 60{,}000 \times 250 = 1.5 \times 10^7\,\text{N}\cdot\text{mm}$

Critical radius $r = 60\,\text{mm}$ (farthest weld edge from centroid)

$$\tau_t = \frac{M \cdot r}{J_w} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7 \times 60}{916{,}272 \cdot s} = \frac{9 \times 10^8}{916{,}272 \cdot s} = \frac{982.2}{s}\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5: Maximum Combined Shear (Critical Point)**

At the critical point, direct and torsional shears are parallel, so:

$$\tau_{\max} = \tau_d + \tau_t = \frac{235.7}{s} + \frac{982.2}{s} = \frac{1217.9}{s}\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 6: Apply the Strength Condition**

$$\tau_{\max} \leq \tau_{\text{allow}}$$

$$\frac{1217.9}{s} \leq 70$$

$$s \geq \frac{1217.9}{70} = 17.40\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 7: Select Standard Leg Size**

Adopt the next higher standard size: $\boxed{s = 18\,\text{mm}}$.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (b) — Theory: Parallel vs. Transverse Fillet Welds

**Parallel Fillet Weld:**
- The weld axis is **parallel** to the direction of the applied load.
- The failure plane (throat) is subjected to **pure shear** because the load is parallel to the weld.
- Permissible shear stress $\tau = 0.7 \cdot \sigma_t$ of the parent plate.
- Strength per unit length: $P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \tau$.

**Transverse Fillet Weld:**
- The weld axis is **perpendicular** to the direction of the applied load.
