Use $b = 1.5w$ to $2w$ — not equal to $w$. > 2. **Taper sign convention:** Always specify whether taper is on **width** or **height**; KTU standard is on the **top surface (height)** at 1 in 100. > 3. **Unit consistency:** $T = 5 \text{ kN·m} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$ — converting this incorrectly is the **#1 reason** students lose 2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Keys Module** **Core Concepts:** - Keys transmit torque from shaft to hub by resisting **shear** and **crushing** simultaneously. - **Sunk keys** = positive drive (always preferred). **Saddle keys** = friction drive (avoid for precision). - **Woodruff keys** use reduced diameter $D - w$ for force calculation. **Critical Formulas (Memorize):** - $P = \dfrac{2T}{D}$ - $l_s = \dfrac{2T}{D \cdot w \cdot \tau}$ - $l_c = \dfrac{4T}{D \cdot h \cdot \sigma_c}$ **Standard Proportions:** - Square Key: $w = h = D/4$ - Flat Key: $w = D/4$, $h = D/6$ - Taper: 1 in 100 on top surface - Woodruff: Standard numbered sizes (e.g., 6×16, 8×19, 10×22.5) **Material Allowables (Medium Carbon Steel):** - $\tau = 56$ MPa - $\sigma_c = 112$ MPa (ratio 1:2) **Design Procedure:** 1. Compute $T$ from power and speed. 2. Select key type. 3. Compute $w$ and $h$ from $D$. 4. Calculate $l_s$ and $l_c$ — take the **maximum**. 5. Round up to next standard length. 6. Add gib-head allowance if taper key is used. **Common Pitfalls (KTU Exam):** - Forgetting unit conversion ($T$ in N·mm, not N·m). - Taking the **smaller** length instead of the larger. - Using $w = D/6$ instead of $h = D/6$ for flat keys. - Ignoring gib-head shear check for taper keys. - Failing to specify **standard** lengths. **Real-World Extension:** For high-cycle fatigue applications, keyways act as **stress raisers**. Always apply a **stress concentration factor** $K_t \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ to the shaft's bending/torsion fatigue analysis when keys are present. KTU Advanced Machine Design courses (Module 5) build upon this directly.

topics.title: Spline and Bolted Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spline and Bolted Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SPLINES

### Formal Definition
A **spline** is a permanent, integral, multi-tooth mechanical joint used to transmit torque between two rotating machine components, such as a shaft and a gear, pulley, or coupling hub. Unlike a discrete key, the splines consist of a series of longitudinal ridges (teeth) machined directly along the shaft circumference, which seat into matching grooves machined inside the bore of the mating hub.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement:** Splines are considered an extension of the shaft-key system. The teeth, key, and shaft are made from a single piece of metal, making splines a **monolithic** torque-transmission member.

### Types of Splines (KTU Focus)
1. **Straight-Side (Parallel-Side) Splines** – Most common. Per Indian Standard **IS 2048**, the proportions are standardized for:
   - **Light Duty (L-Series):** $D = 1.25d + 4 \text{ mm}$
   - **Medium Duty (M-Series):** $D = 1.4d + 8 \text{ mm}$
   - **Heavy Duty (H-Series):** $D = 1.75d + 12 \text{ mm}$
   where $d$ = minor diameter, $D$ = major (outer) diameter.
2. **Involute Splines** – Teeth have involute profile (similar to gear teeth). Used for larger torques and self-centering action.
3. **Saw-Tooth Splines** – Asymmetric, used for unidirectional high torque.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you have a wooden log (the shaft) and a wooden wheel (the gear/hub) that needs to spin with the log. If you glue one rectangular wooden stick between them (this is a **key**), the stick can take only a small load and may slide out.
> 
> Now, instead of one stick, you machine **5 or 6 ridges** directly into the log itself and cut matching slots into the wheel. Push the wheel onto the log. Now, *all 6 ridges* share the rotational load together, the joint is automatically aligned on the centreline, and the wheel can slide **axially** (think of a gearbox shift). This multi-ridge, integral arrangement is a **spline**.

### Advantages over Keys
- **Load Distribution:** Load is shared across multiple teeth → lower bearing pressure.
- **Self-Centering:** The hub is automatically aligned to the shaft axis → concentricity maintained.
- **Higher Torque Capacity:** Can transmit 2–3 times more torque than a single key of the same shaft size.
- **Sliding Capability:** Hub can slide axially (used in gearboxes for shifting gears).
- **Reduced Stress Concentration:** No sharp corners like in keyways.

---

## 1.2 BOLTED JOINTS

### Formal Definition
A **bolted joint** is a detachable fastening arrangement in which a threaded fastener (bolt, screw, or stud) is used to clamp two or more members together, often with the help of a nut and a washer. In machine design, bolted joints are analysed for their ability to resist **axial tensile loads**, **shear loads**, and to develop the required **clamping (pre-load) force** without failing.

### Types of Bolted Joints
| Type | Description | KTU Use Case |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| **Through Bolt (Bolt + Nut)** | Bolt passes through clearance holes in both members; nut tightened on the other side. | Most common in structural and machine frames. |
| **Stud (Tap Bolt)** | A stud screwed permanently into one member; a nut is tightened on the other end. | Cylinder head of IC engines. |
| **Cap Screw** | Screwed into a tapped (threaded) hole of one member. | Machine tool bodies, gear boxes. |
| **Machine Screw** | Smaller screws for thin sheet-metal parts. | Covers, nameplates, instrument panels. |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Hold two wooden planks together. If you drill a hole through them and push a nail in, the nail holds them — but the joint is weak and the nail may pull out.
> 
> Now use a **bolt** with threads. As you tighten the nut, the bolt stretches slightly like a **stretched spring**, and this stretching pulls the two planks together with a very large compressive force. This clamping force is what resists external pulling loads. Even before any external load is applied, the bolt is already stretched. This pre-stretching is called **pre-load (initial tightening force)**, and it is the secret of why bolted joints are so strong.
> 
> You can imagine the bolt as a **stretched rubber band** holding the planks together. The more you stretch it (pre-tension), the harder it is to pull apart.

### KTU 2024 Syllabus Coverage
The Module 2 focus on bolted joints includes:
- **Metric thread geometry** (ISO metric coarse and fine threads)
- **Stresses in the bolt** (tensile, shear, combined)
- **Initial tightening & pre-load ($F_i$)**
- **Effective length and stiffness approach** (bolt stiffness $K_b$ and joint stiffness $K_m$)
- **Equivalent stress using Von-Mises / Maximum Shear Stress Theory**
- **Eccentrically loaded bolted joints in shear**

### Important Physical / Standard Metrics
- **Standard metric threads:** M6, M8, M10, M12, M16, M20, M24, M30, M36 (per **ISO 261 / IS 1366**).
- **Bolt grade markings:** e.g., **4.6, 8.8, 9.8, 10.9, 12.9** per **ISO 898-1**. The first number × 100 = ultimate tensile strength (MPa); the product of the two = 0.1 × yield strength (MPa).
- **Proof load ($F_p$):** The maximum load a bolt can sustain without permanent set. **$F_p = S_p \times A_s$**, where $S_p$ = proof stress and $A_s$ = stress area of the thread.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SPLINE — DEEP ANALYSIS

### 2.1.1 Standard Proportions (IS 2048)
Let:
- $d$ = Minor (root) diameter of spline (≈ nominal shaft diameter)
- $D$ = Major (outer) diameter of spline
- $L$ = Total length of spline
- $n$ = Number of teeth
- $w$ = Width of each tooth
- $h$ = Depth of tooth = $\dfrac{D - d}{2}$

### 2.1.2 Design of Straight-Side Splines
When torque $T$ is transmitted, two failure modes are considered:

**(a) Shear Failure of Spline Teeth**

The shear area of one tooth is $A_s = L \times w$.
Total shear area for $n$ teeth: $A_{s,\text{total}} = n \times L \times w$.

$$T = n \cdot w \cdot L \cdot \tau \cdot r_m$$

where $r_m$ = mean radius = $\dfrac{d + D}{4}$, and $\tau$ = allowable shear stress for the spline material (typically 40–80 MPa).

**(b) Crushing (Compressive) Failure of Spline Teeth**

The projected bearing area of one tooth = $h \times L$.
Total bearing area: $A_{b,\text{total}} = n \times h \times L$.

$$T = n \cdot h \cdot L \cdot \sigma_c \cdot r_m$$

where $\sigma_c$ = allowable bearing (compressive) pressure (typically 50–100 MPa).

### 2.1.3 Material & Allowable Stress
| Material | $\sigma_c$ (MPa) | $\tau$ (MPa) |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| Mild Steel (Hub) | 50–60 | 40–50 |
| Medium Carbon Steel | 70–90 | 55–70 |
| Alloy Steel (Hardened) | 100–140 | 80–110 |

### 2.1.4 Why Use Splines? Engineering Utility
Splines are used in:
- **Automotive gearboxes** (sliding mesh gears).
- **Machine tool spindles** for transmitting high torque.
- **Propeller shafts** of aircraft and heavy vehicles.
- **Hydraulic pump drives.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Splines are preferred over keys when (a) torque is high, (b) axial sliding is required, (c) concentricity must be maintained, or (d) the shaft is small and a single keyway would weaken it too much.

---

## 2.2 BOLTED JOINT — DEEP ANALYSIS

### 2.2.1 Metric Thread Geometry (KTU 2024)
For an ISO metric thread of nominal diameter $d$:
- **Pitch ($P$):** Distance between adjacent thread crests (mm). For **coarse series**: $P = 0.6\sqrt{d}$ (approx).
- **Core (root) diameter:** $d_c = d - 1.2269P$
- **Stress area ($A_s$):** $A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(\dfrac{d_2 + d_3}{2}\right)^2$, where $d_2$ = pitch diameter, $d_3$ = minor diameter. A standard table gives values, e.g., **M10 → $A_s = 58.0 \text{ mm}^2$**, **M12 → $A_s = 84.3 \text{ mm}^2$**, **M16 → $A_s = 157 \text{ mm}^2$**.
- **Lead angle ($\alpha$):** $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{P}{\pi d_2}$

### 2.2.2 Stresses in a Bolted Joint
A bolt under external axial load $P$ and pre-load $F_i$ experiences:

| Stress | Formula | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| **Direct tensile stress** | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A_s}$ | $F$ = total bolt load |
| **Torsional shear stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{T_{\text{tight}}}{W_t}$ | $T_{\text{tight}} = F_i \cdot \dfrac{d_2}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ |
| **Maximum principal stress** | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_t}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$ | |
| **Von-Mises equivalent stress** | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | Most widely used in KTU. |

### 2.2.3 The Stiffness Model
The bolt (or the clamped members) behaves like a **spring**. Let:
- $K_b$ = stiffness of the bolt (N/mm)
- $K_m$ = stiffness of the members (N/mm)
- $C = \dfrac{K_b}{K_b + K_m}$ = **joint stiffness constant** (typically 0.2–0.3 for steel-on-steel)

When an external load $P$ is applied:
- **Increment in bolt tension:** $\Delta F_b = P \cdot C$
- **Reduction in clamping force on members:** $\Delta F_m = P \cdot (1 - C)$
- **Total bolt load:** $F_b = F_i + P \cdot C$
- **Residual clamping load:** $F_m = F_i - P(1 - C)$

### 2.2.4 Bolt Stiffness
$$K_b = \dfrac{A_s \cdot E_b}{L_e}$$

where $L_e$ = effective length of the bolt (sum of grip length + half the head + half the nut), $E_b$ = Young's modulus of bolt material.

### 2.2.5 Member Stiffness (Frömmel-Andrews / Shigley's Cone Model)
A frustum-shaped cone of half-angle $\theta \approx 30°$ (varies $25°$–$45°$) forms under the bolt head/nut.

$$K_m = \dfrac{0.5774 \pi E_m d}{2 \ln\left[\dfrac{5.346 L}{d}\right]}$$

For an approximate KTU-level approach, a simpler uniform-frustum formula may be used.

### 2.2.6 Pre-load and Factor of Safety
- **Pre-load $F_i$** is set such that the bolt does not exceed proof load: $F_i \leq 0.75 \times S_p \times A_s$ for permanent joints, $0.90 \times S_p \times A_s$ for non-permanent.
- **Factor of safety against proof load:** $n = \dfrac{S_p \cdot A_s}{F_b}$
- **Factor of safety against yielding:** $n_y = \dfrac{S_y \cdot A_s}{F_b}$

### 2.2.7 Eccentrically Loaded Bolted Joints
When a load $P$ acts at an eccentricity $e$ from the bolt group centroid, each bolt experiences:
- **Direct shear:** $F_{s1} = \dfrac{P}{n}$
- **Torsional shear:** $F_{s2} = \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum r_i^2}$ for the most-loaded bolt
- **Resultant shear:** $F_s = \sqrt{F_{s1}^2 + F_{s2}^2 + 2F_{s1}F_{s2}\cos\theta}$

The bolt is then checked in shear using: $\tau = \dfrac{F_s}{A_s}$ (using the bolt shank area, since shear occurs on the unthreaded shank).

---

## KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### Splines
| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Mean radius | $r_m = \dfrac{d + D}{4}$ | Use in torque equation |
| Torque (crushing) | $T = n \cdot h \cdot L \cdot \sigma_c \cdot r_m$ | Bearing check |
| Torque (shear) | $T = n \cdot w \cdot L \cdot \tau \cdot r_m$ | Tooth shear check |
| Light-duty (L) | $D = 1.25d + 4$ | IS 2048 |
| Medium-duty (M) | $D = 1.4d + 8$ | IS 2048 |
| Heavy-duty (H) | $D = 1.75d + 12$ | IS 2048 |
| Tooth depth | $h = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ | |

### Bolted Joints
| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Stress area (use tables) | $A_s$ from IS/ISO tables | M10 → 58 mm²; M12 → 84.3 mm²; M16 → 157 mm²; M20 → 245 mm² |
| Proof load | $F_p = S_p \times A_s$ | |
| Pre-load limit | $F_i \leq 0.75 \, S_p A_s$ | Permanent joint |
| Torsional moment on tightening | $T_t = F_i \dfrac{d_2}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | |
| Von-Mises equivalent | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | |
| Bolt load | $F_b = F_i + P \cdot C$ | |
| Residual clamping | $F_c = F_i - P(1 - C)$ | |
| Joint constant | $C = \dfrac{K_b}{K_b + K_m}$ | |
| Stiffness of bolt | $K_b = \dfrac{A_s E_b}{L_e}$ | |
| Factor of safety | $n = \dfrac{S_p A_s}{F_b}$ | |
| Eccentric shear (most-loaded bolt) | $F_s = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\theta}$ | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the Von-Mises formula, students often forget the factor of **3** multiplying $\tau^2$. This single error costs 2 marks routinely.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION: Torque Capacity of a Spline Joint

### Setup
Consider a shaft of minor (root) diameter $d$, carrying $n$ straight-side splines of major diameter $D$, tooth width $w$, and engaged length $L$. Let $\sigma_c$ be the allowable bearing (crushing) stress and $\tau$ the allowable shear stress on the tooth root.

### Step 1 — Geometry
The depth of one tooth:
$$h = \frac{D - d}{2}$$
The mean radius (centroid of bearing surface):
$$r_m = \frac{d + D}{4} = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{h}{2}$$
The area resisting crushing per tooth:
$$A_c = h \times L$$
The area resisting shear per tooth:
$$A_s = w \times L$$

### Step 2 — Force on Each Tooth
Assume torque $T$ is shared equally by all $n$ teeth. The tangential force on one tooth at the mean radius:
$$F = \frac{T}{n \cdot r_m}$$

### Step 3 — Crushing Stress Check
$$\sigma_c = \frac{F}{A_c} = \frac{T}{n \cdot r_m \cdot h \cdot L}$$
Rearranging for torque:
$$T_c = n \cdot h \cdot L \cdot \sigma_c \cdot r_m \quad \text{(Crushing-limited torque)}$$

### Step 4 — Shear Stress Check
$$\tau = \frac{F}{A_s} = \frac{T}{n \cdot r_m \cdot w \cdot L}$$
Rearranging for torque:
$$T_s = n \cdot w \cdot L \cdot \tau \cdot r_m \quad \text{(Shear-limited torque)}$$

### Step 5 — Selection
The design torque must satisfy:
$$T_{\text{design}} \leq \min(T_c, T_s)$$

The actual **allowable torque** is the smaller of the two.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU problems, you will usually be given $d$, $D$, $L$, $n$, $w$, $\sigma_c$, and $\tau$, and asked to find the **safe torque** the spline can transmit.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION: Tightening Torque and Equivalent Stress in a Bolt

### Setup
A metric bolt of nominal diameter $d$ is tightened against a member. The thread pitch diameter is $d_2$ and the lead angle is $\alpha$. The friction angle in the threads is $\phi$ ($\tan \phi = \mu_t$). The bolt develops a pre-load $F_i$.

### Step 1 — Torque Required to Raise the Load (P)
The axial component of force on the thread helix is $F_i$. The total torque to overcome thread friction is:
$$T_{t1} = F_i \cdot \frac{d_2}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

### Step 2 — Torque to Overcome Friction at the Bearing Surface (NUT Face)
If $d_w$ is the mean bearing diameter (≈$1.5d$ for hex nut) and $\mu_b$ the bearing friction:
$$T_{t2} = F_i \cdot \mu_b \cdot \frac{d_w}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Total Tightening Torque
$$T_t = T_{t1} + T_{t2}$$
For KTU 2024 problems, often only $T_{t1}$ is considered and the bearing friction is taken as a fixed coefficient (≈$0.2 d$).

### Step 4 — Stresses in the Bolt
**Direct tensile stress:**
$$\sigma_t = \frac{F_i}{A_s}$$

**Torsional shear stress in the bolt shank:**
$$\tau = \frac{T_{t1}}{W_t} = \frac{F_i \cdot \frac{d_2}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)}{\frac{\pi d^3}{16}} = \frac{8 F_i d_2 \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{\pi d^3}$$

**Von-Mises Equivalent Stress:**
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^2 + 3\tau^2}$$

### Step 5 — Why Use 1.3 Times Proof Load? (KTU Frequent Question)
Setting $\sigma_{VM} = S_p / 1.3$ (i.e., $\sigma_{VM} \leq S_p / 1.3$ for proof load safety) gives the **maximum permissible pre-load**. Empirically, taking:
$$F_i = 0.75 \times S_p \times A_s$$
provides a **safety factor of 1.3** against proof load when torsion during tightening is included.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Significance:** The $1/1.3$ factor arises because tightening adds torsion (which makes the bolt act as if it is carrying 30% more tensile load via Von-Mises). This is why the pre-load is limited to 75% of proof load.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION: Eccentrically Loaded Bolt Group — Most-Loaded Bolt

### Setup
A bracket is bolted to a support by $n$ identical bolts. The bracket carries a load $P$ in the plane of the bolt cross-section, at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroid $G$ of the bolt pattern. The bolts lie on a circle of radius $r$ (or general positions $r_i$ from $G$).

### Step 1 — Direct Shear Component
Each bolt shares the direct load equally:
$$F_1 = \frac{P}{n}$$
This force acts on every bolt in the direction of $P$.

### Step 2 — Torsional Shear Component
The eccentricity produces a twisting moment $M = P \cdot e$. This moment is resisted by tangential forces in the bolts:
$$F_{2,\text{max}} = \frac{M \cdot r_{\max}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i^2}$$
The direction of $F_2$ on each bolt is perpendicular to the radius vector from $G$ to the bolt.

### Step 3 — Resultant Force on Most-Loaded Bolt
Let $\theta$ be the angle between $F_1$ and $F_2$ for the critical bolt. Then:
$$F_{\text{res}} = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta}$$

### Step 4 — Shear Stress
Using the bolt shank area (clearance hole side):
$$\tau = \frac{F_{\text{res}}}{A_b} = \frac{F_{\text{res}}}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2}$$

### Step 5 — Shear Safety
$$n_s = \frac{S_{sy}}{\tau} = \frac{0.5 S_{ut}}{\tau}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In uniform bolt circles, $\sum r_i^2 = n r^2$, simplifying $F_{2,\text{max}} = P e / r$. For $n$ bolts on a circle of radius $r$, the critical bolt is the one for which $\cos\theta = 1$, i.e., the bolt lying on the load line, *on the side away from the load*.

---

## 3.4 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — DECISION FLOWCHART

| Situation | Primary Failure Mode | Key Formula | KTU Marks Weightage |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Splines – Torque given, find $L$ | Bearing (crushing) | $L = \dfrac{T}{n h \sigma_c r_m}$ | 7 marks |
| Splines – Torque given, find $L$ | Shear | $L = \dfrac{T}{n w \tau r_m}$ | 7 marks |
| Bolts – Tightening torque | Torsion + tension | $T_t = F_i \dfrac{d_2}{2}\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | 4 marks |
| Bolts – Equivalent stress | Combined | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | 4 marks |
| Bolts – Pre-load with external load | Bolt + member stiffness | $F_b = F_i + P \cdot C$ | 6 marks |
| Eccentric bolt group | Bolt shear | $F_s = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1F_2\cos\theta}$ | 14 marks (full) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spline Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shaft Rotating with Torque T] --> B[Spline Teeth Machined on Shaft Surface]
    B --> C{Mating Hub with Internal Grooves}
    C --> D[Force Distributed Across n Teeth]
    D --> E1[Tooth 1 — Bears Torque T/1]
    D --> E2[Tooth 2 — Bears Torque T/2]
    D --> E3[Tooth n — Bears Torque T/n]
    E1 --> F1[Crushing Stress sigma_c at Mean Radius r_m]
    E2 --> F2[Shear Stress tau at Tooth Root]
    E3 --> F3[Combined Loading Check]
    F1 --> G[Compare with Allowable sigma_c and tau]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{Safe?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Design Acceptable]
    H -- No --> J[Increase L or D or n]
```

## 4.2 Bolted Joint Architecture — Tightening & Loading Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Tightening Torque Tt to Nut] --> B[Friction in Threads: Tt1]
    A --> C[Friction at Bearing Face: Tt2]
    B --> D[Axial Pre-load Fi Generated]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Bolt is Stretched Like a Spring}
    E --> F[Members are Compressed]
    F --> G[Apply External Load P to Joint]
    G --> H[Increment C*P goes to Bolt]
    G --> I[Decrement 1-C*P reduces Clamping]
    H --> J[Total Bolt Load Fb = Fi + C*P]
    I --> K[Residual Clamp Force Fc = Fi - 1-C*P]
    J --> L[Check sigma_VM less than Sp/1.3]
    K --> M[Check that Fc remains greater than 0]
    L --> N{Design Safe?}
    M --> N
    N -- Yes --> O[Accept Design]
    N -- No --> P[Increase Bolt Size or Grade]
```

## 4.3 Eccentric Bolt Group — Force Resolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Load P at Eccentricity e] --> B[Direct Shear F1 = P/n on every Bolt]
    A --> C[Twisting Moment M = P*e]
    C --> D[Tangential Shear F2 on each Bolt]
    D --> E[F2_max = P*e*r_max divided by sum of r_i^2]
    B --> F[Critical Bolt Receives F1]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Angle theta between F1 and F2]
    G --> H[Resultant Fs = sqrt F1^2 + F2^2 + 2 F1 F2 cos theta]
    H --> I[Shear Stress tau = Fs / A_b]
    I --> J[Check Factor of Safety greater than Specified]
```

## 4.4 Spline Standard Proportions — Reference Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IS2048 [IS 2048 Spline Standard Proportions]
        direction TB
        A1[Nominal Shaft Diameter d] --> A2{Light / Medium / Heavy Series}
        A2 -- Light L --> A3[Major Diameter D = 1.25d + 4]
        A2 -- Medium M --> A4[Major Diameter D = 1.4d + 8]
        A2 -- Heavy H --> A5[Major Diameter D = 1.75d + 12]
        A3 --> A6[Tooth Depth h = D - d over 2]
        A4 --> A6
        A5 --> A6
        A6 --> A7[Mean Radius r_m = d + D over 4]
    end
```

## 4.5 Bolt Under Tension & Torsion — Stress State Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Bolt with Pre-load Fi] --> B[Tensile Stress sigma_t = Fi / A_s]
    A --> C[Torsional Shear tau = 8 Tt divided by pi d^3]
    B --> D[Plane Stress State on Bolt Surface]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Principal Stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    E --> F[Von-Mises Equivalent sigma_VM = sqrt sigma_t^2 + 3 tau^2]
    F --> G{sigma_VM less than S_p / 1.3 ?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Safely Pre-loaded]
    G -- No --> I[Reduce Fi or Increase Bolt Size]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four advantages of splines over keys.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The torque is transmitted by **multiple teeth**, so each tooth carries only a fraction of the load → lower bearing pressure.
2. Splines are **integral with the shaft**, eliminating the risk of a key slipping or rotating in its keyway.
3. Splines provide **self-centering** of the hub on the shaft, ensuring better concentricity.
4. Splines permit **axial sliding** of the hub on the shaft (useful in gear-changing mechanisms).

*(1 mark for each correctly stated advantage — 4 advantages for 3 marks, pick best 3 with elaboration = 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is meant by the term "pre-load" in a bolted joint? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Definition (1.5 marks):* **Pre-load ($F_i$)** is the initial tensile force induced in a bolt when the nut is tightened before any external load is applied. It produces a clamping force on the members being joined.

*Importance (1.5 marks):*
- It ensures the joint does not separate under small external loads.
- It reduces the **fluctuating stress amplitude** in the bolt, improving fatigue life.
- It enables use of a smaller, cheaper bolt for the same external load.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU ESE Pattern: Module Internal Choice — **Solve either (a) OR (b)**, not both.

---

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**A mild-steel shaft of diameter 60 mm is to be provided with straight-side splines. The number of splines is 8, and the design torque is 1200 N·m. Using IS 2048 medium-duty proportions and allowable stresses $\sigma_c = 60$ MPa and $\tau = 50$ MPa, determine:**
- **(a)** The major and mean diameters of the spline.
- **(b)** The required length of the splines.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d = 60$ mm (minor diameter)
- $n = 8$
- $T = 1200$ N·m $= 1.2 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- Medium duty: $D = 1.4d + 8$
- $\sigma_c = 60$ N/mm², $\tau = 50$ N/mm²

**Part (a) — Diameters (7 marks):**

Step 1: Major diameter
$$D = 1.4(60) + 8 = 84 + 8 = 92 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Tooth depth
$$h = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{92 - 60}{2} = 16 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3: Mean radius
$$r_m = \frac{d + D}{4} = \frac{60 + 92}{4} = 38 \text{ mm}$$

*(These three steps carry 1, 2, 2 marks respectively; presentation 2 marks = total 7.)*

**Part (b) — Length of Splines (7 marks):**

**Case 1 — Crushing (Bearing) Failure:**

The torque equation:
$$T = n \cdot h \cdot L \cdot \sigma_c \cdot r_m$$
$$1.2 \times 10^6 = 8 \times 16 \times L \times 60 \times 38$$
$$1.2 \times 10^6 = 292{,}320 \cdot L$$
$$L_c = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{292{,}320} = 4.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Case 2 — Shear Failure:**

Assuming standard tooth width $w = h/2 = 8$ mm (for straight-side splines, $w \approx h/2$ per IS 2048):
$$T = n \cdot w \cdot L \cdot \tau \cdot r_m$$
$$1.2 \times 10^6 = 8 \times 8 \times L \times 50 \times 38$$
$$1.2 \times 10^6 = 121{,}600 \cdot L$$
$$L_s = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{121{,}600} = 9.87 \text{ mm}$$

**Selection:** Length must satisfy both → $L = 9.87$ mm ≈ **10 mm (use next standard length, e.g., 12 mm or 16 mm)**.

*(2 marks for each case, 1 mark for selection logic, 2 marks for presentation = 7 marks.)*

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**A cylinder head is fastened to the cylinder block of an IC engine by 8 studs of nominal diameter M16. The pre-load is set to 75% of the proof load. The bolt grade is 9.8, the thread is ISO metric coarse, and the friction angle is $\phi = 11°$ (thread friction coefficient $\mu_t = 0.20$), with a lead angle $\alpha = 2°$ on the thread. Take the mean bearing diameter as $1.5d$. The external load is 50 kN per bolt. The joint stiffness constant is $C = 0.25$. Find:**
- **(a)** The initial pre-load $F_i$ and the tightening torque required.
- **(b)** The total bolt load and the factor of safety against proof load.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d$ = 16 mm, $A_s$ for M16 = **157 mm²** (from standard table)
- Bolt grade 9.8: $S_u = 900$ MPa, $S_p = 0.85 S_u = 0.85 \times 900 = 765$ MPa
- $P_{\text{ext}} = 50$ kN = 50,000 N
- $\alpha = 2°$, $\phi = 11°$, $C = 0.25$
- $d_2$ for M16 ≈ 14.701 mm (ISO metric table)

**Part (a) — Pre-load & Tightening Torque (7 marks):**

Step 1: Pre-load
$$F_i = 0.75 \times S_p \times A_s = 0.75 \times 765 \times 157 = 90{,}108 \text{ N} \approx 90.1 \text{ kN}$$

Step 2: Torque component in threads
$$T_{t1} = F_i \cdot \frac{d_2}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi) = 90{,}108 \times \frac{14.701}{2} \times \tan(13°)$$
$$= 90{,}108 \times 7.3505 \times 0.2309 = 152{,}948 \text{ N·mm} = 153 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 3: Torque component at bearing face
$$T_{t2} = F_i \cdot \mu_b \cdot \frac{d_w}{2} = 90{,}108 \times 0.20 \times \frac{1.5 \times 16}{2} = 90{,}108 \times 0.2 \times 12 = 216{,}259 \text{ N·mm} \approx 216 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 4: Total tightening torque
$$T_t = T_{t1} + T_{t2} = 153 + 216 = 369 \text{ N·m}$$

*(2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Total Bolt Load and FOS (7 marks):**

Step 1: Increment of bolt load due to external load
$$\Delta F_b = P \cdot C = 50{,}000 \times 0.25 = 12{,}500 \text{ N} = 12.5 \text{ kN}$$

Step 2: Total bolt load
$$F_b = F_i + \Delta F_b = 90{,}108 + 12{,}500 = 102{,}608 \text{ N} \approx 102.6 \text{ kN}$$

Step 3: Proof load
$$F_p = S_p \times A_s = 765 \times 157 = 120{,}105 \text{ N} \approx 120.1 \text{ kN}$$

Step 4: Factor of safety against proof load
$$n_p = \frac{F_p}{F_b} = \frac{120{,}105}{102{,}608} = 1.17$$

The FOS is **1.17** (low; the design should be reviewed — the bolt may need a higher grade or larger size). For a permanent joint, a FOS of 1.2–1.5 is typical.

*(2 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 7 marks.)*

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Spline & Bolted Joint Problems:**
> 1. **Using $D/2$ instead of $r_m = (d + D)/4$ for the moment arm in spline torque — costs 2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting the `3` in the Von-Mises equation** ($\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_t^2 + 3\tau^2}$) — costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Using full nominal area $\pi d^2/4$ instead of the stress area $A_s$ for thread stress calculation** — yields a non-conservative answer; costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing "shear of bolt" (for eccentric loading) with "shear of thread root"** — these are different failure surfaces. For eccentric loading, use the unthreaded shank area $\pi d^2/4$.
> 5. **Neglecting the bearing-face torque $T_{t2}$** in tightening problems — answer comes out 30–50% too low.
> 6. **Not converting eccentricity into twisting moment correctly:** $M = P \times e$ (NOT $P \times e \times n$).
> 7. **Using $r$ instead of $r^2$** when computing $\sum r_i^2$ — a classic error costing 3 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Splines — Rapid Revision Checklist
- [ ] Splines are **integral, multi-tooth** torque transmitters on shafts.
- [ ] Three IS 2048 series: **Light (L)**, **Medium (M)**, **Heavy (H)**.
- [ ] Always use the **mean radius** $r_m = (d + D)/4$ in torque equations.
- [ ] Two failure checks: **crushing (bearing)** and **shear (root)**.
- [ ] Design length is the **larger** of the two required lengths.
- [ ] Splines permit **axial sliding** of the hub (used in sliding-mesh gearboxes).
- [ ] Tooth width $w \approx h/2$ for straight-side splines.

### Bolted Joints — Rapid Revision Checklist
- [ ] A bolt must be pre-loaded before any external load acts.
- [ ] Pre-load is typically $F_i = 0.75 \times S_p \times A_s$ (permanent joints).
- [ ] Use **stress area $A_s$** (NOT nominal area) for bolt stress — get it from tables.
- [ ] **Von-Mises equivalent** stress accounts for combined tension + tightening torsion.
- [ ] Empirical relation: $F_i \leq S_p A_s / 1.3$ accounts for torsion during tightening.
- [ ] **Joint stiffness constant** $C$ depends on relative stiffness $K_b$ and $K_m$.
- [ ] External load $P$ splits as $C \cdot P$ into bolt tension and $(1-C)P$ reduces member clamping.
- [ ] For **eccentrically loaded** bolt groups, the critical bolt is at maximum $r_i$ and on the load line.
- [ ] Use the **bolt shank area** $\pi d^2/4$ for shear (NOT $A_s$) — shear occurs on the unthreaded shank.
- [ ] **Tightening torque** has two components: thread friction and bearing-face friction.
- [ ] Bolts must be sized so that **residual clamping force $F_c > 0$** (joint does not separate).
- [ ] Bolts fatigue better with **higher pre-load and lower external load increment** (i.e., lower $C$).

### KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Pattern (Module 2)
| Topic | 3-Mark Q | 14-Mark Q | Total |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------|
| Splines | 1 | 0.5 (often combined with keys/shafts) | ~7 marks |
| Bolted Joints — Stiffness & Pre-load | 1 | 1 | ~10 marks |
| Eccentric Bolt Group | 0 | 0.5 | ~7 marks |
| Combined (full module) | 2 | 1 choice | 14 marks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Insight:** In ESE problems, always **draw the free-body diagram of the bolt + members** to visualize the force distribution. Examiners award full marks for clear FBDs, even if numerical answers have minor slips. Also, state the **assumed failure theory** (Von-Mises for ductile, Maximum Normal Stress for brittle) explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Detachable Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Detachable Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Non-Detachable Joints: Rigorous Definition & Intuitive Foundation

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

A **Non-Detachable Joint** (also termed a *permanent joint*) is a mechanical connection between two or more machine or structural components that, once fabricated, **cannot be disassembled without causing irreversible damage** to the parent components or the connecting element. These joints transfer axial, transverse, or combined loads by exploiting either **mechanical interlocking**, **metallurgical bonding**, or **atomic diffusion** across the joint interface.

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PENST744), non-detachable joints fall under three principal categories:

1. **Riveted Joints** — Mechanical fastening using ductile metal pins upset at the tail end.
2. **Welded Joints** — Metallurgical fusion of base metals (covered in greater depth in later modules).
3. **Brazed and Soldered Joints** — Capillary-driven bonding using a filler metal below the parent metal's melting point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PENST744 Module 2):** The dominant non-detachable joint studied is the **Riveted Joint**. Welded joints are conceptually introduced but design of welded joints is treated in Module 3.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The "Stitch vs. Glue vs. Weld" Metaphor

Imagine you have two sheets of leather that you wish to join permanently:

| Real-World Action | Engineering Equivalent | Key Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Sewing with thread and knotting | **Riveted Joint** | Mechanical Interlock |
| Gluing with adhesive | **Adhesive / Bonded Joint** | Molecular Adhesion |
| Burning/fusing with heat | **Welded Joint** | Atomic Diffusion |
| Soldering with a filler metal | **Brazed / Soldered Joint** | Capillary Diffusion |

The **rivet** behaves like a thread-and-knot: a soft metal pin is pushed through aligned holes in the plates, and the protruding tail is hammered (upset) to form a second head, locking the joint. Once upset, the rivet head cannot be re-formed without destroying the joint.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Riveted Joints?

> [!NOTE]
> Even though fusion welding has largely replaced riveting in modern structural fabrication, **riveted joints remain critical** in:
> - Aerospace primary structures (legacy aircraft skins)
> - Pressure vessel manholes and inspection covers
> - Railway rolling stock bogies
> - Light-load sheet metal assemblies in HVAC and automotive body panels
> - Situations requiring **predictable, ductile failure** rather than brittle fracture

The KTU examiner expects students to understand that riveted joints **fail progressively**, providing a visible warning before catastrophic collapse — a property welding does not guarantee.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Non-Detachable Joints

Non-detachable joints may be systematically classified by the **mode of load transfer**, **geometry of the parent parts**, and the **number of shear planes per rivet**.

### A. Based on Method of Fastening
- **Riveted Joints** (primary focus)
- **Welded Joints**
- **Brazed / Soldered Joints**
- **Adhesive Bonded Joints**

### B. Riveted Joint Geometry (Detailed)

| Type | Description | Shear Planes per Rivet |
|---|---|---|
| **Lap Joint (Single Riveted)** | Plates overlap; rivets in single row | 1 (Single Shear) |
| **Lap Joint (Double Riveted)** | Plates overlap; two rows of rivets zigzag or chain | 1 (Single Shear) |
| **Butt Joint with Single Cover Plate** | Plates butt against each other; one cover plate | 1 (Single Shear) |
| **Butt Joint with Double Cover Plates** | Plates butt; two cover plates (one each side) | 2 (Double Shear) |

### C. Rivet Head Classification
- **Snap Head** (most common, hemispherical)
- **Pan Head** (low-profile)
- **Counter-sunk Head** (flush surface)
- **Conical Head** (tapered for high-stress applications)

![Figure: Schematic showing lap vs. butt riveted joints — to be visualized in Section 4]

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard permissible stress values are conventionally adopted (per IS 800 and Shigley/V.B. Bhandari) when not explicitly specified in KTU exam problems:

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Structural Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Ultimate Tensile Strength of plate | $\sigma_{tu}$ | **400 – 460 MPa** |
| Ultimate Shear Strength of rivet | $\tau_u$ | **320 – 380 MPa** |
| Ultimate Crushing Strength of rivet | $\sigma_{cu}$ | **640 – 700 MPa** |
| Factor of Safety | $n$ | **4 to 6** (static) |
| Permissible Tensile Stress | $\sigma_t$ | **80 – 100 MPa** |
| Permissible Shear Stress | $\tau$ | **60 – 80 MPa** |
| Permissible Crushing Stress | $\sigma_c$ | **120 – 150 MPa** |

These allowable values are **bold-marked constants** that the examiner frequently supplies or expects you to assume.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Riveted Joint — The Four Critical Dimensions

Every riveted joint design revolves around four geometric parameters:

1. **Diameter of rivet hole ($d$)** — Standardized as per IS standards.
2. **Pitch ($p$)** — Centre-to-centre distance between consecutive rivets in a row.
3. **Transverse Pitch / Back Pitch ($p_b$)** — Perpendicular distance between rows of rivets.
4. **Edge Distance ($e$)** — Distance from the centre of the rivet hole to the nearest edge of the plate (typically $e \geq 1.5d$).

The **gross plate width** failing in tension through the rivet holes defines the load capacity:

$$
\text{Gross Width} = p \quad ; \quad \text{Net (effective) Width} = p - d
$$

---

## 2.2 Three Failure Modes of a Riveted Joint — The Design Trinity

When load is transmitted through a riveted joint, **three concurrent failure pathways** must be evaluated. The joint strength is governed by the **weakest** of these three.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Failure Modes (MUST memorize for KTU ESE):**
> 1. **Shearing of the rivet** (along its cylindrical cross-section).
> 2. **Tearing / Tearing of the plate** (tensile rupture across the weakest net section).
> 3. **Crushing / Bearing of the rivet or plate** (compression failure at the cylindrical contact area).

---

### Failure Mode 1: Shearing of the Rivet

A rivet in single shear has **one shear plane** perpendicular to the load. The shear area is the circular cross-section of the rivet:

$$
A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2
$$

If the joint has $n$ rivets per pitch (single shear), the **shear strength per pitch length** is:

$$
P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau
$$

where $\tau$ is the permissible shear stress of the rivet material.

For a **double-shear butt joint** (with double cover plates), each rivet has **two shear planes**:

$$
P_s = n \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau
$$

---

### Failure Mode 2: Tearing of the Plate (Tensile Failure)

The plate fails in tension through the **net section** (the row containing the rivet holes). The net width resisting tension is $(p - d)$. The **tearing strength per pitch length** is:

$$
P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

where $t$ is the plate thickness and $\sigma_t$ is the permissible tensile stress of the plate material.

For a **double-riveted chain or zigzag joint**, the tearing resistance must be evaluated at **each row** separately (the first row carries full load, the second row carries half, etc.), and the **minimum** tearing resistance governs.

---

### Failure Mode 3: Crushing of the Rivet (Bearing Failure)

The rivet bears against the plate hole's cylindrical wall over a projected contact area $(d \times t)$. The **crushing strength per pitch length** is:

$$
P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c
$$

where $\sigma_c$ is the permissible crushing stress. Note that doubling the rivets doubles $P_c$, but doubling the shear planes does NOT change $P_c$ — crushing depends only on projected diameter.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The table below consolidates **every formula you must memorize** for KTU ESE Module 2 problems. Note the use of `\vert` (vertical bar rendered mathematically) instead of the markdown-breaking pipe character.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shear area of one rivet | $A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | mm² | Single shear |
| 2 | Shear area (double shear) | $A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{2} d^2$ | mm² | Butt joint with 2 cover plates |
| 3 | Shear strength per pitch | $P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ | N | Single shear, $n$ rivets |
| 4 | Tearing strength per pitch | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | N | Plate failure across rivet hole |
| 5 | Crushing strength per pitch | $P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$ | N | Bearing failure at hole wall |
| 6 | Gross plate strength | $P_p = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | N | Strength of un-riveted plate |
| 7 | Unwin's empirical formula | $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ | mm (both) | Initial rivet diameter estimate |
| 8 | Optimum pitch (single shear) | $p = d + \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}$ | mm | When $P_s = P_t$ |
| 9 | Optimum pitch (double shear) | $p = d + \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}$ | mm | When $P_s = P_t$ (double cover) |
| 10 | Rivet diameter (shear = crush) | $d = \dfrac{4 \sigma_c t}{\pi \tau}$ | mm | Theoretical from $P_s = P_c$ |
| 11 | Efficiency of joint | $\eta = \dfrac{\min(P_s, P_t, P_c)}{P_p}$ | dimensionless | As a percentage |
| 12 | Number of rivets (shear) | $n_s = \dfrac{P}{\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau}$ | integer | Total load $P$ resisted by shear |
| 13 | Number of rivets (crushing) | $n_c = \dfrac{P}{d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c}$ | integer | Total load $P$ resisted by crushing |
| 14 | Back pitch (zigzag) | $p_b \geq 0.6 p + 3d$ | mm | Recommended spacing for zigzag |

---

## 2.4 The Efficiency Concept — Why It Matters

**Efficiency** quantifies how much of the un-riveted plate's strength is utilized in the jointed configuration.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Least of } (P_s, P_t, P_c)}{P_p} = \frac{\text{Least of } (P_s, P_t, P_c)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is efficiency always less than 1?**
> Because the rivet holes reduce the net load-bearing cross-section, the rivet itself is weaker in shear than the plate's gross tension, and crushing can occur at the bearing surface. A well-designed joint reaches efficiencies of **60 – 80%**; anything below 50% indicates poor design.

**Real-world utility**: In pressure vessel design, low efficiency means more material is consumed for the same load capacity, increasing vessel weight and cost. Aerospace riveted lap splices aim for $\eta \geq 70\%$ to minimize weight penalty.

---

## 2.5 The Optimum Condition — Designing for Maximum Efficiency

Maximum efficiency is achieved when all three failure modes are **equally probable** (i.e., the joint is *balanced*):

$$
P_s = P_t = P_c
$$

Solving the **first equality** ($P_s = P_t$) gives the **optimum pitch**:

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

$$
\boxed{\,p = d + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}\,}
$$

Solving the **second equality** ($P_s = P_c$) gives the **theoretical rivet diameter**:

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d = \frac{4 \sigma_c t}{\pi \tau}\,}
$$

Once the joint is balanced, **efficiency simplifies** to a function of stress ratios and diameter-to-thickness ratio. This is derived in Section 3.

---

## 2.6 Practical Design Sequence (Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Step KTU Design Algorithm for a Single-Riveted Lap Joint:**
> 1. **Estimate $d$** using Unwin's formula: $d = 6\sqrt{t}$.
> 2. **Standardize $d$** to the next higher standard value from IS tables.
> 3. **Compute the theoretical optimum pitch** from $p = d + (\pi/4) \cdot (d^2 \tau)/(t \sigma_t)$.
> 4. **Apply minimum pitch constraint**: $p_{\min} = 2.5 d$ (to prevent rivet interference).
> 5. **Apply maximum pitch constraint**: $p_{\max} = 32 t$ or $300$ mm (whichever is less) per IS 800.
> 6. **Verify** the chosen $p$ lies in $[p_{\min}, p_{\max}]$.
> 7. **Compute efficiency** $\eta$ and check if it is acceptable.

---

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Efficiency of a Balanced Single-Riveted Lap Joint

We aim to derive the closed-form expression for efficiency when the joint is perfectly balanced ($P_s = P_t = P_c$).

**Given:**
- Single rivet per pitch ($n = 1$).
- Single shear configuration.
- Plate thickness $= t$, rivet diameter $= d$, pitch $= p$.

**Step 1 — Start with the balanced condition:**

$$
P_s = P_t = P_c = P_{\text{joint}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express each strength term:**

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau
$$

$$
P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

$$
P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $P_s = P_t$ and solve for the optimum pitch $p$:**

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = (p - d) t \sigma_t
$$

$$
p - d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,p = d + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}\,} \quad \text{...(Equation A)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gross plate strength $P_p$ for this pitch:**

$$
P_p = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = \left(d + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}\right) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

$$
P_p = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t + \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau
$$

Notice that the second term is exactly $P_s$. Therefore:

$$
P_p = P_s + P_t
$$

But since $P_s = P_t$ at the balanced condition, we can substitute:

$$
P_p = 2 P_s
$$

Therefore, the maximum efficiency becomes:

$$
\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_s}{P_p} = \frac{P_s}{2 P_s} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Wait — this assumes $P_s = P_t$ alone.** We must also satisfy $P_s = P_c$. Let us check consistency.

**Step 5 — Apply $P_s = P_c$:**

$$
\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c
$$

$$
d = \frac{4 \sigma_c t}{\pi \tau}
$$

$$
\frac{d}{t} = \frac{4 \sigma_c}{\pi \tau}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute this $d/t$ ratio into the efficiency expression:**

Using the more general efficiency formula before assuming balance, we start fresh:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_s}{P_p} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

Substituting $p$ from Equation A:

$$
p = d + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau}{\left(d + \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}\right) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $d$:

$$
\eta = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d \tau}{\left(1 + \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{d \tau}{t \sigma_t}\right) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\dfrac{4}{\pi d \tau}$:

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{\dfrac{4 t \sigma_t}{\pi d \tau} + 1}
$$

Let $\alpha = \dfrac{t \sigma_t}{d \tau}$. Then:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{1}{\frac{4}{\pi} \alpha + 1}\,} \quad \text{...(Equation B)}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the relation $d = \dfrac{4 \sigma_c t}{\pi \tau}$ into $\alpha$:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{t \sigma_t}{d \tau} = \frac{t \sigma_t}{\left(\dfrac{4 \sigma_c t}{\pi \tau}\right) \tau} = \frac{\pi \sigma_t}{4 \sigma_c}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{4}{\pi} \alpha = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi \sigma_t}{4 \sigma_c} = \frac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_c}
$$

Substituting back into Equation B:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\sigma_c}}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Efficiency of a Double-Shear (Butt) Joint

For a butt joint with **double cover plates**, each rivet has two shear planes. The shear strength per rivet doubles:

$$
P_s = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = \frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau
$$

**Step 1 — Apply $P_s = P_t$:**

$$
\frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau = (p - d) t \sigma_t
$$

$$
p = d + \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross plate strength:**

$$
P_p = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = d t \sigma_t + \frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\max}^{\text{butt}} = \frac{P_s}{P_p} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau}{d t \sigma_t + \dfrac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau}
$$

Following the same algebraic manipulation as Section 3.1:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\max}^{\text{butt}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{t \sigma_t}{d \tau}}\,}
$$

With $d/t = 4 \sigma_c / (\pi \tau)$ substituted:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{\max}^{\text{butt}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\sigma_t}{2 \sigma_c}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compare the two efficiencies using typical ratios** ($\sigma_t / \sigma_c \approx 2$):
> - Single shear lap: $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 33.3\%$
> - Double shear butt: $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 1} = 50\%$

This quantitatively proves that **butt joints with double cover plates are more efficient** — a key KTU examiner-tested concept.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Rivets Required for a Given Load

For a joint transmitting a **total axial load $P$**:

**Step 1 — Rivets required by shear consideration:**

$$
n_s = \frac{P}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau}
$$

**Step 2 — Rivets required by crushing consideration:**

$$
n_c = \frac{P}{d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Total rivets required (Lap joint, single shear):**

$$
\boxed{\,n = \max(n_s, n_c) \text{ rounded up to next integer}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Total rivets required (Butt joint, double shear):**

Here, crushing is unchanged ($n_c$ same), but shear capacity doubles, so:

$$
n_s^{\text{butt}} = \frac{P}{\dfrac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau}
$$

$$
n^{\text{butt}} = \max(n_s^{\text{butt}}, n_c)
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step Model Solution)

**Problem Statement:**

A single-riveted lap joint connects two mild steel plates, each **12 mm thick**. The rivet diameter is **18 mm**, and the pitch is **50 mm**. Permissible stresses are:
- $\sigma_t = 80$ MPa (plate tension)
- $\tau = 60$ MPa (rivet shear)
- $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa (rivet crushing)

**Find:** (a) Shear strength per pitch, (b) Tearing strength per pitch, (c) Crushing strength per pitch, (d) Efficiency of the joint.

---

### Solution:

**(a) Shear Strength $P_s$:**

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = \frac{\pi}{4} (18)^2 (60)
$$

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 324 \cdot 60 = \pi \cdot 81 \cdot 60 = \pi \cdot 4860
$$

$$
P_s = 4860 \pi \approx 15268.14 \text{ N} \approx 15.27 \text{ kN}
$$

**(b) Tearing Strength $P_t$:**

$$
P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (50 - 18) \cdot 12 \cdot 80
$$

$$
P_t = 32 \cdot 12 \cdot 80 = 32 \cdot 960 = 30720 \text{ N} = 30.72 \text{ kN}
$$

**(c) Crushing Strength $P_c$:**

$$
P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 18 \cdot 12 \cdot 120
$$

$$
P_c = 216 \cdot 120 = 25920 \text{ N} = 25.92 \text{ kN}
$$

**(d) Efficiency $\eta$:**

The joint's actual strength is the **minimum** of the three:

$$
P_{\text{joint}} = \min(P_s, P_t, P_c) = \min(15.27, 30.72, 25.92) = 15.27 \text{ kN}
$$

Gross plate strength:

$$
P_p = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 50 \cdot 12 \cdot 80 = 48000 \text{ N} = 48.00 \text{ kN}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_p} = \frac{15.27}{48.00} = 0.3181
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta \approx 31.81\%\,}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When Each Failure Mode Governs

| Ratio of Stresses | Dominant Failure | Joint Improvement Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_t \gg \tau$ | Rivet shear | Increase $d$ or use more rivets |
| $\sigma_t \ll \tau$ | Plate tearing | Increase $p$ (more plate material) |
| $\sigma_c \ll \tau, \sigma_t$ | Crushing | Increase $d$ or $t$ |
| All three balanced | Maximum efficiency | Use $p = d + (\pi/4) d^2 \tau / (t \sigma_t)$ |

---

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Riveted Joints

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the hierarchical taxonomy of riveted joints as per KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[RIVETED JOINTS] --> B0[LAP JOINT]
    A0 --> C0[BUTT JOINT]

    B0 --> B1[Single Riveted]
    B0 --> B2[Double Riveted Chain]
    B0 --> B3[Double Riveted Zigzag]

    C0 --> C1[Single Cover Plate]
    C0 --> C2[Double Cover Plates]

    C1 --> C1a[Single Riveted]
    C1 --> C1b[Double Riveted]

    C2 --> C2a[Single Riveted]
    C2 --> C2b[Double Riveted]

    B1 --> S1[One Shear Plane]
    B2 --> S1
    C1a --> S1
    C1b --> S1

    C2a --> S2[Two Shear Planes]
    C2b --> S2

    S1 --> M1[Failure Shear Strength P_s equals pi over 4 times d squared times tau]
    S1 --> M2[Failure Tearing Strength P_t equals p minus d times t times sigma_t]
    S1 --> M3[Failure Crushing Strength P_c equals d times t times sigma_c]

    S2 --> N1[Failure Shear Strength P_s equals pi over 2 times d squared times tau]
    S2 --> N2[Same Tearing and Crushing as Single Shear]
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic Flowchart: Design Sequence for a Riveted Lap Joint

This diagram represents the **decision logic flow** a designer follows when sizing a riveted joint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START with plate thickness t and load P] --> S1[Estimate d using Unwins Formula d equals 6 times square root of t]
    S1 --> S2[Standardize d to next IS standard value]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Optimum Pitch p using p equals d plus pi over 4 times d squared tau over t sigma_t]
    S3 --> S4{Is p greater than 2.5 d?}
    S4 -- YES --> S5{Is p less than 32 t?}
    S4 -- NO --> S6[Set p to 2.5 d]
    S5 -- YES --> S7[Accept p as design pitch]
    S5 -- NO --> S8[Set p to 32 t]
    S6 --> S7
    S8 --> S7
    S7 --> S9[Compute P_s, P_t, P_c at this pitch]
    S9 --> S10[Joint Strength equals minimum of P_s, P_t, P_c]
    S10 --> S11[Compute Efficiency eta equals Joint Strength over p times t times sigma_t]
    S11 --> S12{Eta greater than 50 percent?}
    S12 -- YES --> S13[DESIGN ACCEPTABLE]
    S12 -- NO --> S14[Consider Butt Joint with Double Covers]
    S14 --> S15[Recompute and Re-verify]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: The Three-Mode Failure Logic

The following diagram depicts how the three failure modes interact within the joint's load-transfer mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOAD[Applied Load P] --> RIV[Rivet]
    LOAD --> PLATE[Plate Section]

    RIV --> R1[Shear Plane Check]
    RIV --> R2[Bearing Contact d times t]

    PLATE --> T1[Net Section Check p minus d times t]

    R1 --> R1A[P_s equals pi over 4 d squared tau]
    R2 --> R2A[P_c equals d t sigma_c]
    T1 --> T1A[P_t equals p minus d times t times sigma_t]

    R1A --> GATE[Governing Strength equals MIN of P_s P_t P_c]
    R2A --> GATE
    T1A --> GATE

    GATE --> EFF[Efficiency eta equals Governing Strength over p t sigma_t]
    EFF --> OUT[Final Joint Rating]
```

---

            ║            ║

                  p
