# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define a *non-detachable joint*. List any two types with one engineering application of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **non-detachable joint** is a permanent connection between two or more components that **cannot be disassembled without causing destruction** of the joint or the parent parts.

**Two types:**
1. **Riveted Joint** — Used in **aircraft fuselage skin splicing** (e.g., legacy Boeing 737 lap splice rivets).
2. **Welded Joint** — Used in **pressure vessel circumferential seams** carrying hydrostatic pressure loads.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU marker expects the word *permanent* and at least one real-world example for full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State Unwin's formula for estimating rivet diameter. What are its limitations?

**Model Answer:**

Unwin's empirical formula is:

$$
\boxed{\,d = 6\sqrt{t}\,}
$$

where $d$ is the rivet diameter in **mm** and $t$ is the plate thickness in **mm**.

**Limitations:**
1. It is purely **empirical**, derived for structural steel joints — not valid for aluminium alloys or high-strength steels.
2. It assumes a **fixed ratio** of permissible stresses ($\sigma_c / \tau \approx$ constant), which may not hold for all materials.
3. It provides only an **initial estimate**; final design must satisfy shear, tearing, and crushing checks simultaneously.
4. It is **not applicable** to riveted joints carrying eccentric or dynamic loads.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> Students most commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Mixing up single-shear and double-shear formulas** — always draw the figure and label the shear planes.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $n$ (number of rivets per pitch)** — joint strength is per pitch length, not per single rivet.
> 3. **Using gross width instead of net width** for tearing strength — this overestimates strength and triggers valuation penalties.
> 4. **Skipping the efficiency computation** — efficiency is the *final* design metric; omitting it costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Reporting the wrong units** (MPa vs N vs kN) — examiners in KTU deduct half-marks for unit errors.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

A single-riveted lap joint is to be designed for two mild steel plates of thickness **$t = 15$ mm** to transmit a load of **$P = 80$ kN**. Permissible stresses are:
- $\sigma_t = 84$ MPa
- $\tau = 70$ MPa
- $\sigma_c = 140$ MPa

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Estimate the **rivet diameter** using Unwin's formula, **standardize** it, and determine the **optimum pitch**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

Calculate the **shear, tearing, and crushing strengths per pitch**, determine the **joint efficiency**, and verify whether the joint is adequately designed.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

##### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 — Estimate $d$ using Unwin's formula:**

$$
d = 6\sqrt{t} = 6\sqrt{15} = 6 \times 3.873 = 23.24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Standardize $d$:**

Round up to the next standard size. Standard rivet diameters (per IS 1929): ... 22, 25, 28 ...

Choose $d = 25$ mm.

**Step 3 — Compute optimum pitch (single shear, single riveted):**

$$
p = d + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2 \tau}{t \sigma_t}
$$

$$
p = 25 + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{(25)^2 \cdot 70}{15 \cdot 84}
$$

$$
p = 25 + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{625 \cdot 70}{1260}
$$

$$
p = 25 + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{43750}{1260}
$$

$$
p = 25 + \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 34.722
$$

$$
p = 25 + 0.7854 \cdot 34.722 = 25 + 27.27 = 52.27 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply minimum pitch check:**

$$
p_{\min} = 2.5 d = 2.5 \times 25 = 62.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Since calculated $p = 52.27$ mm $< p_{\min} = 62.5$ mm, **adopt $p = 62.5$ mm** to avoid rivet interference.

**Step 5 — Apply maximum pitch check:**

$$
p_{\max} = 32 t = 32 \times 15 = 480 \text{ mm (much larger than 62.5)}
$$

So $p = 62.5$ mm is acceptable.

##### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Shear strength per pitch:**

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \tau = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (25)^2 \times 70
$$

$$
P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 625 \times 70 = \pi \times 10937.5 = 34361.6 \text{ N} \approx 34.36 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Tearing strength per pitch:**

$$
P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (62.5 - 25) \cdot 15 \cdot 84
$$

$$
P_t = 37.5 \times 15 \times 84 = 37.5 \times 1260 = 47250 \text{ N} = 47.25 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Crushing strength per pitch:**

$$
P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 25 \times 15 \times 140 = 52500 \text{ N} = 52.50 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Governing joint strength:**

$$
P_{\text{joint}} = \min(34.36, 47.25, 52.50) = 34.36 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of rivets required for $P = 80$ kN:**

By shear:

$$
n_s = \frac{P}{P_s} = \frac{80}{34.36} = 2.33 \rightarrow \text{use } 3 \text{ rivets per pitch row}
$$

By crushing:

$$
n_c = \frac{P}{P_c} = \frac{80}{52.50} = 1.52 \rightarrow \text{use } 2 \text{ rivets}
$$

Therefore, design with **3 rivets per pitch** (governed by shear).

**Step 6 — Efficiency check (single rivet per pitch row):**

Gross plate strength:

$$
P_p = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 62.5 \times 15 \times 84 = 78750 \text{ N} = 78.75 \text{ kN}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_p} = \frac{34.36}{78.75} = 0.4363
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta \approx 43.63\%\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** The joint is **acceptable** but of moderate efficiency. For higher efficiency, consider switching to a **double-riveted zigzag lap** or a **butt joint with double cover plates**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

A butt joint with **double cover plates** is used to connect two steel plates of thickness **$t = 20$ mm**. Rivet diameter is **$d = 28$ mm**, pitch is **$p = 90$ mm**, and the joint uses **two rivets per pitch** in a chain arrangement. Permissible stresses:
- $\sigma_t = 90$ MPa
- $\tau = 75$ MPa
- $\sigma_c = 150$ MPa

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Calculate the **shear, tearing, and crushing strengths per pitch length** for this double-shear butt joint.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

Determine the **efficiency of the joint** and state **two design modifications** to improve it.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

##### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:**
- Double shear configuration: each rivet has 2 shear planes.
- $n = 2$ rivets per pitch (chain arrangement).
- $t = 20$ mm, $d = 28$ mm, $p = 90$ mm.

**Step 1 — Shear strength per pitch (double shear):**

$$
P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} d^2 \tau = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{2} \times (28)^2 \times 75
$$

$$
P_s = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{2} \times 784 \times 75 = \pi \times 784 \times 75
$$

$$
P_s = 58800 \pi \approx 184726.4 \text{ N} \approx 184.73 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Tearing strength per pitch:**

For a chain double-riveted joint, the tearing must be checked at each row. The first row carries full load, the second row carries half load (load is shared equally only at the section beyond both rows, but at the first row, full load $P$ passes).

**Critical tearing section — first row (carrying full load):**

$$
P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (90 - 28) \times 20 \times 90
$$

$$
P_t = 62 \times 20 \times 90 = 111600 \text{ N} = 111.60 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Crushing strength per pitch:**

Crushing is independent of number of shear planes but depends on $n$ rivets:

$$
P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 2 \times 28 \times 20 \times 150
$$

$$
P_c = 168000 \text{ N} = 168.00 \text{ kN}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Failure Mode | Strength (kN) |
|---|---|
| Shear | 184.73 |
| Tearing | 111.60 |
| Crushing | 168.00 |

##### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Governing strength:**

$$
P_{\text{joint}} = \min(184.73, 111.60, 168.00) = 111.60 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(Tearing governs)}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross plate strength:**

$$
P_p = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 90 \times 20 \times 90 = 162000 \text{ N} = 162.00 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_p} = \frac{111.60}{162.00} = 0.689
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta \approx 68.9\%\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Two design modifications to improve efficiency:**

1. **Increase the pitch $p$**: Since tearing is the governing mode, increasing $p$ enlarges the net section $(p - d)$ and thereby raises $P_t$. The new pitch should not exceed $p_{\max} = 32 t = 640$ mm.

2. **Switch from chain to zigzag arrangement**: In a zigzag pattern, the second row's holes are offset, increasing the effective net width at the first row's section to $(p - d)$ instead of $(2p - d)$. This significantly boosts tearing resistance.

3. **Alternative — Increase rivet diameter**: A larger $d$ raises both shear and crushing strength proportionally, but may reduce tearing strength (since net width decreases). Verify with full recalculation.

4. **Alternative — Use higher-grade plate material**: Increasing $\sigma_t$ (by selecting a stronger steel) directly increases tearing strength without changing joint geometry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin to your study wall!):**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- Non-detachable joints = permanent connections (riveted, welded, brazed).
- Riveted joints are the dominant non-detachable joint in KTU Module 2.
- Three failure modes: **shear, tearing, crushing** — joint strength = minimum of the three.

### 🔹 Must-Memorize Formulas
- $P_s = n \cdot (\pi/4) \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau$ (single shear)
- $P_s = n \cdot (\pi/2) \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau$ (double shear)
- $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$
- $P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c$
- $\eta = \min(P_s, P_t, P_c) / (p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t)$
- Unwin's: $d = 6\sqrt{t}$
- Optimum pitch: $p = d + (\pi/4) \cdot d^2 \tau / (t \sigma_t)$

### 🔹 Design Constraints (IS 800)
- $p_{\min} = 2.5 d$
- $p_{\max} = 32 t$ or $300$ mm (whichever is smaller)
- Edge distance $e \geq 1.5 d$
- Back pitch $p_b \geq 0.6 p + 3d$ (zigzag)

### 🔹 Examiner Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing single-shear and double-shear shear-area formulas.
- ❌ Using gross width for tearing calculation.
- ❌ Forgetting to multiply by $n$ (rivets per pitch).
- ❌ Reporting numbers without units (kN vs N).
- ❌ Skipping the efficiency computation in final design.

### 🔹 Comparative Insights (High-Yield for KTU)
- Butt joint (double cover) > Butt joint (single cover) > Lap joint (efficiency ranking).
- Zigzag double-riveted > Chain double-riveted (for tearing resistance).
- For higher loads: increase $d$ first, then $n$, then $p$.

### 🔹 Real-World Design Targets
- Pressure vessels: $\eta \geq 60\%$
- Aerospace lap splices: $\eta \geq 70\%$
- Structural bridges: $\eta \geq 50\%$
- General machinery: $\eta \geq 40\%$ (acceptable minimum)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welded Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welded Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Welded Joints

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welded Joint:** A permanent joint formed by coalescence (metallurgical fusion) of two or more metal parts at their contacting surfaces, produced by heating the metal to a plastic or fluid state, with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of filler metal.

In the context of **Design of Machine Elements (PENST744)**, welded joints are treated as **strength-limited components** in which the critical design concern is the **strength of the weld metal in the throat region**, not the base metal itself. The **Indian Standard IS 822:1970 (Reaffirmed 2007)** — *Code of Procedure for Welding of Mild Steel Structures* — and **AWS D1.1** govern the design rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two principal categories of welded joints used in machine design:**
> 1. **Butt (Square, Single-V, Double-V) Welds** — used for joining plates edge-to-edge.
> 2. **Fillet Welds** — the *most common* in machine design; formed in the corner of two intersecting surfaces.

Plug and slot welds are auxiliary and rarely used in primary load paths.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have two pieces of chalk and you want to join them end-to-end. If you melt a small drop of candle wax into the joint and let it solidify, the wax essentially "glues" them together by flowing into the crevices. A **weld is exactly this idea — but with molten parent metal (or filler metal of nearly identical composition)**.

| Real-World Object | Welding Analogy |
|---|---|
| Steel beams in a building | Long **butt welds** join them end-to-end |
| Bicycle frame at the T-joint | **Fillet welds** (triangular cross-section) at every joint |
| Soldering wires on a PCB | A low-temperature cousin — *brazing* / *soldering* |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** In a fillet weld, the *actual* load-carrying region is **NOT** the visible leg of the triangle. It is the *throat* — the shortest distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse face. Designers must size the **leg (s)** so that the **throat thickness (t = 0.707 s)** can carry the applied load.

---

## 1.3 Geometry of a Fillet Weld (The Most Critical Weld in Machine Design)

A fillet weld is essentially a triangle in cross-section. When two plates meet at right angles, the molten metal fills the corner, forming a roughly triangular bead.

| Symbol | Quantity | Default |
|---|---|---|
| $s$ | **Leg size** (size of fillet weld) | Stated by designer |
| $t$ | **Throat thickness** | $t = 0.707\,s$ (for equal legs at 90°) |
| $L$ | **Length of weld** | Perimeter or available length |
| $q$ | Weld quality factor | **0.7 to 1.0** depending on process |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Computing weld area as $s \times L$. The correct shear area for a fillet weld is the **throat area**: $A_{\text{throat}} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L$. This single correction typically saves 3 to 4 marks in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 Fillet Weld vs. Butt Weld — When to Use Which

| Feature | Fillet Weld | Butt Weld |
|---|---|---|
| Plate arrangement | Plates at right angles (or overlap) | Plates in same plane |
| Loaded by | **Shear** in the throat | Tension/compression normal to plate |
| Strength efficiency | ~70 % of butt weld | 100 % (full penetration) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (edge preparation required) |
| Typical application | Brackets, lugs, machine frames | Pressure vessels, shafts, primary structure |

---

## 1.5 Failure Modes of Welded Joints (KTU-Significant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner expects you to recognize **all five** failure modes, not just the simple shear case.

1. **Shear failure in the throat** (most common in fillet welds) — plane at 45° from leg faces.
2. **Tensile failure across the throat** — when the weld is loaded in tension normal to the throat.
3. **Shear failure along the leg / fusion plane** — poor fusion.
4. **Tensile failure in the base metal (heat-affected zone, HAZ).**
5. **Cracking due to residual stresses or stress concentration at the weld toe.**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The **Design of Welded Joints** rests on three classical assumptions adopted by IS 822 and used throughout KTU problems:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Weld Strength Theory:**
> 1. The fillet weld is taken as a triangle of leg size $s$ — the *critical plane* is the throat at 45°.
> 2. Stress distribution along the length is **uniform** (rigorous analysis shows it is non-uniform — a conservative simplification).
> 3. Weld metal strength is governed by its **allowable shear stress** $\tau_{\text{all}}$, which is taken as a fraction of ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u$.

The relationship most often used in KTU problems is:

$$
\tau_{\text{all}} \;\approx\; 0.30 \, \sigma_u \quad \text{(for static loads, mild steel)}
$$

For **dynamic / fatigue loads**, IS 822 prescribes a lower allowable, typically $\tau_{\text{all}} \approx 0.20\,\sigma_u$.

---

## 2.2 Standard Permissible Stresses in Welds (KTU Table)

| Material / Condition | Shear Stress $\tau$ (MPa) | Tensile Stress $\sigma$ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Mild steel (static) | **75** | 110 |
| Mild steel (dynamic) | 55 | 80 |
| For standard $0.30\sigma_u$ rule | $0.30\,\sigma_u$ | $0.50\,\sigma_u$ |
| For standard $0.20\sigma_u$ rule | $0.20\,\sigma_u$ | $0.35\,\sigma_u$ |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numerical problems, the problem statement *always* gives either the material's $\sigma_u$ or a directly stated permissible shear stress. **Do not assume** — read carefully whether it's a *static* or *dynamic* problem.

---

## 2.3 The Master Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

### A. Fillet Weld — Direct / Parallel Loading (Shear in Throat)

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{P}{t \cdot L} \;=\; \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L}
$$

where:
- $P$ = applied shear load parallel to weld axis (N)
- $t = 0.707\,s$ = throat thickness (mm)
- $L$ = total effective weld length (mm)

### B. Butt Weld — Direct Tension / Compression

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{P}{t \, L}
$$

where $t$ is now the **plate thickness** (since butt weld strength = plate strength in the limit).

### C. Fillet Weld — Combined Direct Shear AND Bending

For a fillet weld group subjected to a direct shear force $P$ and a bending moment $M$:

$$
\tau_{\text{shear}} \;=\; \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{bending}} \;=\; \frac{M \cdot c}{I_w} \qquad \text{where} \quad I_w = \frac{s \cdot L^{3}}{12} \;\; \text{(for a single line of weld)}
$$

**Principal (maximum) stress in the throat:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \;+\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}
$$

### D. Fillet Weld — Eccentric Loading (Most Frequent KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

When a load $P$ acts at an eccentricity $e$ from the **centroid** of the weld group:

| Component | Formula |
|---|---|
| Direct shear stress | $\tau_d = \dfrac{P}{A_w} = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, s \, L_{\text{total}}}$ |
| Torsional shear stress | $\tau_t = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J_w}$ |
| Polar moment of weld group | $J_w = I_{xx} + I_{yy}$ |
| For rectangular weld group (length $L$, height $h$) | $J_w = \dfrac{s \, h \, L^{3}}{12} \;+\; \dfrac{s \, L \, h^{3}}{12}$ |
| Resultant maximum shear | $\tau_{\max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^{\,2} + \tau_t^{\,2} + 2\,\tau_d\,\tau_t\,\cos\theta}$ |

> Here $\theta$ is the angle between the **direct shear vector** and the **radial line** to the critical (corner) point.

### E. Fillet Weld Under Pure Torsion (Brackets Loaded Out-of-Plane)

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{T \, r_{\max}}{J_w} \qquad \text{with} \quad J_w = \sum_i (0.707\,s\,L_i)\,r_i^{2}
$$

### F. Bending + Shear on Bracket Weld (Hanger Plate)

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{M \, c}{I_w} \qquad\quad \tau_v \;=\; \frac{V}{0.707\,s\,L_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_v^{\,2}}
$$

---

## 2.4 The Three Design Variables — Choose Two, Solve for the Third

For fillet welds, KTU problems routinely ask you to find one of:

| Given | Find | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| $P$, $\tau_{\text{all}}$, $L$ | leg size $s$ | $s = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, \tau_{\text{all}} \, L}$ |
| $P$, $\tau_{\text{all}}$, $s$ | length $L$ | $L = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, s \, \tau_{\text{all}}}$ |
| $s$, $L$, $\tau_{\text{all}}$ | maximum $P$ | $P = 0.707\,s\,L\,\tau_{\text{all}}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "P, T, s, L all linked":** $\boxed{P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau}$ is the only formula you need to remember for a *direct-shear fillet weld* problem. Everything else is a permutation of this single relation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Welded Joint Application | Critical Design Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure vessel / Boiler | Circumferential & longitudinal butt welds | Full penetration, radiographic inspection |
| Cranes / Hoists | Welded plate girders, lug brackets | **Fatigue** at weld toe |
| Truck / Railway chassis | Fillet-welded box sections | Dynamic + impact loading |
| Machine tool frames | Cast-steel fabricated beds | Vibration damping vs. rigidity |
| Steel structural frames (IS 800) | Fillet welds on brackets | Eccentric shear + torsion |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throat Thickness of an Equal-Leg Fillet Weld

**Statement:** *Show that for an equal-leg right-angle fillet weld, the throat thickness equals $0.707\,s$.*

**Geometric Setup:**

Consider a right triangle representing the fillet weld cross-section:
- Two legs of length $s$ each (the two plate faces meeting at 90°)
- The hypotenuse is the outer surface of the weld bead
- The throat $t$ is the perpendicular distance from the **root** (the inner corner) to the **hypotenuse**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

By Pythagoras:

$$
\text{Hypotenuse} \;=\; \sqrt{s^{2} + s^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{2\,s^{2}} \;=\; s\sqrt{2}
$$

The throat $t$ is the altitude from the right angle to the hypotenuse. For a right triangle:

$$
t \;=\; \frac{\text{leg}_1 \times \text{leg}_2}{\text{hypotenuse}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
t \;=\; \frac{s \cdot s}{s\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{s^{2}}{s\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Rationalizing the denominator:

$$
t \;=\; \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} \;=\; 0.707\,s
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t \;=\; 0.707\,s\,}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $0.707$ arises purely from geometry — it is $\sin 45°$ (or $\cos 45°$), the projection of the leg onto the 45° inclined plane of maximum shear.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Direct Shear Stress in a Fillet Weld Loaded Parallel to Weld Axis

**Statement:** *A bracket carries a tensile load $P$ acting parallel to the weld length. Derive the expression for average shear stress in the throat.*

**Conceptual Setup:**

A fillet weld of leg size $s$ and total length $L$ is loaded in shear along its length. The **critical plane** on which shear failure is postulated is the throat plane, inclined at **45°** to both leg faces. The area of this plane is:

$$
A_{\text{throat}} \;=\; t \cdot L \;=\; 0.707\,s\,L
$$

By definition of average shear stress:

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{P}{A_{\text{throat}}} \;=\; \frac{P}{0.707\,s\,L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\tau \;=\; \frac{P}{0.707\,s\,L}\,}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $0.707$ converts the leg size into the effective load-carrying thickness. If a student mistakenly uses $s$ directly, the design becomes **unsafe by ~30 %** — a critical exam penalty.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torsional Shear Stress in an Eccentrically Loaded Weld Group (KTU Favourite)

**Statement:** *A weld group of multiple line segments resists a torque $T$ about its centroid. Show that the maximum shear stress occurs at the point of maximum radial distance $r_{\max}$ from the centroid.*

**Setup (Thin-Walled Tube Analogy):**

A fillet weld is modelled as a *thin-walled open section*. Each weld line segment is approximated as a thin rectangular strip of width $0.707\,s$ and length $L_i$, located at distance $r_i$ from the centroid of the weld group.

**Step A — Polar Moment of the Weld Group:**

For each individual weld line, the contribution to the polar moment of inertia (treating the line as a slender strip) is:

$$
I_{i} \;=\; (0.707\,s\,L_i) \cdot r_i^{2}
$$

Summing over all segments:

$$
J_w \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} (0.707\,s\,L_i)\,r_i^{2}
$$

**Step B — Torsional Shear Stress:**

Analogous to the torsion of a thin-walled tube, the shear flow $q$ is:

$$
q \;=\; \frac{T}{J_w} \quad \text{(shear force per unit length)}
$$

The shear stress on the throat at any weld line $i$ is:

$$
\tau_{t,i} \;=\; \frac{q}{0.707\,s} \;=\; \frac{T}{(0.707\,s)\,J_w}
$$

But since $J_w$ already contains $(0.707\,s)$ as a factor in each term, a more direct form (commonly used in textbooks) is:

$$
\tau_{t,i} \;=\; \frac{T\,r_i}{0.707\,s\,J_w / (0.707\,s)} \;=\; \frac{T\,r_i}{J_w}
$$

The maximum value occurs at the point of **maximum** $r$:

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{t,\max} \;=\; \frac{T\,r_{\max}}{J_w}\,}
$$

where $J_w = \displaystyle\sum_i (0.707\,s\,L_i)\,r_i^{2}$ for a general shape, or, for the standard rectangular C-shape:

$$
J_w \;=\; 0.707\,s\left[\frac{h\,L^{3}}{12} + \frac{L\,h^{3}}{12} + 2\,\overline{x}^{2}\,A_{\text{web}}\right]
$$

(where $\overline{x}$ is the horizontal distance of the vertical webs from the centroid).

**Step C — Combining with Direct Shear:**

For the case of an eccentric load $P$ at eccentricity $e$ producing torque $T = P \cdot e$, the **direct shear** $\tau_d$ is uniform across the whole group:

$$
\tau_d \;=\; \frac{P}{0.707\,s\,L_{\text{total}}}
$$

The **resultant** maximum shear at the *critical point* (corner of weld group, distance $r_{\max}$ from centroid) is:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\tau_d^{\,2} + \tau_t^{\,2} + 2\,\tau_d\,\tau_t\,\cos\theta}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The $\cos\theta$ term accounts for the **vector angle** between the direct shear (acting in the direction of $P$) and the radial torsional shear (acting perpendicular to the radius). At the critical corner point, this angle is typically small (often $\cos\theta \approx 1$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combined Bending and Direct Shear (Bracket Welded to Column)

**Statement:** *A cantilevered bracket is welded to a vertical column by two vertical fillet welds of length $L$ spaced distance $h$ apart. A vertical load $P$ acts at the tip of the bracket. Determine the critical weld stresses.*

**Setup:**

- The two weld lines together resist:
  - A **direct vertical shear** $= P$
  - A **bending moment** $M = P \cdot a$ where $a$ is the horizontal distance from the weld centroid to the load line.

**Step A — Section Properties of the Weld Group (Two Vertical Lines):**

For two parallel vertical weld lines, each length $L$, spaced $h$ apart, the centroid lies on the centreline. The moment of inertia about the horizontal centroidal axis:

$$
I_w \;=\; 2 \cdot \left[\frac{(0.707\,s)\,L^{3}}{12}\right] \;+\; 2 \cdot \left[(0.707\,s)\,L \cdot \left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

$$
I_w \;=\; \frac{0.707\,s\,L^{3}}{6} \;+\; \frac{0.707\,s\,L\,h^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
I_w \;=\; 0.707\,s\,L\left[\frac{L^{2}}{6} + \frac{h^{2}}{2}\right]
$$

**Step B — Bending Stress:**

Maximum bending stress occurs at the **top** (or bottom) of each weld line, where $c = L/2$:

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{M \cdot c}{I_w} \;=\; \frac{M\,(L/2)}{I_w}
$$

**Step C — Direct Vertical Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_v \;=\; \frac{P}{2 \cdot 0.707\,s\,L} \;=\; \frac{P}{1.414\,s\,L}
$$

**Step D — Principal (Maximum) Stress:**

At the extreme fibre, both bending stress (normal to throat) and shear act simultaneously:

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_v^{\,2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_v^{\,2}}\,}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **maximum principal stress** in 2D for a state of plane stress $(\sigma_b, 0, \tau_v)$. If the calculated $\sigma_{\max}$ exceeds the allowable stress of the weld metal, the design fails and leg size must be increased.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — End-to-End KTU Pattern

**Problem Statement:**
A steel bracket is welded to a column by two parallel vertical fillet welds, each **80 mm long** and spaced **120 mm apart**. The bracket carries a vertical load of **$P = 30\,\text{kN}$** acting at a horizontal eccentricity of **$e = 200\,\text{mm}$** from the centroid of the weld group. Take allowable shear stress in the weld as **$\tau_{\text{all}} = 75\,\text{MPa}$**. Determine the required leg size $s$.

**Step 1 — Direct Shear Component:**

Total weld length $L_T = 2 \times 80 = 160\,\text{mm}$. Direct shear:

$$
\tau_d \;=\; \frac{P}{0.707\,s\,L_T} \;=\; \frac{30\,000}{0.707\,s \cdot 160} \;=\; \frac{265.25}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Torsional Component:**

Torque $T = P \cdot e = 30\,000 \times 200 = 6 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N·mm}$.

Polar moment of the weld group (two lines, each at distance $h/2 = 60\,\text{mm}$ from centroid):

$$
J_w \;=\; 2 \cdot (0.707\,s \cdot 80) \cdot (60)^{2} \;=\; 2 \cdot 56.56\,s \cdot 3600 \;=\; 407\,232\,s \;\; \text{mm}^{3}\,\cdot\,\text{mm}
$$

For the critical corner point, $r_{\max} = \sqrt{(L/2)^{2} + (h/2)^{2}} = \sqrt{40^{2} + 60^{2}} = \sqrt{1600 + 3600} = \sqrt{5200} \approx 72.11\,\text{mm}$.

Torsional shear:

$$
\tau_t \;=\; \frac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J_w} \;=\; \frac{6 \times 10^{6} \times 72.11}{407\,232\,s} \;=\; \frac{1062.5}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Vector Angle $\theta$:**

For the corner point, $\tan\phi = \dfrac{h/2}{L/2} = \dfrac{60}{40} = 1.5$, so $\phi \approx 56.3°$.

The direct shear $\tau_d$ acts vertically (downward). The torsional shear $\tau_t$ acts perpendicular to the radius (i.e., at angle $90° + \phi = 146.3°$ from the vertical). The angle between them is:

$$
\theta \;=\; 180° - \phi \;\approx\; 123.7° \quad\Rightarrow\quad \cos\theta \;\approx\; -0.557
$$

**Step 4 — Resultant Maximum Shear:**

$$
\tau_{\max}^{2} \;=\; \tau_d^{2} + \tau_t^{2} + 2\,\tau_d\,\tau_t\,\cos\theta
$$

Substituting (taking $x = 1/s$):

$$
(75)^{2} \;=\; (265.25\,x)^{2} + (1062.5\,x)^{2} + 2(265.25\,x)(1062.5\,x)(-0.557)
$$

$$
5625 \;=\; x^{2}\,[70\,357 + 1\,128\,906 - 314\,028] \;=\; x^{2} \cdot 885\,235
$$

$$
x^{2} \;=\; \frac{5625}{885\,235} \;=\; 0.006355 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x \;=\; 0.0797
$$

Therefore:

$$
s \;=\; \frac{1}{0.0797} \;\approx\; 12.55\,\text{mm}
$$

Adopt the **next standard size**, $s = 13\,\text{mm}$ (or 14 mm to be safe).

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{s_{\text{required}} \approx 13\,\text{mm}}$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Welded Joint Design (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Applied Load P] --> B[Identify Weld Type]
    B --> C{Type?}
    C -- Butt --> D[Strength = Base Metal]
    C -- Fillet --> E[Strength = Throat 0.707 s]
    E --> F{Loading Mode?}
    F -- Direct Shear --> G[tau = P divided by 0.707 s L]
    F -- Eccentric --> H[tau_d plus tau_t Vector Sum]
    F -- Bending --> I[sigma_b plus tau_v Principal Stress]
    G --> J[Compare with tau_allow]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -- Safe --> K[Adopt Leg Size s]
    J -- Unsafe --> L[Increase s or L]
    L --> J
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Fillet Weld Geometry (Cross-Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plate 1 Horizontal] --> B[Root Point at 90 Degree Corner]
    C[Plate 2 Vertical] --> B
    B --> D[Hypotenuse Face of Weld]
    B -- perpendicular distance t = 0.707 s --> E[Throat Plane at 45 Degree]
    A -- leg size s --> B
    C -- leg size s --> B
    D -- length s root 2 --> F[Outer Bead Surface]
```

> The diagram conveys the **three critical points**: the **root** (corner where plates meet), the **throat plane** (the critical 45° failure plane), and the **outer bead** (visible weld surface).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Loading Modes in Machine-Element Welds

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LOADCASE [LOAD CASES ON WELDED JOINTS]
    LC1[Direct Shear Pure Parallel]
    LC2[Eccentric Shear with Torsion]
    LC3[Pure Bending No Axial]
    LC4[Combined Bending plus Vertical Shear]
    LC5[Pure Torsion no Direct Load]
    end

    LOADCASE --> EQ[Equations from Formula Sheet]
    EQ --> CHK[Check against tau_allow or sigma_allow]
    CHK --> RES[Leg Size s or Length L Selected]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Fillet vs. Butt Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Plate edges in same plane?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Strength critical?}
    Q1 -- No --> FW[Use Fillet Weld]
    Q2 -- Yes --> BW[Use Butt Weld with Full Penetration]
    Q2 -- No --> FW2[Use Fillet Weld]
```

---

## 4.5 Vector Diagram — Eccentric Loading Resultant Shear

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Direct Shear tau_d: Uniform across weld] --> C[Resultant tau_max at Corner]
    B[Torsional Shear tau_t: Radial vector] --> C
    C --> D[Compare with Allowable]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualisation Rule:** The maximum shear stress in an eccentrically loaded weld group **always occurs at the corner point farthest from the centroid** — never at the centre. This is because $r_{\max}$ is largest at the corner, maximising the torsional component.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define a fillet weld. Why is the throat thickness considered for strength calculations and not the leg size?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces at an angle, most commonly at **90°**.

The **throat thickness** $t = 0.707\,s$ is the **shortest distance** from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse face. It represents the **critical plane** inclined at 45° along which the weld is most likely to fail in shear. Since the **shear stress acts on the throat plane**, the leg size $s$ alone does not represent the load-carrying area.

Therefore:

$$
A_{\text{throat}} \;=\; t \cdot L \;=\; 0.707\,s\,L
$$

Using the leg size $s$ directly would **overestimate** the strength by about **41 %**, leading to an unsafe design.

*(Award full marks for stating the geometric relationship and the 45° plane concept.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: State any three advantages and two limitations of welded joints over riveted joints.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio** — no holes drilled, so no stress concentration.
2. **Permanent and rigid** — better sealing, useful for pressure vessels.
3. **Versatile geometry** — can join complex shapes impossible for riveting.
4. Faster and cheaper for mass production.
5. Smooth exterior surfaces; less noise.

**Limitations:**
1. **Residual stresses** and **distortion** due to uneven heating and cooling.
2. **Inspection is difficult** — internal defects need X-ray or ultrasonic testing.
3. Heat-affected zone (HAZ) may have reduced ductility/toughness.
4. Requires skilled labour and special equipment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A rectangular bracket is welded to a vertical column by means of two parallel vertical fillet welds, each of length $L = 100\,\text{mm}$, spaced $h = 150\,\text{mm}$ apart. A vertical load $P = 60\,\text{kN}$ is applied at a horizontal eccentricity $e = 250\,\text{mm}$ from the weld group centroid.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Determine the **size of the weld leg $s$** if the permissible shear stress in the weld metal is $\tau_{\text{all}} = 90\,\text{MPa}$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** If the bracket is also subjected to a horizontal force $H = 20\,\text{kN}$ at the same location, recalculate the **leg size** for combined loading.

---

### Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Pure Eccentric Vertical Load

**Step 1: Direct Shear in the Weld Group**

Total weld length $L_T = 2 \times 100 = 200\,\text{mm}$.

$$
\tau_d \;=\; \frac{P}{0.707\,s\,L_T} \;=\; \frac{60\,000}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 200} \;=\; \frac{424.34}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Torque Due to Eccentricity**

$$
T \;=\; P \cdot e \;=\; 60\,000 \times 250 \;=\; 15 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N·mm}
$$

**Step 3: Polar Moment of Weld Group**

For two parallel lines, each at $r = h/2 = 75\,\text{mm}$ from centroid:

$$
J_w \;=\; 2 \times (0.707\,s \times 100) \times (75)^{2} \;=\; 2 \times 70.7\,s \times 5625 \;=\; 795\,375\,s \;\; \text{mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 4: Critical Point Geometry**

For a corner of the weld rectangle:

$$
r_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{50^{2} + 75^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{2500 + 5625} \;=\; 90.14\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5: Torsional Shear**

$$
\tau_t \;=\; \frac{T\,r_{\max}}{J_w} \;=\; \frac{15 \times 10^{6} \times 90.14}{795\,375\,s} \;=\; \frac{1699.5}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 6: Vector Angle**

At the corner, the line from centroid to corner makes angle $\phi$ above the horizontal where $\tan\phi = \dfrac{L/2}{h/2} = \dfrac{50}{75} = 0.667$. So $\phi = 33.69°$.

Direct shear $\tau_d$ is **vertical**. Torsional shear $\tau_t$ is **perpendicular to $r_{\max}$**, i.e., at angle $90° + \phi = 123.69°$ from vertical.

Angle between $\tau_d$ and $\tau_t$:

$$
\theta \;=\; 180° - \phi \;=\; 146.31° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta \;\approx\; -0.832
$$

**Step 7: Resultant Shear Equation**

$$
\tau_{\max}^{2} \;=\; \tau_d^{\,2} + \tau_t^{\,2} + 2\,\tau_d\,\tau_t\,\cos\theta
$$

Let $x = 1/s$. Then:

$$
(90)^{2} \;=\; (424.34\,x)^{2} + (1699.5\,x)^{2} + 2(424.34\,x)(1699.5\,x)(-0.832)
$$

$$
8100 \;=\; x^{2}\,[180\,062 + 2\,888\,300 - 1\,200\,045] \;=\; x^{2} \cdot 1\,868\,317
$$

$$
x^{2} \;=\; \frac{8100}{1\,868\,317} \;=\; 0.004336 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x \;=\; 0.06585
$$

$$
s \;=\; 15.19\,\text{mm}
$$

**Adopt $s = 16\,\text{mm}$** (next preferred size).

#### Part (b) — Combined Vertical and Horizontal Eccentric Load

Now there is a horizontal force $H$ producing **additional direct shear** in the horizontal direction. Since the weld group has two vertical lines, the horizontal shear must be resisted by shear across the throat in the horizontal direction.

Horizontal direct shear (assuming $H$ acts along the line through the centroid in the plane of welds):

$$
\tau_{dH} \;=\; \frac{H}{0.707\,s\,L_T} \;=\; \frac{20\,000}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 200} \;=\; \frac{141.45}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

The vertical direct shear is unchanged: $\tau_{dV} = 424.34/s$.

These two direct shears are **perpendicular vectors** at the centroid. The **resultant direct shear magnitude** at any point is found by combining appropriately. In standard KTU practice, we compute the vector sum at the critical corner:

At the corner, the horizontal shear $\tau_{dH}$ acts along the horizontal direction, and the vertical shear $\tau_{dV}$ acts along the vertical. These are resolved along the line perpendicular to $r_{\max}$.

A simplified combined approach:

$$
\tau_d^{\text{total at corner}} \;=\; \sqrt{\tau_{dV}^{2} + \tau_{dH}^{2} + 2\,\tau_{dV}\,\tau_{dH}\,\cos\alpha}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between $\tau_{dV}$ and the radial direction to the corner. This depends on geometry; for typical problem statements we conservatively take vector sum of magnitudes.

For brevity in the exam context, after combining direct and torsional components using the same formula as before (with a modified $\tau_d$):

**Conservative estimate using total vector direct shear:**

$$
\tau_d^{\text{eff}} \;\approx\; \frac{\sqrt{P^{2} + H^{2}}}{0.707\,s\,L_T} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{60^{2} + 20^{2}} \times 1000}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 200} \;=\; \frac{63.25\,000}{141.4\,s} \;=\; \frac{447.31}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

Proceeding similarly to Part (a) with this updated $\tau_d$:

$$
\tau_t \;=\; \frac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J_w}
$$

The eccentricity due to $H$ must also be checked separately. For a clean KTU answer, the combined eccentricity produces:

$$
T_{\text{combined}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{(P\,e)^{2} + (H\,e')^{2}} \;\;\text{(if eccentricities are similar)}
$$

where $e'$ is the vertical eccentricity. Assuming same eccentricity $e = 250\,\text{mm}$:

$$
T_{\text{combined}} \;=\; \sqrt{(60 \cdot 250)^{2} + (20 \cdot 250)^{2}} \times 1000 \;=\; 63.25\,\text{kN·m} \times 250\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
T_{\text{combined}} \;=\; 15.81 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N·mm}
$$

$$
\tau_t \;=\; \frac{15.81 \times 10^{6} \times 90.14}{795\,375\,s} \;=\; \frac{1791.7}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

Solving with $\tau_{\max} = 90$ MPa and $\cos\theta \approx -0.832$:

$$
8100 \;=\; x^{2}\left[(447.31)^{2} + (1791.7)^{2} - 2(447.31)(1791.7)(0.832)\right]
$$

$$
8100 \;=\; x^{2}\,[200\,086 + 3\,210\,190 - 1\,334\,076] \;=\; x^{2} \cdot 2\,076\,200
$$

$$
x^{2} \;=\; 0.003902 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x \;=\; 0.0625
$$

$$
s \;=\; 16.0\,\text{mm}
$$

**Adopt $s = 18\,\text{mm}$** for safe design under combined loading.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A circular shaft of diameter $100\,\text{mm}$ is welded to a flat plate by a fillet weld around the circumference. The shaft transmits a torque of $T = 5\,\text{kN·m}$ and an axial tensile force of $F = 40\,\text{kN}$.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **required leg size $s$** of the fillet weld. Take allowable shear stress in weld = **$80\,\text{MPa}$**.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** If the joint is also subjected to a **bending moment of $M = 3\,\text{kN·m}$** about a horizontal axis, find the **maximum principal stress in the throat**.

---

### Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Combined Torque and Axial Force

**Circumferential Weld Geometry:**

Mean radius of weld $r_m = d/2 = 50\,\text{mm}$. Weld circumference:

$$
L \;=\; 2\pi r_m \;=\; 2\pi \times 50 \;=\; 314.16\,\text{mm}
$$

**Direct (Axial) Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{\text{axial}} \;=\; \frac{F}{0.707\,s\,L} \;=\; \frac{40\,000}{0.707\,s \times 314.16} \;=\; \frac{179.86}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Torsional Shear Stress:**

The polar moment of the thin circular weld ring:

$$
J_w \;=\; 2\pi\,r_m^{3} \cdot (0.707\,s) \;=\; 2\pi \times 50^{3} \times 0.707\,s \;=\; 555\,360\,s \;\; \text{mm}^{4}
$$

$$
\tau_{\text{torsion}} \;=\; \frac{T\,r_m}{J_w} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 10^{6} \times 50}{555\,360\,s} \;=\; \frac{450.16}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Resultant (axial and torsional are perpendicular at any point on the ring):**

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \sqrt{\tau_{\text{axial}}^{2} + \tau_{\text{torsion}}^{2}}
$$

Setting equal to allowable:

$$
\tau_{\text{all}}^{2} \;=\; \tau_{\text{axial}}^{2} + \tau_{\text{torsion}}^{2}
$$

$$
(80)^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{179.86}{s}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{450.16}{s}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
6400 \cdot s^{2} \;=\; 179.86^{2} + 450.16^{2} \;=\; 32\,350 + 202\,644 \;=\; 234\,994
$$

$$
s^{2} \;=\; \frac{234\,994}{6400} \;=\; 36.72 \quad\Rightarrow\quad s \;=\; 6.06\,\text{mm}
$$

**Adopt $s = 8\,\text{mm}$** (minimum practical leg size for a 100 mm shaft is often governed by plate thickness, not by strength).

#### Part (b) — Add Bending Moment

The bending moment produces **bending stress (normal)** on the throat at the extreme fibres (top and bottom of the weld):

**Bending Stress:**

The moment of inertia of the weld ring about the horizontal centroidal axis:

$$
I_w \;=\; \pi\,r_m^{3} \cdot (0.707\,s) \;=\; \pi \times 50^{3} \times 0.707\,s \;=\; 277\,680\,s \;\; \text{mm}^{4}
$$

Maximum bending stress at top/bottom ($c = r_m = 50\,\text{mm}$):

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{M\,c}{I_w} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{6} \times 50}{277\,680\,s} \;=\; \frac{540.20}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Shear at Critical Point (Top of Weld):**

At the topmost point of the weld ring, **axial shear** $\tau_{\text{axial}}$ is vertical, and **torsional shear** is also vertical (radially outward at $r_{\max}$). Therefore, **at the top point, both shears add algebraically** (they are aligned):

$$
\tau_{\text{total}} \;=\; \tau_{\text{axial}} + \tau_{\text{torsion}} \;=\; \frac{179.86}{s} + \frac{450.16}{s} \;=\; \frac{630.02}{s} \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

**Maximum Principal Stress at Top of Weld:**

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{\text{total}}^{2}}
$$

Using $s = 8\,\text{mm}$:

$$
\sigma_b \;=\; \frac{540.20}{8} \;=\; 67.53 \;\; \text{MPa}, \qquad \tau_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{630.02}{8} \;=\; 78.75 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{67.53}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{67.53}{2}\right)^{2} + (78.75)^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;=\; 33.77 + \sqrt{1140.6 + 6201.6} \;=\; 33.77 + \sqrt{7342.2} \;=\; 33.77 + 85.69
$$

$$
\sigma_{\max} \;\approx\; 119.46 \;\; \text{MPa}
$$

> **Interpretation:** If allowable tensile stress of weld = $0.5 \sigma_u$, and assuming $\sigma_u = 410\,\text{MPa}$ for mild steel, $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 205\,\text{MPa}$. The design is **safe**.

**Final Result:** $\sigma_{\max} \approx 119.5\,\text{MPa}$ — design is safe under combined loading.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Weld Problems:**
>
> 1. **Using leg size $s$ instead of throat thickness $0.707\,s$** in the area calculation. This single error invalidates the entire solution.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 2** when two parallel weld lines resist the load (the throat area of *each* line is $0.707\,s\,L$, and there are *two* of them).
> 3. **Computing the polar moment of inertia using the rectangle formula $J = bh^3/12$** for the *welded area* instead of the line formula $J = \sum (0.707\,s\,L_i)\,r_i^{2}$ for the *weld group*.
> 4. **Taking $\cos\theta = +1$** in the resultant shear formula. The angle is typically obtuse, so $\cos\theta$ is **negative** — a positive value over-predicts stress and produces an unsafe (over-designed) but mathematically **wrong** result.
> 5. **Choosing the centroid as the critical point.** The maximum stress in an eccentric-loaded weld group is at the **corner farthest from the centroid**, not the centre.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Welded Joints**

- **Fillet weld** = triangle in cross-section; **throat** = the 45° plane; **throat thickness** $t = 0.707\,s$.
- **Butt weld** = plate edges in same plane; strength computed on **plate thickness** $t$.
- **Direct shear stress** in fillet weld: $\tau = P/(0.707\,s\,L)$.
- **Eccentric load** = combination of **direct shear** + **torsional shear** on weld group.
- **Polar moment of weld group** (open thin-walled): $J_w = \sum (0.707\,s\,L_i)\,r_i^{2}$.
- **Critical point** in eccentric load = **corner** with **maximum $r$** from centroid.
- **Resultant shear at critical point:** $\tau_{\max}^{2} = \tau_d^{2} + \tau_t^{2} + 2\,\tau_d\,\tau_t\,\cos\theta$ (vector sum).
- **Bending + shear:** compute principal stress using $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_b/2 + \sqrt{(\sigma_b/2)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$.
- **Allowable shear stress (mild steel, static):** $\tau_{\text{all}} \approx 0.30\,\sigma_u$; for dynamic: $0.20\,\sigma_u$.
- **Always use throat thickness $0.707\,s$** — *never* the leg size alone for shear area.
- **For circular/shaft welds:** use $J_w = 2\pi r_m^{3}(0.707\,s)$ and $I_w = \pi r_m^{3}(0.707\,s)$.
- **Round up** to nearest standard leg size: $6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20$ mm.
- **Code reference:** IS 822 (Indian), AWS D1.1 (American), Eurocode 3 (European).
- **Maximum permissible leg size** = plate thickness (to avoid burning through).
- **Minimum weld length** $\geq 4 \times$ leg size (or 40 mm) to avoid end-arc defects.
- **End returns** are mandatory for welds carrying dynamic load — add $2s$ at each end.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soldered and Glued Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Soldered and Glued Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — SOLDERED AND GLUED JOINTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Soldered Joints?

A **soldered joint** is a permanent mechanical fastening formed by introducing a **filler metal (solder)** whose melting point is **below 450 °C** (per ISO 857 standard) into the gap between two close-fitting base metals. The base metals are **not melted**; only the solder melts, wets the surfaces, and metallurgically bonds to them via diffusion and adhesion. Soldered joints are commonly used in electronics, sheet-metal assemblies, plumbing, and light-load machine housings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Soldering is a metal-joining process in which a filler metal (solder) is heated to its melting temperature (typically 180 °C – 450 °C) and caused to flow over the base metals, forming a metallurgical bond without melting the base materials. The resulting joint relies on **shear strength of the solder fillet** and the **wetted interface area**.*

### 1.2 What are Glued (Adhesive-Bonded) Joints?

A **glued joint** (also called an **adhesive-bonded joint**) is a permanent joint formed by the **chemical adhesion and mechanical interlocking** of a **non-metallic polymer adhesive** (epoxy, phenolic, cyanoacrylate, polyurethane, etc.) between two adherends. No melting of base materials occurs; instead, the adhesive cures by chemical reaction, solvent loss, or cooling. Such joints are extensively used in aerospace panels, automotive body panels, laminated structures, and composite assemblies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Sticky Tape" Model:**
> Think of a glued joint like a piece of double-sided tape sticking two pieces of paper together. The tape (adhesive) does not melt the paper (adherend), but holds them by surface adhesion. If you **pull the paper sideways (shear)**, the tape resists strongly; if you **peel it from the edge (cleavage/peel)**, it fails easily. Similarly, a solder joint is like a thin metallic "melted glue" that is **strong in shear but weak in tension perpendicular to the bond line** — this anisotropy is the **central design concern** in both joint types.

### 1.3 Classification of Soldered Joints

| Category | Examples | Filler Material | Strength | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft soldered** | Tin–Lead (60/40, 63/37), Lead-free (SAC) | Sn-Pb / Sn-Ag-Cu | 30 – 70 MPa | Electronics, plumbing |
| **Hard soldered (Brazing)** | Brass, Silver solder | Cu-Zn / Ag-Cu-Zn | 200 – 500 MPa | Cutting tools, refrigeration |
| **Brazed (above 450 °C)** | Copper brazing, Bronze | Cu, Cu-Sn | 200 – 400 MPa | Heavy machinery |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme question papers, **soft-soldered (tin-lead) joints** are the **most frequently tested** for design calculation, because their low strength makes failure-mode analysis straightforward.

### 1.4 Classification of Adhesive Joints

| Type | Configuration | Load Transfer | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Lap** | Overlap of two adherends | Shear | Sheet-metal splicing |
| **Double Lap** | Symmetric overlap | Shear, balanced | Aerospace skin joints |
| **Butt Joint** | End-to-end adherends | Tension / Cleavage | Rod splicing |
| **Scarf Joint** | Angled taper | Combined shear-tension | High-strength tube joints |
| **Strap / Double-Butt** | Two straps on either side | Shear | Pipe repairs |

### 1.5 Why Study These Joints in Machine Design?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:**
> 1. **Light-weight structures** — Adhesive joints avoid stress concentrations caused by drilled holes (rivets/bolts).
> 2. **Dissimilar material joining** — Soldering joins copper to brass, aluminium to steel, etc., where welding is impossible.
> 3. **Electrical continuity** — Solder joints provide both **mechanical bond** and **electrical conduction** in PCBs.
> 4. **Vibration damping** — Adhesive layers damp vibrations, unlike rigid fasteners.
> 5. **Corrosion resistance** — Closed joints exclude moisture and atmospheric attack.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stresses in Soldered Joints

When a load **F** is transmitted through a soldered overlap joint, the load is carried entirely by the **solder fillet in shear**. The fundamental assumption (valid for ductile soft solders) is that **shear stress is uniformly distributed** over the wetted area.

#### 2.1.1 Lap (Parallel) Soldered Joint

The simplest and most common configuration. Two plates overlap by a length **l**, and solder fills the small gap between them.

**Load-transfer mechanism:**
1. Load enters Plate 1 from the left.
2. Transferred to solder layer through **shear** at the interface.
3. Carried across the overlap length **l**.
4. Exits into Plate 2 on the right.

**Average shear stress:**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{b \cdot l}$$

where:
- **F** = axial tensile load (N)
- **b** = width of the plate (mm)
- **l** = length of overlap (mm)

**Peak shear stress (at the ends of overlap)** is higher than average due to **strain mismatch** between adherends. For ductile solders, the peak is approximately:

$$\tau_{peak} \approx 1.5 \cdot \tau_{avg}$$

#### 2.1.2 Butt Soldered Joint

Two plates joined end-to-end with a thin solder layer. The load is purely tensile, and the joint fails by **tensile rupture of the solder**.

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{b \cdot t}$$

where **t** = plate thickness.

> [!WARNING]
> Butt joints are **structurally weak** because the **full cross-section** must carry the load in tension, and the solder's tensile strength is low. **Always prefer lap joints** when possible.

#### 2.1.3 Tapered (Scarf) Soldered Joint

To reduce the peak shear stress at the ends of an overlap, the plate ends are **tapered** so that the cross-sectional area varies linearly along the overlap. This produces a **nearly uniform shear stress distribution**.

**Optimum taper angle:**

$$\tan \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{solder}}{\sigma_{plate}}}$$

where:
- **β** = taper half-angle
- **τ_solder** = allowable shear stress of solder
- **σ_plate** = allowable tensile stress of plate

### 2.2 Stresses in Adhesive (Glued) Joints

Unlike solder, an **elastic adhesive** cannot plastically yield to redistribute stress. The **Volkersen analysis (1938)** is the cornerstone of adhesive-joint design.

#### 2.2.1 Single-Lap Adhesive Joint — Volkersen Theory

For a single-lap joint with **adherends of equal stiffness**, the **shear stress distribution** along the overlap is non-uniform:

$$\tau(x) = \frac{F}{b} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{\cosh(\omega x / l)}{\sinh(\omega / 2)} - \frac{(1 - \psi) \sinh(\omega x / l)}{(1 + \psi) \cosh(\omega / 2)} \right]$$

where:
- **x** = position along the overlap (−l/2 to +l/2)
- **l** = overlap length
- **b** = width
- **t₁, t₂** = adherend thicknesses
- **E₁, E₂** = adherend Young's moduli
- **G_a** = adhesive shear modulus
- **t_a** = adhesive thickness
- **ψ = t₁E₁ / (t₂E₂)** = adherend stiffness ratio
- **ω** = non-dimensional overlap parameter

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{G_a}{t_a} \cdot l^2 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right)}$$

**Peak shear stress at the ends (x = ±l/2):**

$$\tau_{peak} = \frac{F}{b \cdot l} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2} \cdot \left[ \coth\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) + \frac{1 - \psi}{1 + \psi} \cdot \tanh\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** Increasing overlap length **does NOT proportionally increase joint strength** because the **peak stress at the ends** grows. The optimum overlap is typically **l ≈ 25 – 50 × t (plate thickness)**. Beyond this, additional overlap gives diminishing returns.

#### 2.2.2 Tapered Adhesive Joint (Goland & Reissner Extension)

By tapering the adherend ends (similar to soldered scarfed joints), the **stress concentration is reduced** and **failure load increases by 30 – 60 %** compared to a uniform-thickness lap joint.

### 2.3 Comparison: Soldered vs. Adhesive Joints

| Property | Soldered Joint | Adhesive (Glued) Joint |
|---|---|---|
| Filler material | Metallic alloy | Polymer resin |
| Bond mechanism | Metallurgical wetting | Chemical adhesion + mechanical interlocking |
| Melting of base metal? | **No** | **No** |
| Typical strength (MPa) | 30 – 70 (soft); 200 – 500 (brazed) | 10 – 40 (typical epoxy) |
| Temperature resistance | High (up to solder m.p.) | Moderate (curie-dependent) |
| Stress concentration | Moderate (ductile yield) | High (elastic) |
| Electrical conduction | **Yes** | **No** (insulating) |
| Inspection | Easy (visual) | Difficult (NDT required) |
| Reversibility | De-soldering possible | Difficult |

### 2.4 ⚡ KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only** reference you need for solving Module 2 problems in the KTU University Exam. Memorize it.

| # | Joint Type | Governing Equation | Variables | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lap (uniform shear) | $\tau = F / (b \cdot l)$ | F, b, l | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Butt (tension) | $\sigma_t = F / (b \cdot t)$ | F, b, t | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Required overlap length | $l = F / (b \cdot \tau_{allow})$ | F, b, τ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Strength of joint | $F = b \cdot l \cdot \tau_{allow}$ | b, l, τ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Taper angle (solder) | $\tan \beta = \sqrt{\tau_{solder} / \sigma_{plate}}$ | β, τ, σ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Volkersen overlap parameter | $\omega = l \sqrt{(G_a / t_a)(1/E_1 t_1 + 1/E_2 t_2)}$ | All | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Peak shear (adhesive) | $\tau_{peak} \approx \tau_{avg} \cdot (\omega/2) \coth(\omega/2)$ | ω | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Optimum overlap | $l_{opt} \approx (25 \text{ to } 50) \cdot t_{plate}$ | t | ⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Factor of safety | $FoS = \tau_{ult} / \tau_{allow}$ | τ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Joint efficiency | $\eta = (\sigma_{joint} / \sigma_{plate}) \times 100 \,\%$ | σ | ⭐⭐ |

> **Standard values to remember:**
> - **Soft solder (60/40 Sn-Pb):** τ_ult ≈ 40 – 55 MPa, τ_allow ≈ 15 – 20 MPa
> - **Silver solder:** τ_ult ≈ 200 – 250 MPa
> - **Epoxy adhesive:** τ_ult ≈ 15 – 30 MPa
> - **Pure copper (annealed):** σ_ult ≈ 210 MPa, σ_allow ≈ 105 MPa

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Shear Stress in a Single-Lap Soldered Joint

**Given:** A lap joint of width **b**, overlap length **l**, transmitting axial load **F**.
**Find:** Average shear stress **τ** in the solder.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of Plate 1**

Consider Plate 1 alone. It is loaded at the left end by force **F** and supported on the right by the **shear force at the solder interface**. The solder layer transmits a uniformly distributed shear stress **τ** over the area **b × l**.

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium of the Solder Layer**

For the solder fillet to be in static equilibrium, the integral of shear stress over the bonded area must equal the applied load:

$$\int_0^l \tau(x) \cdot b \, dx = F$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Uniform Shear Assumption**

Assuming the shear stress is constant (justified for ductile soft solders which yield locally and redistribute load):

$$\int_0^l \tau \cdot b \, dx = \tau \cdot b \cdot l = F$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Shear Stress**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{b \cdot l}$$

> ✅ This is the **fundamental design equation** for soldered lap joints. KTU expects you to derive this in the ESE if asked for "show that the average shear stress in a soldered overlap is F/(b×l)."

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Overlap Length for a Given Load

**Given:** Load **F**, width **b**, allowable shear stress **τ_allow**.
**Find:** Minimum overlap length **l_min**.

**Step 1 — Start from the basic shear equation**

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{F}{b \cdot l}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for l**

$$l = \frac{F}{b \cdot \tau_{allow}}$$

**Step 3 — Add Factor of Safety Margin**

For design, use the **ultimate shear stress** divided by the factor of safety **N**:

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{\tau_{ult}}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Final Design Equation**

$$l_{min} = \frac{F \cdot N}{b \cdot \tau_{ult}}$$

**Numerical Example:**

Given: **F = 8 kN**, **b = 30 mm**, solder ultimate shear stress **τ_ult = 40 MPa**, **N = 4**.

**Step 1:** Compute allowable stress

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute minimum overlap

$$l_{min} = \frac{8000 \, \text{N}}{30 \, \text{mm} \times 10 \, \text{N/mm}^2} = \frac{8000}{300} = 26.67 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Round up to standard value

$$l_{min} = 27 \, \text{mm} \approx 30 \, \text{mm (standard)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Taper Angle for a Scarfed Soldered Joint

**Given:** A plate of uniform thickness **t**, overlap length **l**, taper half-angle **β**.
**Objective:** Find **β** that produces uniform shear stress in the solder.

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area Variation**

For a linearly tapered adherend, the cross-sectional area at distance **x** from the start of the taper is:

$$A(x) = b \cdot t(x) = b \cdot t \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x}{l}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Tensile Stress in the Plate at Position x**

The plate carries the full load **F** (minus the load already transferred to the solder). At position **x**, the un-transferred load is:

$$F(x) = F - \int_0^x \tau \cdot b \, dx'$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Across the Solder Plane**

For uniform shear stress **τ**, the tensile stress in the plate is:

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{F - \tau \cdot b \cdot x}{b \cdot t(x)}$$

**Step 4 — Set Condition for Uniformity**

For both τ and σ to be constant, the geometry must satisfy:

$$\frac{t(x)}{l - x} = \text{const} = \tan \beta$$

**Step 5 — Geometric Relation**

From the geometry of the taper:

$$\tan \beta = \frac{t_{plate}}{\sigma_{plate}} \cdot \frac{\tau_{solder}}{1} = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{solder}}{\sigma_{plate}}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\tan \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{solder}}{\sigma_{plate}}}}$$

> 📌 **KTU Board Tip:** When the taper angle satisfies this equation, the shear stress in the solder and the tensile stress in the plate reach their allowable values **simultaneously**, giving the most efficient material usage.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Volkersen's Peak Shear Stress (Adhesive Joint)

**Step 1 — Differential Equation of Adherend**

The axial strain in adherend 1 varies due to the shear load transferred through the adhesive:

$$\frac{d\sigma_1}{dx} = \frac{\tau(x)}{t_1}$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility of Adherends**

The relative displacement between adherends is related to the adhesive shear strain:

$$\Delta u(x) = \int_0^x \frac{F - b \int_0^{x'} \tau \, dx''}{b E_1 t_1} dx' - \int_0^x \frac{b \int_0^{x'} \tau \, dx''}{b E_2 t_2} dx'$$

**Step 3 — Adhesive Constitutive Law**

The adhesive shear stress is proportional to the relative displacement divided by its thickness:

$$\tau(x) = \frac{G_a}{t_a} \cdot \Delta u(x)$$

**Step 4 — Combine to Form 2nd-Order ODE**

Differentiating twice and applying boundary conditions:

$$\frac{d^2 \tau}{dx^2} - \lambda^2 \tau = 0$$

where:

$$\lambda^2 = \frac{G_a}{t_a}\left(\frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Non-Dimensional Overlap Parameter**

Define:

$$\omega = l \cdot \lambda = l \sqrt{\frac{G_a}{t_a}\left(\frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Solution with Boundary Conditions**

Solving the ODE with **τ(±l/2) at free ends** (no external shear at overlap edges), and **equal adherends (ψ = 1)**:

$$\tau_{peak} = \tau_{avg} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2} \cdot \coth\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Limiting Behavior**

- For **short overlaps (ω → 0):** $\coth(\omega/2) \to \infty$ → severe stress concentration.
- For **long overlaps (ω → ∞):** $\coth(\omega/2) \to 1$ → stress becomes uniform, but absolute strength is low.
- **Practical design range:** **ω ≈ 1.5 – 2.5** (peak-to-average ratio ≈ 1.2 – 1.6).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Parameter | Uniform Lap Solder | Tapered Lap Solder | Uniform Lap Adhesive | Tapered Lap Adhesive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stress distribution | Uniform (approx.) | Uniform | **Highly non-uniform** | Nearly uniform |
| Peak stress location | Edge of overlap | Distributed | **Ends of overlap** | Distributed |
| Failure load (normalized) | 1.0× | 1.3 – 1.5× | 1.0× | 1.5 – 2.0× |
| Design equation | $\tau = F/(bl)$ | $\tan \beta = \sqrt{\tau/\sigma}$ | $\tau_{peak} = \tau_{avg} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2}\coth(\omega/2)$ | Goland-Reissner |
| KTU mark weight | **3 marks** | **5 marks** | **5 marks** | **7 marks** (Part B) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Soldered Joint Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Load F] --> B[Select Joint Type: Lap or Butt]
    B --> C{Is Lap Joint?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Choose Width b from Standard Plates]
    D --> E[Select Solder Material and tau_ult]
    E --> F[Compute Allowable Shear Stress tau_allow = tau_ult / N]
    F --> G[Compute Minimum Overlap l_min = F / b tau_allow]
    G --> H{Is Stress Concentration Concern?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply Tapered Scarf Joint]
    H -- No --> J[Adopt Uniform Lap]
    I --> K[Compute Taper Angle tan beta = sqrt tau / sigma]
    J --> L[Validate: tau_peak approx 1.5 tau_avg]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Check Factor of Safety against Yield]
    M --> N[Final Design Output: l, b, beta]
    C -- No --> O[Butt Joint Design]
    O --> P[Check sigma_t = F / b t less than sigma_allow]
    P --> N
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Adhesive Joint Design Workflow (Volkersen Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Load F, Adherend Geometry] --> B[Compute Stiffness Ratio psi = t1E1 / t2E2]
    B --> C[Compute Adherend Compliances]
    C --> D[Compute Overlap Parameter omega = l sqrt G_a t_a times 1 E1t1 plus 1 E2t2]
    D --> E{Check omega Range 1.5 to 2.5}
    E -- No --> F[Adjust Overlap Length l]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Compute Average Shear tau_avg = F / b l]
    G --> H[Compute Peak Shear tau_peak = tau_avg times omega over 2 times coth omega over 2]
    H --> I{Tau_peak less than tau_allow?}
    I -- No --> J[Increase l or Use Tapered Joint]
    J --> D
    I -- Yes --> K[Compute Peeling Stress sigma_peel at Ends]
    K --> L{Sigma_peel less than sigma_adhesive_allow?}
    L -- No --> M[Add Spew Fillet or Use Double Lap]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N[Final Joint Approved]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Soldered / Glued Joint] --> F1[Shear Failure in Fillet]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a soldered joint. List the two main types of solder used in engineering practice and give one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **soldered joint** is a permanent joint formed by introducing a **filler metal (solder)** with melting point **below 450 °C** between two close-fitting base metals, which themselves are not melted. The solder wets the surfaces and forms a **metallurgical bond** upon solidification.
>
> **Two main types:**
> 1. **Soft solder (e.g., 60 % Sn – 40 % Pb):** melting point ≈ 190 °C; **application** — printed circuit board (PCB) component soldering.
> 2. **Hard solder / Brazing alloy (e.g., brass, silver solder):** melting point 600 – 800 °C; **application** — joining of refrigeration copper tubes, carbide tool tips.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for types, 1 mark for application)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain the term "peel stress" in an adhesive joint. Why is it critical in design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Peel stress** is the **tensile stress acting at the edge of an adhesive overlap** due to the eccentricity of the load path. When a single-lap joint is loaded, the adherends rotate slightly because their centroidal axes are offset, causing a **concentrated tensile (peel) stress at the end of the overlap**.
>
> **Why it is critical:**
> - Adhesives have **very low peel strength** (typically < 10 % of their shear strength).
> - The peel stress causes **premature failure** at the joint ends, often at loads far below the average shear capacity.
> - **Design countermeasures:** use **double-lap joints** (eliminate eccentricity), add **spew fillets**, or use **tapered adherends**.
>
> *(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for criticality)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Soldered & Glued Joints:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of safety (N):** Many students use ultimate stress directly. **Always divide by N to get allowable stress** unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. **−1 mark deduction** if missed.
> 2. **Mixing up shear and tensile stresses:** A **lap joint** fails in **shear**; a **butt joint** fails in **tension**. Writing τ = F/(bt) for a lap joint is **wrong** and loses 2 marks.
> 3. **No unit consistency:** Convert all to **N and mm** (giving MPa = N/mm²). Mixing kN with mm² and reporting GPa is a common error.
> 4. **Skipping the free-body diagram (FBD):** For derivations, the **FBD and equilibrium equation** carry **1 – 2 marks** by themselves. Always draw and label.
> 5. **For Volkersen-type questions:** Students often **omit the ω parameter** definition. Examiners specifically look for it. **−2 marks** if not derived.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state at least **2 assumptions** (e.g., uniform shear, no bending, elastic adherends).

---

#### **🟢 PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`**

**A single-lap soldered joint is used to connect two mild-steel plates, each of width b = 40 mm and thickness t = 6 mm. The joint must transmit an axial tensile load F = 12 kN. The ultimate shear strength of the 60/40 tin-lead solder is τ_u = 42 MPa, and a factor of safety of 4 is to be used. The ultimate tensile strength of the plate material is σ_u = 380 MPa.**

**(a) Determine the minimum required overlap length of the joint.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) If the same joint is redesigned using a tapered (scarfed) configuration, calculate the optimum taper angle, and comment on the improvement in strength.** **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Given data**

- Load **F = 12 kN = 12 000 N**
- Width **b = 40 mm**
- Plate thickness **t = 6 mm** (not directly needed for lap shear)
- Ultimate solder shear stress **τ_u = 42 MPa = 42 N/mm²**
- Factor of safety **N = 4**

**Step 2 — Compute allowable shear stress (1 mark)**

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{\tau_u}{N} = \frac{42}{4} = 10.5 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the basic lap-shear equation (1 mark)**

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{F}{b \cdot l_{min}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for l_min (2 marks)**

$$l_{min} = \frac{F}{b \cdot \tau_{allow}} = \frac{12\,000}{40 \times 10.5} = \frac{12\,000}{420} = 28.57 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply practical design margin (1 mark)**

Round up to next standard value: **l_min = 30 mm**

**Step 6 — Verify plate tensile stress (1 mark)**

$$\sigma_{plate} = \frac{F}{b \cdot t} = \frac{12\,000}{40 \times 6} = 50 \, \text{N/mm}^2 \ll \sigma_{allow} = 380/4 = 95 \, \text{N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Comment (1 mark)**

The plate is **safe** in tension; the joint is **governed by solder shear**. Use **l = 30 mm**.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Recall the optimum taper-angle formula (1 mark)**

$$\tan \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_{solder}}{\sigma_{plate}}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify allowable stresses (1 mark)**

Using **allowable** values (since the same FoS applies to both):

$$\tau_{solder} = 10.5 \, \text{N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_{plate} = 95 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute ratio (1 mark)**

$$\frac{\tau_{solder}}{\sigma_{plate}} = \frac{10.5}{95} = 0.1105$$

**Step 4 — Take square root (1 mark)**

$$\tan \beta = \sqrt{0.1105} = 0.3325$$

**Step 5 — Compute angle (1 mark)**

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.3325) = 18.39^\circ \approx 18.4^\circ$$

**Step 6 — Comment on improvement (2 marks)**

- In a **uniform lap**, peak shear stress is ~ **1.5 × average** at the overlap ends, leading to premature failure.
- In a **tapered joint**, the **cross-section is reduced near the high-stress ends**, which **lowers the peak stress** and **equalizes** the shear distribution.
- **Result:** Tapered joints can carry **30 – 50 % higher load** for the same overlap, OR achieve the same strength with a **shorter overlap**, saving material and weight.

---

#### **🟢 PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`**

**A single-lap adhesive joint bonds two aluminium adherends, each of width b = 25 mm and thickness t₁ = t₂ = 1.6 mm, with an overlap length l = 20 mm. The adhesive is an epoxy with shear modulus G_a = 800 MPa and bondline thickness t_a = 0.1 mm. The adherend Young's modulus is E₁ = E₂ = 70 GPa. The applied tensile load is F = 4 kN.**

**(a) Calculate the Volkersen overlap parameter ω and the average shear stress in the adhesive. Comment on whether the design is safe if the adhesive allowable shear stress is τ_allow = 12 MPa.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Suggest TWO design modifications to improve the joint strength, with quantitative justification.** **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the overlap parameter ω (2 marks)**

$$\omega = l \sqrt{\frac{G_a}{t_a} \left( \frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right)}$$

Substituting values:

$$\omega = 20 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{800}{0.1} \left( \frac{1}{70\,000 \times 1.6} + \frac{1}{70\,000 \times 1.6} \right)}$$

$$\omega = 20 \cdot \sqrt{8000 \times \left( 8.93 \times 10^{-6} + 8.93 \times 10^{-6} \right)}$$

$$\omega = 20 \cdot \sqrt{8000 \times 1.786 \times 10^{-5}} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{0.1429} = 20 \times 0.378 = 7.56$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average shear stress (1 mark)**

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{F}{b \cdot l} = \frac{4\,000}{25 \times 20} = \frac{4\,000}{500} = 8 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute peak shear stress using Volkersen (2 marks)**

For equal adherends (ψ = 1):

$$\tau_{peak} = \tau_{avg} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2} \cdot \coth\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

$$\frac{\omega}{2} = \frac{7.56}{2} = 3.78, \quad \coth(3.78) = \frac{\cosh(3.78)}{\sinh(3.78)} = \frac{21.81}{21.51} \approx 1.0138$$

$$\tau_{peak} = 8 \times \frac{7.56}{2} \times 1.0138 = 8 \times 3.78 \times 1.0138 \approx 30.66 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compare with allowable (1 mark)**

$$\tau_{peak} = 30.66 \, \text{MPa} \gg \tau_{allow} = 12 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark)**

> ❌ **The joint is NOT safe.** The peak shear stress at the overlap ends is **2.5 times the allowable value**. The design will fail at the **joint ends** before reaching the design load.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]**

**Modification 1 — Use a Double-Lap (Scarf) Joint (3.5 marks)**

- **Effect:** Eliminates the load-path eccentricity, reducing the **peel stress by 70 – 80 %** and halving the shear stress in each bondline.
- **Quantitative result:** Each bondline carries only **F/2 = 2 kN**, so:
  $$\tau_{avg,new} = \frac{2\,000}{25 \times 20} = 4 \, \text{MPa}, \quad \tau_{peak,new} = 4 \times 3.78 \times 1.0138 \approx 15.3 \, \text{MPa}$$
- Although still slightly above allowable, this is much closer to safe design. Combined with a **shorter overlap (l = 10 mm)** to reduce ω:
  $$\omega_{new} = 10 \times 0.378 = 3.78, \quad \tau_{peak} = \frac{F/2}{25 \times 10} \times \frac{3.78}{2} \times \coth(1.89)$$
  $$\tau_{peak} = 4 \times 1.89 \times 1.075 \approx 8.13 \, \text{MPa} < 12 \, \text{MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Modification 2 — Taper the Adherend Ends (3.5 marks)**

- **Effect:** Geometrically redistributes stiffness so that the **strain mismatch** between adherends is reduced, lowering the peak shear concentration.
- **Quantitative result:** For a **30 % taper over the overlap**, the peak-to-average ratio drops from **~ 3.8** to **~ 1.8**, giving:
  $$\tau_{peak,new} \approx 8 \times 1.8 = 14.4 \, \text{MPa (slightly high)}$$
  Further increasing taper to **50 %** brings the ratio down to **~ 1.3**:
  $$\tau_{peak,new} \approx 8 \times 1.3 = 10.4 \, \text{MPa} < 12 \, \text{MPa} \quad \checkmark$$
- **Final recommendation:** Combine both modifications (double-lap + tapered ends) for **maximum strength** and **deflection stiffness**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Module 2 — Soldered & Glued Joints: Quick-Recall Checklist**

#### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Soldered joint:** Metallic filler, melting point **< 450 °C**, base metal not melted.
- **Adhesive joint:** Polymer filler, **chemical adhesion**, no melting of adherends.
- **Lap joint** carries load in **shear**; **butt joint** carries load in **tension**.
- **Uniform shear** assumption is valid for **ductile soft solders**; **not** for elastic adhesives.

#### 📐 Master Equations (Must Memorize)
- Lap shear: $\tau = F/(b \cdot l)$
- Required overlap: $l = FN/(b \cdot \tau_u)$
- Taper angle: $\tan \beta = \sqrt{\tau / \sigma}$
- Volkersen parameter: $\omega = l \sqrt{(G_a/t_a)(1/E_1 t_1 + 1/E_2 t_2)}$
- Peak adhesive shear: $\tau_{peak} = \tau_{avg} \cdot (\omega/2) \coth(\omega/2)$

#### ⚠️ Common Failure Modes
- **Soldered:** Shear in fillet (most common), interfacial debonding, plate yielding.
- **Adhesive:** **Peel at ends** (most critical), shear in mid-bond, cohesive failure.

#### 🛠️ Design Best Practices
- **Always use factor of safety N = 3 – 5** for both joint types.
- **Optimum overlap:** $l \approx 25 - 50 \times t_{plate}$ — more is wasteful.
- **Taper adherends** for joints where ω > 3.
- **Double-lap configuration** eliminates peel stress.
- **Surface preparation** (degreasing, abrasion) increases joint strength by **30 – 50 %**.

#### 📊 KTU 2024 Exam Weightage
- **Part A (3 marks):** Definitions, types, applications — **2 questions expected**.
- **Part B (14 marks):** Numerical design + taper-angle derivation — **1 question guaranteed**.
- **Internal choice:** Always provide **two alternatives** in Part B.

#### 🎯 Top 5 Things to Revise Night-Before Exam
1. Derivation of $l_{min} = FN/(b \cdot \tau_u)$.
2. Taper angle formula: $\tan \beta = \sqrt{\tau_{solder}/\sigma_{plate}}$.
3. Volkersen parameter definition and physical meaning.
4. Difference between **lap** and **butt** joints (load transfer mode).
5. Why **peel stress** is the killer of adhesive joints.

---

> ✅ **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — SOLDERED AND GLUED JOINTS**
> *Next recommended topic: Module 2 — Welded Joints (Butt and Fillet).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Riveted Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Riveted Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RIVETED JOINT (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **permanent fastening** formed by inserting a ductile metal pin (called a **rivet**) through precisely aligned holes in two or more overlapping/butting members, followed by **plastic deformation** of the protruding tail to form a second head. The resulting joint resists transverse (shear) and tensile loads through the combined action of shear, bearing, and friction at the rivet–plate interface.

In the language of machine design (per **Shigley, V.B. Bhandari, and the KTU PENST744 syllabus**), a riveted joint is classified under **non-permanent → permanent mechanical joints**, alongside welded, brazed, and threaded joints. The term "permanent" implies the joint **cannot be dismantled without destroying the rivet**.

### 1.1.1 Anatomy of a Standard Rivet
A rivet consists of three principal geometric zones:

| Zone | Engineering Name | Function |
|------|------------------|----------|
| (i) | **Shop Head / Pre-formed Head** | Manufactured during forging; gives the rivet its initial identity. |
| (ii) | **Shank (Body)** | The cylindrical portion that fills the rivet hole; carries shear/bearing load. |
| (iii) | **Point / Tail** | The portion that is hammered or hydraulically upset to form the second head (called the **rivet point**). |

> [!NOTE]
> The diameter of the rivet as driven (i.e., the shank diameter after placing) is the **gross diameter `d`**, which is the fundamental design dimension.
