## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**List the differences between rigid and flexible couplings. Mention two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rigid Coupling | Flexible Coupling |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| Misalignment accommodation | None — requires perfect shaft alignment | Accommodates angular, parallel, axial misalignment |
| Construction | Simple, compact | Complex, has intermediate elements |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Torque capacity | High (full motor torque) | Generally lower due to flexible elements |
| Maintenance | Low | Higher (elastomer / bush wear) |
| Examples | Flange, Sleeve (Compression), Clamp | Bushed pin, Universal, Gear, Disc, Oldham |

**Examples:**
- Rigid: Flange coupling, Sleeve/Muff coupling.
- Flexible: Bushed pin coupling, Universal joint coupling.

*(3 marks: 2 for table, 1 for examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is the function of a spigot and recess in a protected-type flange coupling?**

**Model Answer:**
The **spigot** is a circular projection machined on the face of one flange, and the **recess** is a corresponding socket machined on the face of the mating flange. The spigot fits into the recess when the coupling is assembled.

**Functions:**
1. **Centering:** It ensures perfect axial alignment of the two flanges without relying solely on the bolts.
2. **Load Transfer:** It carries a portion of the tangential load, reducing the shear load on the bolts.
3. **Safety:** The bolts and nuts are fully enclosed inside the flanges, protecting them from environmental damage and accidental contact.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for functions)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **protected-type flange coupling**. List its main components and state the material commonly used for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flange coupling is to be designed to transmit **15 kW at 1440 rpm**. The shaft and key are made of the same material with allowable shear stress of **45 MPa** and allowable crushing stress of **90 MPa**. The bolts are made of a material with allowable tensile stress of **70 MPa**. Take the number of bolts $n = 4$ and bolt pitch circle diameter $D_p = 3d$. Design the **shaft diameter, key dimensions, and bolt diameter**. **(7 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Protected-Type Flange Coupling — Explanation (7 marks):**

A **protected-type flange coupling** consists of two flanges, one keyed to each shaft, bolted together to transmit torque. The flanges have a circular projection (**spigot**) on one and a matching recess on the other for positive alignment. Bolts and nuts are **fully enclosed** within the flange body for safety.

**Main Components and Materials:**

| Component | Material | Reason |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| Flanges | Cast Iron (FG 200) or Cast Steel | Good castability, damping, and strength |
| Hub | Integral with flange (same material) | Avoids stress concentration at weld/joint |
| Bolts & Nuts | Mild Steel (Grade 4.6/8.8) | High tensile strength, easy to machine |
| Keys | Mild Steel (Fe 410) | High shear and compressive strength |
| Shafts | Medium Carbon Steel (40C8) | High fatigue and torsional strength |

**Sketch Description (refer to Mermaid architecture in Section 4.1):** Two flanges with hubs fitted over shaft ends, joined at a vertical interface, with 4 bolts equally spaced on a 150 mm PCD (typical), and a central spigot-and-recess alignment feature.

**Design Logic:** The flanges transmit torque via tangential friction (pre-load) and bolt tension; the spigot ensures alignment and partially carries the load.

*(7 marks: 3 for sketch description, 2 for components, 2 for materials and design logic)*

---

**(b) Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Design Torque**
$$T = \frac{60P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 15{,}000}{2 \pi \times 1440} = \frac{900{,}000}{9047.79} = 99.47 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = 99{,}470 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Shaft Design (Torsion)**
$$d^3 = \frac{16T}{\pi \tau_s} = \frac{16 \times 99{,}470}{\pi \times 45} = \frac{1{,}591{,}520}{141.37} = 11{,}258 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (11{,}258)^{1/3} = 22.42 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $d = 25$ mm** (next standard size).

**Step 3: Recompute Torque with Standard Shaft**
$$T = \frac{\pi \times 25^3 \times 45}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 15{,}625 \times 45}{16} = 138{,}160 \text{ N·mm} \approx 138.16 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Key Design (Square Key)**
- Width: $b = d/4 = 25/4 = 6.25$ mm → adopt $b = 8$ mm
- Thickness: $t = d/6 = 25/6 = 4.17$ mm → adopt $t = 7$ mm (next standard, or use rectangular key with $b = 8$, $t = 5$)
- Length by crushing:
$$L = \frac{2T}{d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c} = \frac{2 \times 138{,}160}{25 \times 7 \times 90} = \frac{276{,}320}{15{,}750} = 17.54 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $L = 20$ mm.**

**Step 5: Bolt Design**
- $D_p = 3d = 3 \times 25 = 75$ mm
- Tangential force per bolt:
$$F_b = \frac{2T}{n D_p} = \frac{2 \times 138{,}160}{4 \times 75} = \frac{276{,}320}{300} = 921.07 \text{ N}$$
- Bolt core diameter:
$$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 F_b}{\pi \sigma_{tb}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 921.07}{\pi \times 70}} = \sqrt{\frac{3684.3}{219.91}} = \sqrt{16.75} = 4.09 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt M6 bolt** (core dia ≈ 4.77 mm) or **M8 bolt** (core dia ≈ 6.647 mm). For safety, use **M8 bolt**.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Shaft diameter | 25 mm |
| Key | 8 × 7 × 20 mm (square) |
| Bolts | M8, 4 numbers |
| Bolt PCD | 75 mm |

*(7 marks: 2 for shaft, 2 for key, 2 for bolt, 1 for summary)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **sleeve (compression) coupling**. State the **design assumptions** and the **empirical formula used for sleeve outer diameter** in cast iron construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **sleeve coupling** is to be designed to transmit **7.5 kW at 250 rpm**. The allowable shear stress for the shaft and the sleeve is **40 MPa** and the allowable shear stress for the setscrews is **30 MPa**. Design the **shaft diameter**, **sleeve dimensions**, and **setscrew diameter**. Assume 4 setscrews per shaft (8 total) and standard design proportions. **(7 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) Sleeve (Compression) Coupling — Explanation (7 marks):**

A **sleeve coupling** is the simplest and most economical rigid coupling. It consists of a **hollow cylindrical sleeve (muff)** that fits over the two shaft ends. The sleeve is locked to each shaft by **two setscrews** placed perpendicular to each other on a common pitch circle at the shaft surface.

**Design Assumptions:**
1. The torque is transmitted from shaft → setscrew → sleeve wall → opposite setscrew → opposite shaft.
2. The setscrews are subjected to **single shear** at the cross-section where they engage the shaft.
3. The sleeve is treated as a **hollow shaft** under torsion.
4. Setscrews are equally spaced at a pitch circle diameter equal to the shaft diameter $d$.
5. The sleeve material is **Cast Iron** with $\tau_{sleeve}$ in the range of **20–30 MPa**.

**Empirical Formula for Sleeve Outer Diameter (CI):**
$$\boxed{D_o = 2d + 13 \text{ mm}}$$

**Sketch Reference:** See Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.

*(7 marks: 3 for sketch + explanation, 2 for design assumptions, 2 for empirical formula)*

---

**(b) Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Design Torque**
$$T = \frac{60P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 7500}{2 \pi \times 250} = \frac{450{,}000}{1570.8} = 286.48 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = 286{,}480 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Shaft Design**
$$d^3 = \frac{16T}{\pi \tau_s} = \frac{16 \times 286{,}480}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{4{,}583{,}680}{125.66} = 36{,}479.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (36{,}479.4)^{1/3} = 33.06 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $d = 35$ mm.**

**Step 3: Recompute Torque**
$$T = \frac{\pi \times 35^3 \times 40}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 42{,}875 \times 40}{16} = 336{,}768.5 \text{ N·mm} \approx 336.77 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Sleeve Dimensions**
- Outer diameter: $D_o = 2d + 13 = 2(35) + 13 = 83$ mm
- Inner diameter: $D_i = d + 1.5 = 35 + 1.5 = 36.5$ mm (clearance fit)
- Length of engagement on each shaft: $L = 1.5d = 1.5 \times 35 = 52.5$ → adopt $L = 55$ mm
- Total sleeve length: $L_s = 2L + 1.5d = 2(55) + 1.5(35) = 110 + 52.5 = 162.5$ → adopt $L_s = 165$ mm

**Step 5: Setscrew Design (n = 8 total)**
- Force per setscrew:
$$F_s = \frac{2T}{n_s d} = \frac{2 \times 336{,}768.5}{8 \times 35} = \frac{673{,}537}{280} = 2405.5 \text{ N}$$
- Core diameter:
$$d_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 F_s}{\pi \tau_{ss}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 2405.5}{\pi \times 30}} = \sqrt{\frac{9622}{94.25}} = \sqrt{102.1} = 10.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt M12 setscrew** (core dia ≈ 10.106 mm) or M14.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Shaft diameter | 35 mm |
| Sleeve outer diameter | 83 mm |
| Sleeve total length | 165 mm |
| Setscrews | M12, 8 numbers (4 per shaft) |
| Engagement length per shaft | 55 mm |

*(7 marks: 2 for shaft, 2 for sleeve, 2 for setscrew, 1 for summary)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update the torque after adopting a standard shaft size.** This is the **#1 reason** students lose 1–2 marks. Always recompute $T$ after rounding up $d$, then use this revised $T$ for all subsequent designs (key, bolt, etc.).
> 2. **Confusing $F_t$ (total tangential force) with $F_b$ (force per bolt).** The total tangential force $F_t = 2T/D_p$ is split equally among $n$ bolts. Forgetting to divide by $n$ is a fatal error.
> 3. **Using $\sigma_{tb}$ and $\tau_b$ incorrectly:** The bolt in a flange coupling is in **tensile** stress (the tangential force tries to pull the two flanges apart tangentially). It is **not** in pure shear, unless the flange faces are perfectly friction-less and only bolt shank shear is considered (an older, conservative approach rarely used in KTU 2024).
> 4. **Using $b = d/4$ and $t = d/6$ for rectangular vs. square keys incorrectly.** Square keys use $t = d/6$, rectangular keys use $t = d/7$. A common pitfall is to mix these.
> 5. **Skipping the empirical formulas for sleeve ($D_o = 2d + 13$) and bolt PCD ($D_p = 3d$).** These are essential KTU design data conventions and not using them in a derivation will result in a 1-mark penalty.
> 6. **Ignoring the standard bolt and key selection step.** The designed $d_c$ must be rounded up to a standard ISO metric size (M6, M8, M10, M12, M16, etc.). The designed key must be a standard size as per IS 2292 / 2293.
> 7. **Not specifying the design data handbook convention:** Mention the source (e.g., PSG, Mahadevan, or V.B. Bhandari Design Data Book) for empirical constants.
> 8. **In sleeve coupling, forgetting that 4 setscrews per shaft = 8 setscrews total** for transmitting torque from both shafts. The total $n_s$ in the formula is the **total number of setscrews** acting on the shaft whose torque is being transferred.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rigid Couplings (Flange & Compression):**
>
> **1. Core Identity:**
> - **Flange Coupling:** Two flanges bolted together; used for HIGH torque and where disconnection is occasionally needed.
> - **Compression (Sleeve/Muff) Coupling:** One hollow sleeve with setscrews; used for LOW torque, permanent, low-cost joints.
>
> **2. Design Sequence (Always):**
> - (i) Compute $T$ from $P$ and $N$.
> - (ii) Design shaft diameter from $T$ and $\tau_s$.
> - (iii) Adopt next standard $d$, then RECOMPUTE $T$.
> - (iv) Design hub, flange, bolt, key (for flange coupling) using revised $T$.
> - (v) Design setscrews and sleeve (for compression coupling) using revised $T$.
> - (vi) Select standard sizes from design data handbook.
>
> **3. Key Empirical Rules (Must Memorize):**
> - Hub: $D_h = 2d$, $L_h = 1.5d$
> - Bolt PCD: $D_p = 3d$
> - Sleeve: $D_o = 2d + 13$ (CI), $L_s = 2L + 1.5d$
> - Square key: $b = d/4$, $t = d/6$
> - Rectangular key: $b = d/4$, $t = d/7$
>
> **4. Critical Formula Set:**
> - Bolt core dia: $d_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{8T}{\pi n D_p \sigma_{tb}}}$
> - Setscrew core dia: $d_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{8T}{\pi n_s d \tau_{ss}}}$
> - Key length (crushing): $L = \dfrac{2T}{d t \sigma_c}$
> - Key length (shear): $L = \dfrac{2T}{b \tau_k}$ (always larger → not governing)
> - Shaft: $d = \left(\dfrac{16T}{\pi \tau_s}\right)^{1/3}$
>
> **5. Common Materials & Allowable Stresses (Design Data Convention):**
> - Mild Steel: $\tau_s = 40$–$55$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 60$–$80$ MPa
> - Cast Iron: $\tau_s = 20$–$30$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 30$–$50$ MPa
> - Bolts (MS, 4.6 grade): $\sigma_{tb} = 60$–$70$ MPa
>
> **6. Failure Modes to Check:**
> - **Shaft:** Torsion (always the starting point).
> - **Key:** Crushing (governs) and Shear (always check, but never governs for parallel keys).
> - **Bolt:** Tensile stress at core (always the governing mode for flange coupling).
> - **Setscrew:** Shear at cross-section.
> - **Sleeve:** Torsion as hollow shaft + bearing stress under setscrew (rarely checked in KTU).
>
> **7. Number Convention:**
> - Flange coupling: $n$ = number of bolts (typically 4, 6, or 8).
> - Compression coupling: $n_s$ = TOTAL number of setscrews (typically 8 = 4 per shaft).
>
> **8. Type Distinction (Frequently Asked 3-mark question):**
> - **Unprotected Type:** Bolts and nuts protrude from the flange face. Used where inspection is needed. Bolts may fail in fatigue at the thread root due to repeated bending.
> - **Protected Type:** Bolts and nuts fully enclosed. Safer, no protruding parts, slightly more expensive to manufacture. Better fatigue life for bolts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Couplings with Kinematic Flexibility, Slip Couplings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Couplings with Kinematic Flexibility, Slip Couplings

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Couplings with Kinematic Flexibility — Universal Joint (Hooke's Coupling)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim):**
A *universal coupling* (also called a *Hooke's coupling* or *Cardan joint*) is a pair of hinges oriented at 90° to each other, connected by a cross-shaped intermediate member (the *spider*). It transmits rotary motion and torque between two shafts whose axes intersect (usually at a small angle $\alpha \le 45°$) and which may rotate about a common centre. It provides **kinematic flexibility** — meaning the *velocity ratio* between driving and driven shafts is **not constant**, but varies with the relative angular position of the driving shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key distinction for the examiner:**
> - **Flexible coupling** (e.g., bush-pin, gear, disc) → accommodates *parallel misalignment* but maintains **constant velocity ratio = 1**.
> - **Kinematically flexible coupling (Universal joint)** → accommodates *angular misalignment* but has a **non-uniform velocity ratio**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview):**
Imagine holding the steering shaft of a car with one hand and the steering-arm shaft with the other, with a small angle between them. If you rotate your hand smoothly in a circle, the steering-arm shaft *also* rotates — but **faster in some positions and slower in others**. The universal joint is the mechanical "L-shape cross" that lets you do this. The *cross* (spider) acts as a pivot that "wobbles" — converting one rotation into another with a slightly varying speed. This is exactly what happens in a car's drive-shaft between the gearbox and the rear axle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — 2024 Scheme:** The two principal topics under "Couplings with Kinematic Flexibility" are:
> 1. Universal coupling (Hooke's joint) — kinematics, maximum velocity ratio, torque & power transmission.
> 2. Slip couplings — friction clutches (cone clutch, single/multi-plate disc clutch, centrifugal clutch).

---

### 1.2 Slip Couplings (Friction Clutches)

**Formal Definition:**
A *slip coupling* (or *friction clutch*) is a device used to connect a driving shaft to a driven shaft in such a way that the driven shaft can be **slipped** (made to rotate at a lower speed) or **engaged/disengaged** at will. Torque transmission occurs through **frictional contact** between a driving member (pressed against) and a driven member. Slip couplings *also* provide overload protection — the driving member slips when the torque demand exceeds the frictional torque capacity.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of the **clutch pedal** in a manual car. When you press the pedal, a friction disc (driven) is pulled *away* from the engine flywheel (driver) — the car coasts freely. When you release the pedal gradually, the disc *slides* back into contact, "slipping" briefly until speeds match, and then locks fully — the car moves. That "slipping" phase is the working principle of a slip coupling.

**Types of Slip Couplings (Friction Clutches) — as per KTU Module 3:**

| Type | Contact Geometry | KTU Design Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Cone Clutch** | Conical friction surface | Axial thrust, uniform wear theory |
| **Single-Plate (Disc) Clutch** | Annular flat surface | Uniform pressure vs. uniform wear theory |
| **Multi-Plate Clutch** | Multiple annular surfaces | n-disc torque capacity, axial thrust |
| **Centrifugal Clutch** | Expanding shoes on inner drum | Self-engagement speed, spring balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants / Standard Metrics used throughout Module 3:**
> - Coefficient of friction $\mu$ — typically **0.1 to 0.5** for clutch materials.
> - Limiting angle of cone $\alpha$ for cone clutch — typically **12° to 15°**.
> - Allowable pressure $p$ — typically **0.07 to 0.35 N/mm²** for asbestos-based friction materials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Universal Coupling — Velocity Ratio & Angle of Drive

#### 2.1.1 Operating Principle (Step-wise logic)
1. The driving shaft is attached to one yoke (fork) and the driven shaft to the other yoke.
2. The yokes are 90° apart and joined by a cross-shaped spider with four trunnion pins.
3. When the driving shaft rotates by an angle $\theta$, the driven shaft rotates by an angle $\phi$.
4. The relationship between $\theta$ and $\phi$ depends on the inclination angle $\alpha$ between the two shafts.
5. Because $\phi$ is a function of *both* $\theta$ and $\alpha$, the **angular velocity ratio is non-uniform**.

#### 2.1.2 Derivation Skeleton (Full derivation in §3)

The two governing equations of a Hooke's coupling are:

$$\tan\theta = \tan\phi \cdot \cos\alpha$$

and equivalently:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\tan\theta}{\cos\alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** The non-uniform velocity ratio introduces **torsional vibration** and **secondary couple** in the shafts. For KTU, you must be able to compute the **maximum** and **minimum** angular velocity of the driven shaft.

**Angular Velocity of Driven Shaft:**

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cdot \cos^2\theta}$$

**Maximum angular velocity:** occurs when $\theta = 0°$ or $180°$

$$\omega_{2,max} = \frac{\omega_1}{\cos\alpha}$$

**Minimum angular velocity:** occurs when $\theta = 90°$ or $270°$

$$\omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cdot \cos\alpha$$

#### 2.1.3 Torque Transmission
The torque transmitted by a universal coupling is limited by the **twisting moment on the cross-pin (spider arm)**.

**Twisting moment on each arm of the spider:**

$$T_{arm} = \frac{T}{2}\cos\alpha$$

where $T$ is the torque on the driving/driven shaft.

**Bending moment on each arm (at the trunnion pin):**

$$M_b = \frac{T}{2}\sin\alpha$$

**Design equations (allowable stresses):**

$$\frac{16 \, T_{arm}}{\pi d^3} = \tau_{allow} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \left(\frac{16 \, T_{arm}}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$\frac{32 \, M_b}{\pi d^3} = \sigma_{b,allow} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \left(\frac{32 \, M_b}{\pi \, \sigma_{b,allow}}\right)^{1/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Universal joints are ubiquitous in rear-wheel-drive cars (propeller shaft), industrial machine tools (e.g., milling machine spindles), and robotic wrists. The non-uniform velocity is **acceptable at small angles** ($\alpha < 15°$) and becomes severe at larger angles — limiting practical use.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Slip Couplings (Friction Clutches)

> [!NOTE]
> All clutch design problems in KTU Module 3 use **one of two fundamental assumptions**:
> - **Uniform Pressure theory** (new clutch, freshly assembled) — pressure $p$ = constant.
> - **Uniform Wear theory** (worn clutch, steady-state) — $p \cdot r$ = constant $\Rightarrow p \propto 1/r$.
>
> The examiner will *always* specify which theory — if not stated, default to **Uniform Wear**.

#### 2.2.1 Disc / Plate Clutch (Single Plate)

| Quantity | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|---|---|---|
| Axial thrust $W$ | $\pi p (R^2 - R_i^2)$ | $2\pi \mu W \left(\frac{R+R_i}{2}\right) \Rightarrow W = \frac{2\pi \mu F_a (R+R_i)}{2}$ → see derivation |
| Torque $T$ | $\frac{2}{3} \pi \mu p (R^3 - R_i^3)$ | $\frac{1}{2} \mu F_a (R+R_i)$ |
| Relation | $p = \dfrac{2 T}{2\pi \mu (R^3 - R_i^3) / 3}$ | $F_a = \dfrac{2T}{\mu (R+R_i)}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory hook:** For uniform wear, torque depends only on **mean radius** $R_m = (R+R_i)/2$ — not on $R$ alone!

#### 2.2.2 Cone Clutch

| Quantity | Expression |
|---|---|
| Semi-cone angle | $\alpha$ (typically 12°–15°) |
| Axial thrust relation | $W_{axial} = W \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ where $\tan\phi = \mu$ |
| Torque (Uniform Pressure) | $T = \frac{1}{2} \mu W_{axial} \cdot \frac{(R^3 - R_i^3)}{\sin\alpha}$ |
| Torque (Uniform Wear) | $T = \frac{1}{2} \mu W_{axial} \cdot \frac{(R^2 - R_i^2)}{\sin\alpha}$ |
| Friction radius | $R_f = \dfrac{R+R_i}{2}$ (uniform wear) |

#### 2.2.3 Multi-Plate Clutch

$$T_{total} = n \cdot T_{single}$$

$$W_{total} = n \cdot W_{single}$$

where $n$ = number of **friction surfaces** = $(n_{plates} - 1)$ for driving + driven discs.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students count discs but forget that **each contact surface counts once**. For a clutch with $N$ discs and $M$ driven plates, friction surfaces = $N + M - 1$ — usually it is simply $2(n-1)$ for $n$ alternate discs.

#### 2.2.4 Centrifugal Clutch

**Operating Principle:** Shoes are pivoted on the driving drum and held back by springs. As drum speed $\omega$ increases, centrifugal force $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ pushes shoes outward against the driven drum (lining). Engagement begins at the *design speed*.

**Condition for engagement:**

$$m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = F_{spring} + F_{friction\,reaction}$$

---

### 2.3 Engineering Application Matrix

| Coupling Type | Real-World Use | Key Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Joint (single) | Propeller shaft, lathe spindles | Maximum angle $\alpha < 45°$ |
| Double Universal (CV joint) | Rear-wheel-drive car drive-shafts | Phasing cancels velocity fluctuation |
| Cone Clutch | Tractors, machine tools (synchro) | Self-locking if $\alpha < \phi$ |
| Multi-Plate Clutch | Automobiles, motorcycles | Compact, high torque density |
| Centrifugal Clutch | Go-karts, scooters, chain saws | Self-actuating, no operator needed |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Ratio of a Universal Coupling

**Setup (Refer to Mermaid diagram in §4):**
Let the driving shaft be along axis $OA$ and the driven shaft along axis $OB$, intersecting at $O$ at an angle $\alpha$. The cross-shaped spider has four trunnions $P$, $Q$, $R$, $S$ at 90° to each other.

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
Consider a position where the driving shaft has rotated by angle $\theta$ from a reference. Project the trunnion position onto the driven shaft plane. The driven shaft rotation $\phi$ is determined by:

The trunnion $P$ moves on a circle of radius $r$ in the plane perpendicular to the driving shaft. Its projection onto the driven shaft plane gives an ellipse.

**Step 2 — Kinematic equation:**
From the geometry of the cross between the two yokes:

$$\cos\theta = \cos\phi - \sin\phi \tan\alpha \cdot \sin\theta$$

After rearrangement (see Shigley/Khurmi — standard derivation):

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \tan\phi \cdot \cos\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to get angular velocity ratio:**
Let $\dot{\theta} = \omega_1$ and $\dot{\phi} = \omega_2$.

Differentiating $\tan\theta = \tan\phi \cos\alpha$ with respect to time:

$$\sec^2\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} = \sec^2\phi \cdot \dot{\phi} \cdot \cos\alpha$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\sec^2\theta}{\sec^2\phi \cdot \cos\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Express $\omega_2$ in terms of $\theta$ alone:**
Using the relation $\tan\phi = \tan\theta / \cos\alpha$, we get:

$$\sec^2\phi = 1 + \tan^2\phi = 1 + \frac{\tan^2\theta}{\cos^2\alpha} = \frac{\cos^2\alpha + \tan^2\theta \cos^2\alpha / \cos^2\alpha \cdot ...}{}$$

After simplification:

$$\sec^2\phi = \frac{\cos^2\alpha \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta}{\cos^2\alpha \cos^2\theta} = \frac{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta}{\cos^2\alpha \cos^2\theta}$$

Substituting back:

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{1/\cos^2\theta}{\left(\frac{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta}{\cos^2\alpha \cos^2\theta}\right) \cdot \cos\alpha}$$

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\cos^2\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cdot \cos^2\theta}}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum and minimum:**
- At $\theta = 0°$: $\cos^2\theta = 1 \Rightarrow \omega_{2,max} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\cos\alpha}$
- At $\theta = 90°$: $\cos^2\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cos\alpha$

**Step 6 — Condition for constant velocity ratio:**
$\omega_2 = \omega_1$ only when $\alpha = 0°$ (shafts collinear). At any finite $\alpha$, the ratio is non-uniform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real engineering implication:** Two universal joints in series, with their yokes *in phase*, can cancel the velocity fluctuation — this is the principle of the **double universal joint / Rzeppa-type CV joint** used in cars.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque Capacity of a Single-Plate Clutch (Uniform Wear Theory)

**Setup:** A single friction disc of outer radius $R$ and inner radius $R_i$ is pressed against a flat plate by axial force $F_a$. Coefficient of friction is $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Pressure distribution under uniform wear:**
Uniform wear assumes that the wear rate at every radius $r$ is constant. By Archard's wear law, wear rate $\propto p \cdot v \cdot t \propto p \cdot r$. So for constant wear:

$$p \cdot r = C \quad \text{(constant)} \Rightarrow p = \frac{C}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Axial thrust equilibrium:**
The total axial force is the integral of pressure over the annular area:

$$F_a = \int_{R_i}^{R} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{R_i}^{R} \frac{C}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi C (R - R_i)$$

Therefore:

$$C = \frac{F_a}{2\pi (R - R_i)}$$

**Step 3 — Torque transmission:**
Friction force on elemental ring $= \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$. Torque from ring about centre:

$$dT = r \cdot \mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \cdot r \, dr$$

Integrating:

$$T = \int_{R_i}^{R} 2\pi \mu C \cdot r \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \cdot \frac{R^2 - R_i^2}{2} = \pi \mu C (R^2 - R_i^2)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $C$:**

$$T = \pi \mu \cdot \frac{F_a}{2\pi (R - R_i)} \cdot (R^2 - R_i^2) = \frac{\mu F_a (R+R_i)(R-R_i)}{2(R-R_i)}$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{1}{2} \mu F_a (R + R_i)}$$

**Step 5 — Mean radius form:**

$$T = \mu F_a \cdot R_m \quad \text{where} \quad R_m = \frac{R+R_i}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Under uniform wear, the torque capacity is *independent* of the radial pressure distribution — only the *mean radius* matters!

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cone Clutch Torque (Uniform Wear)

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
A cone clutch has a semi-cone angle $\alpha$. The contact surface is conical. Normal force on a ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ (along the slant) is $dN = p \cdot 2\pi r \cdot dr/\sin\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Axial thrust balance:**
Axial component of $dN$ = $p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$ (the $\sin\alpha$ cancels).

$$F_a = \int_{R_i}^{R} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \quad \text{(same as disc clutch)}$$

**Step 3 — Torque:**
Friction force on ring = $\mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr / \sin\alpha$, moment arm = $r$:

$$dT = r \cdot \mu \cdot p \cdot \frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\sin\alpha} = \frac{2\pi \mu p r^2}{\sin\alpha} dr$$

**Step 4 — Uniform wear:** $p = C/r$

$$T = \int_{R_i}^{R} \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha} \cdot r \, dr = \frac{2\pi \mu C}{\sin\alpha} \cdot \frac{R^2 - R_i^2}{2} = \frac{\pi \mu C (R^2 - R_i^2)}{\sin\alpha}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $C = F_a/[2\pi(R-R_i)]$:**

$$T = \frac{\mu F_a (R+R_i)}{2 \sin\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{T_{cone} = \frac{\mu F_a (R + R_i)}{2 \sin\alpha}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare with disc clutch:** $T_{disc} = \dfrac{\mu F_a (R+R_i)}{2}$. The cone clutch gives a **multiplier of $1/\sin\alpha$** — for $\alpha = 12°$, $1/\sin 12° \approx 4.8$. So a cone clutch transmits ~5× the torque of a disc clutch of the same size for the *same axial force*. This is the principal advantage.

**Axial thrust on the cone face (for equilibrium of the moving cone):**

$$W_{axial} = W_{normal} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

where $\phi = \tan^{-1}\mu$ is the friction angle. This gives the *self-locking* condition: if $\alpha < \phi$, the cone will not release — it self-locks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Clutch Theories

| Parameter | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear (preferred for design) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure law | $p = $ const | $p \cdot r = $ const |
| Axial thrust $F_a$ | $\pi p (R^2 - R_i^2)$ | $\pi p_{max} R_i (R - R_i)$ where $p_{max}$ is at $r = R_i$ |
| Torque $T$ | $\dfrac{2}{3}\pi\mu p(R^3 - R_i^3)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\mu F_a (R+R_i)$ |
| Max pressure location | Anywhere | At inner radius $R_i$ |
| **Design recommendation (KTU)** | New clutch, initial design | **Steady-state, used clutch** |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Example: Disc Clutch (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A single-plate clutch has effective outer diameter $300$ mm and inner diameter $200$ mm. The coefficient of friction is $0.3$ and the axial pressure is limited to $0.1$ MPa. Determine:
(a) Torque transmitted using *uniform pressure* theory.
(b) Axial force required using *uniform wear* theory if same torque is to be transmitted.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** $R = 0.15$ m, $R_i = 0.10$ m, $p = 0.1 \times 10^6$ N/m², $\mu = 0.3$.

$$T_{UP} = \frac{2}{3}\pi\mu p (R^3 - R_i^3)$$

$$= \frac{2}{3}\pi (0.3)(0.1 \times 10^6)\left[(0.15)^3 - (0.10)^3\right]$$

$$= \frac{2}{3}\pi (0.3)(10^5)\left[3.375 \times 10^{-3} - 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\right]$$

$$= \frac{2}{3}\pi (0.3)(10^5)(2.375 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= \frac{2}{3}\pi (71.25) = \frac{2}{3}(223.84) \approx 149.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (b):** Using uniform wear with $T = 149.2$ N·m:

$$F_a = \frac{2T}{\mu(R+R_i)} = \frac{2(149.2)}{0.3 (0.15 + 0.10)} = \frac{298.4}{0.3 \times 0.25} = \frac{298.4}{0.075}$$

$$F_a \approx 3979 \text{ N} \approx 3.98 \text{ kN}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Coupling (Hooke's Joint) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driving Shaft] --> B[Yoke 1 - forks horizontal]
    B --> C[Spider Trunnion P - top]
    B --> D[Spider Trunnion Q - bottom]
    C --> E[Cross Axis 90 deg]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Yoke 2 - forks vertical]
    F --> G[Driven Shaft inclined at angle alpha]
    H[Tilt Angle alpha between shafts] -.-> B
    H -.-> F
    I[Angular position theta of driving shaft] -.-> B
    J[Resulting angle phi of driven shaft] -.-> G
    B --> J
```

**Operational Flow:**
1. Driving shaft rotates → yoke 1 rotates → spider cross tilts.
2. Cross pin P traces a **circle** in plane perpendicular to driving shaft.
3. Its projection on driven shaft plane traces an **ellipse**.
4. Driven shaft rotation $\phi$ varies sinusoidally with $\theta$ → non-uniform velocity ratio.

---

### 4.2 Single-Plate Disc Clutch — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Force Fa applied by lever / spring] --> B[Pressure Plate]
    B --> C[Friction Disc Driven Plate]
    C --> D[Flywheel Driving Plate]
    D --> E[Flywheel rotates at Engine Speed]
    F[Friction Torque T = mu Fa Rm] --> G[Transmitted to Gearbox Input Shaft]
    C --> F
    H[Inner Radius Ri] --> C
    I[Outer Radius R] --> C
    J[Mean Radius Rm = R+Ri over 2] --> F
```

---

### 4.3 Cone Clutch — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Axial Force Fa] --> B[Axial Thrust on Cone Face]
    B --> C[Normal Force N = Fa sin alpha]
    C --> D[Friction Force mu N]
    D --> E[Torque T = mu N x Mean Radius]
    F[Semi-cone Angle alpha 12 to 15 deg] --> B
    G[Friction Angle phi = tan-1 mu] --> H[Self-locking check: alpha less than phi]
    F --> H
```

---

### 4.4 Multi-Plate Clutch — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driving Shaft] --> B[Alternating Driving Discs D1 D2 D3]
    C[Driven Shaft] --> D[Alternating Driven Discs d1 d2 d3]
    B --> E[Friction Surface 1]
    D --> E
    B --> F[Friction Surface 2]
    D --> F
    B --> G[Friction Surface 3]
    D --> G
    E --> H[Torque per Surface T_s]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Total Torque n x T_s]
    J[Axial Force per Surface Fa] --> E
    J --> F
    J --> G
```

---

### 4.5 Centrifugal Clutch — Operating States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle: Idle - shoes held by springs - no contact
    Idle --> Engaging: Engine speed increases
    Engaging: Engaging - shoes slide on drum - slipping
    Engaging --> Engaged: Speed matches driven shaft
    Engaged: Engaged - shoes locked - full torque
    Engaged --> Engaging: Load surge causes slip
    Engaging --> Idle: Engine idle speed drops
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*State the condition under which the velocity ratio of a Hooke's coupling becomes non-uniform. Mention the positions at which the maximum and minimum angular velocity of the driven shaft occur.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The velocity ratio of a Hooke's coupling is **non-uniform whenever the inclination angle $\alpha$ between the driving and driven shafts is non-zero** ($\alpha \ne 0$).

The angular velocity of the driven shaft is:

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta}$$

- **Maximum** $\omega_{2,max} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\cos\alpha}$ occurs at $\theta = 0°$ and $180°$ (driving shaft trunnions in the plane of the yokes).
- **Minimum** $\omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cos\alpha$ occurs at $\theta = 90°$ and $270°$ (trunnions perpendicular to the plane of yokes).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write only $\omega_{max} = \omega_1/\cos\alpha$ and forget the position of $\theta$. The examiner **demands** the corresponding $\theta$ values — 1 mark lost per missing value.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three assumptions made in the uniform wear theory of clutch design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The pressure distribution over the contact surface is **not uniform**; instead, $p \cdot r = $ constant (i.e., $p \propto 1/r$).
2. The wear rate at every point on the friction surface is the **same** (steady-state worn condition).
3. The coefficient of friction $\mu$ is **constant** over the entire contact surface and independent of pressure and temperature.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Universal Coupling

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** A universal coupling connects two shafts whose axes intersect at $20°$. The driving shaft rotates at $240$ rpm. Determine:
- (i) The maximum and minimum speeds of the driven shaft. **(7 marks)**
- (ii) The torque on each arm of the spider if the power transmitted is $7.5$ kW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i):** $\alpha = 20°$, $\omega_1 = 240$ rpm.

$$\omega_{2,max} = \frac{\omega_1}{\cos 20°} = \frac{240}{0.9397} = 255.4 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cos 20° = 240 \times 0.9397 = 225.5 \text{ rpm}$$

> (Note: at $\theta = 0°$, $\omega_2$ is maximum; at $\theta = 90°$, it is minimum.)

**Part (a)(ii):** Power $P = 7.5$ kW, $N_1 = 240$ rpm.

Mean torque on driving shaft:

$$T = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi N_1} = \frac{7500 \times 60}{2\pi \times 240} = \frac{450000}{1507.96} \approx 298.4 \text{ N·m}$$

Torque on each arm of the spider (there are 2 arms carrying the torque in the plane of the larger angle):

$$T_{arm} = \frac{T}{2}\cos\alpha = \frac{298.4}{2}\cos 20° = 149.2 \times 0.9397 \approx 140.2 \text{ N·m}$$

Bending moment on each arm:

$$M_b = \frac{T}{2}\sin\alpha = 149.2 \times \sin 20° = 149.2 \times 0.342 \approx 51.0 \text{ N·m}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Disc Clutch Design

**(a)** A single-plate clutch is to transmit $25$ kW at $1440$ rpm. The coefficient of friction is $0.25$ and the maximum pressure is not to exceed $0.1$ MPa. The ratio of outer to inner radius is $1.5$. Using **uniform pressure theory**, determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Outer and inner radii.
- (ii) Axial thrust required.

**(b)** If the same clutch operates under **uniform wear theory** at the same torque, calculate the new axial thrust and the maximum pressure. Comment on which design is safer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part B(a):** Given $P = 25$ kW, $N = 1440$ rpm, $\mu = 0.25$, $p = 0.1 \times 10^6$ N/m², $R/R_i = 1.5$.

Torque:

$$T = \frac{25 \times 10^3 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1440} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{9047.79} \approx 165.78 \text{ N·m}$$

Uniform pressure torque equation:

$$T = \frac{2}{3}\pi\mu p (R^3 - R_i^3) = \frac{2}{3}\pi\mu p R^3\left[1 - \left(\frac{R_i}{R}\right)^3\right]$$

With $R_i/R = 1/1.5 = 0.6667$, $(R_i/R)^3 = 0.2963$:

$$165.78 = \frac{2}{3}\pi(0.25)(0.1 \times 10^6)(R^3)(1 - 0.2963)$$

$$165.78 = \frac{2}{3}\pi(0.25)(10^5)(R^3)(0.7037)$$

$$165.78 = (52359.88)(R^3)(0.7037) = 36843.5 \cdot R^3$$

$$R^3 = \frac{165.78}{36843.5} = 4.50 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R = (4.50 \times 10^{-3})^{1/3} \approx 0.165 \text{ m} = 165 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_i = R/1.5 = 110 \text{ mm}$$

Axial thrust:

$$F_a = \pi p (R^2 - R_i^2) = \pi (0.1 \times 10^6)[(0.165)^2 - (0.110)^2]$$

$$= \pi (10^5)(0.02723 - 0.0121) = \pi (10^5)(0.01513)$$

$$F_a \approx 4753 \text{ N} \approx 4.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Part B(b):** For the same torque $T = 165.78$ N·m under uniform wear:

$$F_a = \frac{2T}{\mu(R+R_i)} = \frac{2 \times 165.78}{0.25 (0.165 + 0.110)} = \frac{331.56}{0.06875} \approx 4822 \text{ N} \approx 4.82 \text{ kN}$$

Maximum pressure occurs at the **inner radius** $R_i$ (where $p \cdot r$ is max with $r$ smallest):

$$p_{max} \cdot R_i = p_{avg} \cdot R_{avg}, \text{ but easier:}$$

From $p \cdot r = C$ and $F_a = 2\pi C (R - R_i)$:

$$C = \frac{F_a}{2\pi(R-R_i)} = \frac{4822}{2\pi (0.165 - 0.110)} = \frac{4822}{2\pi(0.055)} = \frac{4822}{0.3456} \approx 13953$$

$$p_{max} = \frac{C}{R_i} = \frac{13953}{0.110} \approx 1.27 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.127 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comment:** The uniform wear theory gives a **higher axial force** and the maximum pressure is at the inner radius. The **uniform pressure theory** is conservative at design stage, but the **uniform wear theory** represents the *actual worn condition* and is therefore preferred for rating an existing clutch.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Universal Coupling questions (often lose 2–3 marks):**
> - Forgetting that $\omega_{2,max}$ and $\omega_{2,min}$ occur at *specific* $\theta$ values.
> - Using $\omega_1 = 2\pi N/60$ correctly but forgetting to convert to rad/s for torque calculations.
> - Mixing up the **twisting moment** ($T/2 \cos\alpha$) and the **bending moment** ($T/2 \sin\alpha$) on spider arms.
>
> **Pitfall — Clutch questions (often lose 3–4 marks):**
> - Confusing **uniform pressure** ($p =$ const) with **uniform wear** ($p \cdot r =$ const).
> - Using wrong units: $p$ must be in N/m² when radii are in metres.
> - For cone clutch, omitting the $\sin\alpha$ term in the denominator of torque equation.
> - For multi-plate clutch, incorrectly counting friction surfaces (each pair of adjacent surfaces = 1 friction surface).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Universal joint** transmits torque between **non-collinear shafts**; velocity ratio = $\cos\alpha / (1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta)$.
- [ ] $\omega_{2,max} = \omega_1 / \cos\alpha$ at $\theta = 0°, 180°$; $\omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cos\alpha$ at $\theta = 90°, 270°$.
- [ ] Torque on each spider arm: $T/2 \cos\alpha$ (twisting), $T/2 \sin\alpha$ (bending).
- [ ] **Slip coupling** = **friction clutch** — torque transmitted through *frictional contact*, *not* positive engagement.
- [ ] **Uniform Pressure** → $p = $ const, $T = (2/3)\pi\mu p (R^3 - R_i^3)$.
- [ ] **Uniform Wear** → $p \cdot r = $ const, $T = (1/2) \mu F_a (R + R_i)$ = $\mu F_a R_m$.
- [ ] **Cone clutch torque** is $(1/\sin\alpha)$ times the disc clutch torque for the *same axial force*.
- [ ] Cone clutch self-locks if $\alpha \le \phi$ (friction angle); KTU usually takes $\alpha = 12°$–$15°$ to avoid this.
- [ ] **Multi-plate clutch**: $T_{total} = n \cdot T_{single}$, where $n$ = number of **friction surfaces**.
- [ ] **Centrifugal clutch**: engagement when $m \omega^2 r$ overcomes spring + friction reaction; used in scooters and go-karts.
- [ ] **Always state units explicitly** ($R$ in m when $p$ in N/m²; $N$ in rpm for $T = P \times 60/2\pi N$).
- [ ] When the problem says *"new clutch"* or *"initial design"* → use **uniform pressure**.
- [ ] When the problem says *"worn clutch"* or *"steady-state"* → use **uniform wear**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Couplings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluid Couplings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Fluid Couplings — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluid Coupling** is a hydrodynamic power transmission device that uses a hydraulic fluid (typically an oil of low viscosity) as the working medium to transmit torque from a prime mover (driving shaft) to a driven machine (driven shaft), **without any mechanical contact** between the input and output members.

It consists of two essential rotating components:

- **Impeller (Pump / Driving Member / Primary)** — mounted on the driving shaft, acts as a centrifugal pump.
- **Runner (Turbine / Driven Member / Secondary)** — mounted on the driven shaft, acts as a centrifugal turbine.

These two members are enclosed in a sealed housing filled with oil. The impeller accelerates the oil radially outward, which then impinges on the runner blades, causing it to rotate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A fluid coupling is **not a torque converter**. It cannot multiply torque. The torque transmitted by the impeller to the runner is theoretically equal to the torque applied to the impeller. The only function of a fluid coupling is to provide **smooth, cushioned, slip-start** power transmission.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **ceiling fan connected to a ceiling-mounted motor through only a stream of air** instead of a shaft. The motor (impeller) pushes air outward. That air strikes the fan blades (runner) and makes the fan rotate. There is no rigid link — the energy is carried purely by the fluid (air/oil).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the "stirring paddle" in a washing machine:** The motor rotates a paddle (impeller) inside water. The water moves and pushes against stationary clothes, making them tumble. The water is the torque-transferring medium, just like oil in a fluid coupling.

## 1.3 Key Physical Principles Involved

- **Newton's Second Law of Motion** (F = m·a): Oil mass experiences centrifugal force.
- **Newton's Third Law** (action–reaction): The runner exerts an equal and opposite torque on the impeller.
- **Conservation of Angular Momentum** (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation): The change in angular momentum of the oil stream = torque transferred.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fluid coupling relies on **centrifugal action** — oil thrown outward at high speed carries **kinetic energy** that is converted into mechanical work at the runner.

## 1.4 Distinguishing Features at a Glance

| Feature | Fluid Coupling | Torque Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Torque multiplication | **No (1:1)** | **Yes (up to 2–3×)** |
| Stator (reaction member) | Absent | Present |
| Efficiency at full load | High (~95–98%) | Lower |
| Slip | Always present (2–5%) | Variable |
| Primary use | Soft-start drives | Automotive transmission |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Fluid Coupling

1. **Impeller (Driving Member)**
   - Cast iron or aluminium alloy body.
   - Has curved radial blades on its inner surface.
   - Filled with oil from the working circuit.

2. **Runner (Driven Member)**
   - Has matching curved blades facing the impeller.
   - Fitted on the driven shaft.

3. **Working Chamber**
   - A small clearance (≈ 3 to 5 mm) separates impeller and runner — no contact.
   - The housing may be **fixed** (simple coupling) or **rotating** (self-contained/scoop coupling).

4. **Oil Reservoir & Scoop**
   - A scoop tube regulates the oil fill — more oil = higher transmitted torque.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

1. The prime mover (electric motor / IC engine) rotates the **impeller at speed N₁**.
2. Centrifugal force throws oil from the impeller blades outward against the runner blades.
3. The oil impacts the runner blades tangentially and pushes the runner forward in the same direction.
4. The oil loses kinetic energy and returns toward the impeller centre (the "vortex" or return flow).
5. The runner rotates at **N₂ < N₁** — the difference is called **slip**.
6. The oil circuit is continuous: centre → impeller tip → runner tip → runner centre → back to impeller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why must slip exist?**
> If N₂ = N₁, the oil would have no relative velocity to transfer energy, and torque transmission would stop. A small **slip (2 to 5%)** is essential and is a normal operating feature, not a defect.

## 2.3 Slip, Speed Ratio & Efficiency

The three performance indices of a fluid coupling are interrelated:

- **Slip (s):** $\quad s = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} = 1 - \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$
- **Speed Ratio (i):** $\quad i = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 1 - s$
- **Efficiency (η):** $\quad \eta = \dfrac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} = \dfrac{T \cdot N_2}{T \cdot N_1} = 1 - s$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Favourite):** In a fluid coupling, **torque in = torque out** (no multiplication). Hence the efficiency is **numerically equal to the speed ratio**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slip (fraction) | $s = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1}$ | dimensionless | Always positive |
| 2 | Slip (%) | $s\% = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} \times 100$ | % | Typical 2–5% |
| 3 | Speed ratio | $i = N_2 / N_1$ | — | $= 1 - s$ |
| 4 | Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - s$ | — | $= i$ |
| 5 | Power | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W (N, T in rps, N·m) | Same for input & output torque |
| 6 | Centrifugal head | $H_c = \dfrac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g} = \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2 r^2}{1800\,g}$ | m | $\omega = \pi N / 30$ |
| 7 | Tangential (whirl) velocity at radius r | $V_w = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | For radial-blade machines |
| 8 | Torque transmitted (Euler) | $T = \rho \, Q \,(V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$ | N·m | $\rho$ = fluid density, Q = discharge |
| 9 | Torque for radial blades, shockless exit | $T = \rho \, Q \, V_{w1} \, r_1$ | N·m | $V_{w2} = 0$ |
| 10 | Torque (in terms of dimensions) | $T = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3600} \, \rho \, Q \, D_1^2 \, N_1$ | N·m | For purely radial vane design |
| 11 | Discharge through coupling | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Oil flow rate}}{60} \times 10^{-3}$ | m³/s | If Q is in L/min |
| 12 | Slip in rps | $\Delta N = N_1 - N_2$ | rps | Used in torque–speed plots |

> [!NOTE]
> The radial-blade assumption means the **absolute velocity at impeller exit (and runner entry) is purely tangential**, simplifying Euler's equation considerably. This is the most common design case asked in KTU papers.

## 2.5 Why Are Fluid Couplings Used in Engineering?

- **Soft start / cushioned start** — prevents shock loading of the motor and driven machine.
- **Overload protection** — when the driven load stalls, the oil simply slips harmlessly.
- **Damping torsional vibrations** — protects crankshafts of diesel engines.
- **Torsional isolation** — protects the motor from back-reflected loads.
- **No mechanical wear** — no gears, no friction surfaces in contact.

> **Common applications:** Diesel-electric locomotives, large conveyor drives, crusher mills, centrifugal pumps, marine propulsion shaft lines, and fans in boiler feed systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Centrifugal Head Developed by Impeller

The oil at the tip of the impeller (radius $r_1$) rotates with the impeller at angular velocity $\omega_1 = \pi N_1 / 30$.

The tangential velocity of the oil particle at the tip is:
$V_{w1} = \omega_1 \, r_1 = \dfrac{\pi N_1}{30} \cdot r_1$

**Step 1 — Centripetal acceleration** of an oil particle moving in a circle:
$a = \omega_1^{\,2} \, r_1$

**Step 2 — Equivalent head** of liquid raised by this acceleration (kinetic energy per unit weight):
$H_c = \dfrac{V_{w1}^{\,2}}{2g} = \dfrac{\omega_1^{\,2} \, r_1^{\,2}}{2g}$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $\omega_1 = \pi N_1 / 30$:
$H_c = \dfrac{\pi^2 N_1^{\,2} r_1^{\,2}}{2 \times 900 \times g} = \dfrac{\pi^2 N_1^{\,2} D_1^{\,2}}{8 \times 900 \times g}$

$$
\boxed{\,H_c = \dfrac{\pi^2 N_1^{\,2} D_1^{\,2}}{7200\,g} \;\;\text{metres of oil}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation for Torque

Consider a mass $m$ of oil leaving the impeller at radius $r_1$ with tangential velocity $V_{w1}$ and entering the runner. The rate of mass flow is $\dot{m} = \rho Q$.

**Step 1 — Angular momentum at impeller outlet:**
$L_1 = m \, V_{w1} \, r_1$

**Step 2 — Angular momentum at runner outlet** (radial exit, $V_{w2} = 0$):
$L_2 = m \, V_{w2} \, r_2 = 0$

**Step 3 — Torque (rate of change of angular momentum):**
$T = \dot{m}\,(V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2)$

**Step 4 — Substituting** $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ and assuming **radial exit** ($V_{w2} = 0$):
$T = \rho \, Q \, V_{w1} \, r_1$

**Step 5 — Substitute** $V_{w1} = \pi D_1 N_1 / 60$ and $r_1 = D_1 / 2$:

$$
T = \rho \, Q \cdot \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} \cdot \frac{D_1}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3600} \, \rho \, Q \, D_1^{\,2} \, N_1 \;\;\text{N·m}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the **workhorse of KTU numericals** on fluid couplings. Given any three of $\{\rho,\, Q,\, D_1,\, N_1,\, T\}$, the fourth can be computed directly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Slip, Speed Ratio & Efficiency

**Step 1 — Power input to the coupling** (at the impeller):
$P_{\text{in}} = T \cdot \omega_1 = T \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N_1}{60}$

**Step 2 — Power output from the coupling** (at the runner):
$P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega_2 = T \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N_2}{60}$

**Step 3 — Efficiency** (no torque multiplication in a fluid coupling, so torques cancel):
$\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{T \cdot N_2}{T \cdot N_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$

**Step 4 — Define slip:**
$s = 1 - \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 1 - s$

**Step 5 — Combine:**
$\eta = 1 - s$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta = 1 - s = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = i\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often write $\eta = (1-s)^2$ (mistaking it with a torque converter). In a pure fluid coupling, $\eta = 1 - s$ **only**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Oil Discharge (Working Fluid Flow Rate)

The oil is the working medium and the **flow rate $Q$ is treated as a design parameter** (not derived from first principles, but specified).

**Step 1 — Given the volumetric flow rate** in L/min, say $Q_L$:
$Q \left(\text{m}^3/\text{s}\right) = \dfrac{Q_L \times 10^{-3}}{60}$

**Step 2 — Useful tip for KTU numericals** — if **discharge through coupling** $Q_c$ is related to oil quantity per second in a **scoop tube** system:
$Q_c = V \cdot A_{\text{orifice}}$, where $V = \sqrt{2 g H_c}$ (Torricelli's law) and $A_{\text{orifice}}$ is the cross-section of the scoop.

$$
V = \sqrt{2 g H_c} = \sqrt{2 g \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2 N_1^{\,2} D_1^{\,2}}{7200\,g}} = \dfrac{\pi N_1 D_1}{60\sqrt{2}}
$$

This tangential velocity equals the impeller tip velocity divided by $\sqrt{2}$ (assuming shockless oil return).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Variable Map for Numerical Problems

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | Impeller outer diameter | 0.2 m – 1.5 m |
| $D_2$ | Runner inner diameter | $D_1 - (6$ to $10)\,\text{mm}$ |
| $N_1$ | Input speed | 750 – 3000 rpm |
| $s$ | Slip | 0.02 – 0.05 (2 – 5%) |
| $\rho$ | Density of oil | 850 – 900 kg/m³ |
| $Q$ | Oil flow rate | 0.005 – 0.05 m³/s |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency | 0.95 – 0.98 |
| $T$ | Torque | 100 – 50000 N·m |

## 3.6 Master Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem (Model):** A fluid coupling has an impeller diameter of **400 mm** and is supplied with oil of density **860 kg/m³** at a flow rate of **0.012 m³/s**. The driving motor runs at **1440 rpm**. Assuming radial blade design and shockless exit, determine:

(a) the torque transmitted,
(b) the power transmitted,
(c) the runner speed if slip is 4%,

**Solution:**

**(a) Torque:**
Using $T = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3600} \, \rho \, Q \, D_1^{\,2} \, N_1$:
- $D_1 = 0.4$ m, $D_1^2 = 0.16$ m²
- $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$
- $N_1 = 1440$ rpm

$T = \dfrac{9.8696}{3600} \times 860 \times 0.012 \times 0.16 \times 1440$

$T = 0.002742 \times 860 \times 0.012 \times 0.16 \times 1440$

$T = 0.002742 \times 860 \times 2.7648$

$T = 0.002742 \times 2377.7$

$\boxed{T \approx 6.52 \;\text{N·m}}$

**(b) Power:**
$P = \dfrac{2 \pi N_1 T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1440 \times 6.52}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 9388.8}{60} = \dfrac{58991}{60}$

$\boxed{P \approx 983.2\;\text{W} \approx 0.98\;\text{kW}}$

**(c) Runner Speed** (4% slip):
$N_2 = N_1 (1 - s) = 1440 \times 0.96$

$\boxed{N_2 = 1382.4 \;\text{rpm}}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

The schematic below maps the **functional flow of energy and oil** inside a fluid coupling, since a 2-D physical drawing of curved blades is difficult to render in ASCII/Mermaid. The diagram is fully KTU 2024-compliant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRIME MOVER MOTOR OR DIESEL ENGINE] --> B[DRIVING SHAFT]
    B --> C[IMPELLER PUMP MEMBER]
    C --> D[WORKING FLUID OIL CIRCULATION CHAMBER]
    D --> E[RUNNER TURBINE MEMBER]
    E --> F[DRIVEN SHAFT]
    F --> G[DRIVEN MACHINE LOAD]

    D --> H[OIL RESERVOIR AND SCOOP TUBE]
    H --> C

    C -. TORQUE INPUT TIN .-> D
    D -. TORQUE OUTPUT TOUT EQUAL TO TIN .-> E

    C -. SLIP SPEED N1 MINUS N2 .-> D
    D -. POWER LOSS DUE TO SLIP .-> I[HEAT DISSIPATED IN OIL]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#808b96,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Description (KTU 2024 Visual Aid)

| Block Label | Component | Function in Fluid Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| PRIME MOVER | Electric motor or IC engine | Provides input mechanical power at speed $N_1$ |
| DRIVING SHAFT | Input shaft | Carries torque from motor to impeller |
| IMPELLER | Pump / Primary member | Throws oil outward by centrifugal action |
| WORKING CHAMBER | Sealed housing with oil | Site of energy transfer between impeller and runner |
| RUNNER | Turbine / Secondary member | Receives oil kinetic energy and rotates at $N_2$ |
| DRIVEN SHAFT | Output shaft | Delivers torque to the load |
| DRIVEN MACHINE | Pump, fan, conveyor, etc. | Final user of transmitted power |
| OIL RESERVOIR | Scoop / sump | Maintains and replenishes working oil |
| HEAT LOSS | Thermal dissipation | Energy lost = $(N_1 - N_2) \cdot T$ |

## 4.3 Conceptual Cross-Section (Verbal Schematic)

A fluid coupling cross-section can be described verbally as follows (for your notebook sketch):

- **Outer shell** of the coupling is a stationary or rotating sealed housing.
- **Inside**, two **D-shaped** or **ring-shaped** castings (impeller and runner) face each other across a small gap.
- Each has **20 to 30 curved radial vanes** on its inner/outer face.
- The impeller is keyed to the **input shaft**, the runner to the **output shaft**.
- Oil fills **about 80%** of the chamber — leaving 20% as an air cushion to allow thermal expansion.
- A **scoop tube** enters through the housing and dips into the rotating oil to feed or drain the coupling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why curved vanes?** Curved (or "radial with backward sweep") blades ensure **shockless entry** of oil into the runner, reducing cavitation and noise. KTU may ask why pure radial blades are commonly used — answer: simplicity of manufacture and matching of impeller/runner geometry for shockless transfer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A, Module 3]**
*Define slip in a fluid coupling. Why is slip necessary for its operation?*

**Model Answer:**
Slip in a fluid coupling is defined as the **difference between the driving (impeller) speed $N_1$ and the driven (runner) speed $N_2$, expressed as a fraction of $N_1$**:
$s = (N_1 - N_2) / N_1$

**Slip is necessary** because torque transmission depends on the **relative velocity of the oil with respect to the runner blades**. If the runner rotated at the same speed as the impeller, the oil would have no relative motion across the runner blades, and no tangential force (and hence no torque) would be developed. A small slip (2–5%) ensures continuous energy transfer and also provides overload protection — the runner simply slows down further under high load.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B, Module 3]**
*List any four advantages of fluid couplings over mechanical (rigid) couplings.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Smooth, cushioned start** — no shock load on the motor or driven machine.
2. **Overload protection** — if the load stalls, oil simply slips, preventing mechanical failure.
3. **Torsional vibration damping** — protects the crankshaft of IC engines.
4. **No mechanical wear** — no gears or friction surfaces in contact, hence long life.
5. (Bonus) Self-adjusting to load changes; no lubrication of moving parts other than oil.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q-Set A]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a fluid coupling. How does it differ from a torque converter?*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
- **Impeller (1 m):** Mounted on the driving shaft, fitted with curved radial blades on its inner/outer surface, acts as a centrifugal pump.
- **Runner (1 m):** Mounted on the driven shaft, has matching blades facing the impeller, acts as a turbine.
- **Working chamber (1 m):** Sealed housing filled with oil (≈ 80% fill, 20% air cushion); small gap of 3–5 mm between impeller and runner.
- **Scoop tube and oil reservoir (1 m):** Regulates the quantity of oil entering the working circuit.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Motor rotates impeller at $N_1$. Centrifugal force throws oil outward.
- Oil strikes runner blades and pushes the runner in the same direction at speed $N_2$ ($N_2 < N_1$).
- Oil returns to impeller centre — continuous circulation.
- Torque $T$ is transmitted by virtue of change in angular momentum of the oil.

**Differences from a Torque Converter (1 m):**

| Feature | Fluid Coupling | Torque Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Stator | Absent | Present |
| Torque multiplication | 1:1 | 1 : 2 or 1 : 3 |
| Efficiency curve | Peaks at design point | Drops at high slip |
| Use | Industrial drives | Automobiles |

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** *A fluid coupling of impeller diameter 500 mm receives oil of density 870 kg/m³ at a flow rate of 0.018 m³/s. The driving motor runs at 960 rpm. Assuming radial vanes and shockless exit, calculate:*
- *(i) the torque transmitted,*
- *(ii) the power transmitted, and*
- *(iii) the runner speed at 3% slip.*

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $D_1 = 0.5$ m
- $\rho = 870$ kg/m³
- $Q = 0.018$ m³/s
- $N_1 = 960$ rpm
- $s = 0.03$

**(i) Torque (3 marks):**
$T = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3600} \, \rho \, Q \, D_1^{\,2} \, N_1$

$T = \dfrac{9.8696}{3600} \times 870 \times 0.018 \times 0.25 \times 960$

$T = 0.002742 \times 870 \times 0.018 \times 240$

$T = 0.002742 \times 870 \times 4.32$

$T = 0.002742 \times 3758.4$

$\boxed{T \approx 10.31 \;\text{N·m}}$

**(ii) Power (2 marks):**
$P = \dfrac{2 \pi N_1 T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 960 \times 10.31}{60} = \dfrac{62\,189}{60}$

$\boxed{P \approx 1036.5\;\text{W} \approx 1.04\;\text{kW}}$

**(iii) Runner Speed (2 marks):**
$N_2 = N_1 (1 - s) = 960 \times 0.97$

$\boxed{N_2 = 931.2\;\text{rpm}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Q-Set B]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Derive an expression for the torque transmitted by a fluid coupling, assuming radial vanes at impeller exit and shockless entry at the runner.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 m):** Consider a mass flow rate of oil $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ leaving the impeller tip at radius $r_1$ with tangential velocity $V_{w1}$.

**Step 2 (1 m):** For **radial vanes at impeller exit**, the absolute velocity is purely tangential, so:
$V_{w1} = \omega_1 r_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60}$

**Step 3 (1 m):** At the **runner exit**, with shockless radial discharge, $V_{w2} = 0$.

**Step 4 (2 m):** Apply Euler's turbomachinery equation (rate of change of angular momentum):
$T = \rho Q (V_{w1} r_1 - V_{w2} r_2) = \rho Q \, V_{w1} r_1$

**Step 5 (1 m):** Substitute $V_{w1}$ and $r_1 = D_1/2$:
$T = \rho Q \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} \cdot \dfrac{D_1}{2} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3600} \, \rho \, Q \, D_1^{\,2} \, N_1$

**Step 6 (1 m):** This is the governing design equation; with $\rho$ in kg/m³, $Q$ in m³/s, $D_1$ in m, $N_1$ in rpm, $T$ is obtained in N·m.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** *A fluid coupling is used to drive a centrifugal pump from a 1450 rpm electric motor. The impeller diameter is 350 mm, and the oil (ρ = 880 kg/m³) flow rate is 0.015 m³/s. If the slip is 4% and 10% of the input power is lost as heat, verify whether the design is acceptable. (Hint: compare the actual efficiency with the theoretical efficiency.)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 m):** Theoretical efficiency from slip:
$\eta_{\text{theory}} = 1 - s = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 = 96\%$

**Step 2 (2 m):** Power loss specified = 10%, so:
$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - 0.10 = 0.90 = 90\%$

**Step 3 (1 m):** Since $\eta_{\text{actual}} < \eta_{\text{theory}}$, the **heat loss is more than that predicted by slip alone**, indicating additional losses (bearing friction, windage, churning).

**Step 4 (2 m):** However, 90% is still an acceptable industrial efficiency for a fluid coupling (typical: 95–98%). The design is **marginally acceptable** but not optimal. Recommendation: improve sealing, use a higher viscosity oil, or reduce slip by enlarging impeller diameter.

**Numerical check (bonus):**
$T = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3600} \times 880 \times 0.015 \times 0.1225 \times 1450 = 6.41\;\text{N·m}$
$P_{\text{in}} = 2\pi \times 1450 \times 6.41 / 60 = 973\;\text{W}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing fluid coupling with torque converter** — writing $\eta = (1-s)^2$ instead of $\eta = 1 - s$. Maximum 2-mark penalty per question.
> 2. **Unit mix-up in $N$** — using $N$ in rps instead of rpm, or vice versa, in $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ vs $T = (\pi^2 / 3600) \rho Q D^2 N$. Write the unit check explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the radial-blade / shockless assumptions** in derivations. Examiners allocate 1 mark for this declaration.
> 4. **Confusing slip direction** — slip is always $N_1 - N_2$, never negative. If $N_2 > N_1$ appears in a problem, the system is acting as a pump/turbine, not a coupling.
> 5. **Neglecting to specify oil density** in numericals — always quote $\rho$ value used; if assumed (e.g., 870 kg/m³), state it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **fluid coupling transmits torque through oil only** — no mechanical contact.
- It has **two parts**: impeller (pump) and runner (turbine). **No stator.**
- **No torque multiplication** — $T_{\text{in}} = T_{\text{out}}$; efficiency is purely a function of speed ratio.
- **Slip $s = (N_1 - N_2)/N_1$** is **necessary** for energy transfer; typical value 2–5%.
- **Efficiency $\eta = 1 - s$** (KTU favourite identity).
- **Master equation:** $T = \dfrac{\pi^2}{3600} \, \rho \, Q \, D_1^{\,2} \, N_1$ — applicable for **radial vanes, shockless exit**.
- **Power equation:** $P = 2 \pi N T / 60$ — use $N$ in rps.
- **Centrifugal head:** $H_c = \pi^2 N_1^2 D_1^2 / (7200 g)$ — needed for oil flow problems.
- **Whirl velocity:** $V_w = \pi D N / 60$ — same as impeller tip speed for radial design.
- Fluid couplings are **NOT torque converters** — do not multiply torque; they only **cushion** the drive.
- **Applications:** diesel-electric locomotives, conveyor belts, large fans, crusher mills, marine propulsion.
- **Advantages:** soft start, overload protection, vibration damping, long life, no wear.
- **Limitation:** always 2–5% power loss due to slip; cannot be used where exact synchronism is required.
- **Assumptions to declare in KTU answers:** steady-state, incompressible oil, radial vanes, shockless entry/exit, no windage/friction losses (unless stated).
- **Common KTU question patterns:** (1) Construction + working + diagram; (2) Derivation of torque equation; (3) Numerical on $T$, $P$, $N_2$ given $D_1$, $\rho$, $Q$, $N_1$, $s$; (4) Comparison with torque converter; (5) Slip–efficiency relationship.
- **Memory anchor for $\pi^2$ values:** $\pi^2/3600 \approx 0.002742$ — use this constant directly in numericals to save time.
- **Always state** $g = 9.81$ m/s² explicitly when using head-based equations.
- **Always convert** L/min to m³/s by multiplying by $10^{-3}/60$ before substituting into $Q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Golden Rule:** If a numerical asks for "torque transmitted" and gives you $\rho$, $Q$, $D_1$, $N_1$ — it is the **Euler equation** in disguise. If it asks for "head of oil developed" — it is the **centrifugal head** equation. If it asks for "efficiency" — slip is your friend. Recognise the question type and write the formula first; marks are awarded for the correct formula even if arithmetic slips later.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: BEARINGS – KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY NOTES

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **bearing** is a machine element that constrains relative motion to a desired degree of freedom, reduces friction between moving parts, and transmits loads. In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4 – Bearings), bearings are formally classified into two principal families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:**
> *"A bearing is a mechanical device that supports a rotating shaft or journal, allowing smooth relative motion (rotational or translational) while carrying radial, axial, or combined loads with minimum friction and wear."*

The two main families studied are:

1. **Sliding Contact Bearings (Plain Bearings / Journal Bearings)** – where the load is carried by a continuous film of lubricant between two sliding surfaces.
2. **Rolling Contact Bearings (Antifriction Bearings)** – where the load is transmitted through rolling elements (balls or rollers) that reduce sliding friction to near-rolling friction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The "Ice Skater on Rink" Model:**
> Imagine you are pushing a heavy wooden crate across a room. If you drag it on bare concrete, the sliding friction is enormous. If you place it on rollers (logs), the crate glides easily — this is exactly how a **rolling contact bearing** works. Now imagine a heavy merry-go-round rotating on a thin film of oil between its shaft and a sleeve. The oil film "floats" the shaft, so metal never touches metal — this is the principle of a **hydrodynamic journal (sliding) bearing**. Bearings are essentially the engineering toolkit to make rotation "effortless."

### 1.3 Classification of Bearings (KTU Taxonomy)

| Class | Sub-Type | Loading | Friction | Speed | Lubrication |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sliding** | Hydrodynamic Journal | Radial | Low (µ ≈ 0.001–0.01) | High | Continuous oil film |
| **Sliding** | Hydrostatic Journal | Radial | Very Low | Variable | External pump |
| **Sliding** | Boundary / Mixed | Radial/Thrust | Moderate | Low/Start-Stop | Partial film |
| **Rolling** | Deep Groove Ball | Radial + Some Axial | Very Low | Very High | Grease/Oil |
| **Rolling** | Cylindrical Roller | Heavy Radial | Very Low | High | Grease/Oil |
| **Rolling** | Tapered Roller | Radial + Axial | Low | Moderate | Oil bath |
| **Rolling** | Angular Contact | Radial + Axial | Low | High | Grease/Oil |
| **Rolling** | Thrust Ball | Axial Only | Low | Moderate | Oil |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Petroff's friction coefficient assumption: **f = 0** (frictionless shaft)
> - Bearing characteristic number range: **S = 0.001 to 50**
> - L10 standard life = **1 million revolutions**
> - Exponential life factor: **k = 3 (ball), 10/3 (roller)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Lubrication Regimes in Sliding Bearings (Thick vs. Thin Film)

The load-carrying capacity of a journal bearing depends entirely on the **lubrication regime** in which it operates. The minimum lubricant film thickness (h₀) compared to the surface roughness (R) defines the regime.

| Regime | Condition | Film Pressure | Friction | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrodynamic (Thick)** | h₀ > 3R | Full film | Lowest | High-speed steady operation |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | 0 < h₀ < 3R | Partial film | Moderate | Start-up / shutdown |
| **Boundary Lubrication** | h₀ ≈ 0 | Asperity contact | High | Low speed, high load |
| **Hydrostatic** | External pump | Continuous | Very low | Machine tool spindles |

### 2.2 Petroff's Equation – Friction in Unloaded Journal

Petroff analyzed a frictionless, concentric, lightly loaded shaft floating in a lubricant. The torque is generated solely by viscous shearing.

**KTU Standard Petroff's Equations:**

Friction Force:
$$F = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu \, r^3 \, l \, N}{c}$$

Friction Torque:
$$T = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu \, r^3 \, l \, N}{c} \cdot r = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu \, r^4 \, l \, N}{c}$$

Where:
- μ = dynamic viscosity of lubricant (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- r = radius of journal (m)
- l = length of bearing (m)
- N = rotational speed (rev/s)
- c = radial clearance (m)

Frictional Power Loss:
$$P = 2 \pi N \cdot T = \frac{8 \pi^3 \mu \, r^4 \, l \, N^2}{c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Petroff's Equation matters in KTU exams:** It gives the *minimum* friction (no load). Real loaded bearings always have higher friction because the journal is eccentric.

### 2.3 McKee's Bearing Investigation – The Sommerfeld Number

S.A. McKee (1923) extended Petroff's work by studying a **loaded eccentric journal**. He introduced a dimensionless parameter called the **Sommerfeld Number (So)** or **Bearing Characteristic Number**.

**Sommerfeld Number Definition:**
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, N}{P} \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^2$$

Equivalently, in terms of load (W), pressure (p), and clearance:
$$S_o = \frac{\mu \, N}{p} \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^2 = \frac{Z N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

Where:
- μ = absolute viscosity (reyns or N·s/m²)
- N = journal speed (rev/s)
- P = W/(l·d) = bearing pressure (N/m² or Pa)
- r = journal radius
- c = radial clearance = R − r

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** McKee's discovery was that **all geometrically similar bearings operating at the same Sommerfeld number have the same coefficient of friction, the same eccentricity ratio, and the same minimum film thickness ratio.** This is the foundation of bearing design charts.

### 2.4 Design Variables for Journal Bearings

For a hydrodynamic journal bearing, the KTU design procedure (following Shigley/V.B. Bhandari) uses the following variables:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| W | Load on bearing (N) | Given |
| N | Speed (rpm or rev/s) | Given |
| l/d | Length-to-diameter ratio | 0.5 to 2.0 (optimum ≈ 1.0) |
| c/r | Clearance ratio | 0.001 to 0.002 |
| μ (or Z) | Oil viscosity (reyns) | 0.001 to 0.1 |
| p | Bearing pressure = W/(l·d) | 0.7 to 14 MPa |
| pv | Pressure-velocity product | < 15 MPa·m/s for babbitt |
| T_max | Max oil temperature | 60–80 °C |
| T_in | Inlet oil temperature | 30–50 °C |
| h₀ | Min film thickness | > 0.025 mm |
| S | Sommerfeld number | 0.001 to 50 |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet – Sliding Bearings

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{bearing} = \dfrac{W}{l \cdot d}$ | Bearing pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $pv = \dfrac{W}{l \cdot d} \cdot \pi d N$ | Pressure-velocity product | Pa·m/s |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r c \omega (R^2 - r^2) l}{\text{const}}$ | Lubricant flow (side leakage) | m³/s |
| 4 | $F = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^3 l N}{c}$ | Petroff's friction force | N |
| 5 | $P_{loss} = \dfrac{8 \pi^3 \mu r^4 l N^2}{c}$ | Petroff's friction power | W |
| 6 | $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | Sommerfeld number | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{\rho C_p Q}$ | Temperature rise of oil | K or °C |
| 8 | $h_o = r \, (1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 9 | $h_o / c = (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot (r/c)$ | Min film thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\pi (1 - \varepsilon^2)}{2\varepsilon}$ | Attitude angle | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **Hydrodynamic journal bearings** are used in I.C. engine crankshafts, turbines, gear boxes, and electric motors.
> - **Rolling bearings** are used in bicycles, automotive wheels, conveyors, machine tool spindles, and aerospace applications where starting friction must be low and precision high.
> - The **Sommerfeld number** is the dimensionless "fingerprint" of a bearing's operating regime — used universally in bearing selection charts (published in Shigley, Hamrock, etc.).

### 2.6 Rolling Contact Bearings – KTU Formulation

**Standard Life Equation (L10 Life):**

$$L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^k \text{ million revolutions}$$

Equivalent Dynamic Load (P):
$$P = X \, F_r + Y \, F_a$$

Equivalent Static Load (for static checks):
$$P_o = X_o \, F_r + Y_o \, F_a$$

Where:
- C = dynamic load capacity (from manufacturer catalogue)
- P = equivalent dynamic radial load
- k = 3 for ball bearings, 10/3 for roller bearings
- F_r = radial load
- F_a = axial / thrust load
- X, Y = radial and thrust factors (from catalogue)
- X₀, Y₀ = static factors

**Life in Hours:**
$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \, N} \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^k = \frac{16666}{N} \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^k$$

**Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C) from Catalogue Test:** The load that a bearing can carry for **1 million revolutions** with 90% survival probability.

**Basic Static Load Rating (C₀):** The load that produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 × D** (ball diameter) at the most heavily stressed contact point.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet – Rolling Bearings

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^k$ | Life in million revolutions | 10⁶ rev |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = \dfrac{16666}{N} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^k$ | Life in hours | h |
| 3 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load | N |
| 4 | $P_o = X_o F_r + Y_o F_a$ | Equivalent static load | N |
| 5 | $C = P \left(\dfrac{L_{10h} \cdot N}{16666}\right)^{1/k}$ | Required dynamic capacity | N |
| 6 | $\dfrac{F_a}{C_o} = \text{from catalogue}$ | Determines e, X, Y | — |
| 7 | $k = 3$ (ball), $\dfrac{10}{3}$ (roller) | Exponent | — |
| 8 | $\theta_{rel} = \dfrac{\theta_b}{\theta_{bearing}}$ | Reliability factor | — |
