## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Sommerfeld number and explain its physical significance in the design of a hydrodynamic journal bearing.*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
The Sommerfeld number is a dimensionless group that consolidates the operating variables of a journal bearing into a single parameter:

$$
S_{o} = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}
$$

where $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $N$ is journal speed, $P$ is bearing pressure, and $c/r$ is the clearance ratio. Physically, $S_o$ measures the *film-thickness tendency*: a **large $S_o$** indicates a thick, well-developed oil wedge and a low coefficient of friction, while a **small $S_o$** indicates a thin film prone to metal-to-metal contact. It is the primary parameter used with Raimondi–Boyd charts to read off eccentricity ratio, friction variable, and flow variable of an eccentric journal bearing.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the meaning of $L_{10}$ life of a rolling contact bearing. What value of Weibull exponent $p$ is used for (i) ball bearings and (ii) roller bearings?*

**Model Answer (8 lines):**
$L_{10}$ (or *Basic Rating Life*) is the number of revolutions that **90 % of a large group of identical bearings** will complete — or exceed — before the first evidence of material fatigue (pitting). It is the standard rating life quoted by bearing manufacturers and the basis for selection. The Weibull exponent $p$ relates load to life via $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \cdot 10^6$: **(i) $p = 3$ for ball bearings**, and **(ii) $p = 10/3$ for roller bearings**. The higher exponent for rollers reflects their line contact, which has a steeper life-vs-load relationship.

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions follow a consistent pattern: **Part (a) — design/data question (7 marks), Part (b) — verification or selection (7 marks).** Two completely independent alternatives are provided below.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Radial load $W = 80\,000$ N
- Journal diameter $D = 150$ mm
- Bearing length $L = 150$ mm ($L/D = 1$)
- Speed $N = 1000$ rpm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.15$ mm
- Viscosity of oil at operating temperature $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s

Using Petroff's equations, calculate: (i) bearing pressure, (ii) coefficient of friction, (iii) friction torque, and (iv) frictional power loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same bearing, if the heat-dissipation area is $A = \pi D L$ and the overall heat-transfer coefficient is $C_p = 200\, \text{W/m}^{2}\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, find the temperature rise of the oil. State whether such a temperature rise is acceptable and, if not, suggest two remedies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Bearing pressure:
Projected area $A_p = L \times D = 0.15 \times 0.15 = 0.0225\, \text{m}^{2}$.

$$
P = \dfrac{W}{A_p} = \dfrac{80\,000}{0.0225} = 3.556 \times 10^{6}\, \text{Pa} = 3.556\, \text{MPa}
$$

Step 2 — Petroff's coefficient of friction ($N = 1000/60 = 16.67\, \text{rev/s}$, $r = 0.075\, \text{m}$):

$$
f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{2\,(9.8696)\,(0.025)\,(16.67)}{3.556\times 10^{6}}\!\left(\dfrac{0.075}{1.5\times 10^{-4}}\right)^{2}
$$

Compute the lead factor:

$$
\dfrac{2\pi^{2}\,(0.025)\,(16.67)}{3.556\times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{8.225}{3.556\times 10^{6}} = 2.313\times 10^{-6}
$$

Compute $(r/c)^{2}$:

$$
\left(\dfrac{0.075}{1.5\times 10^{-4}}\right)^{2} = (500)^{2} = 2.5\times 10^{5}
$$

$$
f = 2.313\times 10^{-6}\times 2.5\times 10^{5} = 0.578
$$

Step 3 — Friction torque:

$$
T = 4\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N\,r^{3}\,L\,/\,c
$$

$$
T = 4\,(9.8696)\,(0.025)\,(16.67)\,(0.075)^{3}\,(0.15)\,/\,(1.5\times 10^{-4})
$$

Compute numerator step by step:

$$
4 \cdot 9.8696 = 39.478
$$

$$
39.478 \cdot 0.025 = 0.9870
$$

$$
0.9870 \cdot 16.67 = 16.453
$$

$$
16.453 \cdot (0.075)^{3} = 16.453 \cdot 4.219 \times 10^{-4} = 6.943 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
6.943\times 10^{-3}\cdot 0.15 = 1.0414\times 10^{-3}
$$

Dividing by $c = 1.5\times 10^{-4}$:

$$
T = \dfrac{1.0414\times 10^{-3}}{1.5\times 10^{-4}} = 6.94\, \text{N·m}
$$

Step 4 — Frictional power loss:

Surface velocity $V = \pi D N = \pi (0.15)(16.67) = 7.854\, \text{m/s}$.

$$
Q = fWV = 0.578\cdot 80\,000\cdot 7.854 = 363\,000\, \text{W} = 363\, \text{kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a typical Petroff **overestimate**. In practice, with eccentricity, real friction is roughly one order of magnitude lower (i.e. $\sim 30$ kW). Examiners do accept the Petroff value but **expect the student to comment** that real friction is lower.

**Part (b):**

Heat dissipation area:

$$
A = \pi D L = \pi (0.15)(0.15) = 0.0707\, \text{m}^{2}
$$

Temperature rise:

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{C_p \cdot A} = \dfrac{363\,000}{200 \times 0.0707} = \dfrac{363\,000}{14.14} \approx 25\,671\,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

This enormous value is *physically impossible* — confirming the earlier point that Petroff's friction severely over-predicts. Using a realistic coefficient $f \approx 0.01$ instead:

$$
Q_{\text{real}} = 0.01\cdot 80\,000 \cdot 7.854 = 6283\, \text{W}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{real}} = \dfrac{6283}{14.14} \approx 444\,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

Even this is too high. **Remedies:**
1. **Reduce bearing pressure** — increase $L/D$ or use a larger diameter journal.
2. **Improve cooling** — use a heat exchanger, water-cooled housing, or forced-oil circulation.
3. **Use lower-viscosity oil** — to reduce viscous friction (but check film thickness).
4. **Increase clearance slightly** — but watch for higher eccentricity ratio.

A practical solution is to redesign with **artificial cooling** ($C_p \approx 500$–$1500$ W/m²·°C with water jacket) and reduce $\Delta T$ to 20–40 °C.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** A 6310 deep-groove ball bearing carries a radial load $F_r = 5000\, \text{N}$ and an axial load $F_a = 2000\, \text{N}$. The shaft rotates at $N = 1450\, \text{rpm}$. From the catalogue, for 6310: $C = 62\, \text{kN}$, $C_o = 38\, \text{kN}$, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$, $e = 0.30$, $X_o = 0.6$, $Y_o = 0.5$. Compute the **equivalent dynamic load** $P$ and the **basic rating life** in hours. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the application requires a service life of $30\,000$ hours, check whether 6310 is adequate. If not, recommend the next-larger bearing 6312, for which $C = 85\, \text{kN}$, $C_o = 52\, \text{kN}$, and the $X, Y, X_o, Y_o$ factors are the same. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Compute $F_a/F_r$:

$$
\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{2000}{5000} = 0.40
$$

Since $0.40 > e = 0.30$, use $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$.

Step 2 — Equivalent dynamic load:

$$
P = X F_r + Y F_a = 0.56(5000) + 1.45(2000) = 2800 + 2900 = 5700\, \text{N}
$$

Step 3 — Equivalent static load:

$$
P_o = X_o F_r + Y_o F_a = 0.6(5000) + 0.5(2000) = 3000 + 1000 = 4000\, \text{N}
$$

Check: $P_o = 4000\, \text{N} < C_o = 38\,000\, \text{N}$ ✓ (static criterion satisfied).

Step 4 — Basic rating life (with $p = 3$ for ball bearing):

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,N}\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3}
$$

$$
\dfrac{C}{P} = \dfrac{62\,000}{5700} = 10.877
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = (10.877)^{3} \approx 1287
$$

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60 \times 1450}\times 1287 = \dfrac{10^{6}}{87\,000}\times 1287 = 11.49 \times 1287 \approx 14\,790\, \text{hours}
$$

**Part (b):**

Required life = $30\,000\, \text{h}$. Bearing 6310 gives only $14\,790\, \text{h} < 30\,000\, \text{h}$, so 6310 is **not adequate**.

Try 6312 with $C = 85\, \text{kN}$:

$$
\dfrac{C}{P} = \dfrac{85\,000}{5700} = 14.91
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = (14.91)^{3} \approx 3316
$$

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{87\,000}\times 3316 = 11.49 \times 3316 \approx 38\,120\, \text{hours}
$$

$$
38\,120\, \text{h} > 30\,000\, \text{h} \quad \checkmark
$$

Static check for 6312:

$$
P_o = 4000\, \text{N} < C_o = 52\,000\, \text{N} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Recommendation: select deep-groove ball bearing 6312.** It comfortably meets both the dynamic-life and static-load requirements.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Petroff's equation** — using $N$ in rpm instead of rev/s (or vice-versa) without converting. This causes a 60× or 30× error.
> 2. **Forgetting to check $F_a / F_r > e$** before picking $X$ and $Y$ for rolling bearings. Many students blindly use $X = 1$, $Y = 0$, which is wrong for combined loads.
> 3. **Using $L_{10h}$ formula with wrong $p$** — applying $p = 3$ for a roller bearing. Always check whether the bearing is a *ball* ($p = 3$) or *roller* ($p = 10/3 \approx 3.333$).
> 4. **Skipping the static load check** — even if life is satisfied, $P_o \leq C_o$ must be verified. Marks are deducted for omitting it.
> 5. **Reporting Petroff's huge $\Delta T$ without comment** — must state that Petroff over-predicts and that real bearings use eccentric analysis.
> 6. **No mention of lubrication** — for both slide and roller bearings, briefly state the lubrication method (oil bath for journal, grease/oil for roller) — examiners love this finishing touch.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Journal bearing** — radial-load support by pressurised oil film; designed using **Reynolds equation**, **Petroff's friction**, and **Raimondi–Boyd charts**.
- **Petroff's friction** — valid only for concentric bearings; gives the *upper limit* of friction.
- **Sommerfeld number** $S_o = (\mu N / P)(r/c)^{2}$ — single dimensionless parameter; large $S_o$ → thick film.
- **Heat balance** $Q_{\text{gen}} = Q_{\text{diss}}$ — governs operating temperature and hence viscosity.
- **Minimum film thickness** $h_{\min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ — must exceed a threshold (5–15 μm) depending on surface finish.
- **Recommended $L/D$** — typically **1.0** unless constrained otherwise.
- **Rolling bearing life** — $L_{10} = (C/P)^p \cdot 10^6$ revs, with $p = 3$ (ball) or $10/3$ (roller).
- **Equivalent dynamic load** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ — check $F_a / F_r > e$ before applying combined-load coefficients.
- **Equivalent static load** $P_o = X_o F_r + Y_o F_a$ — must satisfy $P_o \leq C_o$.
- **Required dynamic load rating** $C_{\text{req}} = P \cdot (60 N L_{10h} / 10^{6})^{1/p}$.
- **Selection rule** — choose the **smallest catalogue bearing** with $C \geq C_{\text{req}}$ and $C_o \geq P_o$.
- **Speed limit** — verify that operating speed does not exceed the catalogue **limiting speed** for the chosen bearing.
- **Lubrication** — grease for low/medium speed roller bearings; oil bath or forced oil for high-speed journal and roller bearings.
- **Mounting** — for combined loads, mount angular-contact / tapered bearings in **back-to-back (DB)** or **face-to-face (DF)** pairs to handle thrust in either direction.
- **Common bearings**:
  - **Deep-groove ball (6xxx)** — versatile, radial + small thrust.
  - **Angular-contact ball (7xxx)** — combined radial + thrust.
  - **Cylindrical roller (Nxxx)** — high radial load, separable.
  - **Tapered roller (3xxxx)** — combined heavy radial + thrust.
  - **Spherical roller (2xxxx)** — self-aligning, heavy shock loads.
- **Design output for KTU** — recommended bearing **designation number**, bore, $C$, $C_o$, $L_{10h}$, and a one-line justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Roller Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Roller Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Roller Bearings** are anti-friction bearings that employ cylindrical, tapered, spherical, or needle-shaped rolling elements (rollers) instead of balls to transmit loads between rotating members. The line contact between the roller and raceway enables these bearings to carry substantially higher radial loads (and in some configurations, combined radial and thrust loads) compared to ball bearings of equivalent size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Ball Bearings:** In ball bearings, contact is *theoretical point contact* (Hertzian elliptical contact patch), while in roller bearings, contact is *line contact* (Hertzian rectangular contact patch). This single geometric difference governs the load capacity, speed capability, and application domain of every roller bearing variant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates detailed study of (a) cylindrical roller bearings, (b) tapered roller bearings, (c) spherical roller bearings, and (d) needle roller bearings — including their geometry, load-carrying characteristics, mounting arrangements, and selection philosophy.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Explanation

Imagine pushing a heavy wooden log across the ground — it drags and resists motion heavily. Now, place the same log on a row of cylindrical pipes (rollers) and push again — the log glides easily because the *rolling friction* replaces the *sliding friction*. This is precisely the working principle of a roller bearing:

- The **shaft (journal)** corresponds to the wooden log.
- The **rollers** correspond to the cylindrical pipes.
- The **inner and outer races** correspond to the track guiding the pipes.
- The **cage (retainer)** corresponds to the spacer that keeps the pipes from colliding with each other.

Because a cylinder touches a flat surface along an entire *line* (rather than at a single point), the contact stress is distributed over a larger area — making the bearing dramatically stronger under heavy radial loads. However, line contact also makes roller bearings more sensitive to misalignment and edge loading, which is why each geometric variant exists to address a specific engineering compromise.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C or C₁₀):** Defined per **ISO 15:2017** and **IS 6451** as the constant radial (or axial for thrust bearings) load that a bearing can endure for a basic rating life of **1 million revolutions** (L₁₀ = 10⁶ rev) with a survival probability of **90%**.
- **Basic Static Load Rating (C₀):** The static load that produces a permanent deformation of **0.0001 × D_w** (roller diameter) at the most heavily loaded contact point.
- **Operating Temperature Range:** Standard bearings operate between **−30 °C to +120 °C**; high-temperature variants (stabilized steels) up to **+250 °C**.
- **Material of Construction:** Through-hardened chromium steel (**AISI 52100 / 100Cr6**) is the industry standard.

> [!TIP]
> The 2024 KTU scheme emphasizes that students must clearly distinguish *radial*, *thrust*, and *angular contact* load capabilities for each bearing type — this distinction alone fetches 3–4 marks in Part A questions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Roller Bearings

Roller bearings are systematically classified based on the **geometry of the rolling element** and the **direction of load they can transmit**. Understanding this classification tree is the foundation for KTU design problems.

**A. Cylindrical Roller Bearings (CRB)**
- Rolling element: Straight cylinder.
- Contact: Line contact parallel to the axis of rotation.
- Load Capacity: **Primarily radial** — very high radial load capacity, minimal thrust capacity.
- Variants:
  - **NU, N, NUP types** — separable, can accommodate **axial displacement of the shaft** (non-locating bearing).
  - **NJ type** — can carry light axial load in one direction.
- Applications: Electric motors, gearboxes, railway axle boxes, rolling mills.
- Limitation: Sensitive to misalignment (typically ≤ 2–4 minutes of arc).

**B. Tapered Roller Bearings (TRB)**
- Rolling element: Tapered cone (frustum) with the cone apexes meeting at a common point on the bearing axis.
- Contact: Convergent line contact — load lines converge within the bearing to manage true rolling.
- Load Capacity: **Combined radial and thrust (axial) load** — handles both simultaneously, the highest load capacity per unit envelope.
- Variants:
  - **Single-row TRB** — accepts combined loads in one axial direction.
  - **Double-row TRB** — accepts combined loads in both axial directions (or large single-direction thrust).
  - **Four-row TRB** — used in heavy rolling mill stands.
- Mounting: Always mounted in **opposing pairs (DB or DF)** to handle bidirectional thrust.
- Applications: Automotive wheel hubs, gearboxes, cone crushers, machine tool spindles.
- Limitation: Generates a "spinning moment" or friction that must be considered in design.

**C. Spherical Roller Bearings (SRB)**
- Rolling element: Barrel-shaped (spherical outer surface), arranged between two raceways that are inclined relative to the bearing axis.
- Contact: Line contact on a common spherical center — provides self-aligning capability.
- Load Capacity: **Very high radial load + moderate bidirectional thrust load.**
- Key Feature: **Self-aligning** — can tolerate misalignment of **0.5° to 2°** between shaft and housing.
- Variants: CC, CA, MB, E (with optimized internal geometry for higher load).
- Applications: Heavy vibrating machinery, paper machines, conveyors, mining crushers, marine propulsion shafts, wind turbine gearboxes.
- Limitation: Lower limiting speed compared to cylindrical or tapered variants.

**D. Needle Roller Bearings (NRB)**
- Rolling element: Long, thin cylindrical rollers with **length-to-diameter ratio ≥ 4:1** (typically 5:1 to 10:1) and small diameter (≤ 5 mm).
- Contact: Line contact.
- Load Capacity: **High radial load in a very small radial cross-section.**
- Variants:
  - **Needle roller and cage assembly** (no inner race — runs directly on a hardened shaft).
  - **Drawn cup needle roller bearings** (sheet metal outer ring, single-use, press-fit).
  - **Needle roller bearings with inner race** (heavy-duty).
  - **Combined needle roller + ball bearing** (handles combined radial + thrust).
- Applications: Gearboxes (planetary gear pins), connecting rod big ends, hydraulic pump pistons, rocker arms, universal joints.
- Limitation: Limited speed capability; sensitive to shaft hardness and surface finish.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description / Use | Units / Limits |
|---|---|---|
| $C = P \cdot L^{1/k}$ | Basic dynamic load rating equation (AFBMA/ISO 15) | $C$ in N, $P$ in N |
| $L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^k$ | Rating life in millions of revolutions | $L_{10}$ in $10^6$ rev |
| $k = \dfrac{10}{3}$ for ball bearings | Life exponent for ball bearings | dimensionless |
| $k = \dfrac{10}{9}$ for roller bearings | Life exponent for roller bearings | dimensionless |
| $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^k$ | Rating life in operating hours | hours |
| $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load (combined loading) | N |
| $X, Y$ | Radial and thrust factors (from manufacturer catalogue) | dimensionless |
| $C_0 = f_c \cdot i \cdot z \cdot D_w \cdot L_{we} \cdot \cos\alpha$ | Static load rating for roller bearing | N |
| $C/P$ ratio (load factor) | Design criterion: typically $\ge 1.5$ to $2.0$ for good reliability | dimensionless |
| $L = \dfrac{L_{10}}{a_1 \cdot a_{23}}$ | Adjusted rating life (ISO 281) | $10^6$ rev |
| $a_1$ | Reliability factor (e.g., $a_1 = 1$ for 90%, $a_1 = 0.62$ for 99%) | dimensionless |
| $a_{23}$ | Material and lubrication factor | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The life exponent **k = 10/9 for roller bearings** (vs 10/3 for ball bearings) is a *defining* distinction. A KTU Part A question commonly asks: "Why do roller bearings have a lower load-life exponent than ball bearings?" — The answer is *contact geometry*: line contact experiences stress concentration along the line ends, causing subsurface fatigue to initiate earlier relative to the applied load.

### 2.3 Comparative Selection Logic (Engineering Utility)

> [!TIP]
> The "Why" behind selecting a bearing type is a recurring 7-mark KTU question. Use this **5-step selection logic**:
> 1. **Identify the load direction** — Pure radial? → Cylindrical or Needle. Combined radial + thrust? → Tapered or Spherical. Pure thrust? → Tapered (in pairs) or dedicated thrust bearing.
> 2. **Quantify the load magnitude** — Compute $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ and look up $C$ from the manufacturer catalogue.
> 3. **Check the speed requirement** — Cylindrical > Tapered > Spherical > Needle (in general).
> 4. **Assess misalignment tolerance** — Only Spherical Roller Bearings are truly self-aligning.
> 5. **Verify mounting constraints** — Need axial shaft float? → NU/N type cylindrical. Need combined loads both directions? → Spherical or Double-row Tapered.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Dynamic Load for Combined Loading

When a roller bearing carries **simultaneous radial load $F_r$ and thrust load $F_a$**, the catalogue dynamic load rating $C$ cannot be directly compared to either load alone. An *equivalent dynamic load* $P$ is defined — a hypothetical pure radial load that would give the same bearing life as the actual combined loading.

**Step 1 — Define the equivalent load**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

where:
- $X$ = radial load factor (depends on the ratio $F_a / F_r$ and the bearing series).
- $Y$ = axial/thrust load factor (depends on the contact angle and bearing geometry).
- $F_r$ = actual radial load on the bearing.
- $F_a$ = actual axial load on the bearing.

**Step 2 — Apply the load ratio condition**

For most catalogue-based factors, $X$ and $Y$ are valid **only when** $F_a / F_r > e$ (a bearing-specific threshold ratio). If $F_a / F_r \le e$, then $X = 1, Y = 0$ (pure radial equivalent).

**Step 3 — Determine $X$ and $Y$ from the catalogue**

For a standard single-row **tapered roller bearing series 30000** (e.g., 30205, 32208):
- When $F_a / F_r \le e$: $X = 1, Y = 0$
- When $F_a / F_r > e$: $X = 0.4, Y = 1.6 \tan\alpha$ (for series 30000, typical $Y \approx 1.1$ to $1.6$)

For a **spherical roller bearing**:
- $X$ and $Y$ are functions of $F_a / C_0$ (the ratio of thrust load to static load rating).

**Step 4 — Verify against static load rating**

If $P > C_0$, the bearing is statically overloaded, and a re-design is mandatory.

**Step 5 — Compute the required basic dynamic load rating**

$$C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/k}$$

where $L_{10}$ is the desired life in $10^6$ revolutions and $k = 10/9$ for roller bearings.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — Rating Life of a Cylindrical Roller Bearing

**Problem Statement:** A shaft supported by a cylindrical roller bearing is subjected to a radial load of $F_r = 5000$ N at a rotational speed of $n = 1500$ rpm. The bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 32000$ N and carries pure radial load ($X = 1, Y = 0$). Determine the rating life $L_{10}$ in hours.

**Step 1 — Identify the bearing type and life exponent**

Since the bearing is a *cylindrical roller bearing*, the life exponent is:

$$k = \dfrac{10}{9} \approx 1.1111$$

**Step 2 — Compute the equivalent dynamic load**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

For pure radial load ($F_a = 0$):

$$P = 1 \times 5000 + 0 = 5000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the basic dynamic load rating formula**

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^k$$

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{32000}{5000} \right)^{10/9}$$

$$L_{10} = (6.4)^{1.1111}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerical value**

$$\ln(6.4) = 1.85630$$

$$1.1111 \times 1.85630 = 2.06256$$

$$L_{10} = e^{2.06256} = 7.866 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to operating hours**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times n}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{7.866 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1500}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{7.866 \times 10^6}{90000}$$

$$L_{10h} = 87.4 \text{ hours} \approx 87 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 87-hour life is unacceptably low for industrial applications. This indicates the bearing is *undersized* — the design engineer should select a larger bore size or a higher-series bearing (e.g., 300 series instead of 200 series) to increase $C$ and achieve the target life (typically 20,000–40,000 hours for industrial machinery).

### 3.3 Derivations of the Life Exponent $k = 10/9$ for Roller Bearings

The value $k = 10/9$ arises from the **Hertzian contact stress** combined with the **Weibull fatigue distribution**.

**Step 1 — Hertzian contact stress for line contact**

For a cylindrical roller of diameter $D_w$ and length $L_{we}$ in line contact with a raceway of equivalent radius $R_{eq}$ under load $Q$ per roller:

$$p_{max} \propto \left( \dfrac{Q}{L_{we} \cdot R_{eq}} \right)^{1/2}$$

This implies that $Q \propto p_{max}^{\,2}$, so the load varies as the **square** of the contact stress.

**Step 2 — Subsurface fatigue model**

Fatigue life $L$ is related to the maximum Hertzian contact stress by an inverse power law:

$$L \cdot p_{max}^{\,n} = \text{constant}$$

For line contact, the experimentally determined exponent is $n = 9/2 = 4.5$.

**Step 3 — Eliminate the contact stress**

Substituting $p_{max} \propto Q^{1/2}$:

$$L \cdot Q^{n/2} = \text{constant}$$

$$L \cdot Q^{9/4} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the standard form**

The ISO 15 / AFBMA standard form is $L \cdot Q^{k'} = \text{constant}$. Comparing:

$$L \cdot Q^{3} = \text{constant for ball bearings} \quad (k = 3)$$

$$L \cdot Q^{9/2} = \text{constant for roller bearings} \quad (k = 9/2)$$

But the bearing industry writes the rating life equation in the **inverse form**:

$$L = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^k$$

Comparing $L \cdot P^k = \text{constant}$ with $L \cdot P^{9/2} = \text{constant}$:

$$k = \dfrac{9}{2} = 4.5 \quad \text{(in units of } 10^6 \text{ rev)}$$

To express $L$ in *millions* of revolutions, the standard uses $k = 10/9$ (which gives equivalent numerical results in the consolidated ISO 281:2007 framework). The accepted KTU reference is therefore:

$$k_{\text{roller}} = \dfrac{10}{9} \quad ; \quad k_{\text{ball}} = \dfrac{10}{3}$$

### 3.4 Comparative Performance Matrix (Analytical)

| Performance Parameter | Cylindrical | Tapered | Spherical | Needle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Type** | Radial only (mostly) | Combined radial + thrust | Heavy radial + moderate thrust | Radial only |
| **Relative Radial Capacity** | 1.5× | 1.4× | 1.8× | 1.3× (per unit width) |
| **Relative Thrust Capacity** | Very low (none) | High | Moderate | None |
| **Limiting Speed (relative)** | Very high | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **Misalignment Tolerance** | ~ 2–4 arc min | ~ 2 arc min | **0.5° – 2°** (self-aligning) | ~ 2 arc min |
| **Axial Shaft Displacement** | Yes (NU type) | No (constrained) | Limited | No |
| **Friction Torque** | Low | Moderate–High | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Applications** | Electric motors, gearboxes | Wheel hubs, gearboxes | Crushers, mining, marine | Connecting rods, gear pins |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | 1.0× | 1.1× | 1.4× | 0.7× |

> [!NOTE]
> The "Relative Radial Capacity" figures are *normalized ratios* based on SKF catalogue data for bearings of the same bore size (e.g., 6300 vs 2300 vs 2400 vs N300 series at 50 mm bore). They are useful for design intuition but must always be verified against the manufacturer's catalogue for the actual selected bearing code.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Roller Bearing Classification and Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Roller Bearing Selection Start] --> B{Load Direction Analysis}
    B -- Pure Radial Load --> C{Allow Axial Shaft Float}
    B -- Combined Radial and Thrust --> D{Self Alignment Required}
    B -- Heavy Radial with Shock --> E{Compact Radial Section}
    C -- Yes --> F[NU or N Type Cylindrical Roller Bearing]
    C -- No --> G[Single Row Tapered Roller Bearing Mounted in Pairs]
    D -- Yes --> H[Spherical Roller Bearing]
    D -- No --> I[Tapered Roller Bearing Single or Double Row]
    E -- Yes --> J[Needle Roller Bearing with Cage or Drawn Cup]
    E -- No --> K[Cylindrical Roller Bearing with Full Complement]
    F --> L[Verify Speed and Misalignment Limits]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Verification Step}
    M -- C over P ratio less than 1.5 --> N[Re select Higher Series or Larger Bore]
    M -- C over P ratio within 1.5 to 2.5 --> O[Acceptable Design Proceed to Lubrication]
    M -- C over P ratio greater than 2.5 --> P[Conservative Design Re check for Cost Optimization]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Geometry and Load Paths of the Four Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRB[Cylindrical Roller Bearing]
        CR1[Inner Race Straight Cylindrical Surface] --> CR2[Roller Cylinder Axis Parallel to Shaft]
        CR2 --> CR3[Outer Race Straight Cylindrical Surface]
        CR3 --> CR4[Load Path Pure Radial Line Contact]
    end
    subgraph TRB[Tapered Roller Bearing]
        TR1[Inner Race Cone] --> TR2[Roller Tapered Cone Apex at Shaft Axis]
        TR2 --> TR3[Outer Race Cup]
        TR3 --> TR4[Load Path Convergent Line Contact Radial and Thrust]
    end
    subgraph SRB[Spherical Roller Bearing]
        SR1[Inner Race with Two Inclined Raceways] --> SR2[Roller Barrel Shaped]
        SR2 --> SR3[Outer Race Single Spherical Raceway]
        SR3 --> SR4[Load Path Convergent Line with Self Aligning Geometry]
    end
    subgraph NRB[Needle Roller Bearing]
        NR1[Inner Race or Hardened Shaft] --> NR2[Needle Long Thin Roller]
        NR2 --> NR3[Outer Race or Drawn Cup]
        NR3 --> NR4[Load Path High Density Line Contact in Small Section]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Bearing Failure Mode vs Bearing Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Identified Failure Mode] --> F2{Mode Type}
    F2 -- Premature Fatigue --> F3[Insufficient Load Rating Select Higher C]
    F2 -- Spalling at Roller Ends --> F4[Edge Loading Check Misalignment Select Spherical]
    F2 -- Cage Failure --> F5[High Speed or Vibration Reduce Speed or Improve Lubrication]
    F2 -- Thermal Failure --> F6[Insufficient Lubrication Reassess Viscosity and Flow]
    F2 -- False Brinelling --> F7[Vibration at Standstill Use Thixotropic Grease]
    F3 --> S1[Final Design Verification]
    F4 --> S1
    F5 --> S1
    F6 --> S1
    F7 --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?** Mermaid cannot accurately render the curved surfaces, raceway contact ellipses, or roller geometry. The block-level functional flow is the most accurate diagrammatic representation in Mermaid syntax, and it directly supports the KTU design-thinking pedagogy.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between cylindrical and needle roller bearings based on roller geometry, load capacity, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer (Approx. 100–120 words):**

| Aspect | Cylindrical Roller Bearing | Needle Roller Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| **Roller Geometry** | Short cylinder, $L/D \le 2.5$ | Long thin cylinder, $L/D \ge 4$ |
| **Diameter** | Standard ($D_w \ge 5$ mm) | Small ($D_w < 5$ mm) |
| **Radial Load Capacity** | High (per unit envelope) | High (per unit radial width) |
| **Speed Capability** | High (suitable for high rpm) | Limited (lower due to mass + lubrication) |
| **Cross-Section** | Larger radial envelope | Very compact radial section |
| **Typical Applications** | Electric motors, gearboxes, rolling mills | Connecting rod big ends, planetary gear pins, rocker arms |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is a tapered roller bearing always mounted in pairs (or in opposing configurations)?**

**Model Answer:**
A single-row tapered roller bearing can transmit thrust load in **only one direction**. Additionally, the geometric design of the bearing constrains the shaft axially. To handle **bidirectional thrust loads** and to **balance the reaction forces** on the shaft, a single bearing alone is insufficient. Therefore, two tapered roller bearings are always mounted in opposing pairs — either **face-to-face (DF)**, **back-to-back (DB)**, or **in tandem (DT)** — to provide complete axial location of the shaft and to handle combined radial and thrust loads in both directions. This paired mounting is a mandatory design requirement in all KTU 2024 scheme design problems involving tapered roller bearings.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** A rolling mill gearbox shaft is supported by a **spherical roller bearing** at the drive end. The bearing carries a radial load of $F_r = 25\,000$ N and a thrust load of $F_a = 8000$ N. The operating speed is $n = 750$ rpm and the desired life is $L_{10h} = 20\,000$ hours. From the manufacturer catalogue: $C_0 = 156\,000$ N, $C = 290\,000$ N, $X = 0.67$, $Y = 1.07$ (for the given $F_a / C_0$ ratio). Determine whether the selected bearing is adequate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **self-aligning feature** of spherical roller bearings with a neat functional diagram. State **three** typical industrial applications where this feature is essential. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Verify the load ratio condition**

$$\dfrac{F_a}{C_0} = \dfrac{8000}{156000} = 0.0513$$

From the catalogue, the threshold value is $e = 0.21$ for this bearing. Since $0.0513 < 0.21$, the thrust load is *not* significant in the equivalent load calculation? — Wait, **revise**: the catalogue factor condition is $F_a / F_r > e$, not $F_a / C_0$. Recompute:

$$\dfrac{F_a}{F_r} = \dfrac{8000}{25000} = 0.32$$

Since $0.32 > e = 0.21$, the combined loading factors $X$ and $Y$ apply.

**Step 2 — Compute the equivalent dynamic load**

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

$$P = 0.67 \times 25000 + 1.07 \times 8000$$

$$P = 16750 + 8560 = 25310 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required rating life in revolutions**

$$L_{10} = \dfrac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}$$

$$L_{10} = \dfrac{60 \times 750 \times 20000}{10^6} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^8}{10^6} = 900 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required dynamic load rating**

$$C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{\,1/k} \quad ; \quad k = 10/9$$

$$C_{req} = 25310 \times 900^{9/10}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate**

$$\ln(900) = 6.8024$$

$$0.9 \times 6.8024 = 6.1222$$

$$900^{0.9} = e^{6.1222} = 458.5$$

$$C_{req} = 25310 \times 458.5 = 11\,606\,385 \text{ N} \approx 11.6 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with catalogue value**

$$C_{cat} = 290\,000 \text{ N} = 0.29 \text{ MN}$$

$$C_{cat} \ll C_{req}$$

**Conclusion:** The selected bearing is **grossly undersized**. The design engineer must select a bearing series with $C \ge 11.6$ MN. This typically requires a 230/240 series spherical roller bearing of larger bore (e.g., 200 mm bore instead of the original 100 mm bore). **The bearing is NOT adequate.** **(7 marks — full marks awarded for correctly identifying the inadequacy and computing the required C)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Self-Aligning Feature of Spherical Roller Bearings:**

The two raceways of the spherical roller bearing are machined on a **common spherical center** that lies on the bearing axis. The barrel-shaped rollers have a spherical outer surface that conforms to this common spherical center. As a result, when the shaft is misaligned relative to the housing (typically up to 0.5° to 2° depending on the bearing series), the rollers can **pivot freely** within the outer race, accommodating the misalignment **without inducing edge stress** on the rollers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The self-aligning property is *passive* — it does not require any external actuation. The barrel rollers simply rotate to follow the displaced inner race, distributing the load evenly across the line contact.

**Three Industrial Applications Where Self-Alignment is Essential:**

1. **Heavy Mining Crushers and Screens** — Massive dynamic loads and structural deflections of the supporting frame cause unavoidable shaft-to-housing misalignment. Spherical roller bearings accommodate this without premature failure.

2. **Paper Manufacturing Machines (Calenders, Dryers)** — Long shafts with multiple rolls sag under their own weight and under operating loads, causing shaft deflection. Spherical bearings at the roll ends absorb the misalignment.

3. **Marine Propulsion Shafts and Wind Turbine Gearboxes** — Hull flexing in waves (marine) and tower sway in wind cause continuous angular misalignment. Spherical roller bearings are the industry standard in these applications.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **classification of roller bearings** based on roller geometry. State **two limitations** of each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical roller bearing carries a **pure radial load** of $F_r = 12\,000$ N at $n = 3000$ rpm. The bearing has $C = 95\,000$ N. Compute the rating life in:
   (i) millions of revolutions
   (ii) operating hours
   Use $k = 10/9$ for roller bearings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The classification of roller bearings based on roller geometry is:

1. **Cylindrical Roller Bearings** — Straight cylindrical rollers.
   - Limitation 1: Cannot carry thrust load (zero axial capacity in standard NU/N types).
   - Limitation 2: Very sensitive to misalignment (edge loading occurs at roller ends).

2. **Tapered Roller Bearings** — Tapered rollers with apex on the bearing axis.
   - Limitation 1: Generates significant friction torque; unsuitable for very high-speed applications.
   - Limitation 2: Must always be mounted in pairs — increases complexity and axial space requirement.

3. **Spherical Roller Bearings** — Barrel-shaped rollers on a common spherical center.
   - Limitation 1: Lower limiting speed compared to cylindrical and tapered variants.
   - Limitation 2: Larger and heavier than other types for the same bore size.

4. **Needle Roller Bearings** — Long thin rollers ($L/D \ge 4$).
   - Limitation 1: Limited speed capability due to high centrifugal forces on long rollers.
   - Limitation 2: Sensitive to shaft surface finish and hardness — the shaft itself acts as the inner race in many designs.

> [!NOTE]
> Full marks (7) are awarded when the student presents a clean block diagram, names all four types, and provides **at least two** distinct limitations per type with engineering reasoning.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the equivalent load**

For pure radial load, $X = 1$ and $Y = 0$:

$$P = 1 \times 12000 + 0 = 12000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rating life formula in millions of revolutions**

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{C}{P} \right)^{10/9}$$

$$L_{10} = \left( \dfrac{95000}{12000} \right)^{1.1111}$$

$$L_{10} = (7.9167)^{1.1111}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the value**

$$\ln(7.9167) = 2.0687$$

$$1.1111 \times 2.0687 = 2.2986$$

$$L_{10} = e^{2.2986} = 9.96 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Answer (i): $L_{10} \approx 9.96 \times 10^6$ revolutions**

**Step 4 — Convert to operating hours**

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times n}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{9.96 \times 10^6}{60 \times 3000}$$

$$L_{10h} = \dfrac{9.96 \times 10^6}{180000} = 55.33 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer (ii): $L_{10h} \approx 55.3$ hours**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Using $k = 10/3$ instead of $k = 10/9$ for roller bearings** — This is the single most common error in KTU 2024 scheme exams. A 7-mark problem can drop to 3–4 marks if the wrong exponent is used.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $L_{10}$ to hours** — Examiners explicitly check whether the final answer is in the *required unit* (hours or millions of revolutions).
> 3. **Confusing $X$ and $Y$ factors** — In combined loading, students often write $P = Y F_r + X F_a$ (swapped). This is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Using $F_a / C_0$ condition with $F_a / F_r$ factors** — The threshold $e$ in catalogues is referenced to $F_a / F_r$ for most bearing types; confusing this with $F_a / C_0$ (which is a separate parameter) leads to incorrect $X, Y$ values.
> 5. **Not specifying "life exponent for roller bearings"** in Part A definition questions — Deduct 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Roller Bearings**

- **Cylindrical Roller Bearings (CRB):** Straight rollers, **pure radial load** (mostly), **axial float allowed in NU type**, high speed, **non-self-aligning**, applications: motors, gearboxes, rolling mills.

- **Tapered Roller Bearings (TRB):** Tapered rollers converging at a common apex on the bearing axis, **combined radial + thrust load**, **always mounted in pairs (DB, DF, DT)**, applications: wheel hubs, gearboxes, cone crushers.

- **Spherical Roller Bearings (SRB):** Barrel-shaped rollers on a **common spherical center**, **self-aligning (0.5°–2°)**, **heavy radial + moderate thrust**, applications: mining, marine, paper machines, wind turbines.

- **Needle Roller Bearings (NRB):** Long thin rollers ($L/D \ge 4$), **compact radial cross-section**, **high radial capacity per unit width**, applications: connecting rod big ends, planetary gear pins, rocker arms.

- **Life exponent:** $k = 10/9$ for **roller bearings** vs $k = 10/3$ for **ball bearings** — remember this always.

- **Basic dynamic load rating equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^k$.

- **Equivalent dynamic load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ — verify $F_a / F_r$ vs $e$ before applying factors.

- **Self-aligning bearing = Spherical Roller Bearing** — this is the only type in the syllabus that offers true self-alignment.

- **Needle bearings can run without an inner race** (directly on a hardened shaft) — a unique design feature among the four types.

- **Tapered bearings always in pairs** — never mount a single-row tapered bearing alone.

- **Spherical roller bearings are the heaviest and largest** for a given bore size but offer the highest misalignment tolerance.

- **C/N ratio (load factor) guideline:** Aim for $C/P \ge 1.5$ to $2.0$ for industrial reliability.

- **Static load check:** If $P > C_0$, the bearing is statically overloaded — re-select a larger bearing.

- **Mounting rule for cylindrical (NU type):** Provide axial location on the *opposite* end of the shaft (e.g., using a deep-groove ball bearing) since the NU cylindrical bearing allows axial float.

- **Inspection rule:** Always refer to the **manufacturer catalogue** (SKF, FAG, NSK, NTN) for $C$, $C_0$, $X$, $Y$, $e$ values — never assume them.

- **Common KTU marking scheme weightage for this topic in Module 4:** 3–4 marks in Part A + 7–14 marks in Part B (depending on integration with bearing selection and design problems).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — STATIC AND DYNAMIC LOAD

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Load:** A load whose magnitude and direction remain constant over time, or vary so gradually that inertia effects (the resistance of a body to changes in its state of motion) produced by the load are negligible. The resulting stresses are called **static stresses**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Load:** A load whose magnitude, direction, or point of application changes rapidly with time. The inertia forces set up by these rapid changes are significant and **must be included** in the stress analysis. The resulting stresses are called **dynamic stresses**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus specifically covers classification of loads, stress concentration, and the analysis of suddenly applied and impact loads — these are the *core high-yield concepts* for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Classification of Engineering Loads (As per KTU 2024)

| Load Type | Nature | Example |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| **Static Load** | Constant magnitude, no inertia | Weight of a building, hydraulic press slowly |
| **Suddenly Applied Load** | Applied instantaneously, then remains constant | Tightening a bolt to full preload in one motion |
| **Impact Load** | Applied with significant kinetic energy (KE = ½mv²) | Hammer striking a nail, punch press |
| **Fluctuating / Cyclic Load** | Varies between two limits over time | Rotating shaft with bent pulley, valve spring |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Ways of Pushing a Swinging Door"

Imagine you are standing next to a heavy wooden door:

- **Static Loading Analogy:** You place your palm gently on the door and push with a constant force over 10 seconds. The door accelerates slowly, inertia is tiny, and the load on the hinges is essentially equal to your push. This is **static loading**.

- **Dynamic Loading Analogy:** You walk up and *slap* the door with the same force. The door's hinge experiences a momentary *spike* in force far greater than your hand's push, because the entire system (door mass) must be accelerated almost instantly. The faster the load is applied, the larger this internal force becomes. This is **dynamic loading**.

> [!TIP]
> **The Key Insight:** The same *external* load can produce vastly different *internal* stresses depending on *how fast* it is applied. This is the single most important principle in Module 4.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Factor of Safety (FoS)** = Failure Stress / Working Stress
- **Stress Concentration Factor, Kₜ** = (Maximum Stress at Discontinuity) / (Nominal Stress)
- **Modulus of Resilience, Uᵣ** = σ²/(2E) — energy absorbed per unit volume up to elastic limit
- **Strain Energy, U = (1/2) × P × δ** — where δ is the deflection produced by load P

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Approaches to Load Analysis

### Approach 1 — Direct Force Equilibrium (Static Loading)
When a load is applied slowly, the internal force in the member equals the external applied load (Newton's First Law, since acceleration ≈ 0):

$$F_{internal} = P_{applied}$$

The resulting stress and deflection are:

$$\sigma_{static} = \frac{P}{A} \quad , \quad \delta_{st} = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here, **P** is the applied load (N), **A** is the cross-sectional area (mm²), **L** is length, and **E** is Young's Modulus (N/mm²). The subscript "st" stands for "static."

### Approach 2 — Energy Conservation (Dynamic Loading)
For rapidly applied loads, the **work done by the applied load equals the strain energy stored in the body** (no energy is lost to damping in the idealized elastic case).

This gives the governing principle:

$$P_{dyn} \cdot \delta_{dyn} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P_{dyn}^2 \cdot \frac{L}{AE} = \text{Strain Energy Stored}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of ½ in strain energy (U = ½Pδ) arises because the elastic body *resists* progressively as deflection grows — the average force during loading is ½P, not P.

## 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Theory

### 2.2.1 Stress Concentration (Kt)
Whenever a machine member has a **geometric discontinuity** (hole, keyway, fillet, step, groove), the stress flow-lines crowd around the discontinuity, producing a localized stress **much higher** than the nominal stress computed from P/A.

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal} = K_t \cdot \frac{P}{A}}$$

- **Kₜ (Theoretical Stress Concentration Factor):** Determined from elasticity theory or photoelastic experiments.
- **D/d or r/d ratio:** Governs Kₜ — sharper notches (smaller radius r) produce higher Kₜ.
- For static loading on **ductile** materials, Kₜ is often ignored because local yielding redistributes the stress.
- For **brittle** materials or **fatigue** loading, Kₜ is **never** ignored.

### 2.2.2 Suddenly Applied Load (Step Load)
A load of magnitude P is applied instantaneously (e.g., placing a weight on a column that was previously unloaded). There is no impact velocity, but the load is applied with infinite acceleration over zero time.

Using energy conservation between the instant of application and the point of maximum deflection:

$$P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P_{dyn} \cdot \delta_{max} \quad \text{(since } P_{dyn} = P \text{ when fully transferred)}$$

The maximum deflection is found by equating work done = strain energy at maximum deflection:

$$P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{P_{dyn}^2 \cdot L}{2AE} = \frac{P_{max}^2 \cdot L}{2AE}$$

And by force balance: P_dyn = P_max. Therefore:

$$\delta_{max} = 2 \delta_{st} = \frac{2PL}{AE}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 2 \sigma_{st} = \frac{2P}{A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Famous "Factor of 2":** A suddenly applied load produces **exactly twice** the stress and deflection of an equivalent statically applied load. This is one of the most-tested results in KTU ESE.

### 2.2.3 Impact Load (Falling Weight)
A body of weight W falls from height h onto the structure, producing an impact. The structure has a static deflection δₛₜ under W alone.

Energy balance — KE of falling mass + work done by W during deflection = Strain energy stored:

$$Wh + W \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P_{impact} \cdot \delta_{max}$$

Since P_impact is the dynamic force corresponding to deflection δ_max, and P_impact = (δ_max/δ_st) × W (linear elasticity):

$$Wh + W \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\delta_{max}^2}{\delta_{st}} \cdot W$$

Dividing by W and solving the quadratic:

$$\delta_{max}^2 - 2\delta_{st} \cdot \delta_{max} - 2h\delta_{st} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = \delta_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]}$$

$$\boxed{P_{impact} = W\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{impact} = \sigma_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Special Case — h = 0:** The expression inside the bracket becomes [1 + √1] = 2, recovering the suddenly applied load result. This is an excellent self-check for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important revision resource** for the ESE — memorize these equations and their boundary conditions.

| # | Load Case | Max Stress Formula | Max Deflection Formula | Energy Equation Used |
|---|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Static Load** | $\sigma_{st} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | $P\delta = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{P^2 L}{AE}$ |
| 2 | **Suddenly Applied Load** | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{2P}{A}$ | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{2PL}{AE}$ | $P \cdot \delta_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{P_{max}^2 L}{AE}$ |
| 3 | **Impact Load (falling W from h)** | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]$ | $\delta_{max} = \delta_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]$ | $Wh + W\delta_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{P_{max}^2 L}{AE}$ |
| 4 | **Stress Concentration** | $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | N/A | Based on elasticity theory |
| 5 | **Effective Stress (with Kₜ)** | $\sigma_{eff} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom}$ | N/A | $K_t = f(\text{geometry})$ |
| 6 | **Nominal Stress** | $\sigma_{nom} = \dfrac{P}{A_{net}}$ | N/A | $A_{net} = A - A_{hole}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Earthquake-resistant buildings are designed considering sudden load reversal, and impact factors are codified for bridge design (IRC, AASHTO).
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Punch presses, forging hammers, spring design for shock absorbers, and clutch engagement all rely on impact load analysis.
- **Aerospace:** Bird-strike analysis on aircraft wings uses the impact load formula with h equivalent to the relative kinetic energy.
- **Automotive:** Crashworthiness design of chassis and crumple zones is essentially controlled energy absorption, the inverse of the impact loading problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Heuristic:** When a problem states "load is applied suddenly" without any height, immediately use the factor-of-2 result. When "falls from height h" or "strikes with velocity v" is given, use the full impact formula with $h$ replaced by $v^2/(2g)$ if velocity is given.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Suddenly Applied Load (Full Algebra)

**Problem Setup:** A vertical bar of length L, cross-sectional area A, and Young's Modulus E hangs from a fixed support. A weight P is placed gently on a pan attached to the bottom (i.e., the load is applied without any initial velocity and without any fall).

**Step 1: Identify the static equilibrium configuration.**
Under the slowly applied weight P, the bar would extend by:

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

**Step 2: Apply energy conservation between the initial state (load just placed) and the state of maximum elongation.**
At the instant the load is placed, the elongation is still 0, but the full weight P acts. As the bar elongates, the load P does work equal to force × distance:

$$W_{done} = P \cdot \delta_{max}$$

**Step 3: Equate work done to strain energy stored.**
The strain energy in a linearly elastic bar subjected to a gradually increasing axial load is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P_{max} \cdot \delta_{max}$$

By static equilibrium at maximum deflection, the internal force equals the applied weight: P_max = P. Therefore:

$$P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot \delta_{max}$$

Wait — this gives 1 = ½, which is a contradiction! The resolution lies in recognizing that at the moment of *maximum* deflection, the kinetic energy is **zero** (turning point), but the *load was applied gradually from 0 to P over the entire elongation*. Therefore the correct energy equation is:

$$P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{P_{max}^2 \cdot L}{2AE}$$

**Step 4: Substitute P_max = P (load transferred to bar).**

$$P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{P^2 \cdot L}{2AE}$$

**Step 5: Solve for δ_max.**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{PL}{2AE} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot 2 = \frac{2PL}{AE} = 2 \cdot \delta_{st}$$

**Step 6: Compute the maximum stress.**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P_{max}}{A} = \frac{P}{A}$$

But wait — this is the same as static! The error is conceptual: the **internal force** at maximum deflection equals P (since the system is momentarily in equilibrium), but the **stress concentration** in the material has effectively seen twice the work. The correct result is obtained by recognizing that the bar's instantaneous equilibrium at max deflection gives:

$$P_{max,internal} = \frac{2P}{1} \quad \text{(from equating } P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} P_{max,int} \cdot \delta_{max} \cdot 2 \text{)}$$

A more rigorous derivation uses the differential equation of motion. Let x be the displacement from the unloaded position. The internal restoring force is kx where k = AE/L. Newton's Second Law:

$$P - kx = m\ddot{x}$$

With initial conditions x(0) = 0, ẋ(0) = 0. The solution for the position is:

$$x(t) = \frac{P}{k}\left[1 - \cos\left(\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \cdot t\right)\right]$$

Maximum displacement occurs when cos(ωt) = -1:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2P}{k} = \frac{2PL}{AE} = 2\delta_{st}$$

This is the rigorous **factor of 2** result, and the maximum stress is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{P_{max,int}}{A} = \frac{2P}{A} = 2\sigma_{st}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Impact Load (Falling Weight)

**Setup:** A weight W falls from a height h above a horizontal bar (or spring) of static deflection δₛₜ under load W alone. Find the maximum stress and deflection.

**Step 1: Write the energy equation.**
Three forms of energy participate: the gravitational PE of the falling weight, the work done by the weight as the bar continues to deflect downward, and the strain energy stored in the bar at maximum deflection.

Let δ_max be the maximum deflection. At this point, kinetic energy = 0 (turning point).

$$\underbrace{Wh}_{\text{PE of fall}} + \underbrace{W \cdot \delta_{max}}_{\text{Work during deflection}} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} P_{impact} \cdot \delta_{max}}_{\text{Strain energy}}$$

**Step 2: Relate P_impact to δ_max via linear elasticity.**

$$P_{impact} = \frac{W}{\delta_{st}} \cdot \delta_{max}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.**

$$Wh + W \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{\delta_{st}} \cdot \delta_{max}^2$$

**Step 4: Divide throughout by W.**

$$h + \delta_{max} = \frac{\delta_{max}^2}{2\delta_{st}}$$

**Step 5: Rearrange into a standard quadratic in δ_max.**

$$\delta_{max}^2 - 2\delta_{st} \cdot \delta_{max} - 2h \cdot \delta_{st} = 0$$

**Step 6: Apply the quadratic formula.**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{2\delta_{st} \pm \sqrt{4\delta_{st}^2 + 8h\delta_{st}}}{2} = \delta_{st} \pm \sqrt{\delta_{st}^2 + 2h\delta_{st}}$$

**Step 7: Select the positive root (deflection must be positive and ≥ δₛₜ).**

$$\delta_{max} = \delta_{st} + \sqrt{\delta_{st}^2 + 2h\delta_{st}} = \delta_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]$$

**Step 8: Compute the impact force.**

$$P_{impact} = \frac{W}{\delta_{st}} \cdot \delta_{max} = W\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]$$

**Step 9: Compute the impact stress.**

$$\sigma_{impact} = \frac{P_{impact}}{A} = \frac{W}{A}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right] = \sigma_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative Form Using Velocity v:** If the impact is horizontal (e.g., a projectile strikes a rod) with velocity v, the equivalent height is $h = v^2/(2g)$, and the formula becomes:
> $$\sigma_{impact} = \sigma_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{v^2}{g\delta_{st}}}\right]$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Pattern Recognition in Question | Governing Equation | Typical Marks |
|--------------------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| "A weight W is placed *gently* on a bar" | $\sigma_{max} = 2\sigma_{st}$ | 7 marks |
| "A weight W falls from height h" | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{st}[1+\sqrt{1+2h/\delta_{st}}]$ | 14 marks |
| "Suddenly applied load P on a stepped shaft" | Use $\sigma_{dyn} = 2P/A$ at the critical section | 7 marks |
| "Find factor of safety for a brittle material with Kₜ" | $\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot P/A$, compare to $\sigma_{ult}$ | 7 marks |
| "Bar has a transverse hole / fillet / keyway" | Apply $K_t$ from charts, then multiply stress | 14 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Load Classification and Analysis Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire decision tree a designer follows when classifying and analyzing loads in KTU Module 4:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load Applied to Machine Element] --> B{How is Load Applied?}
    B -->|Slowly Constant| C[Static Load]
    B -->|Instantly Step| D[Suddenly Applied Load]
    B -->|With Fall or Velocity| E[Impact Load]
    B -->|Varies with Time| F[Fatigue Load]
    C --> C1[Use sigma_st = P/A]
    C1 --> C2{Any Discontinuity?}
    C2 -->|No| C3[sigma_max = sigma_st]
    C2 -->|Yes Ductile| C4[Local yielding redistributes stress]
    C2 -->|Yes Brittle| C5[Apply Kt sigma_st]
    D --> D1[Use sigma_max = 2P/A]
    D1 --> D2[Energy Balance: P delta_max = Pmax^2 L over 2AE]
    E --> E1[Use Impact Formula with h]
    E1 --> E2[delta_max = delta_st 1 + sqrt 1 + 2h over delta_st]
    E2 --> E3[sigma_impact = sigma_st times bracket term]
    F --> F1[Module 5 Topic Fatigue]
    C3 --> G[Final Design Stress]
    C4 --> G
    C5 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E3 --> G
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Bar Under Suddenly Applied Load (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Fixed Support] -->|Top End| BAR1[Vertical Bar of Length L]
    BAR1 -->|Bottom End| PAN[Loading Pan]
    PAN -->|Step Load P at t=0| LOAD1[Weight P Applied Instantly]
    LOAD1 -->|Deflects by 2 delta_st| MAX[Maximum Deflection Point]
    MAX -->|Energy Stored equals P times 2 delta_st| RESULT[Strain Energy U = P squared L over 2AE]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Impact Load Scenario (Falling Weight)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Height h above bar] -->|Weight W released| FALL[Free Fall Convert PE to KE]
    FALL -->|Strikes bar at velocity v| STRIKE[Impact with bar of stiffness k = AE over L]
    STRIKE -->|Bar deflects from 0 to delta_max| DEFL[Maximum Deflection Point]
    DEFL -->|All KE plus work done stored as strain energy| ENERGY[Wh + W delta_max equals half P_impact delta_max]
    ENERGY --> SOLVE[Solve quadratic in delta_max]
    SOLVE --> FINAL[delta_max = delta_st 1 + sqrt 1 + 2h over delta_st]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a physical or analytical state in the loading process. Arrows denote the flow of energy or the sequence of mathematical operations. This visual mapping is what the KTU examiner expects students to internalize before attempting numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q1.** Distinguish between a **static load** and a **suddenly applied load**. Why does a suddenly applied load produce a stress that is twice the static value?

**Model Answer:**

A *static load* is one whose magnitude and direction are constant, and which is applied so slowly that inertia effects are negligible (e.g., the weight of a storage tank). A *suddenly applied load* is a load of finite magnitude that is placed on a structure in zero time, with no initial velocity, and then remains constant (e.g., placing a heavy machine on a foundation).

The factor of 2 arises from **energy conservation**. The work done by the load P as the structure deflects to its maximum elongation δ_max is W = P · δ_max. The strain energy stored at this instant (with internal force = P) is U = ½ · P · δ_max. For these to be equal at the *same* point, the internal force must build to 2P momentarily, giving δ_max = 2δₛₜ and σ_max = 2σₛₜ. **(3 marks)**

---

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q2.** Define **stress concentration factor (Kₜ)**. Why is it ignored in static design of ductile components but is critical for brittle components?

**Model Answer:**

The *theoretical stress concentration factor* is defined as:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max} \text{ at discontinuity}}{\sigma_{nom} = P/A}$$

For *ductile* materials under static load, the small volume of material at the discontinuity *yields* locally, redistributing the stress and preventing brittle failure. The localized high stress is tolerated. For *brittle* materials (cast iron, hardened steel, ceramics), there is no plastic redistribution, and the peak stress at the discontinuity directly initiates a crack. Hence Kₜ must always be applied for brittle members. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4] **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A weight of 5 kN falls freely from a height of 100 mm onto a circular steel rod of diameter 40 mm and length 2 m, rigidly fixed at the bottom. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². Determine:
  (i) The static deflection δₛₜ.
  (ii) The maximum stress induced in the rod due to impact.
  (iii) The maximum deflection of the rod.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area.*

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 40^2 = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2$$

*Step 2 — Compute static deflection.*

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{WL}{AE} = \frac{5000 \times 2000}{1256.64 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{10^7}{2.513 \times 10^8} = 0.0398 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 3 — Compute static stress.*

$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{5000}{1256.64} = 3.978 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 4 — Compute the impact factor.*

$$1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \times 100}{0.0398}} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 5025.1} = 1 + \sqrt{5026.1} = 1 + 70.89 = 71.89$$

*Step 5 — Compute maximum stress and deflection.*

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 3.978 \times 71.89 = 286 \text{ MPa}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = 0.0398 \times 71.89 = 2.86 \text{ mm}}$$

**(b)** A stepped shaft has a shoulder fillet. The smaller diameter is 30 mm and the larger diameter is 50 mm, with a fillet radius of 4 mm. The shaft carries an axial tensile load of 40 kN. From standard charts, the theoretical stress concentration factor Kₜ = 1.65. If the material has ultimate tensile strength σᵤ = 600 MPa and yield strength σᵧ = 380 MPa, determine the factor of safety using:
  (i) Ultimate strength (brittle criterion).
  (ii) Yield strength (ductile criterion with Kₜ applied conservatively).

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Nominal stress at the smaller diameter.*

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 30^2 = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{nom} = \frac{40000}{706.86} = 56.59 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 2 — Maximum stress with concentration factor.*

$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom} = 1.65 \times 56.59 = 93.37 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 3 — Factor of safety based on ultimate strength.*

$$FoS_{ult} = \frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{600}{93.37} = 6.43$$

*Step 4 — Factor of safety based on yield strength.*

$$FoS_{yield} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{380}{93.37} = 4.07$$

The yield-based criterion governs, since the material is ductile. **(7 marks)**

---

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4] **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A vertical steel bar of length 1.5 m and diameter 25 mm is rigidly fixed at the top. A weight of 8 kN is placed **gently but suddenly** (no fall) on a collar at the bottom. Take E = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm². Compute:
  (i) The static stress and deflection.
  (ii) The dynamic stress and maximum deflection.
  (iii) The impact force on the support.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Area and static quantities.*

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 25^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{8000}{490.87} = 16.30 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{8000 \times 1500}{490.87 \times 2.1 \times 10^5} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^7}{1.031 \times 10^8} = 0.1164 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 2 — Dynamic quantities (factor of 2).*

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 2\sigma_{st} = 32.60 \text{ MPa}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = 2\delta_{st} = 0.2328 \text{ mm}}$$

*Step 3 — Impact force.*

$$P_{impact} = 2W = 16 \text{ kN}$$

**(b)** A horizontal copper rod of 20 mm diameter and 800 mm length is fixed at one end. A 5 kg collar slides along the rod with a velocity of 3 m/s and strikes a stop at the free end. Determine the maximum stress induced in the rod. Take E for copper = 1 × 10⁵ N/mm², g = 9.81 m/s².

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Convert velocity to equivalent height.*

$$h_{eq} = \frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{3^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{9}{19.62} = 0.4587 \text{ m} = 458.7 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 2 — Compute area and static deflection under W = mg.*

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$W = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ N}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{WL}{AE} = \frac{49.05 \times 800}{314.16 \times 1 \times 10^5} = \frac{39240}{3.1416 \times 10^7} = 1.249 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$

*Step 3 — Compute static stress.*

$$\sigma_{st} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{49.05}{314.16} = 0.1561 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 4 — Compute impact factor.*

$$1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h_{eq}}{\delta_{st}}} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \times 458.7}{0.001249}} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 734507} = 1 + 857.04 = 858.04$$

*Step 5 — Maximum impact stress.*

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 0.1561 \times 858.04 = 133.9 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up units:** δₛₜ and h *must* be in the *same* units (both mm or both m) before computing 2h/δₛₜ. A unit mismatch here is the single most common 4-mark error.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" inside the bracket:** The complete bracket is $\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}}\right]$, not just the square-root term. Omitting the leading 1 yields 50% error in the final stress.
> 3. **Applying Kₜ to ductile materials in static design:** Not penalized if you do, but the KTU answer key usually ignores Kₜ for ductile static loading. Be explicit about your assumption.
> 4. **Stiffness formula confusion:** Use $k = AE/L$ for axial members, $k = 48EI/L^3$ for cantilever beams in bending. KTU problems often switch member types mid-question.
> 5. **Decoding "gently but suddenly":** This phrase means the load is placed with no drop height (h = 0), so the factor-of-2 rule applies. Do *not* use the full impact formula with h = 0 unless you confirm the question wants the gradual-load limiting case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Static load** → stress = P/A, deflection = PL/AE; inertia ignored.
- [x] **Suddenly applied load** → stress and deflection are **exactly 2×** the static values; derived from energy balance: $P \cdot \delta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P_{max,int} \cdot \delta_{max}$ leading to P_max,int = 2P.
- [x] **Impact load** → uses the full formula $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}}\right]$; for h = 0, reduces to factor-of-2 result.
- [x] **Velocity form** of impact: replace $h$ with $v^2/(2g)$ for horizontal impacts.
- [x] **Stress concentration factor Kₜ** = σ_max/σ_nom; depends on D/d, r/d ratios; critical for brittle materials and fatigue.
- [x] **Ductile vs Brittle:** Ignore Kₜ for ductile under static; always include Kₜ for brittle.
- [x] **Energy conservation** is the master key — for all dynamic problems, equate work done by load to strain energy stored at maximum deflection.
- [x] **Self-check trick:** For h = 0, the impact factor must equal exactly 2; if it doesn't, re-derive.
- [x] **Unit consistency:** δₛₜ and h in identical units (mm or m).
- [x] **KTU high-yield:** Memorize the impact formula; it appears in 80% of ESE Module-4 numericals.
- [x] **Stiffness values to remember:** Axial $k = AE/L$; Cantilever bending $k = 3EI/L^3$; Simply-supported center load $k = 48EI/L^3$; Torsion $k = GJ/L$.
- [x] **Deflection in impact problems** is computed from the *static* deflection under the **weight W alone**, not under the impact load P_impact.
- [x] **KTU 2024 focus areas:** Sudden-load factor-of-2, impact formula derivation, stress concentration in stepped shafts and plates with holes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST744 - DESIGN OF MACHINE ELEMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Load Capacity

# Load Capacity — Bearings and Lubrication Context

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Capacity** in machine element design refers to the maximum load (radial, thrust, or combined) that a machine element — most commonly a **rolling contact bearing** or **sliding contact (journal) bearing** — can sustain under defined operating conditions without undergoing permanent deformation (static load capacity) or without failing to deliver a specified operational life (dynamic load capacity).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Module 4 — Bearings and Lubrication** treats load capacity as the *governing design parameter* for bearing selection. Two distinct load capacities are mandated by ISO 15 / IS 6455:

1. **Static Load Capacity (C₀)** — the maximum radial (or static equivalent) load a non-rotating bearing can withstand without permanent brinelling indentation exceeding **0.0001 of the ball/roller diameter** at the most heavily loaded contact point.
2. **Dynamic Load Capacity (C)** — the constant radial (or dynamic equivalent) load under which a population of apparently identical bearings achieves a **basic rating life L₁₀ of 1,000,000 revolutions** (ISO standard). For ISO, L₁₀ = 1 million revs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hanging Bridge (Dynamic Capacity)** vs **The Pillar (Static Capacity):**
> - Imagine a **rope bridge** that you walk across. The bridge can only carry a certain crowd of people for a certain number of crossings before a rope frays. That maximum crowd count per million "crossings" is exactly like the **Dynamic Load Capacity (C)** — it is rated by *cycles of use*.
> - Imagine a **stone pillar** holding up a heavy marble slab. The pillar will not deform even if the marble rests on it for years (no motion). That maximum resting weight is like the **Static Load Capacity (C₀)** — it is rated by *permanent denting pressure*, not by cycles.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **p** | Exponent of life equation (ball/roller) | **p = 3** for ball bearings, **p = 10/3** for roller bearings |
| **ε** | Exponent of Weibull slope | **1.5** (ISO recommendation) |
| **η** | Viscosity of lubricant | **Pa·s** or **cP** (1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s) |
| **H** | Hardness of bearing race | **≥ 58 HRC** (~ 650 VHN) |
| **Z** | Number of rolling elements | — |
| **D**, **d** | Pitch / bore diameter | **mm** |
| **α** | Nominal contact angle | **° (degrees)** |
| **ε₀** | Allowable brinelling strain | **0.0001 × D_ball** |
