# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q1]**

**Q1(a).** Differentiate between the **Lewis form factor (Y)** and the **Lewis bending stress (σ_b)**. Why is Y tabulated as a function of the number of teeth (z)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Lewis form factor Y** is a **dimensionless geometric parameter** that accounts for the shape (taper, fillet radius, location of load) of an involute gear tooth. It is **not** a stress.
- **Lewis bending stress σ_b** is the **actual induced fibre stress** at the tooth root, computed (in MPa) from $\sigma_b = W_t C_v / (b \, m \, Y)$.
- Y is tabulated as a function of $z$ because **as the number of teeth increases, the tooth becomes relatively thicker at the root (proportionate to pitch radius)**, which lowers the peak bending stress for the same load.
- Thus Y has no units, while σ_b has units of N/mm².

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q2]**

**Q1(b).** State **Buckingham's equation for wear strength** of a spur gear pair with 20° pressure angle. What material assumption is implicit in the constant "0.564"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Buckingham's contact stress (steel-on-steel, 20° pressure angle):

$$\sigma_c = 0.564 \, \sqrt{ \frac{W_t \, C_v \, (r_1 + r_2)}{b \, r_1 \, r_2 \, \sin\phi} } \quad \text{N/mm}^{2}$$

Implicit assumptions: **both pinion and gear are made of steel** with:
- Young's Modulus $E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm²
- Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$

These two values combine with the geometric terms to yield the constant 0.564. For other material pairs the constant changes.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Design Problems (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 4 Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to **multiply by velocity factor $C_v$** when computing effective load — silent loss of 1 mark.
> 2. Using **gear pitch diameter** in the wear-strength equation — must use **pinion pitch diameter $d_p$** always.
> 3. **Rounding module down** to a lower standard value — the rule is **round UP** to the next preferred number.
> 4. Forgetting to **re-verify C_v** after computing actual pitch-line velocity — iterative mismatch is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 5. For Lewis form factor, ensure you read the value **at the correct pressure angle** (20° vs 14.5°). KTU 2024 default is **20° full-depth**.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**  [KTU 2024 Model – Module 4, Q4(a)]
*Design a pair of spur gears to transmit 15 kW at 1440 rpm of the pinion. The speed reduction is 3:1. The gears are to be made of steel with allowable bending stress 100 N/mm² and allowable contact stress 600 N/mm². Assume velocity factor $C_v = 1.4$ and face-width ratio $\phi_b = 10$. Take 20° full-depth teeth.*

**Part (a) — 7 marks: Compute pitch-line velocity, tangential load, pinion teeth, and form factor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Torque on pinion:**

$$M_t = \frac{60{,}000 \times P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 15{,}000}{2 \pi \times 1440} = 99{,}471.6 \text{ N·mm} \approx 99{,}472 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Assume pinion teeth $z_1 = 18$** (to avoid interference and ensure good Y).

Gear ratio $i = 3$, so $z_2 = 3 \times 18 = 54$.

**Step 3 — Form factor for $z_1 = 18$:** $Y_1 = 0.309$ (interpolated from table).

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Determine module from Lewis and Buckingham, select standard module, verify face width.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 4 — Lewis module:**

$$m_L = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, M_t \, C_v}{\sigma_b \cdot b \cdot Y \cdot z_1}}$$

With $b = \phi_b \cdot m$, this is implicit. We solve the **explicit cubic** form for module:

$$m^{3} = \frac{2 \, M_t \, C_v}{\sigma_b \cdot \phi_b \cdot Y \cdot z_1}$$

$$m^{3} = \frac{2 \times 99{,}472 \times 1.4}{100 \times 10 \times 0.309 \times 18} = \frac{278{,}521.6}{5{,}562} = 50.08$$

$$m_L = (50.08)^{1/3} = 3.685 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Buckingham module:**

Using wear strength for steel, 20°:

$$S_w = \frac{b \, d_p \, Q}{2} \geq W_t \cdot C_v$$

where $W_t = 2M_t / d_p$, so:

$$\frac{b \, d_p \, Q}{2} = \frac{2 M_t \, C_v}{d_p}$$

$$d_p^{2} = \frac{4 M_t \, C_v}{b \, Q}$$

With $Q = \dfrac{2 z_2}{z_1 + z_2} = \dfrac{2 \times 54}{18 + 54} = \dfrac{108}{72} = 1.5$:

$$d_p^{2} = \frac{4 \times 99{,}472 \times 1.4}{b \times 1.5} = \frac{557{,}043.2}{1.5 \, b}$$

With $b = 10 m$ and $d_p = m \, z_1 = 18 m$:

$$(18 m)^{2} = \frac{557{,}043.2}{1.5 \times 10 m}$$

$$324 m^{2} \times 1.5 \times 10 \, m = 557{,}043.2$$

$$4860 \, m^{3} = 557{,}043.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m^{3} = 114.62$$

$$m_W = (114.62)^{1/3} = 4.862 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Select standard module:** $m = \max(3.685, 4.862) = 4.862$ → **round UP to standard m = 5 mm** (preferred series).

**Step 7 — Verify:**

- $d_p = 5 \times 18 = 90$ mm
- $v = \pi d_p N / 60{,}000 = \pi \times 90 \times 1440 / 60{,}000 = 6.79$ m/s → $C_v$ from table ≈ 1.4, **OK assumed value consistent**.
- $b = 10 \times 5 = 50$ mm.
- $W_t = 2 M_t / d_p = 2 \times 99{,}472 / 90 = 2210.5$ N.
- $\sigma_b = W_t \, C_v / (b \, m \, Y) = 2210.5 \times 1.4 / (50 \times 5 \times 0.309) = 3094.7 / 77.25 = 40.06$ N/mm² ≤ 100 ✓
- $\sigma_c = 0.564 \sqrt{2210.5 \times 1.4 \times (45 + 135) / (50 \times 45 \times 135 \times \sin 20°)} = 0.564 \sqrt{554{,}300 / (50 \times 6075 \times 0.342)} = 0.564 \sqrt{554{,}300 / 103{,}882} = 0.564 \sqrt{5.336} = 0.564 \times 2.310 = 1.30$ **N/mm²** ≤ 600 ✓ (very safe)

**Final Design:** $m = 5$ mm, $z_1 = 18$, $z_2 = 54$, $b = 50$ mm, $C_v = 1.4$.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)**  [KTU 2024 Model – Module 4, Q4(b)]
*A spur gear pinion running at 900 rpm transmits 7.5 kW to a gear pair with velocity ratio 4.5:1. Design the gear pair based on the **Lewis equation** only, taking $Y = 0.32$ for the pinion, allowable bending stress 84 N/mm² for the pinion and 105 N/mm² for the gear, and face width 60 mm. Assume the gears are made of cast iron with $C_v = 1.5$.*

**Part (a) — 7 marks: Compute torque, tangential load, and the safe module on the pinion.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pinion torque:**

$$M_t = \frac{60{,}000 \times 7500}{2\pi \times 900} = 79{,}577.5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Pitch line velocity — assume d_p and iterate.** Start with $d_p = 75$ mm:

$$v = \frac{\pi d_p N}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 75 \times 900}{60{,}000} = 3.53 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{commercial-quality matches }C_v \approx 1.5)$$

**Step 3 — Tangential load:**

$$W_t = \frac{2 M_t}{d_p} = \frac{2 \times 79{,}577.5}{75} = 2122.07 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Lewis design equation for module:**

The pinion governs because its Y and σ_b are smaller. Beam strength on pinion:

$$S_b = b \, m \, Y \, [\sigma_b] \geq W_t \, C_v$$

$$m = \frac{W_t \, C_v}{b \, Y \, [\sigma_b]} = \frac{2122.07 \times 1.5}{60 \times 0.32 \times 84} = \frac{3183.1}{1612.8} = 1.974 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Round up to standard module, compute geometry, check for wear indirectly, finalize.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 5 — Standard module:** Round up → **m = 2 mm** (preferred).

**Step 6 — Recompute pitch diameter with chosen z:**

Take $z_1 = 18$ (typical for cast iron, 20° pressure): $d_p = m \, z_1 = 2 \times 18 = 36$ mm.

But this changes $v$ significantly. Iterate:
- New $v = \pi \times 36 \times 900 / 60{,}000 = 1.696$ m/s
- New $C_v$ (commercial) = $4.55 / (4.55 + 1.696) = 4.55 / 6.246 = 0.728$ — **much lower than assumed 1.5!**

This indicates **our assumed z_1 is too small for the assumed loading**. Increase to $z_1 = 24$:
- $d_p = 2 \times 24 = 48$ mm
- $v = \pi \times 48 \times 900 / 60{,}000 = 2.262$ m/s
- $C_v = 4.55 / (4.55 + 2.262) = 4.55 / 6.812 = 0.668$ — still not 1.5

Increase to $z_1 = 32$ and simultaneously consider **larger m**:

Take $z_1 = 20$ and $m = 3$ mm:
- $d_p = 60$ mm
- $v = \pi \times 60 \times 900 / 60{,}000 = 2.827$ m/s
- $C_v = 4.55 / 7.377 = 0.617$

For **cast iron, commercial cut**, the realistic $C_v$ is around 0.5–0.7. We need to **re-state the problem assumption**: $C_v$ in the problem is given as 1.5 — this implies **precision/hobbed quality**. The velocity factor is then $C_v = 3.05 / (3.05 + v)$.

**Re-solve with iteration, using $C_v = 3.05/(3.05+v)$:**

Pick $z_1 = 20$, try $m = 2$ mm:
- $d_p = 40$ mm, $v = 1.885$ m/s, $C_v = 3.05/4.935 = 0.618$
- $W_t = 2 \times 79{,}577.5 / 40 = 3978.9$ N
- Required m: $m = W_t C_v / (b Y \sigma_b) = 3978.9 \times 0.618 / (60 \times 0.32 \times 84) = 2458.96 / 1612.8 = 1.525$ mm

→ Round up to **m = 2 mm** is safe for pinion bending.

For gear bending: $Y_2 = Y(z_2 = 90) \approx 0.485$ (interpolated):

$$S_{b2} = b \, m \, Y_2 \, [\sigma_{b2}] = 60 \times 2 \times 0.485 \times 105 = 6111 \text{ N} \geq 3978.9 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Design:** $m = 2$ mm, $z_1 = 20$, $z_2 = 90$, $b = 60$ mm, $C_v \approx 0.62$ (consistent with hobbed quality at $v = 1.88$ m/s).

> [!TIP]
> **Iterative Note for KTU Students:** Always check whether the *given* $C_v$ in the problem statement matches the *computed* $C_v$ from velocity. If not, **re-iterate** with consistent values. Examiners explicitly look for this re-check.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this box and stick it to your wall before the exam.**

### A. Must-Know Equations (memorize verbatim)

1. $\sigma_b = \dfrac{W_t \, C_v}{b \, m \, Y}$   *(Lewis)*
2. $S_b = b \, m \, Y \, [\sigma_b]$   *(Beam Strength)*
3. $\sigma_c = 0.564 \, \sqrt{ \dfrac{W_t \, C_v \, (r_1 + r_2)}{b \, r_1 \, r_2 \, \sin\phi} }$ *(Buckingham, 20°, steel-steel)*
4. $S_w = \dfrac{b \, d_p \, Q}{2}$ *(Wear Strength, 20°, steel-steel)*
5. $W_t = \dfrac{2 M_t}{d_p} = \dfrac{60{,}000 \, P}{v}$ *(Tangential load)*
6. $v = \dfrac{\pi \, d_p \, N_p}{60{,}000}$ *(Pitch-line velocity in m/s)*
7. $C_v = \dfrac{A}{A + v}$ where A = 3.05 (precision), 4.55 (commercial), 6.10 (cast) *(Velocity factor)*
8. $Q = \dfrac{2 z_2}{z_1 + z_2}$ *(Ratio factor)*

### B. Key Distinctions

- **Lewis** → tooth **bending** failure (root break) → uses $d_p$, $Y$, $[\sigma_b]$.
- **Buckingham** → tooth **surface pitting** failure → uses $d_p$, $Q$, $[\sigma_c]$.
- **Form factor Y** → function of $z$ and φ; rises with $z$; typical: 0.303 (z=17), 0.320 (z=20), 0.485 (z=100) at 20°.
- **Wear strength uses PINION pitch diameter** (NOT gear diameter).
- **Velocity factor C_v is always ≤ 1** for high speeds, equals 1 only for very low speeds; amplifies the static load.

### C. KTU Exam Day Sequence (do it in this order)

1. Convert power to torque ($M_t = 60{,}000 P / 2\pi N$).
2. Pick $z_1 \geq 17$ (for 20° full-depth, no undercut).
3. Read Y from the **form factor table** at $z_1$.
4. Assume $C_v$ and $b/m$ ratio; compute $m_L$ from Lewis.
5. Compute $m_W$ from Buckingham using $[σ_c]$.
6. Take $m = \max(m_L, m_W)$ and **round UP** to standard preferred module.
7. **Re-verify** $C_v$ from actual velocity → if mismatch, iterate.
8. Final answer = (m, z_1, z_2, b, material).

### D. Material Allowable Stresses — Quick Reference (KTU 2024)

| Material | $[\sigma_b]$ N/mm² | $[\sigma_c]$ N/mm² |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Cast Iron (FG 20) | 56 | 350 |
| Cast Steel | 100 | 550 |
| Wrought Steel (1.5 % Ni) | 140 | 700 |
| Case-hardened Ni Steel | 200 | 1050 |

> **Good luck with Module 4. Bend the tooth, don't pit it.** ⚙️
